# 1.5 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**List any three advantages of HVDC transmission over HVAC transmission.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **No stability limit** — DC transmission has no reactance and hence no steady-state/dynamic stability issues; power transfer is limited only by thermal capacity of conductors.
2. **Lower line losses** — No skin effect, no proximity effect, and lower corona loss in DC systems.
3. **No charging current in cables** — DC has zero frequency, so submarine/underground cable length is not limited by capacitive charging current (unlike AC, where it limits length to ~50 km).
4. **Asynchronous interconnection** — HVDC can connect two AC grids of different frequencies (50 Hz ↔ 60 Hz) without synchronization.
5. **Narrower Right of Way** — Bipolar DC requires only 2 conductors, saving ~33% conductor material and tower cost.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**What is the "breakeven distance" in HVDC transmission? State typical values.**

**Model Answer:**
**Breakeven Distance** is the transmission distance at which the **total cost of an HVDC system equals the total cost of an equivalent HVAC system**. Beyond this distance, HVDC is more economical; below it, HVAC is preferred.

**Typical Values:**
- **Overhead transmission:** **500–800 km**
- **Submarine/underground cable:** **40–50 km**

The breakeven distance is shorter for cables because AC cables are expensive (charging current compensation) while DC cables are not affected by charging current.

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q.A.** (a) **Explain in detail the comparison between AC and DC transmission systems, covering technical, economic, and operational aspects.** **(7 Marks)**

(b) **Discuss the various types of HVDC systems with neat diagrams. Which type is most commonly used and why?** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a) [7 Marks]

**1. Technical Comparison (3 Marks):**
- **Conductor Requirement:** AC = 3 conductors, DC (bipolar) = 2 conductors → DC saves ~33% conductor material.
- **Charging Current:** AC cables have $I_c = 2\pi f C V L$, limiting practical cable length to ~50 km. DC has $f = 0$, so no charging current → cables can be hundreds of km long (e.g., NorNed 580 km).
- **Stability:** AC limited by $P_{max} = \frac{V_1 V_2}{X} \sin\delta$. DC has no reactance → no stability limit, power transfer is thermally limited only.
- **Skin Effect:** AC has non-uniform current density (skin effect increases effective resistance). DC has uniform distribution, so $R_{dc}$ is fully utilized.
- **Corona Loss:** $P_{corona} \propto (V_{peak} - V_0)^2$ for AC. For DC, $V_{DC} = V_{peak,AC}/\sqrt{2}$, hence corona loss is lower.
- **Fault Level:** AC adds to system short-circuit MVA. DC converters act as barriers — does not contribute to AC system fault level (useful for weak networks).

**2. Economic Comparison (2 Marks):**
- **DC line cost** < AC line cost (less conductor, narrower RoW, smaller towers)
- **DC converter station cost** >> AC substation cost (thyristor valves, filters, smoothing reactors, control systems)
- **Breakeven Distance:** ~500–800 km for overhead, ~40–50 km for cable
- **Total Cost Equation:** 
$$T_{ac}(L) = (C_{line,ac} + C_{sub,ac}) \cdot L$$
$$T_{dc}(L) = 2 C_{conv} + C_{line,dc} \cdot L$$
- For $L > L_{breakeven}$, $T_{dc} < T_{ac}$.

**3. Operational Comparison (2 Marks):**
- **Power Flow Control:** AC follows Kirchhoff's law (loop flows possible). DC power flow is **explicitly scheduled** via firing angle $\alpha$ — fast, accurate, bi-directional.
- **Reactive Power:** AC transmits both P and Q. DC consumes reactive power at converters (≈50–60% of P) but does not transmit Q on the line.
- **Frequency Independence:** DC decouples two AC systems — different frequencies (50/60 Hz) can be interconnected.
- **Harmonics:** Converters generate characteristic harmonics (11th, 13th, 23rd, 25th for 12-pulse) — need AC and DC filters.
- **DC Circuit Breaking:** DC has no natural zero crossing, so DC breakers are complex and expensive (challenge).

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b) [7 Marks]

**Types of HVDC Systems:**

**1. Monopolar HVDC Link (1.5 Marks):**
- **Configuration:** One high-voltage conductor, return path is either **earth/ground** or a **low-voltage metallic return conductor**.
- **Use Case:** Submarine cable transmission (e.g., **HVDC Cross-Skagerrak** between Norway-Denmark, 240 km).
- **Disadvantage:** Ground return causes **electrode corrosion** and **stray currents** affecting nearby pipelines.
- **Mode 1 (with ground return):** Cheaper, but environmental concerns.
- **Mode 2 (metallic return):** More expensive but environmentally safer.

**2. Bipolar HVDC Link (2 Marks):**
- **Configuration:** **Two conductors** — one at +V_d and one at –V_d. Each pole has its own converter.
- **Normal Operation:** Currents in both poles are equal and opposite → **no ground current flows**.
- **Emergency Operation:** If one pole fails, the other can operate as **monopolar with ground return** → 50% power continuity.
- **Most Common:** Used in majority of HVDC installations worldwide (e.g., Rihand-Delhi ±500 kV, ±800 kV Xiangjiaba-Shanghai).
- **Advantage:** Highest reliability, can operate at half capacity even with one pole out.

**3. Homopolar HVDC Link (1 Mark):**
- **Configuration:** **Two or more conductors**, all of the **same polarity** (either all +ve or all -ve). Ground always returns the current.
- **Advantage:** If one conductor fails, others continue → higher reliability than monopolar.
- **Disadvantage:** Continuous ground current → environmental concerns.
- **Rarely used in modern systems.**

**4. Back-to-Back HVDC Link (1.5 Marks):**
- **Configuration:** Rectifier and inverter are at the **same location** — no DC transmission line.
- **Use Case:** **Asynchronous tie** between two AC systems of different frequencies or phase angles.
- **Examples:** Vindhyachal Back-to-Back (India), Chandrapur Back-to-Back (India-Pakistan), Eel River (Canada, world's first).
- **Advantage:** Small footprint, lower cost (no DC line), fast control.
- **Typical Rating:** 50 MW to 1000 MW.

**5. Multi-Terminal HVDC System (1 Mark):**
- **Configuration:** **Three or more** converter stations connected to a common DC network.
- **Type 1 — Parallel MTDC:** All converters connected in parallel to a common DC voltage.
- **Type 2 — Series MTDC:** All converters connected in series, current is common.
- **Use Case:** Offshore wind farms (multiple wind platforms → one onshore inverter), large interconnected grids.
- **Example:** Sardinia-Corsica-Italy (SACOI) — 3-terminal HVDC.

**Most Commonly Used Type — Bipolar:**
- **Reason:** Highest reliability (can continue at 50% capacity on one pole failure), no continuous ground current in normal operation, scalable to highest voltages (±800 kV, ±1100 kV).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

**Q.B.** (a) **Derive the relationship between AC and DC transmission voltages for transmitting the same power with the same percentage losses.** **(7 Marks)**

(b) **Explain in detail the Bipolar HVDC system with its components, working, advantages, and limitations. Compare it with Monopolar and Back-to-Back systems.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Equate Power (1 Mark)**

Same power $P$ is transmitted by both systems:

$$P = \sqrt{3} V_{LL} I_L \cos\phi = 2 V_d I_d$$

**Step 2: Express Currents (1 Mark)**

$$I_L = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} V_{LL} \cos\phi} \quad \text{and} \quad I_d = \frac{P}{2 V_d}$$

**Step 3: Equate Line Losses (1 Mark)**

For the **same percentage losses**, line losses must be equal. With $R_{ac}$ and $R_{dc}$ being the resistance per conductor (assumed equal for same length, same material):

$$3 I_L^2 R_{ac} = 2 I_d^2 R_{dc}$$

**Step 4: Assume $R_{ac} = R_{dc} = R$ (same conductor, length, material) (1 Mark)**

$$3 I_L^2 = 2 I_d^2$$

**Step 5: Substitute (1 Mark)**

$$3 \left(\frac{P}{\sqrt{3} V_{LL} \cos\phi}\right)^2 = 2 \left(\frac{P}{2 V_d}\right)^2$$

$$\frac{P^2}{V_{LL}^2 \cos^2\phi} = \frac{P^2}{2 V_d^2}$$

**Step 6: Simplify (1 Mark)**

$$2 V_d^2 = V_{LL}^2 \cos^2\phi$$

$$\boxed{V_d = \frac{V_{LL} \cos\phi}{\sqrt{2}}}$$

**Step 7: Interpretation (1 Mark)**
- For $\cos\phi = 1$: $V_d = V_{LL}/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707 \cdot V_{LL}$
- This means for **same power and same losses**, the **DC voltage is 70.7% of the AC line-to-line voltage**.
- This justifies the **lower insulation requirement** in DC systems for equivalent power transfer.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b) [7 Marks]

**1. Bipolar HVDC System Description (2 Marks):**
- Has **two poles** — one with positive DC voltage (+V_d) and one with negative DC voltage (–V_d) with respect to earth.
- **Two separate 12-pulse converter bridges** (one for each pole) at each terminal.
- **DC line has 2 conductors** — positive conductor and negative conductor.
- **Voltage between conductors** = $2 V_d$ (e.g., ±500 kV means conductor-to-conductor voltage = 1000 kV).

**2. Working Principle (2 Marks):**
- AC from sending grid → Step-up transformer → 12-pulse thyristor rectifier → Smoothing reactor → DC line.
- At receiving end: DC line → Smoothing reactor → 12-pulse thyristor inverter → Step-down transformer → AC receiving grid.
- **Normal operation:** Currents in +ve and –ve conductors are equal and **opposite in direction**, so the **net ground current is zero** (no need for electrodes to carry current).
- **Fault on one pole:** The healthy pole can continue operating in **monopolar mode with ground return** (50% capacity).

**3. Components (1.5 Marks):**
- **Thyristor Valves** (12-pulse, ±500 kV typical)
- **Converter Transformers** (with phase shift for harmonic cancellation)
- **Smoothing Reactors** (50–300 mH, limits DC fault current)
- **AC Harmonic Filters** (11th, 13th, 23rd, 25th… characteristic harmonics)
- **DC Harmonic Filters**
- **DC Switchgear** (for pole isolation)
- **Ground Electrodes** (10–50 km from station, used only in emergency)
- **Control & Protection System** (firing angle $\alpha$, extinction angle $\gamma$ control)

**4. Advantages (1 Mark):**
- Highest reliability (50% capacity on one-pole failure)
- No continuous ground current → environmentally friendly
- Can transmit highest power (up to 10,000 MW at ±1100 kV)
- Independent pole control

**5. Limitations (0.5 Marks):**
- Two complete converter stations required → high cost
- More complex control than monopolar

**6. Comparison Table (Bonus 0 Marks — Mentioned verbally):**
- **vs Monopolar:** Bipolar has higher reliability, no continuous ground current, but more cost.
- **vs Back-to-Back:** Bipolar has long DC line and ground electrodes; Back-to-Back has no DC line, used for asynchronous tie.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes & Mark Loss Points (KTU 2024 Exam Pattern):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "Breakeven Distance" with "Critical Distance":** Breakeven is based on **cost**, not technical limits. Do not write "breakeven is the maximum distance AC can transmit" — that's a wrong concept.
> 
> 2. **Skin Effect in DC:** Many students wrongly write "skin effect is reduced in DC" — the correct statement is "skin effect is **absent** in DC" (current distributes uniformly across the cross-section).
> 
> 3. **Stability in DC:** Do NOT write "DC has infinite stability" — write "DC has **no stability limit**; transfer is limited only by thermal capacity and converter rating."
> 
> 4. **Ground Return Current Direction:** In monopolar, return current flows from inverter to rectifier through earth. Many students draw it the wrong way.
> 
> 5. **Bipolar Operation:** When one pole fails, the OTHER pole uses **ground return**, not the failed pole's conductor as return. Many students write wrong.
> 
> 6. **HVDC vs HVDC Light:** In KTU 2024 syllabus, classical HVDC is **thyristor-based LCC**. Do not confuse with VSC-HVDC (often called HVDC Light) unless asked.
> 
> 7. **Reactive Power in DC:** Converters **consume** reactive power (≈50–60% of P), they do not transmit Q on the line. The line itself has **no reactive power flow**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read Before Exam):**
> 
> ### Conceptual Quick-Fire Points
> - **DC saves ~33% conductor material** → narrower RoW
> - **Breakeven distance:** 500–800 km (overhead), 40–50 km (cable)
> - **Skin effect:** Present in AC, **absent in DC**
> - **Charging current:** Limits AC cable to ~50 km; **zero in DC**
> - **Stability:** $P_{max} = \frac{V_1 V_2}{X}\sin\delta$ in AC; **no such limit in DC**
> - **Corona loss:** Lower in DC ($V_{DC} = V_{LL}/\sqrt{2}$ for same RMS)
> - **DC voltage relation:** $V_d = V_{LL} \cos\phi / \sqrt{2}$ for same P and losses
> - **No fault level contribution** in DC → ideal for weak grids
> 
> ### HVDC System Types (Memory Trick: "MBHB M" = **M**onopolar, **B**ipolar, **H**omopolar, **B**ack-to-back, **M**ulti-terminal)
> - **Monopolar:** 1 conductor + ground/metallic return
> - **Bipolar:** 2 conductors (+V, –V) — **most common**
> - **Homopolar:** ≥2 conductors, all same polarity, ground return always
> - **Back-to-back:** Both converters at same station, no DC line
> - **Multi-terminal:** ≥3 converters (parallel or series)
> 
> ### Key Components (Memory: "T-F-R-F-S" in order)
> 1. **T**ransformer (converter)
> 2. **F**ilter (AC side)
> 3. **R**ectifier (thyristor bridge)
> 4. **F**ilter (DC side) + Smoothing **R**eactor
> 5. **S**end DC Line → Inverter → **T**ransformer → AC Grid
> 
> ### Converter Mode
> - **Rectifier:** $\alpha = 5°$ to $20°$ (firing angle)
> - **Inverter:** $\gamma = 15°$ to $20°$ (extinction angle), with $\alpha > 90°$ or $\beta < 90°$
> 
> ### Standard Voltages in Use
> - ±500 kV (most common today)
> - ±800 kV (UHVDC — Xiangjiaba-Shanghai, 6400 MW)
> - ±1100 kV (Changji–Guquan, China — world's first, 12,000 MW)
> 
> ### Real-World Examples
> - **Rihand-Delhi** (India): ±500 kV, 1500 MW, 814 km
> - **Chandrapur-Padghe** (India): ±500 kV, 1500 MW
> - **NorNed** (Norway-Netherlands): ±450 kV, 700 MW, 580 km submarine
> - **NordLink** (Norway-Germany): ±525 kV, 1400 MW, 623 km
> - **North-East Agra** (India): ±800 kV, 6000 MW, 1728 km
> - **Champa-Raigarh** (India, first ±800 kV): 6000 MW

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Current Source Converters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET862 - HVDC AND FACTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Current Source Converters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Current Source Converters

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Current Source Converter (CSC)** is a power electronic converter in which the DC-side current is forced to flow in one direction by a large smoothing inductor, while the DC-side voltage polarity can be reversed to change the direction of power flow. In the HVDC context, the classical CSC is implemented as a **Line-Commutated Converter (LCC)** using **thyristor (SCR) valves** arranged in a **Graetz bridge** (6-pulse) or **double-bridge (12-pulse)** topology. The converter draws a quasi-square-wave line current from the AC system and delivers a nearly constant DC current, hence the name "current source."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the syllabus, CSCs are *always* discussed in conjunction with HVDC. The defining trait is the use of **thyristors** (half-controlled, naturally commutated by the AC grid) and a **DC-side smoothing reactor (Ls)** that enforces unidirectional current flow.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **rotating turnstile at a subway entrance**:
- People (current) can only pass through in one direction; the turnstile mechanically *forces* that direction.
- The direction the turnstile pushes is like the **DC inductor** that forces current to flow in one direction.
- An usher signals *when* a person can enter (the **firing pulse** to the thyristor), but the actual "exit" is decided by the natural flow of the crowd (the **AC grid voltage** turning off the previous thyristor — this is *line commutation*).
- Two turnstiles placed in series but offset in timing let twice as many people through smoothly — this is the **12-pulse converter**.

So, a CSC is essentially a *traffic policeman* (thyristor) who decides which phase of the AC supply is connected to the DC line at any instant, while a large inductor keeps the DC current from surging.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **AC system frequency (f):** **50 Hz** in India (60 Hz in many Western countries)
- **Angular frequency (ω):** **2πf = 314.159 rad/s** (for 50 Hz)
- **Standard HVDC link voltages:** **±500 kV**, **±800 kV** (ultra-HVDC in India)
- **Thyristor blocking voltage class:** Up to **8.5 kV per thyristor**, with hundreds stacked in series per valve
- **Minimum extinction angle (γ_min):** typically **15°–18°** to ensure successful commutation and margin for commutation failure

> [!NOTE]
> A **valve** in HVDC terminology refers to a complete thyristor stack (many series-connected thyristors with their auxiliary circuits) that acts as a controllable switch.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step by Step

The 6-pulse Graetz bridge consists of **6 thyristors** (T1, T2, T3, T4, T5, T6) divided into two groups:

- **Positive group (top):** T1, T3, T5 — connected to the **positive DC terminal**
- **Negative group (bottom):** T2, T4, T6 — connected to the **negative DC terminal**

Each thyristor conducts for **120°** in sequence, and the firing pulses are spaced **60°** apart.

### Step-by-step logic:

1. **DC-side current (Id) is nearly constant** because of the large smoothing reactor (Ls) on the DC side. This is the *defining* property of a CSC.
2. At any instant, **one thyristor from the positive group and one from the negative group** conduct simultaneously, connecting the two most favorably phased AC phases to the DC terminals.
3. The thyristor to be turned ON receives a **firing pulse at firing angle α** measured from the natural commutation point (the instant when the incoming phase voltage becomes higher than the outgoing phase).
4. The thyristor being turned OFF does not stop conducting immediately — there is a brief **overlap period (μ)** during which the outgoing and incoming thyristors conduct together. This happens because the **AC system has source reactance (Xc = ωLs)**.
5. After the overlap ends, the outgoing thyristor is subjected to a reverse bias for the **extinction angle (γ = π − α)** period (in inverter) — this must exceed the thyristor turn-off time, otherwise **commutation failure** occurs.
6. By controlling **α** (firing angle) and **γ** (extinction angle), the converter controls the **DC voltage magnitude and polarity**, and hence the direction of power flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Trap:** Students often confuse the roles of **α**, **μ**, and **γ**. Remember: 
> - **α** = delay in firing (control input)
> - **μ** = duration of commutation overlap (consequence of AC source reactance)
> - **γ** = margin left after commutation ends (must be > thyristor turn-off time)
> - For a rectifier: **α + μ + γ = π** (where γ in this convention is the angle from end of commutation to natural commutation point, sometimes called the extinction advance angle)

## 2.2 The 12-Pulse Converter

Two identical **6-pulse bridges** are connected in **series on the DC side**, but their AC supplies are derived from two transformers:
- One transformer with **Y-Y** connection
- One transformer with **Y-Δ** connection (gives a **30° phase shift**)

This **30° displacement** cancels the **5th and 7th harmonics** on the AC side, leaving only harmonics of order **12k ± 1** (i.e., 11th, 13th, 23rd, 25th…). On the DC side, the **6th and 18th harmonics** in each bridge cancel, leaving **12th, 24th, 36th…** harmonics, which are easier to filter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 12-pulse is the HVDC industry standard:** The elimination of low-order harmonics drastically reduces the size of **AC and DC filters** required, which is a major economic factor in HVDC station design.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Ideal no-load DC voltage (6-pulse) | $V_{d0} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi}\, V_{LL}$ | Volts (V) | $V_{LL}$ is the line-to-line AC RMS voltage; no overlap, α = 0 |
| 2 | DC voltage with firing angle (no overlap) | $V_d = V_{d0} \cos\alpha$ | V | Pure phase-shift control |
| 3 | DC voltage with firing angle AND overlap | $V_d = V_{d0} \cos\alpha - \dfrac{3 X_c}{\pi} I_d$ | V | $X_c = \omega L_c$ is the per-phase commutating reactance |
| 4 | Equivalent form of (3) | $V_d = V_{d0} \cos\alpha - R_c I_d$ | V | $R_c = 3X_c/\pi$ is the *equivalent commutation resistance* |
| 5 | Overlap angle (with delay α) | $\mu = \arccos\!\left(\cos\alpha - \dfrac{\sqrt{2}\, I_d X_c}{V_{LL}}\right) - \alpha$ | rad (or °) | Always positive; increases with $I_d$ and $X_c$ |
| 6 | DC voltage in terms of extinction angle | $V_d = V_{d0} \cos\gamma - \dfrac{3 X_c}{\pi} I_d$ | V | Inverter operation convention; $\gamma = \pi - \alpha - \mu$ in rectifier convention |
| 7 | Angle relationship (rectifier) | $\alpha + \mu + \gamma = \pi$ | — | Or simply: $\beta = \alpha + \mu$, $\gamma = \pi - \beta$ |
| 8 | Displacement power factor | $\cos\phi \approx \cos\!\left(\alpha + \dfrac{\mu}{2}\right)$ | — | Approximate; exact involves Bessel functions of α and μ |
| 9 | Reactive power consumed | $Q = P \tan\phi = V_d I_d \tan\!\left(\alpha + \dfrac{\mu}{2}\right)$ | VAR (or MVAR) | CSC *always* consumes reactive power from AC grid |
| 10 | Active power transferred | $P = V_d I_d$ | W (or MW) | Rectifier: P > 0; Inverter: P > 0 (current reverses sign for reverse power) |
| 11 | RMS line current (fundamental) | $I_{L,1} = \dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi}\, I_d$ | A | For 6-pulse, ideal |
| 12 | RMS thyristor current | $I_{T,rms} = \dfrac{I_d}{\sqrt{3}}$ | A | Each thyristor conducts 120° per cycle |
| 13 | Average thyristor current | $I_{T,avg} = \dfrac{I_d}{3}$ | A | Over 360° |
| 14 | 12-pulse DC voltage | $V_{d,12} = 2 V_{d,6}$ | V | Two 6-pulse bridges in series on DC side |
| 15 | Fundamental displacement angle | $\phi = \alpha + \mu/2$ | rad (or °) | Consequence of natural sampling of the AC waveform |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

CSCs in the form of LCC-HVDC are the **workhorse of long-distance bulk power transmission worldwide** — used in India's **Raigarh-Pugalur ±800 kV UHVDC** link, China's **Xiangjiaba–Shanghai ±800 kV** line, and Brazil's **Itaipu ±600 kV** HVDC. They are preferred over VSC-HVDC for **bulk power transfer (>1000 MW)** and **overhead line transmission** because of their lower losses, higher power density, and decades of operational experience.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Ideal No-Load DC Voltage ($V_{d0}$) for a 6-Pulse Bridge

**Starting point:** Consider the 6-pulse bridge connected to a balanced 3-phase AC source with line-to-line RMS voltage $V_{LL}$. When thyristor T1 is fired, it connects phase A (positive) to the positive DC terminal. We want the *average* DC output voltage.

**Step 1 — Identify the conducting interval.**
Between $\omega t = \pi/6$ and $\omega t = \pi/2$, the thyristors T1 and T6 are conducting, so the DC terminal voltage is the line-to-line voltage $v_{AB}(\omega t)$:

$$
v_{AB}(\omega t) = \sqrt{2}\, V_{LL} \sin\!\left(\omega t + \dfrac{\pi}{6}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that the waveform repeats every 60° (π/3 rad).**
Due to 6-fold symmetry of the bridge, we only need to integrate over one 60° interval, e.g., from $\omega t = \pi/6$ to $\omega t = \pi/2$:

$$
V_{d0} = \dfrac{1}{\pi/3} \int_{\pi/6}^{\pi/2} v_{AB}(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and integrate.**

$$
V_{d0} = \dfrac{3}{\pi} \int_{\pi/6}^{\pi/2} \sqrt{2}\, V_{LL} \sin\!\left(\omega t + \dfrac{\pi}{6}\right) d(\omega t)
$$

Let $u = \omega t + \pi/6$, then $du = d(\omega t)$ and the limits become $u = \pi/3$ to $u = 2\pi/3$:

$$
V_{d0} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}\, V_{LL}}{\pi} \int_{\pi/3}^{2\pi/3} \sin u\, du
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$
\int_{\pi/3}^{2\pi/3} \sin u\, du = \left[-\cos u\right]_{\pi/3}^{2\pi/3} = -\cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi}{3}\right) + \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right) = \dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{1}{2} = 1
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back.**

$$
\boxed{V_{d0} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL} \approx 1.3505\, V_{LL}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** With $V_{LL} = 220$ kV (typical HVDC station AC bus), $V_{d0} \approx 297$ kV per 6-pulse bridge. Two such bridges in series give ≈ 594 kV, close to a ±500 kV bipole. **Checks out.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the DC Voltage with Firing Angle and Overlap

This is the **most important derivation** in the module and is frequently asked in KTU exams.

**Setup:** Firing angle is $\alpha$ (delay from natural commutation point). The source has per-phase commutating reactance $X_c = \omega L_c$. The DC current $I_d$ is assumed constant (large $L_s$).

**Step 1 — Write the line-to-line voltage that would be applied.**
If there were *no* overlap, after firing at angle $\alpha$, the DC terminal voltage would be the *delayed* version of $v_{AB}$:

$$
v_{d,\text{ideal}}(\omega t) = \sqrt{2}\, V_{LL} \sin\!\left(\omega t + \dfrac{\pi}{6} - \alpha\right), \quad \alpha \le \omega t \le \alpha + \pi/3
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that during overlap, both T1 and T3 (or equivalent) conduct.**
During the overlap interval of duration $\mu$, the outgoing thyristor and the incoming thyristor share the current. The DC terminal voltage is the *average* of the two line-to-line voltages, because the AC side is shorted through the source inductors:

$$
v_d(\omega t) = \dfrac{v_{AB}(\omega t) + v_{AC}(\omega t)}{2}, \quad \alpha \le \omega t \le \alpha + \mu
$$

**Step 3 — Express this as a clamped waveform and compute average.**
After the overlap ends, the full line-to-line voltage reappears. The *area lost* due to the delay and overlap is the area between the ideal waveform and the actual waveform over the interval $[\alpha, \alpha + \pi/3]$. The average loss per cycle is:

$$
\Delta V = \dfrac{1}{\pi/3} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \int_{\alpha}^{\alpha+\mu} \!\! \left[\sqrt{2}\,V_{LL}\sin(\omega t + \pi/6) - v_d(\omega t)\right] d(\omega t)
$$

A more standard approach uses the **voltage across the commutating reactance** during overlap. During overlap from phase B to phase C, the difference $v_{BC}$ appears across $2X_c$, driving the commutation current $i$ from 0 to $I_d$:

$$
2 X_c \dfrac{di}{d(\omega t)} = v_{BC}(\omega t) = \sqrt{2}\, V_{LL} \sin(\omega t)
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the commutation current.**

$$
i(\omega t) = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}\, V_{LL}}{2 X_c} \int_{\alpha}^{\omega t} \sin\theta\, d\theta = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}\, V_{LL}}{2 X_c} \left[\cos\alpha - \cos(\omega t)\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the boundary condition $i(\alpha + \mu) = I_d$.**

$$
I_d = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}\, V_{LL}}{2 X_c} \left[\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha + \mu)\right]
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the average DC voltage as the area under the actual waveform.**

The average DC voltage equals the *ideal* average voltage minus the average voltage drop across the commutating reactance. The voltage drop is exactly $\frac{3}{\pi} X_c I_d$ (each commutation has a triangular voltage-area equal to $X_c I_d \cdot \mu$ in the time domain, and there are 3 commutations per cycle for 6-pulse, giving an average of $\frac{X_c I_d \cdot 3\mu}{\pi/3 \cdot \mu}$... standard result):

$$
V_d = V_{d0} \cos\alpha - \dfrac{3 X_c}{\pi} I_d
$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{V_d = V_{d0}\cos\alpha - \dfrac{3 X_c}{\pi} I_d \quad \text{with}\quad \cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha+\mu) = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}\, I_d X_c}{V_{LL}}}
$$

**Numerical sanity check (typical HVDC values):**
- $V_{LL} = 220$ kV, $X_c = 10$ Ω, $I_d = 2000$ A, $\alpha = 15°$
- $V_{d0} = 1.3505 \times 220\,000 \approx 297\,110$ V
- Drop = $\frac{3 \times 10 \times 2000}{\pi} \approx 19\,099$ V
- $V_d = 297\,110 \times \cos 15° - 19\,099 \approx 286\,949 - 19\,099 \approx 267\,850$ V (≈ 268 kV per bridge, 536 kV for 12-pulse — realistic)

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mode of Operation Summary

| Mode | Firing Angle $\alpha$ | DC Voltage $V_d$ | Power Flow | Reactive Power |
|------|-----------------------|------------------|------------|----------------|
| **Rectifier — full conduction limit** | $\alpha = 0°$ | $V_d = V_{d0}$ (ideal) | AC → DC | Smallest (still consumed) |
| **Rectifier — normal operation** | $0° < \alpha < 90°$ | $V_d > 0$, less than $V_{d0}$ | AC → DC | Consumed, increases with $\alpha$ |
| **Inverter operation** | $90° < \alpha < 180°$ | $V_d < 0$ (DC terminal reversed) | DC → AC | Consumed (also high) |
| **Inverter — actual control** | Use $\gamma$ instead | $V_d = V_{d0}\cos\gamma - \frac{3X_c I_d}{\pi}$ | DC → AC | Margin kept via $\gamma$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule:** The same hardware acts as a rectifier *or* an inverter depending on whether the firing angle is below or above 90°. **The current direction never changes** (it is forced by the inductor); only the **voltage polarity** is reversed. This is the very heart of CSC-based HVDC.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Fundamental RMS Line Current

For a 6-pulse bridge with ideal DC current $I_d$, the AC line current is a quasi-square wave of amplitude $I_d$, conducting for 120° per half cycle.

The fundamental component (using Fourier analysis) is:

$$
i_{L,1}(\omega t) = \dfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi}\, I_d \sin(\omega t - \phi)
$$

where $\phi = \alpha + \mu/2$ is the displacement angle. Its RMS value is:

$$
I_{L,1,\text{rms}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi}\, I_d \approx 0.7797\, I_d
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The full RMS line current is $I_{L,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{2/3}\, I_d \approx 0.8165\, I_d$, but only the *fundamental* component transfers useful active power.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Six-Pulse Graetz Bridge — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ACBUS[3 Phase AC Bus VLL]

    subgraph TRAFO["Wye Wye Step Down Transformer"]
        Y1[Y Primary]
        Y2[Y Secondary]
    end

    subgraph BRIDGE["6 Pulse Graetz Bridge"]
        T1[T1 Upper Phase A]
        T3[T3 Upper Phase B]
        T5[T5 Upper Phase C]
        T4[T4 Lower Phase A]
        T6[T6 Lower Phase B]
        T2[T2 Lower Phase C]
    end

    subgraph DCFILTER["DC Side"]
        LS[Smoothing Reactor Ls]
        DCV[DC Voltage Vd]
        DCIDC[DC Current Id]
    end

    DCV_OUT[DC Terminals to HVDC Line]

    ACBUS --> Y1
    Y1 --> Y2
    Y2 --> T1
    Y2 --> T3
    Y2 --> T5
    Y2 --> T4
    Y2 --> T6
    Y2 --> T2

    T1 --> LS
    T3 --> LS
    T5 --> LS
    T4 --> LS
    T6 --> LS
    T2 --> LS

    LS --> DCV
    LS --> DCIDC
    DCV --> DCV_OUT
```

## 4.2 12-Pulse Converter Arrangement (Industry Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GRID[AC Grid]

    YY[Y Y Transformer 0 Degree Shift]
    YD[Y Delta Transformer Plus 30 Degree Shift]

    BR1[Bridge 1 Y Y 6 Pulse]
    BR2[Bridge 2 Y Delta 6 Pulse]

    DCPLUS[Positive DC Terminal]
    DCMINUS[Negative DC Terminal]

    SMOOTH[DC Smoothing Reactor Ls]
    DCLINE[DC Transmission Line to Remote Station]

    GRID --> YY
    GRID --> YD
    YY --> BR1
    YD --> BR2
    BR1 --> DCPLUS
    BR2 --> DCPLUS
    BR1 --> DCMINUS
    BR2 --> DCMINUS
    DCPLUS --> SMOOTH
    SMOOTH --> DCLINE
```

## 4.3 Commutation Sequence and Conduction Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Phase A Most Positive] -->|T1 fires at angle alpha| B[Commutation Overlap mu]
    B -->|T3 takes over| C[Phase B Most Positive]
    C -->|T5 takes over| D[Phase C Most Positive]
    D -->|T1 takes over again| A

    subgraph TIMING["Firing Pulse Sequence 60 degrees apart"]
        P1[Pulse T1]
        P3[Pulse T3]
        P5[Pulse T5]
        P4[Pulse T4]
        P6[Pulse T6]
        P2[Pulse T2]
    end
```

## 4.4 Voltage and Current Waveform Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VLL["AC Line to Line Voltage VLL sinusoidal"]
    VALPHA["Same voltage delayed by firing angle alpha"]
    OVERLAP["Effective DC terminal voltage clamped during overlap mu"]
    VDAVG["Average DC Voltage Vd = Vd0 cos alpha minus 3 Xc Id over pi"]
    ILINE["AC line current quasi square wave amplitude Id"]
    PHI["Displacement angle phi equals alpha plus mu over 2"]

    VLL --> VALPHA
    VALPHA --> OVERLAP
    OVERLAP --> VDAVG
    VDAVG --> ILINE
    ILINE --> PHI
```

## 4.5 Rectifier vs Inverter Mode — Same Hardware, Different Firing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ALPHA_SMALL["alpha less than 90 degrees Rectifier Mode"] -->|Same Bridge| VD_POSITIVE[Vd positive AC to DC]
    ALPHA_LARGE["alpha greater than 90 degrees Inverter Mode"] -->|Same Bridge| VD_NEGATIVE["Vd negative DC to AC"]
    ALPHA_SMALL --> G1[Graetz Bridge]
    ALPHA_LARGE --> G1
    G1 -->|Controlled by alpha| VDC[DC Voltage Vd]
    VDC --> DC_LINK[DC Transmission Link]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading tip for KTU exam:** When asked to draw the *circuit* of a 6-pulse bridge, label the thyristors T1 to T6 in firing order, mark the natural commutation points, and show the direction of Id clearly. Examiners award up to 3 marks just for a clean, fully labeled circuit diagram.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain with a neat sketch the operation of a 6-pulse Graetz bridge used in HVDC systems. State the role of the DC-side smoothing reactor.**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

A 6-pulse Graetz bridge consists of **6 thyristors** (T1–T6) divided into a **positive group** (T1, T3, T5) and a **negative group** (T2, T4, T6). Connected to a 3-phase AC source, each thyristor conducts for **120° per cycle**, and **firing pulses are 60° apart**. At any instant, one thyristor from each group conducts, connecting the most positive and most negative AC phase to the DC terminals. The DC output voltage is a quasi-six-step waveform with **6 pulses per cycle**, hence the name.

The **DC-side smoothing reactor ($L_s$)** serves three critical purposes:
1. **Enforces a near-constant DC current** (Id), making the system behave as a *current source*.
2. **Limits the rate of change of current** during DC line faults.
3. **Prevents current discontinuities** at low load, which would distort the converter operation.

(3 marks: 1 for sketch, 1 for operation, 1 for reactor role)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Why is a 12-pulse converter preferred over a 6-pulse converter in modern HVDC installations?**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

A 12-pulse converter is built from **two 6-pulse bridges in series on the DC side**, fed by a **Y-Y** and a **Y-Δ** transformer (30° phase shift). It is preferred because:

1. **Cancellation of low-order AC harmonics:** The 30° phase shift causes the **5th and 7th harmonics** (and all 6k ± 1 for k = 1) to cancel on the AC side. Only harmonics of order **12k ± 1** (11, 13, 23, 25…) remain, which are much smaller and easier to filter.

2. **Cancellation of low-order DC harmonics:** The **6th harmonic** in each bridge cancels on the DC side, leaving only **12th, 24th…** — much easier to filter, so smaller DC filters are needed.

3. **Double the DC voltage** for the same AC side, reducing the per-valve current rating.

4. **Reduced telephone interference and lower AC filter cost** — a major economic advantage for HVDC stations.

(3 marks: 1 for configuration, 2 for advantages)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the average DC output voltage of a 6-pulse converter with firing angle $\alpha$ and commutation overlap $\mu$. Clearly state the assumptions. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 6-pulse converter is supplied from a 3-phase, 220 kV, 50 Hz AC source through a transformer with per-phase leakage reactance of 10 Ω. The DC current is 2000 A, the firing angle is 15°, and the DC link is operating in rectifier mode. Calculate:
   (i) The ideal no-load DC voltage $V_{d0}$
   (ii) The DC terminal voltage $V_d$ including overlap
   (iii) The overlap angle $\mu$ in degrees
   (iv) The displacement power factor

Take $\cos 15° = 0.9659$, $\cos 18° = 0.9511$, $\cos 33° = 0.8387$, $\cos 47° = 0.6820$, $\cos 60° = 0.5000$, $\cos 90° = 0$. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation**

*(See Section 3.2 of these notes for the full step-by-step derivation. Key steps to include:)*

**Assumptions:**
1. Thyristors are ideal switches (zero on-state voltage drop, zero turn-off time).
2. AC source is balanced, sinusoidal, and stiff.
3. DC current $I_d$ is constant (ripple-free due to large $L_s$).
4. Source has only inductive impedance (pure reactance $X_c$).
5. Firing pulses are equidistant (60° apart) and perfectly symmetric.

**Final boxed result:**

$$
V_d = V_{d0}\cos\alpha - \dfrac{3 X_c}{\pi} I_d
$$

with the overlap relation:

$$
\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha + \mu) = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}\, X_c I_d}{V_{LL}}
$$

(Marks: 2 for assumptions, 3 for derivation steps, 2 for the two final equations)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given:** $V_{LL} = 220$ kV, $f = 50$ Hz, $X_c = 10$ Ω, $I_d = 2000$ A, $\alpha = 15°$.

**(i) Ideal no-load DC voltage:**

$$
V_{d0} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot V_{LL} = \dfrac{3 \times 1.4142}{\pi} \times 220\,000
$$

$$
V_{d0} = \dfrac{4.2426 \times 220\,000}{\pi} = \dfrac{933\,375}{\pi} \approx 297\,110 \text{ V} \approx 297.11 \text{ kV}
$$

**(ii) DC terminal voltage with overlap:**

$$
V_d = V_{d0}\cos\alpha - \dfrac{3 X_c}{\pi} I_d
$$

$$
V_d = 297\,110 \times 0.9659 - \dfrac{3 \times 10 \times 2000}{\pi}
$$

$$
V_d = 286\,945.5 - \dfrac{60\,000}{\pi} = 286\,945.5 - 19\,098.6
$$

$$
\boxed{V_d \approx 267\,847 \text{ V} \approx 267.85 \text{ kV}}
$$

**(iii) Overlap angle $\mu$:**

$$
\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha + \mu) = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}\, X_c I_d}{V_{LL}}
$$

RHS:

$$
\dfrac{1.4142 \times 10 \times 2000}{220\,000} = \dfrac{28\,284}{220\,000} \approx 0.1286
$$

So:

$$
\cos(\alpha + \mu) = \cos 15° - 0.1286 = 0.9659 - 0.1286 = 0.8373
$$

The angle whose cosine is closest to 0.8373 from the given table: $\cos 33° = 0.8387$ — this matches!

So:

$$
\alpha + \mu = 33° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu = 33° - 15° = 18°
$$

$$
\boxed{\mu = 18°}
$$

**(iv) Displacement power factor:**

$$
\phi = \alpha + \dfrac{\mu}{2} = 15° + 9° = 24°
$$

$$
\cos\phi = \cos 24° \approx 0.9135
$$

(For better accuracy, use the more exact relation; the approximate form gives 0.9135. The exact value is very close.)

$$
\boxed{\text{Power factor} \approx 0.91 \text{ lagging}}
$$

(Marks distribution: (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 2 marks, (iv) 1 mark)

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram and waveforms, explain the principle of commutation in a line-commutated current source converter. Define overlap angle, extinction angle, and firing angle, and derive the relation $\alpha + \mu + \gamma = \pi$ for a rectifier. (7 marks)

**(b)** A bipolar ±500 kV HVDC link uses two 12-pulse converter groups per pole at the rectifier end. Each 6-pulse bridge is fed from a 230 kV (line-to-line) AC bus through a transformer with 8 Ω leakage reactance per phase. The rated DC current is 2000 A, and the rectifier operates at a firing angle of 12° in normal operation. Compute:
   (i) The DC voltage per 6-pulse bridge
   (ii) The total DC voltage per pole
   (iii) The reactive power consumed by the converter at this operating point
   (iv) The extinction angle $\gamma$ (in degrees)

Take $\cos 12° = 0.9781$, $\cos 18° = 0.9511$, $\cos 22° = 0.9272$, $\cos 25° = 0.9063$, $\cos 28° = 0.8829$, $\cos 30° = 0.8660$. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Theory**

*(Outline for full marks:)*

1. **Circuit diagram:** 6-pulse bridge with phases A, B, C; thyristors T1–T6; DC terminals; commutating reactance $X_c$. (1 mark)

2. **Waveforms:** Show $v_{AB}$, $v_{BC}$, $v_{CA}$, the firing pulses, the AC line current, and the DC voltage (with overlap region shaded). (2 marks)

3. **Definitions:**
   - **Firing angle $\alpha$:** The delay between the natural commutation instant (where incoming phase voltage equals outgoing phase voltage) and the actual firing pulse.
   - **Overlap angle $\mu$:** The angular duration during which the outgoing and incoming thyristors conduct together.
   - **Extinction angle $\gamma$:** The angular margin between the end of commutation and the next natural commutation point (where reverse bias on the outgoing thyristor ends). (2 marks)

4. **Derivation of $\alpha + \mu + \gamma = \pi$:**
   - The natural commutation points are spaced $\pi/3$ apart.
   - In a rectifier, the next commutation point is $\pi/3$ after the *firing* of the current thyristor.
   - From the firing instant, the overlap takes $\mu$, and the extinction angle is the remaining margin: $\gamma = \pi/3 - \mu$ for a single commutation interval.

   For a *half cycle* ($\pi$ rad) with three commutations:

$$
\gamma = \pi - (\alpha + \mu)
$$

   Rearranged:

$$
\boxed{\alpha + \mu + \gamma = \pi}
$$

(2 marks)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given:** $V_{LL} = 230$ kV, $X_c = 8$ Ω, $I_d = 2000$ A, $\alpha = 12°$, 12-pulse = two 6-pulse bridges in series.

**(i) DC voltage per 6-pulse bridge:**

$$
V_{d0} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \times 230\,000 = 1.3505 \times 230\,000 \approx 310\,615 \text{ V}
$$

Voltage drop:

$$
\dfrac{3 X_c}{\pi} I_d = \dfrac{3 \times 8 \times 2000}{\pi} = \dfrac{48\,000}{\pi} \approx 15\,279 \text{ V}
$$

Per-bridge DC voltage:

$$
V_{d,6} = 310\,615 \times \cos 12° - 15\,279 = 310\,615 \times 0.9781 - 15\,279
$$

$$
V_{d,6} = 303\,806 - 15\,279 \approx 288\,527 \text{ V} \approx 288.53 \text{ kV}
$$

**(ii) Total DC voltage per pole (12-pulse):**

$$
V_{d,12} = 2 \times 288.53 \approx 577.05 \text{ kV}
$$

**(iii) Reactive power consumed:**

First find overlap:

$$
\cos(\alpha + \mu) = \cos 12° - \dfrac{\sqrt{2} \times 8 \times 2000}{230\,000} = 0.9781 - \dfrac{22\,627}{230\,000} = 0.9781 - 0.0984 = 0.8797
$$

The closest from the table: $\cos 28° = 0.8829$, $\cos 30° = 0.8660$. By linear interpolation, $\alpha + \mu \approx 28.4°$, so $\mu \approx 16.4°$.

Displacement angle:

$$
\phi = \alpha + \dfrac{\mu}{2} = 12° + 8.2° = 20.2°
$$

Reactive power per bridge:

$$
Q = V_{d,6} \cdot I_d \cdot \tan\phi = 288\,527 \times 2000 \times \tan 20.2° \approx 577.05 \times 10^6 \times 0.368
$$

$$
Q \approx 212.4 \text{ MVAR per 6-pulse bridge}
$$

For 12-pulse (two bridges):

$$
Q_{\text{total}} \approx 2 \times 212.4 \approx 424.8 \text{ MVAR}
$$

**(iv) Extinction angle:**

$$
\gamma = \pi - \alpha - \mu = 180° - 12° - 16.4° \approx 151.6°
$$

Or in the inverter convention where $\gamma = \pi - \alpha$ (this rectifier equivalent extinction advance angle):

$$
\gamma_{\text{rectifier sense}} = 180° - 12° - 16.4° = 151.6°
$$

(Examiners may use the *inverter-side* definition where the extinction advance is $\pi - (\alpha + \mu)$. The numerical answer remains 151.6°.)

(Alternative cleaner approach: using $V_d = V_{d0}\cos\gamma - \frac{3X_cI_d}{\pi}$ and the per-bridge $V_{d,6}$, students can back-calculate $\gamma$ directly.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign of overlap:** Some students write $\mu$ as negative or confuse it with the extinction advance angle. Always remember: $\mu$ is the *duration* of commutation, hence always **positive** and typically between 15° and 30° in practice.
> 2. **Units in overlap calculation:** The relation $\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha+\mu) = \frac{\sqrt{2}X_cI_d}{V_{LL}}$ has $X_c$ in **ohms**, $I_d$ in **amperes**, and $V_{LL}$ in **volts** (or consistent multiples). Mixing kV and A without converting is the most common error — you will lose 2 marks instantly.
> 3. **Active vs reactive power:** CSCs *always consume* reactive power when operating, whether in rectifier or inverter mode. Forgetting this in the conclusion loses 1 mark.
> 4. **DC polarity in inverter mode:** When $\alpha > 90°$, $V_d$ becomes negative; some students forget to mention the polarity reversal. Always state explicitly.
> 5. **Sketch quality:** In Part A and Part B theory questions, a poorly labeled sketch costs 1–2 marks. Always label thyristors in firing order, mark $\alpha$, $\mu$, and the AC phases.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **CSC = Thyristor-based + DC smoothing inductor = unidirectional DC current, bidirectional DC voltage.**
- **Graetz bridge** = 6 thyristors, two groups, each thyristor conducts **120°**, firing every **60°**.
- **Ideal DC voltage:** $V_{d0} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi}V_{LL} \approx 1.3505\,V_{LL}$.
- **Real DC voltage:** $V_d = V_{d0}\cos\alpha - \dfrac{3X_c}{\pi}I_d$.
- **Overlap relation:** $\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha + \mu) = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}X_cI_d}{V_{LL}}$.
- **Angle identity:** $\alpha + \mu + \gamma = \pi$ (in rectifier convention).
- **Rectifier:** $0° < \alpha < 90°$ → $V_d > 0$, AC → DC.
- **Inverter:** $90° < \alpha < 180°$ → $V_d < 0$, DC → AC. Control via $\gamma$ (typically 15°–18° minimum).
- **12-pulse:** Two 6-pulse bridges in series, fed by Y-Y and Y-Δ transformers (30° shift). Cancels 5th, 7th, 6th harmonics. Industry standard.
- **Fundamental RMS line current:** $I_{L,1} = \dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi}I_d$.
- **Displacement angle:** $\phi = \alpha + \mu/2$ → approximate power factor $\cos\phi$.
- **Reactive power always consumed** by CSC; supply must come from AC filters, shunt capacitors, or synchronous condensers.
- **Commutation failure** in inverter occurs if $\gamma < \gamma_{min}$ (thyristor turn-off time not respected).
- **Equivalent commutation resistance:** $R_c = 3X_c/\pi$ — useful for circuit analysis.
- **Real HVDC examples:** Rihand–Delhi, Chandrapur–Padghe, Talcher–Kolar, Raigarh–Pugalur (all ±500 kV or ±800 kV, all CSC-based).
- **Exam tip:** Numerical problems in KTU *always* give a cosine table; practice using interpolation from the table — examiners do not expect students to use calculators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra for this module:** 
> *"Same hardware, two roles — fire early to rectify, fire late to invert. The inductor keeps current one-way; the angle controls the voltage. Remember $V_{d0} \approx 1.35\,V_{LL}$, and the rest is algebra."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis Without and With Overlap Period, Voltage Source Converters (VSC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET862 - HVDC AND FACTS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Analysis Without and With Overlap Period, Voltage Source Converters (VSC)** # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Fundamental Definition: Converter Analysis in HVDC In HVDC transmission systems, **converter analysis** refers to the mathematical and circuit-level evaluation of the AC-to-DC (rectification) and DC-to-AC (inversion) conversion processes. The analysis is performed in two idealized scenarios: - **Analysis Without Overlap Period**: Assumes ideal thyristor commutation where the outgoing thyristor ceases conduction and the incoming thyristor begins conduction *instantaneously* (i.e., commutation overlap angle $\mu = 0$). - **Analysis With Overlap Period**: Accounts for the *finite* commutation time during which **two thyristors** (one from the outgoing phase and one from the incoming phase) conduct simultaneously, due to the presence of source/transformer reactance $X_c$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: For the HVDC and FACTS course, Module 1 specifically requires a rigorous treatment of 6-pulse and 12-pulse converters under both overlap-free and overlap conditions, including the derivation of the average DC voltage equation. ## 1.2 Definition: Commutation Overlap Period The **commutation overlap period** is the finite electrical angular duration ($\mu$ in degrees or radians) during which the current transfers from one thyristor (or valve) to another. During this interval, the AC source inductance ($L_c$) opposes any instantaneous change in current, leading to simultaneous conduction. Mathematically: $$\mu = \gamma - \alpha \quad \text{(for inverter mode)}$$ where $\alpha$ is the **firing angle (delay angle)** and $\gamma$ is the **extinction angle**. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Overlap Assumption in KTU Problems**: Unless specified, the KTU 2024 examination assumes **symmetrical 3-phase, 6-pulse Graetz bridge** configuration with a commutation reactance $X_c = \omega L_c$ per phase, and ripple-free, smooth DC current $I_d$. ## 1.3 Definition: Voltage Source Converters (VSC) A **Voltage Source Converter (VSC)** is a self-commutated, solid-state power electronic converter that uses *controllable turn-off* semiconductor switches such as **Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistors (IGBTs)**, **Gate Turn-Off (GTO) thyristors**, or **IGCTs**. The DC side employs a *constant DC voltage source* (typically a large DC capacitor) rather than the current source used in classical Line Commutated Converters (LCC). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why VSC matters in modern HVDC**: VSC technology eliminates the need for an external commutation voltage, allows independent control of **active power (P)** and **reactive power (Q)**, and enables feeding into **weak or even passive AC networks** — a key advantage for offshore wind farms and asynchronous grid interconnections. ## 1.4 Intuitive Analogies | Concept | Real-World Analogy | |---------|-------------------| | **Converter without overlap** | A perfectly timed baton pass in a relay race — runners transfer the baton in zero time. Theoretically ideal but unrealistic. | | **Converter with overlap** | Two runners briefly *co-running* during the baton pass; both hold the baton momentarily because the receiver cannot accelerate instantly. | | **Commutation reactance $X_c$** | The inertia of the baton (resistance to instant transfer). | | **Line Commutated Converter (LCC)** | A rower who can only push forward — needs the AC wave to "give" them timing. | | **Voltage Source Converter (VSC)** | A motor-powered paddle — can push or pull the boat independently, with full control. | | **Firing angle $\alpha$** | The precise instant when the "push" is applied relative to the AC wave. | | **Overlap angle $\mu$** | The brief interval where two rowers share the load while transferring the oar. | ## 1.5 Standard Reference Constants > [!NOTE] > The following constants/metrics are repeatedly used in KTU board problems: > - **3-phase RMS line voltage**: $V_{LL}$ (in Volts, typically **100 kV to 800 kV** for HVDC) > - **Peak phase voltage**: $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{ph} = \sqrt{2/3} \cdot V_{LL} = \sqrt{2} V_{LL}/\sqrt{3}$ > - **Per-phase commutation reactance**: $X_c$ (in ohms) > - **DC current**: $I_d$ (smooth, ripple-free) > - **Average DC voltage (no overlap)**: $V_{d0} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL}$ ≈ **1.35 $V_{LL}$** > - **Frequency**: $f = \mathbf{50 \text{ Hz}}$ in India (60 Hz in some countries) > - **DC link voltage** (for VSC-HVDC): typically **±150 kV to ±525 kV** for commercial projects # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: 6-Pulse Graetz Bridge The 6-pulse bridge is composed of **6 thyristors** ($T_1$ to $T_6$) arranged as 3 upper and 3 lower devices. The valves are fired in the sequence $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4, T_5, T_6$ at intervals of **60° (π/3 radians)**. Each thyristor conducts for **120° (2π/3 radians)** in the no-overlap case. ### 2.1.1 Operating Logic — Without Overlap (μ = 0) 1. At any instant, **two thyristors conduct**: one from the positive group and one from the negative group. 2. The phase-to-neutral voltage of the conducting phases sums to produce the instantaneous DC output voltage. 3. Firing is initiated at angle $\alpha$ *after* the natural commutation point. 4. The output waveform is a **6-pulse ripple** with average value $V_d$. ### 2.1.2 Operating Logic — With Overlap (μ > 0) 1. During commutation, **three thyristors conduct** (two from the incoming/outgoing pair plus the return path thyristor). 2. The commutation is **not abrupt** — current in the outgoing thyristor decays exponentially from $I_d$ to 0, while the incoming thyristor current rises from 0 to $I_d$. 3. The DC voltage waveform exhibits **"notches"** of width $\mu$ at each commutation instant. 4. Average DC voltage is **reduced** by a factor depending on $I_d$ and $X_c$. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Overlap Phenomenon > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Physical Reason**: AC source/transformer inductance $L_c$ enforces Faraday's law — current cannot change instantaneously. The voltage equation $V = L \cdot (di/dt)$ means a finite voltage is required to transfer current from one phase to another, which *subtracts* from the available DC output voltage. This voltage loss is the **commutation drop** or **overlap voltage drop** $\Delta V_d$. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Table) > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **exam-critical** and form the core of Module 1 derivations. | # | Formula | Description / Condition | Unit | |---|---------|------------------------|------| | 1 | $V_{d0} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL}$ | Average DC voltage, no overlap, $\alpha = 0$ (rectifier, natural commutation) | Volts | | 2 | $V_d = V_{d0} \cos \alpha$ | Average DC voltage, no overlap, with firing delay $\alpha$ | Volts | | 3 | $V_d = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL} \cos \alpha - \dfrac{3 X_c I_d}{\pi}$ | Average DC voltage **with overlap** (most important!) | Volts | | 4 | $V_d = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL} \cos \alpha - R_c I_d$ | Empirical form — equivalent commutation resistance $R_c = \dfrac{3 X_c}{\pi}$ | Volts | | 5 | $V_{d0} \left[\cos \alpha - \cos(\alpha + \mu)\right] = \dfrac{3 X_c I_d}{\pi}$ | Relation between overlap, firing angle, current and reactance | Volts | | 6 | $\cos \alpha - \cos(\alpha + \mu) = \dfrac{2 X_c I_d}{\sqrt{2} \, V_{LL}}$ | Simplified form of (5) — *most asked in KTU* | dimensionless | | 7 | $\cos(\alpha + \mu) = \cos \alpha - \dfrac{\sqrt{2} X_c I_d}{V_{LL}}$ | Alternative rearrangement of (6) | dimensionless | | 8 | $I_d = \dfrac{V_{d0}}{2 X_c} \left[\cos \alpha - \cos(\alpha + \mu)\right]$ | Direct current expression under overlap | Amps | | 9 | $P_d = V_d \cdot I_d$ | DC power transmitted | Watts | | 10 | $\eta_{rect} = \dfrac{V_d \cos \alpha}{V_{d0}}$ | Rectification efficiency | p.u. | | 11 | $P + jQ = \sqrt{3} V_{LL} I \angle (\delta - \phi)$ | VSC active/reactive power (PQ theory, $\delta$ = voltage angle, $\phi$ = current angle) | VA | | 12 | $V_{dc} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot m \cdot V_{LL}$ | VSC DC link voltage from PWM modulation index $m$ | Volts | | 13 | $V_{out} = m \cdot \dfrac{V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}$ | RMS line-to-line VSC output voltage | Volts | | 14 | $I_{RMS} = m \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}} I_d$ | VSC AC-side RMS current | Amps | > [!NOTE] > In the formulas above, the symbols are: $V_{LL}$ = RMS line-to-line AC voltage, $\alpha$ = firing angle, $\mu$ = overlap angle, $X_c$ = per-phase commutation reactance, $I_d$ = DC current, $m$ = PWM modulation index (0 to 1). ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **LCC-HVDC (with overlap)**: Used in classical bulk power transmission such as the **±800 kV Rihand–Delhi HVDC link** in India. Overlap drop is a critical loss component (typically **3-5% of Vd0**). - **VSC-HVDC**: Used in **offshore wind integration** (e.g., BorWin, DolWin projects in the North Sea), city-center infeeds (e.g., **INELFE** France-Spain interconnection), and **black-start capability** scenarios. - **PQ independence of VSC**: Allows grid operators to *inject* or *absorb* reactive power (STATCOM-like behavior) without changing active power flow — a feature impossible with LCC. - **Black-start capability**: VSC can energize a *dead* grid (no pre-existing AC voltage), making it invaluable for system restoration. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Average DC Voltage Without Overlap (μ = 0) ### Step 1: Identify the Instantaneous Output Voltage For a 6-pulse bridge firing thyristors $T_1, T_3, T_5$ in the upper group and $T_2, T_4, T_6$ in the lower group, the instantaneous DC output voltage is the difference between the phase-to-neutral voltages of the two conducting phases. Let the three-phase voltages be: $$v_a = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$ $$v_b = V_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$$ $$v_c = V_m \sin(\omega t + 120°)$$ where $V_m$ is the peak phase voltage. ### Step 2: Express the Conduction Intervals In the absence of overlap, conduction occurs for exactly **60° per step**. Considering thyristor $T_1$ in the upper group and $T_6$ in the lower group conducting in the interval $\alpha \le \omega t \le \alpha + 60°$, the instantaneous DC output voltage is: $$v_d(\omega t) = v_a - v_b = V_m \sin(\omega t) - V_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$$ ### Step 3: Simplify Using Trigonometric Identity Using the identity $\sin A - \sin B = 2 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{A+B}{2}\right) \sin\!\left(\dfrac{A-B}{2}\right)$: $$v_d = 2 V_m \cos\!\left(\omega t - 60°\right) \sin(60°)$$ $$v_d = 2 V_m \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cos(\omega t - 60°)$$ $$v_d = \sqrt{3} \, V_m \cos(\omega t - 60°)$$ ### Step 4: Compute the Average Value The average DC voltage is obtained by integrating over one conduction interval of 60° (i.e., $\pi/3$ radians): $$V_d = \frac{1}{\pi/3} \int_{\alpha}^{\alpha + \pi/3} v_d(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$ $$V_d = \frac{3}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\alpha + \pi/3} \sqrt{3} V_m \cos(\omega t - 60°) \, d(\omega t)$$ Let $u = \omega t - 60°$, so $du = d(\omega t)$. The integration limits become $(\alpha - 60°)$ to $(\alpha)$: $$V_d = \frac{3\sqrt{3} V_m}{\pi} \int_{\alpha - 60°}^{\alpha} \cos(u) \, du$$ $$V_d = \frac{3\sqrt{3} V_m}{\pi} \left[ \sin(u) \right]_{\alpha - 60°}^{\alpha}$$ $$V_d = \frac{3\sqrt{3} V_m}{\pi} \left[ \sin(\alpha) - \sin(\alpha - 60°) \right]$$ ### Step 5: Apply the Difference of Sines Identity Using $\sin A - \sin B = 2 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{A+B}{2}\right) \sin\!\left(\dfrac{A-B}{2}\right)$ with $A = \alpha$, $B = \alpha - 60°$: $$V_d = \frac{3\sqrt{3} V_m}{\pi} \cdot 2 \cos(\alpha - 30°) \sin(30°)$$ $$V_d = \frac{3\sqrt{3} V_m}{\pi} \cdot 2 \cos(\alpha - 30°) \cdot \frac{1}{2}$$ $$V_d = \frac{3\sqrt{3} V_m}{\pi} \cos(\alpha - 30°)$$ ### Step 6: Relate Peak Phase Voltage to Line Voltage For a star-connected source: $V_{LL} = \sqrt{3} V_{ph} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$, therefore: $$V_m = \frac{\sqrt{2} V_{LL}}{\sqrt{3}}$$ Substituting into the expression for $V_d$: $$V_d = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2} V_{LL}}{\sqrt{3}} \cos(\alpha - 30°)$$ $$V_d = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL} \cos(\alpha - 30°)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Result (No Overlap)**: $\boxed{V_d = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL} \cos(\alpha - 30°)}$ — Note the **30° phase shift** is sometimes absorbed into the firing angle reference, giving the more common form $V_d = V_{d0} \cos \alpha$ where $V_{d0} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL}$ ≈ **1.35 $V_{LL}$**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Average DC Voltage With Overlap (μ > 0) ### Step 1: Setup the Circuit During Commutation Consider commutation from thyristor $T_5$ to $T_1$ in the upper group at angle $\alpha$. During the overlap interval $\alpha \le \omega t \le \alpha + \mu$, both $T_5$ and $T_1$ conduct. The DC current $I_d$ is shared between them, with $i_5$ decreasing from $I_d$ to 0 and $i_1$ increasing from 0 to $I_d$. ### Step 2: Apply KVL to the Commutating Phases The voltage equation across the two commutating phases (a and c) is: $$v_a - v_c = L_c \frac{di_1}{dt} - L_c \frac{di_5}{dt}$$ By Kirchhoff's Current Law: $i_1 + i_5 = I_d$, so $\dfrac{di_5}{dt} = -\dfrac{di_1}{dt}$. Therefore: $$v_a - v_c = 2 L_c \frac{di_1}{dt}$$ ### Step 3: Substitute Phase Voltage Expressions $$V_m \sin(\omega t) - V_m \sin(\omega t - 120°) = 2 L_c \frac{di_1}{dt}$$ Using the identity again: $$2 V_m \cos(\omega t - 60°) \sin(60°) = 2 L_c \frac{di_1}{dt}$$ $$\sqrt{3} V_m \cos(\omega t - 60°) = 2 L_c \frac{di_1}{dt}$$ ### Step 4: Integrate Over the Overlap Interval $$\int_{\alpha}^{\alpha + \mu} \sqrt{3} V_m \cos(\omega t - 60°) \, d(\omega t) = 2 \omega L_c \int_{0}^{I_d} di_1$$ $$\sqrt{3} V_m \left[ \sin(\omega t - 60°) \right]_{\alpha}^{\alpha + \mu} = 2 \omega L_c I_d$$ $$\sqrt{3} V_m \left[ \sin(\alpha + \mu - 60°) - \sin(\alpha - 60°) \right] = 2 X_c I_d$$ ### Step 5: Apply Difference of Sines Identity Using $\sin P - \sin Q = 2 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{P+Q}{2}\right) \sin\!\left(\dfrac{P-Q}{2}\right)$ with $P = \alpha + \mu - 60°$ and $Q = \alpha - 60°$: $$\sin(\alpha + \mu - 60°) - \sin(\alpha - 60°) = 2 \cos(\alpha + \mu/2 - 60°) \sin(\mu/2)$$ Therefore: $$2 \sqrt{3} V_m \cos(\alpha + \mu/2 - 60°) \sin(\mu/2) = 2 X_c I_d$$ ### Step 6: Express the DC Voltage Including the Notch The instantaneous DC voltage during the overlap interval is the average of the two commutating phase voltages (since both conduct): $$v_d = \frac{v_a + v_c}{2} - v_b$$ The reduction in DC voltage from the ideal case equals the area of the "notch", which integrated over the conduction period gives: $$V_{d,\text{overlap}} = V_{d0} \cos \alpha - \frac{3 X_c I_d}{\pi}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Result (With Overlap)**: $\boxed{V_d = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL} \cos \alpha - \dfrac{3 X_c I_d}{\pi} = V_{d0} \cos \alpha - R_c I_d}$ > where $R_c = \dfrac{3 X_c}{\pi}$ is the **equivalent commutation resistance** (a fictitious resistor representing the overlap drop). ### Step 7: Derive the Critical Overlap Angle Relation Combining the commutation KVL result with the overlap voltage drop: $$\cos \alpha - \cos(\alpha + \mu) = \frac{2 X_c I_d}{\sqrt{2} V_{LL}} = \frac{\sqrt{2} X_c I_d}{V_{LL}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **most-frequently tested equation** in KTU 2024 scheme HVDC examinations. Students are often asked to solve for $\mu$ given $V_{LL}$, $X_c$, $I_d$, and $\alpha$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: VSC Steady-State Power Equations ### Step 1: Define the d-q Reference Frame In a synchronously rotating d-q reference frame aligned with the AC voltage vector: $$v_d = V_s \quad \text{(d-axis component)}$$ $$v_q = 0 \quad \text{(q-axis component)}$$ The VSC output AC voltage in the d-q frame is: $$v_{c,d} = m \frac{V_{dc}}{2} \cos \delta$$ $$v_{c,q} = m \frac{V_{dc}}{2} \sin \delta$$ where $m$ is the PWM modulation index and $\delta$ is the angle between the converter AC voltage and the grid voltage (power angle). ### Step 2: Compute Active and Reactive Power The instantaneous complex power at the VSC AC terminal is: $$S = P + jQ = \frac{3}{2} (v_d + j v_q)(i_d - j i_q)$$ For a lossless converter with filter reactance $X_f$: $$P = \frac{3 V_s V_c}{2 X_f} \sin \delta$$ $$Q = \frac{3 V_s}{2 X_f} (V_s - V_c \cos \delta)$$ where $V_c = m V_{dc}/2$. ### Step 3: Interpret the Control Variables > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Key Insight**: > - **$\delta$ controls active power $P$** (phase angle of VSC voltage) > - **$m$ controls reactive power $Q$** (magnitude of VSC voltage) > > This **decoupled control** is the fundamental advantage of VSC over LCC for HVDC applications. --- ## 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) > **Problem**: A 6-pulse Graetz bridge converter operates from a 3-phase, 50 Hz, 220 kV (line-to-line) source with per-phase commutation reactance $X_c = 10 \ \Omega$. The firing angle is $\alpha = 15°$ and the DC current is $I_d = 1000$ A. Calculate (a) the average DC voltage *without* overlap, (b) the average DC voltage *with* overlap, (c) the overlap angle $\mu$. ### Solution **(a) Without overlap:** $$V_{d0} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \times 220{,}000 = 1.35 \times 220{,}000 = 297{,}000 \text{ V}$$ $$V_d = V_{d0} \cos \alpha = 297{,}000 \times \cos(15°) = 297{,}000 \times 0.9659 = 286{,}876 \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{V_d \approx 286.88 \text{ kV}}$$ **(b) With overlap:** $$V_d = V_{d0} \cos \alpha - \frac{3 X_c I_d}{\pi}$$ $$\frac{3 X_c I_d}{\pi} = \frac{3 \times 10 \times 1000}{\pi} = \frac{30{,}000}{3.1416} = 9549.3 \text{ V}$$ $$V_d = 286{,}876 - 9549 = 277{,}327 \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{V_d \approx 277.33 \text{ kV}}$$ **(c) Overlap angle:** $$\cos \alpha - \cos(\alpha + \mu) = \frac{\sqrt{2} X_c I_d}{V_{LL}}$$ $$\frac{\sqrt{2} \times 10 \times 1000}{220{,}000} = \frac{14142}{220000} = 0.0643$$ $$\cos(15° + \mu) = 0.9659 - 0.0643 = 0.9016$$ $$15° + \mu = \cos^{-1}(0.9016) = 25.66°$$ $$\boxed{\mu \approx 10.66°}$$ > [!NOTE] > This problem structure is **highly representative** of KTU 2024 ESE Module 1 question patterns. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 6-Pulse Graetz Bridge Topology ```mermaid graph TB AC["3-Phase AC Source
