Explain the physical reason for its occurrence.** **Model Answer**: Commutation overlap is the finite electrical angular duration ($\mu$) during which the current transfers from one thyristor to the next in a converter. During this period, both the outgoing and incoming thyristors conduct simultaneously. Physically, the overlap occurs due to the presence of source/transformer inductance ($L_c$) in the AC side. Since current through an inductor cannot change instantaneously (Faraday's law: $v = L \cdot di/dt$), the transfer of DC current from one phase to another requires a finite time. The overlap causes a "notch" in the DC output voltage waveform and reduces the average DC voltage by an amount equal to the commutation drop $\Delta V_d = 3 X_c I_d / \pi$. > [!NOTE] > **Expected Length**: 3-4 lines with one equation for marks. (Marks: Definition 1M, Physical reason 1M, Equation 1M) --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]` **Differentiate between Line Commutated Converters (LCC) and Voltage Source Converters (VSC) in HVDC systems.** **Model Answer**: | Feature | LCC | VSC | |---------|-----|-----| | Switch type | Thyristor (half-controlled) | IGBT/GTO (fully controlled) | | Commutation | Depends on AC grid (line commutation) | Self-commutated using PWM | | Reactive power | Consumes reactive power (20-30% of active) | Can supply or absorb reactive power | | DC side | Current source (smoothing reactor) | Voltage source (DC capacitor) | | Weak AC grid | Cannot operate | Can operate (even black-start) | | Losses | Lower (~0.7% per station) | Higher (~1.0-1.2% per station) | | Switching freq | Line frequency (50 Hz) | High frequency PWM (1-2 kHz) | > [!NOTE] > **Expected Length**: Tabular comparison with at least 4 points. (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the average DC output voltage of a 6-pulse Graetz bridge converter connected to a 3-phase AC source of line voltage $V_{LL}$, with a firing angle $\alpha$ and **without** commutation overlap. **(b) [7 Marks]** A 6-pulse HVDC converter operates from 3-phase, 110 kV, 50 Hz source with $X_c = 5 \ \Omega$ per phase. The firing angle is 20° and DC current is 500 A. Calculate: - (i) Average DC voltage without overlap - (ii) Average DC voltage with overlap - (iii) Overlap angle $\mu$ - (iv) DC power transmitted ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a): Derivation Without Overlap — 7 Marks **Step 1: Phase voltage expressions** (1 mark) $$v_a = V_m \sin(\omega t), \quad v_b = V_m \sin(\omega t - 120°), \quad v_c = V_m \sin(\omega t + 120°)$$ **Step 2: Instantaneous DC voltage for one conduction interval** (1 mark) $$v_d = v_a - v_b = \sqrt{3} V_m \cos(\omega t - 60°)$$ **Step 3: Average value computation** (2 marks) $$V_d = \frac{3}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\alpha + \pi/3} \sqrt{3} V_m \cos(\omega t - 60°) \, d(\omega t)$$ $$V_d = \frac{3\sqrt{3} V_m}{\pi} [\sin(\alpha) - \sin(\alpha - 60°)]$$ **Step 4: Trigonometric simplification** (1.5 marks) $$V_d = \frac{3\sqrt{3} V_m}{\pi} \cdot 2 \cos(\alpha - 30°) \cdot \sin(30°) = \frac{3\sqrt{3} V_m}{\pi} \cos(\alpha - 30°)$$ **Step 5: Substitution $V_m = \sqrt{2} V_{LL}/\sqrt{3}$** (1 mark) $$\boxed{V_d = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL} \cos(\alpha - 30°) = V_{d0} \cos \alpha}$$ where $V_{d0} = 1.35 V_{LL}$ is the **maximum no-load DC voltage**. **Physical interpretation** (0.5 mark): The 30° phase shift accounts for the reference point of the natural commutation instant in a 6-pulse bridge. #### Part (b): Numerical Problem — 7 Marks **Given**: $V_{LL} = 110{,}000$ V, $X_c = 5 \ \Omega$, $\alpha = 20°$, $I_d = 500$ A **(i) DC voltage without overlap** (1.5 marks) $$V_{d0} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \times 110{,}000 = 1.35 \times 110{,}000 = 148{,}500 \text{ V}$$ $$V_d = 148{,}500 \times \cos(20°) = 148{,}500 \times 0.9397 = 139{,}545 \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{V_d \approx 139.5 \text{ kV}}$$ **(ii) DC voltage with overlap** (1.5 marks) $$V_d = 139{,}545 - \frac{3 \times 5 \times 500}{\pi} = 139{,}545 - 2387 = 137{,}158 \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{V_d \approx 137.16 \text{ kV}}$$ **(iii) Overlap angle** (2 marks) $$\cos(20°) - \cos(20° + \mu) = \frac{\sqrt{2} \times 5 \times 500}{110{,}000} = \frac{3535.5}{110000} = 0.03214$$ $$\cos(20° + \mu) = 0.9397 - 0.03214 = 0.90756$$ $$20° + \mu = \cos^{-1}(0.90756) = 24.78°$$ $$\boxed{\mu \approx 4.78°}$$ **(iv) DC power transmitted** (2 marks) $$P_d = V_d \times I_d = 137{,}158 \times 500 = 68{,}579{,}000 \text{ W}$$ $$\boxed{P_d \approx 68.58 \text{ MW}}$$ --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]` — 14 Marks **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the average DC output voltage of a 6-pulse converter **with** commutation overlap, and explain the term "equivalent commutation resistance." **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of a Voltage Source Converter (VSC) used in HVDC transmission. With suitable equations, show how active and reactive powers are independently controlled in a VSC. ### Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a): Derivation With Overlap — 7 Marks **Step 1: Setup during commutation** (1 mark) At commutation from $T_5$ to $T_1$, both conduct, sharing DC current: $i_1 + i_5 = I_d$. **Step 2: KVL across commutating phases** (1.5 marks) $$v_a - v_c = 2 L_c \frac{di_1}{dt} \implies \sqrt{3} V_m \cos(\omega t - 60°) = 2 L_c \frac{di_1}{dt}$$ **Step 3: Integration over overlap interval** (2 marks) $$\int_{\alpha}^{\alpha+\mu} \sqrt{3} V_m \cos(\omega t - 60°) d(\omega t) = 2 X_c I_d$$ $$\sqrt{3} V_m [\sin(\alpha + \mu - 60°) - \sin(\alpha - 60°)] = 2 X_c I_d$$ **Step 4: Voltage drop computation** (1.5 marks) The area lost due to the "notch" gives: $$\Delta V_d = \frac{1}{2\pi/3} \int_{\alpha}^{\alpha+\mu} (\text{notch depth}) d(\omega t) = \frac{3 X_c I_d}{\pi}$$ **Step 5: Final equation and equivalent resistance** (1 mark) $$\boxed{V_d = V_{d0} \cos \alpha - \frac{3 X_c I_d}{\pi} = V_{d0} \cos \alpha - R_c I_d}$$ where $R_c = 3 X_c / \pi$ is the **equivalent commutation resistance** — a fictitious linear resistance that produces the same voltage drop as the actual overlap phenomenon. The actual overlap drop is nonlinear (depends on $\mu$), but the equivalent resistance model gives the correct *average* value. #### Part (b): VSC Principle and PQ Control — 7 Marks **Step 1: VSC basic principle** (1.5 marks) A VSC uses self-commutated switches (IGBTs) with antiparallel diodes. The DC side has a **constant voltage source** (capacitor $C_{dc}$), and the AC side produces a PWM-modulated voltage waveform. **Step 2: PWM and modulation index** (1.5 marks) The VSC generates an AC voltage: $$v_c = m \frac{V_{dc}}{2} \cdot \sqrt{2} \angle \delta$$ where $m$ = modulation index (0 to 1), $\delta$ = power angle. **Step 3: Active power equation** (1.5 marks) $$P = \frac{3 V_s V_c}{2 X_f} \sin \delta$$ - $P$ is controlled by $\delta$ - Independent of $m$ (to first order) **Step 4: Reactive power equation** (1.5 marks) $$Q = \frac{3 V_s}{2 X_f} (V_s - V_c \cos \delta) = \frac{3 V_s^2}{2 X_f} - \frac{3 V_s V_c}{2 X_f} \cos \delta$$ - $Q$ is controlled by $V_c$ (i.e., $m$) - Independent of $\delta$ (to first order) **Step 5: Decoupled control block diagram description** (1 mark) - Outer loop: $P^* \rightarrow \delta$ via PI controller - Outer loop: $Q^* \rightarrow m$ via PI controller - Inner current control loop ensures fast dynamic response > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Critical Points Where Students Lose Marks**: > 1. **Mixing up firing angle reference**: Always clarify if $\alpha$ is measured from the natural commutation point (NCP) or from the zero-crossing. The formula $V_d = V_{d0} \cos \alpha$ assumes $\alpha$ is from NCP. Many students forget the **30° shift** in some textbook versions. > 2. **Unit inconsistency**: $V_{LL}$ must be RMS, $V_m$ must be peak. Mixing them loses 1-2 marks. > 3. **Forgetting $\sqrt{2}$ vs $\sqrt{3}$**: The relation $V_m = \sqrt{2} V_{ph} = \sqrt{2/3} V_{LL}$ is the most common error source. > 4. **VSC vs LCC confusion**: Some students write $\alpha$ for VSC. VSC has no firing angle — it has modulation index $m$ and power angle $\delta$. > 5. **Overlap sign error**: The overlap *reduces* DC voltage, so the term is **subtracted**, not added. > 6. **Decoupled PQ control**: A frequent question is "Which parameter controls P and which controls Q?" — Answer: $\delta$ controls $P$, $m$ controls $Q$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module 1 High-Density Summary — Pin This Before Exams** - [x] **6-pulse bridge with no overlap**: $V_d = V_{d0} \cos \alpha$, where $V_{d0} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL} \approx 1.35 V_{LL}$ - [x] **6-pulse bridge with overlap**: $V_d = V_{d0} \cos \alpha - \dfrac{3 X_c I_d}{\pi} = V_{d0} \cos \alpha - R_c I_d$ - [x] **Overlap angle relation** (the *most-tested* equation): $\cos \alpha - \cos(\alpha + \mu) = \dfrac{\sqrt{2} X_c I_d}{V_{LL}}$ - [x] **Equivalent commutation resistance**: $R_c = 3 X_c / \pi \ \Omega$ - [x] **For inverter mode**: Replace $\alpha$ with $\pi - \gamma$ (or use $\gamma$ as extinction angle); $\gamma = \pi - (\alpha + \mu)$ in the overlap case - [x] **VSC uses IGBTs**, not thyristors; uses PWM (SPWM or SVPWM) - [x] **VSC active power**: $P = \dfrac{3 V_s V_c}{2 X_f} \sin \delta$ → controlled by $\delta$ - [x] **VSC reactive power**: $Q = \dfrac{3 V_s^2}{2 X_f} - \dfrac{3 V_s V_c}{2 X_f} \cos \delta$ → controlled by $m$ - [x] **VSC advantages over LCC**: independent P-Q control, no commutation failure, weak grid operation, black-start, no reactive power demand - [x] **VSC disadvantages**: higher switching losses, lower power rating per valve (typically up to ±525 kV, 1400 MW per circuit) - [x] **Indian context**: Major VSC-HVDC projects include the **Pugalur–Trichur ±320 kV, 2000 MW HVDC link** (commercial operation ~2021) - [x] **Conduction pattern**: In LCC without overlap, 2 thyristors conduct at any time; with overlap, 3 thyristors conduct - [x] **Voltage waveform**: Without overlap — clean 6-pulse ripple; with overlap — notches of width $\mu$ at each transition - [x] **Effect of increasing $I_d$**: Larger overlap angle $\mu$, larger voltage drop - [x] **Effect of increasing $X_c$**: Larger overlap angle $\mu$, larger voltage drop - [x] **Effect of increasing $\alpha$**: Smaller $V_d$ (in rectifier mode); minimum $\alpha$ is typically **5°** to ensure valve voltage is positive - [x] **KTU exam tip**: Always state assumptions (e.g., "Assuming 6-pulse bridge, ripple-free $I_d$, balanced 3-phase source")

topics.title: VSC with AC Current Control and VSC with AC Voltage Control, HVDC Controls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET862 - HVDC AND FACTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** VSC with AC Current Control and VSC with AC Voltage Control, HVDC Controls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Voltage Source Converter (VSC) — The Backbone of Modern HVDC

A **Voltage Source Converter (VSC)** is a self-commutated, solid-state power electronic device that uses **Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistors (IGBTs)** with anti-parallel diodes to convert between AC and DC. Unlike the legacy Line Commutated Converters (LCC) that depend on the AC grid voltage for commutation, a VSC can synthesize its own AC voltage waveform using **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** VSC is the enabling technology for HVDC Light, offshore wind integration, and the entire family of FACTS controllers (STATCOM, SSSC, UPFC). All control philosophies in HVDC ultimately revolve around commanding the VSC's behavior.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Water Tap"

Imagine your kitchen water tap (the VSC) is connected to a high-pressure tank (the DC link) and feeds water into your home plumbing (the AC grid). A **traditional tap (LCC)** only opens or closes — it cannot push water against pressure; the house's existing water pressure must "pull" the flow. A **VSC tap**, however, has an **active electric pump** built in. It can:
- Push water at any desired pressure (control **AC voltage**).
- Maintain a precise flow rate regardless of house demand (control **AC current**).
- Even reverse flow direction instantly (reversal of power flow).

## 1.2 VSC with AC Current Control — Definition

**VSC with AC Current Control** is a control strategy where the VSC regulates the **magnitude and phase angle of the AC current** injected into (or drawn from) the grid. The instantaneous AC current is measured, transformed into a rotating reference frame (d-q frame), and forced to track a reference through **Proportional-Integral (PI) controllers**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024):** *AC Current Control is an inner-loop, fast-acting control that decouples the active and reactive current components (Id and Iq) in the synchronous reference frame, enabling independent regulation of active and reactive power.*

### Intuition — The "Cruise Control" Analogy

Think of driving a car. The **cruise control** doesn't manipulate the engine's fuel injection directly; instead, it tracks the **measured speed** and adjusts a hidden variable (throttle). In VSC, the current controller does the same: it measures the AC current (speed), compares it to a reference, and modulates the **PWM switching pulses** (throttle). Because the converter is decoupled into d and q axes, you essentially have **two independent cruise controls** — one for active power, one for reactive power.

## 1.3 VSC with AC Voltage Control — Definition

**VSC with AC Voltage Control** is a control strategy where the VSC regulates the **magnitude of the AC voltage** at its Point of Common Coupling (PCC). Instead of treating the grid as an infinite bus, the VSC behaves as a **synchronous voltage source**, maintaining voltage through droop characteristics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024):** *AC Voltage Control allows the VSC to operate in weak grids or even islanded (grid-forming) mode, where it sets the grid frequency and voltage magnitude as the master reference.*

### Intuition — The "Independent Power House" Analogy

A current-controlled VSC is like a **grid-tied solar inverter** that follows whatever the diesel generator dictates. A voltage-controlled VSC, in contrast, behaves like a **standalone diesel generator** — it dictates the voltage and frequency to all connected loads. In HVDC, voltage-controlled stations are typically the **offshore wind farm side** (acting as the "grid" for the wind turbines) or **islanded load centers**.

## 1.4 HVDC Controls — The Hierarchical Master Plan

HVDC controls refer to the **multi-level coordinated control structure** that governs the converter stations to achieve the desired power transfer while maintaining stability, safety, and economic dispatch.

> [!NOTE]
> The four fundamental HVDC control levels (highest to lowest) are:
> 1. **System / Master Control** — sets the power order for the entire HVDC link.
> 2. **Pole / Bipole Control** — distributes the order between the two poles and converters.
> 3. **Converter / Group Control** — determines the firing angle (α) or modulation index.
> 4. **Valve / Individual Control** — generates the actual gate pulses to the IGBTs/thyristors.

**Standard metrics and constants used throughout these controls:**
- Nominal DC voltage: **±320 kV / ±525 kV / ±800 kV** depending on scheme.
- Nominal DC current: up to **3000 A** per pole.
- Switching frequency (PWM): typically **1 kHz to 2 kHz** for HVDC-scale VSC.
- DC link capacitance sizing: **C = ΔE / (2·V_DC·ΔV)**, where ΔE is allowable energy ripple.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The d-q Reference Frame — Foundation of VSC Control

In a three-phase system with voltages $V_a$, $V_b$, $V_c$, the **Park Transformation** (or d-q transformation) projects these quantities onto a **synchronously rotating reference frame** rotating at $\omega_s = 2\pi f$.

The transformation matrix from $abc$ to $dq0$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{abc \to dq0} &= \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta & \cos\left(\theta - \tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right) & \cos\left(\theta + \tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right) \\
-\sin\theta & -\sin\left(\theta - \tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right) & -\sin\left(\theta + \tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right) \\
\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $\theta = \omega_s t$ is the transformation angle.
- The **d-axis** is aligned with the voltage vector (active power axis).
- The **q-axis** is 90° behind (reactive power axis).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why d-q transformation?** In the d-q frame, balanced three-phase sinusoidal signals become **DC quantities**. This is a game-changer because PI controllers (which work best for DC references) can now be used to track AC waveforms with **zero steady-state error**.

## 2.2 VSC Equations in the Synchronous Reference Frame

Consider a VSC connected to the AC grid through an inductance $L$ and resistance $R$. The voltage equations in the d-q frame are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{cd} &= R \cdot I_d + L \frac{dI_d}{dt} - \omega_s L \cdot I_q + V_{sd} \\
V_{cq} &= R \cdot I_q + L \frac{dI_q}{dt} + \omega_s L \cdot I_d + V_{sq}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $V_{cd}, V_{cq}$ → VSC terminal voltage components.
- $V_{sd}, V_{sq}$ → Grid voltage components.
- $I_d, I_q$ → Line current components.
- $\omega_s L$ → **cross-coupling term** that couples d and q dynamics.

### 2.2.1 Power Equations in d-q Frame

The instantaneous active and reactive powers injected into the grid are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{3}{2} \left( V_{sd} I_d + V_{sq} I_q \right) \\
Q &= \frac{3}{2} \left( V_{sq} I_d - V_{sd} I_q \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

If we align the d-axis with the grid voltage (i.e., $V_{sd} = V_s$ and $V_{sq} = 0$), these simplify to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{3}{2} V_s \cdot I_d \\
Q &= -\frac{3}{2} V_s \cdot I_q
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Decoupled Power Control:** Since $P \propto I_d$ and $Q \propto I_q$, controlling $I_d$ and $I_q$ independently is equivalent to controlling $P$ and $Q$ independently. This is the cornerstone of VSC control.

## 2.3 Inner Current Control Loop — The Heartbeat of VSC

The **inner current loop** uses the d-q voltage equations to derive control signals. To remove the cross-coupling $\omega_s L$ term, we define **feed-forward compensation voltages**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{cd}^{ref} &= -\left( K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} \right)\left( I_d^{ref} - I_d \right) + \omega_s L \cdot I_q + V_{sd} \\
V_{cq}^{ref} &= -\left( K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} \right)\left( I_q^{ref} - I_q \right) - \omega_s L \cdot I_d + V_{sq}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $K_p$ and $K_i$ are the **proportional** and **integral** gains of the PI controller.

### 2.3.1 PI Controller Tuning (Symmetrical Optimum)

For a plant with $G_p(s) = \frac{1}{R + sL}$, the open-loop transfer function with PI becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{OL}(s) &= \frac{K_p \left( s + \frac{K_i}{K_p} \right)}{sL \left( s + \frac{R}{L} \right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

By zero-pole cancellation ($\frac{K_i}{K_p} = \frac{R}{L}$), the closed-loop transfer function simplifies to a first-order system with time constant $\tau = \frac{L}{K_p}$. The KTU-preferred tuning uses:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_p &= \frac{L}{\tau} \quad , \quad K_i = \frac{R}{\tau}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This exact control loop is implemented in HVDC Light stations (e.g., the INELFE Spain-France link, the North Sea Link) to control power flow with **sub-cycle response times of 1–2 ms** during grid disturbances.

## 2.4 Outer Control Loops — Setting the Targets

The **outer control loop** is slower (typically 10–50 ms) and generates the references $I_d^{ref}$ and $I_q^{ref}$ for the inner loop.

### 2.4.1 DC Voltage Control (Active Power Balance)

For a VSC station, power balance gives: $P_{ac} = P_{dc} + P_{loss}$. Any imbalance changes the DC link voltage. The DC voltage controller is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_d^{ref} &= \left( K_{pV} + \frac{K_{iV}}{s} \right) \left( V_{dc}^{ref} - V_{dc} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.4.2 AC Voltage Control (Magnitude Regulation)

The reactive power needed to maintain AC voltage is computed and used to generate $I_q^{ref}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_q^{ref} &= \left( K_{pQ} + \frac{K_{iQ}}{s} \right) \left( V_{ac}^{ref} - V_{ac} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.5 HVDC Control Hierarchy — The Chain of Command

The HVDC control system is organized in **four hierarchical levels** that operate at drastically different time scales:

| Level | Function | Time Scale | Hardware |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Master / System** | Power order, bipole balance, runback | **100 ms – 1 s** | SCADA / EMS |
| **Pole / Bipole** | Current order, DC voltage order | **10 – 100 ms** | Pole Control Computer |
| **Converter / Group** | Firing angle α, extinction angle γ, modulation index M | **1 – 10 ms** | Converter Control Unit |
| **Valve / Individual** | Gate pulse generation, valve monitoring | **µs – ms** | Valve Base Electronics |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \frac{3}{2} V_s I_d$ | Active power in d-q aligned frame |
| 2 | $Q = -\frac{3}{2} V_s I_q$ | Reactive power in d-q aligned frame |
| 3 | $V_{cd} = R I_d + L \frac{dI_d}{dt} - \omega_s L I_q + V_{sd}$ | VSC d-axis voltage equation |
| 4 | $V_{cq} = R I_q + L \frac{dI_q}{dt} + \omega_s L I_d + V_{sq}$ | VSC q-axis voltage equation |
| 5 | $K_p = \frac{L}{\tau} \quad , \quad K_i = \frac{R}{\tau}$ | PI gains for inner current loop (symmetric optimum) |
| 6 | $I_d^{ref} = (K_{pV} + \frac{K_{iV}}{s})(V_{dc}^{ref} - V_{dc})$ | DC voltage outer loop |
| 7 | $I_q^{ref} = (K_{pQ} + \frac{K_{iQ}}{s})(V_{ac}^{ref} - V_{ac})$ | AC voltage outer loop |
| 8 | $m = \frac{\sqrt{2} V_{LL}}{\sqrt{3} V_{DC}}$ | Modulation index (SPWM) |
| 9 | $E_{DC} = \frac{1}{2} C V_{DC}^2$ | Energy stored in DC link capacitor |
| 10 | $\theta_{PLL} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_{sq}}{V_{sd}}\right)$ | PLL angle extraction for synchronization |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 scheme, examiners expect students to know **why** the cross-coupling $\omega_s L$ term is added as feed-forward. It linearizes the system, allowing independent tuning of d and q axes. Omitting this term is a guaranteed **2-mark penalty** in derivations.

### Real-World Utility of These Controls

These equations are not academic exercises. The **BorWin3, DolWin6, and INELFE HVDC schemes** use the exact d-q current control structure described above. During the **2021 Texas grid failure**, HVDC links using these controllers were able to **fast-inject reactive power** within 4 ms, preventing cascading outages — a direct application of AC voltage control in VSC-HVDC.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: VSC Voltage Equations in d-q Frame from abc Phase Equations

We start with the three-phase VSC-grid interface model. The AC side is described by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L \frac{di_a}{dt} + R i_a &= v_{ca} - v_{sa} \\
L \frac{di_b}{dt} + R i_b &= v_{cb} - v_{sb} \\
L \frac{di_c}{dt} + R i_c &= v_{cc} - v_{sc}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Apply the Park Transformation:**
The d-q transformation matrix is applied to both sides of the equation. We define the rotating frame variable:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{f}_{dq} &= T_{abc \to dq0} \cdot \vec{f}_{abc}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $\vec{f}$ represents either current or voltage.

**Step 2 — Derive the General Transformation Property:**
For any three-phase variable, the transformation rule for the time derivative in a rotating frame is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{dt} \vec{f}_{dq} &= T_{abc \to dq0} \frac{d}{dt} \vec{f}_{abc} - \omega_s \mathcal{J} \vec{f}_{dq}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $\mathcal{J} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ is the rotation matrix.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Original Equation:**
Applying the derivative rule and the transformation to $L \frac{d\vec{i}}{dt} + R \vec{i} = \vec{v}_c - \vec{v}_s$, we get:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L \left( \frac{d\vec{i}_{dq}}{dt} + \omega_s \mathcal{J} \vec{i}_{dq} \right) + R \vec{i}_{dq} &= \vec{v}_{c,dq} - \vec{v}_{s,dq}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Expand Matrix Form into Scalar Equations:**
Separating the d and q components of the matrix equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L \frac{di_d}{dt} + R i_d - \omega_s L i_q &= v_{cd} - v_{sd} \\
L \frac{di_q}{dt} + R i_q + \omega_s L i_d &= v_{cq} - v_{sq}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange into the Standard VSC Control Form:**
The final equations used in the inner current loop are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{cd} &= R i_d + L \frac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_s L i_q + v_{sd} \\
v_{cq} &= R i_q + L \frac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_s L i_d + v_{sq}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The $\omega_s L$ terms are called **cross-coupling terms** because the d-axis voltage depends on the q-axis current, and vice versa. Feed-forward compensation decouples them for independent PI control.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Power Flow in d-q Frame

We begin from the general three-phase instantaneous power expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{3\phi} &= v_a i_a + v_b i_b + v_c i_c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Use the Power Invariance Property of Park Transform:**
The Park transform is power-invariant (with the $\sqrt{2/3}$ scaling), so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{3\phi} &= v_d i_d + v_q i_q + v_0 i_0
\end{aligned}
$$

For balanced three-phase systems, $v_0 = i_0 = 0$, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= v_d i_d + v_q i_q \\
Q &= v_q i_d - v_d i_q
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Factor Out 3/2 Due to Amplitude Scaling:**
The Park transform with $\sqrt{2/3}$ scaling preserves RMS values, but the instantaneous power includes the $3/2$ factor. The actual injected active/reactive power are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{3}{2} \left( v_d i_d + v_q i_q \right) \\
Q &= \frac{3}{2} \left( v_q i_d - v_d i_q \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply d-axis Voltage Alignment:**
Aligning d-axis with grid voltage: $v_d = V_s$, $v_q = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{3}{2} V_s i_d \\
Q &= -\frac{3}{2} V_s i_q
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Controlling $i_d$ controls active power $P$, and controlling $i_q$ controls reactive power $Q$. This is the **fundamental basis for decoupled P-Q control** in VSC-HVDC, and it is a guaranteed question in KTU exams.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: PI Controller Gains for Inner Current Loop

The plant transfer function (VSC to grid through L and R) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_p(s) = \frac{I_d(s)}{V_{cd}^{ref}(s) - V_{sd}(s)} = \frac{1}{L s + R}
\end{aligned}
$$

The PI controller is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} = K_p \left( \frac{s + K_i / K_p}{s} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Form the Open-Loop Transfer Function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{OL}(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s) = \frac{K_p \left( s + K_i / K_p \right)}{s(L s + R)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Zero-Pole Cancellation:**
Set $\frac{K_i}{K_p} = \frac{R}{L}$ so that the controller zero cancels the plant pole. This gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{OL}(s) = \frac{K_p}{sL}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Close the Loop:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{CL}(s) = \frac{G_{OL}(s)}{1 + G_{OL}(s)} = \frac{K_p / L}{s + K_p / L} = \frac{1}{\tau s + 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $\tau = \frac{L}{K_p}$. Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_p = \frac{L}{\tau} \quad , \quad K_i = \frac{R}{\tau}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Choice:** Choose $\tau = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \tau_{inner}$ where $\tau_{inner}$ is the desired inner-loop time constant (e.g., 1–2 ms for HVDC). This places the closed-loop bandwidth at $\frac{1}{\tau}$ rad/s, ensuring fast tracking without overshoot.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: DC Voltage Controller Output

The DC link capacitor energy balance is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dE_{DC}}{dt} = P_{dc} - P_{ac} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2} C \frac{dV_{DC}^2}{dt} = P_{dc} - P_{ac}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Linearize around the operating point:**
Let $V_{DC} = V_{DC,0} + \tilde{V}_{DC}$. For small perturbations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C V_{DC,0} \frac{d\tilde{V}_{DC}}{dt} \approx P_{dc} - P_{ac}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Express $P_{ac}$ in d-q Frame:**
$P_{ac} = \frac{3}{2} V_s I_d$. Solving for $I_d$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_d = \frac{2 P_{ac}}{3 V_s} = \frac{2}{3 V_s} \left( P_{dc} - C V_{DC,0} \frac{d\tilde{V}_{DC}}{dt} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the Control Law:**
Using a PI controller to maintain $V_{DC}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_d^{ref} = (K_{pV} + \frac{K_{iV}}{s})(V_{DC}^{ref} - V_{DC})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The DC voltage controller is the **slowest** loop in the cascade. Its bandwidth is typically 1/10 of the inner current loop bandwidth to prevent interaction and instability.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Modulation Index Relationship

For a two-level VSC using Sinusoidal PWM with DC link voltage $V_{DC}$ and line-to-line RMS voltage $V_{LL}$:

**Step 1 — Peak Phase Voltage from PWM:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{phase,peak} = \frac{m \cdot V_{DC}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Peak to RMS Line-to-Line:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{LL,RMS} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{phase,peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot m \cdot V_{DC}}{2 \sqrt{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Modulation Index $m$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m = \frac{2 \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{LL,RMS}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{DC}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Space Vector PWM (SVPWM)**, the linear range extends to $m_{max} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.155$, giving **15.5% more DC voltage utilization** than SPWM.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Explain the decoupled control of active and reactive power in a VSC using the d-q reference frame. Why is d-q transformation necessary for VSC control?**

**Model Answer:**

The decoupled control of active and reactive power in a VSC is achieved by transforming the three-phase AC quantities into the synchronous d-q reference frame using the Park transformation. In this frame, the d-axis is aligned with the grid voltage vector, making $v_d = V_s$ and $v_q = 0$. The instantaneous active and reactive powers become:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{3}{2} V_s \cdot I_d \\
Q &= -\frac{3}{2} V_s \cdot I_q
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $P$ is directly proportional to $I_d$ and $Q$ to $I_q$, regulating $I_d$ and $I_q$ independently is equivalent to controlling $P$ and $Q$ independently. This independence is called **decoupled control**.

The d-q transformation is necessary because:
1. **Steady-state DC quantities:** Three-phase AC signals become DC values in d-q frame, allowing PI controllers to track them with **zero steady-state error**.
2. **Cross-coupling identification:** The transformation exposes the $\omega_s L$ cross-coupling term that can be compensated using feed-forward.
3. **Simplified analysis:** DC analysis tools become applicable to what was previously a complex time-varying system.

**(3 marks — 1 for power equations, 1 for d-q necessity, 1 for PI justification)**

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Differentiate between AC current control and AC voltage control modes of a VSC. State one application where each mode is preferred.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | AC Current Control | AC Voltage Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Controlled variable** | AC current magnitude and phase | AC voltage magnitude and frequency |
| **VSC behavior** | Grid-following (slave) | Grid-forming (master) |
| **Grid dependency** | Requires strong grid (SCR ≥ 3) | Operates in weak/islanded grids |
| **Time scale** | Faster, inner-loop | Slower, outer-loop (cascaded) |
| **Reference source** | External P, Q, V_DC orders | Self-generated $V_{ac}^{ref}$ and $\omega_{ref}$ |
| **Preferred application** | Onshore HVDC links interconnecting strong grids | Offshore wind farm HVDC, islanded microgrids |

**Application of AC current control:** HVDC interconnection between two strong national grids (e.g., INELFE Spain–France, ±320 kV, 1000 MW).

**Application of AC voltage control:** Offshore wind HVDC link where the VSC station acts as the grid for the wind farm before transmitting power onshore (e.g., BorWin3, ±320 kV, 900 MW).

**(3 marks — 1 for differentiation table, 1 for current control app, 1 for voltage control app)**

---

## PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Attempt **either** Question B1A **or** Question B1B.

---

### Question B1A. [KTU University Exam — Module 1 Model Question, 14 Marks]

**(a)** Derive the VSC voltage equations in the synchronous d-q reference frame, starting from the three-phase $abc$ model. Clearly show the origin of the cross-coupling term $\omega_s L$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design the **inner current controller** for the d-axis of a VSC, including the **feed-forward compensation** for the cross-coupling. State the final PI control law and the gains using the **symmetrical optimum method**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Step 1 — Three-Phase Model:**
The VSC connected to the grid through inductance $L$ and resistance $R$ is described by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L \frac{di_a}{dt} + R i_a &= v_{ca} - v_{sa} \\
L \frac{di_b}{dt} + R i_b &= v_{cb} - v_{sb} \\
L \frac{di_c}{dt} + R i_c &= v_{cc} - v_{sc}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Park Transformation:**
Define $\vec{i}_{dq0} = T_{abc \to dq0} \cdot \vec{i}_{abc}$. The transformation rule for the time derivative is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{dt} \vec{f}_{dq} = T \frac{d}{dt} \vec{f}_{abc} - \omega_s \mathcal{J} \vec{f}_{dq}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine and Simplify:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L \left( \frac{d \vec{i}_{dq}}{dt} + \omega_s \mathcal{J} \vec{i}_{dq} \right) + R \vec{i}_{dq} = \vec{v}_{c,dq} - \vec{v}_{s,dq}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Expand to Scalar Form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L \frac{di_d}{dt} + R i_d - \omega_s L i_q + v_{sd} &= v_{cd} \\
L \frac{di_q}{dt} + R i_q + \omega_s L i_d + v_{sq} &= v_{cq}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Origin of Cross-Coupling $\omega_s L$:** The Park transformation rotates the reference frame at $\omega_s$, which mathematically introduces the $\omega_s \mathcal{J}$ term. This represents the **rotational electromotive force (back-EMF)** that physically couples the d and q axes.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Step 1 — Plant Transfer Function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_p(s) = \frac{1}{L s + R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — PI Controller Design:**
The PI controller is $G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}$. The closed-loop transfer function is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{CL}(s) = \frac{G_c(s) G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Feed-Forward Compensation:**
To cancel the $\omega_s L$ term, add it explicitly to the controller output. The d-axis control law is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{cd}^{ref} = -\left( K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} \right) (i_d^{ref} - i_d) + \omega_s L i_q + v_{sd}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Symmetrical Optimum Gains:**
By setting $\frac{K_i}{K_p} = \frac{R}{L}$ (zero-pole cancellation) and choosing bandwidth $1/\tau$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_p = \frac{L}{\tau} \quad , \quad K_i = \frac{R}{\tau}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Closed-Loop:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{CL}(s) = \frac{1}{\tau s + 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **first-order lag** with time constant $\tau$, typically chosen as 1–2 ms for HVDC.

---

### Question B1B. [KTU University Exam — Module 1 Model Question, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete HVDC control hierarchy** (System → Pole → Converter → Valve levels). State the time scales of each level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A VSC-HVDC station has $L = 0.1$ H, $R = 0.5\ \Omega$, and the desired inner current loop time constant is $\tau = 2$ ms. Calculate the **PI controller gains** $K_p$ and $K_i$. Also calculate the **bandwidth** in Hz. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

The HVDC control system is organized into **four hierarchical levels**, each operating at a distinct time scale:

**Level 1 — Master / System Control:**
- Sets the **overall power order** for the HVDC link.
- Manages bipole balancing, runback, and frequency support.
- **Time scale:** 100 ms to 1 second.
- Implemented in SCADA / Energy Management System (EMS).

**Level 2 — Pole / Bipole Control:**
- Distributes the order between positive and negative poles.
- Computes current order $I_{dc}^{ref}$ and DC voltage order $V_{dc}^{ref}$.
- **Time scale:** 10 to 100 ms.

**Level 3 — Converter / Group Control:**
- Determines the **firing angle $\alpha$** (for LCC) or **modulation index $M$** (for VSC).
- **Time scale:** 1 to 10 ms.
- Includes current margin controller, extinction angle controller.

**Level 4 — Valve / Individual Control:**
- Generates **gate pulses** to each thyristor/IGBT.
- Performs valve monitoring and protection.
- **Time scale:** microseconds to milliseconds.

```
Master (Power Order) → Pole (Current/Voltage Order) → 
Converter (α or M) → Valve (Gate Pulses)
[100 ms - 1 s]      [10-100 ms]      [1-10 ms]      [μs-ms]
```

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Given Data:**
- $L = 0.1$ H
- $R = 0.5\ \Omega$
- $\tau = 2$ ms $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ s

**Step 1 — Calculate $K_p$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_p = \frac{L}{\tau} = \frac{0.1}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 50\ \text{V/A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $K_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_i = \frac{R}{\tau} = \frac{0.5}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 250\ \text{V/(A·s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Bandwidth in Hz:**
The closed-loop transfer function is $\frac{1}{\tau s + 1}$. The bandwidth (at $-3$ dB) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{bw} &= \frac{1}{\tau} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 500\ \text{rad/s} \\
f_{bw} &= \frac{\omega_{bw}}{2\pi} = \frac{500}{2\pi} \approx 79.6\ \text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** A bandwidth of ~80 Hz is faster than typical grid dynamics (50/60 Hz), confirming that the inner current loop can track commands well within one cycle. This satisfies KTU 2024 design requirements for VSC-HVDC.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Points in VSC Control Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign in $Q$ equation:** Many students write $Q = \frac{3}{2} V_s I_q$ instead of $Q = -\frac{3}{2} V_s I_q$. The negative sign arises from the direction of rotation. **−1 mark.**
> 2. **Omitting the $\omega_s L$ feed-forward:** The cross-coupling compensation is the most important term for decoupled control. **−2 marks** if missing.
> 3. **Confusing d-q alignment:** Students often forget to set $v_q = 0$ after alignment, leading to incorrect power equations. **−1 mark.**
> 4. **Forgetting $\sqrt{2/3}$ scaling:** The Park transform with power invariance requires this factor. Some texts use a different scaling, leading to confusion. State the convention clearly. **−0.5 marks.**
> 5. **Unit errors in $K_i$:** The integral gain has units of V/(A·s). Writing it as V/A is a unit mistake. **−0.5 marks.**
> 6. **Confusing modulation index limits:** SPWM limit is $m \le 1$; SVPWM limit is $m \le 1.155$. Mixing these up loses marks in HVDC design problems. **−1 mark.**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **VSC = Self-commutated converter** using IGBTs; can synthesize any AC voltage waveform via PWM.
- **Two control modes:** AC current control (grid-following) and AC voltage control (grid-forming).
- **Park transformation** converts 3-phase AC to DC in d-q frame — essential for PI control.
- **Power equations in d-q frame:** $P = \frac{3}{2} V_s I_d$ and $Q = -\frac{3}{2} V_s I_q$ when d-axis is voltage-aligned.
- **Cross-coupling term $\omega_s L$** must be compensated by **feed-forward** for decoupled control.
- **PI gains (symmetric optimum):** $K_p = L/\tau$ and $K_i = R/\tau$.
- **DC voltage control** is the slowest outer loop; it generates $I_d^{ref}$ based on power balance.
- **AC voltage control** generates $I_q^{ref}$ for reactive power dispatch.
- **HVDC control hierarchy:** Master → Pole → Converter → Valve (4 levels).
- **Time scales:** 1 s → 100 ms → 10 ms → 1 ms (decreasing by an order of magnitude at each level).
- **Modulation index:** $m = \frac{2\sqrt{2} V_{LL,RMS}}{\sqrt{3} V_{DC}}$ for SPWM, max $m = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$ for SVPWM.
- **PLL (Phase-Locked Loop)** is required for grid synchronization in current-controlled VSC.
- **Grid-forming VSC (voltage control)** is used for weak grids (SCR < 2) and offshore wind.
- **Grid-following VSC (current control)** is used for strong grids (SCR ≥ 3) and bulk HVDC links.
- **Decoupled P-Q control** is the **key advantage** of VSC over LCC for HVDC.
- **Two-level VSC vs MMC:** Module 1 focuses on two-level; MMC is covered in later modules.
- **Inner current loop bandwidth:** ~50–100 Hz; **outer loop bandwidth:** ~5–20 Hz.
- **Energy balance on DC link:** $\frac{1}{2} C \frac{dV_{DC}^2}{dt} = P_{dc} - P_{ac}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functions of HVDC Controls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET862 - HVDC AND FACTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Functions of HVDC Controls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HVDC Control System:** A hierarchical, closed-loop automatic control framework that regulates the operating parameters of a High Voltage Direct Current (HVDC) converter station — namely DC voltage ($V_d$), DC current ($I_d$), active power ($P_d$), reactive power ($Q$), extinction angle ($\gamma$), and firing/ignition angle ($\alpha$) — to ensure stable, efficient, and secure power transfer between the sending-end (rectifier) and receiving-end (inverter) AC systems.

The **primary functions** of HVDC controls are categorized into two broad operational domains:

**A. Primary (Inner) Control Functions**
1. **Constant Current (CC) Control** — regulates $I_d$ on the rectifier side
2. **Constant Extinction Angle (CEA) Control** — regulates $\gamma$ on the inverter side
3. **Constant Voltage (CV) Control** — regulates $V_d$ at the inverter
4. **Minimum Ignition Angle ($\alpha_{min}$) Control** — protective limit on the rectifier
5. **Tap Changer Control** — slow co-control of converter transformer OLTC

**B. Secondary (Outer) Control Functions**
6. **Active Power Control** (megawatt dispatch order)
7. **Reactive Power / AC Voltage Control** (firing-angle modulation + filters/SVC)
8. **Frequency Control** (via $P_d$ modulation)
9. **Power Oscillation Damping (POD) / Sub-Synchronous Damping (SSD)**
10. **Black-Start Coordination** and emergency changeover

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Highway Toll-Bridge Analogy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a two-lane toll bridge (the DC link) connecting two busy cities (AC grids).**
>
> - The **number of cars allowed on the bridge per minute** = **DC current ($I_d$)** — controlled by the entry gate at City A (rectifier).
> - The **speed limit / safe exit angle** at City B = **extinction angle $\gamma$** — controlled by the exit ramp design (inverter).
> - The **toll collected** = **DC power ($P_d$)** = $V_d \times I_d$.
> - The **traffic police in a control tower** = the **Master Controller** that watches both cities and adjusts gates.
> - The **local booth operator at each gate** = the **Pole / Valve Group Controller** that turns knobs every millisecond.
>
> If cars arrive too fast (over-current), the rectifier gate restricts entry. If the bridge tilts (commutation failure risk), the inverter's $\gamma$ is increased. The whole system stays stable because local booths AND the central tower coordinate continuously.

### 1.3 Standard System Performance Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Converter switching frequency:** 50 Hz (1 cycle = **20 ms**) for 6-pulse, **6.67 ms** for 12-pulse.
> - **Current control response:** typically **20–50 ms** (one cycle of AC).
> - **Power reversal time:** **150–250 ms** standard (modern systems < 80 ms).
> - **Reactive power consumption:** $Q \approx P_d \tan(\alpha)$ (rectifier) and $Q \approx P_d \tan(\gamma)$ (inverter) — converters **always absorb** reactive power.
> - **Availability target:** > **98%** (CIGRÉ benchmark).
> - **DC current ripple limit:** < **5–10%** of rated $I_d$ (smoothing reactor design).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Control Functions — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — The HVDC converter acts as a controllable voltage source.**
The mean DC voltage produced by a 6-pulse Graetz bridge, neglecting commutation overlap, is given by the **fundamental HVDC equation** that the controller manipulates:

$$V_{d0} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \, V_{ac} \cos(\alpha) - \frac{3}{\pi} X_c I_d$$

where $V_{ac}$ is the line-to-line RMS AC voltage, $X_c = \omega L_c$ is the commutation reactance, and $\alpha$ is the firing (ignition) delay angle measured from the natural commutation point.

**Step 2 — Why control $\alpha$ and $\gamma$?**
- $V_d$ is a **direct cosine function of $\alpha$** (and indirectly of $\gamma$). By adjusting $\alpha$ between roughly $5°$ and $20°$, the rectifier can **ramp $V_d$ up or down** to push/pull DC current.
- The inverter controls $\gamma$ (typically $15°$–$18°$) — this is the **margin against commutation failure**. A larger $\gamma$ = safer commutation but more reactive power absorbed.

**Step 3 — The "Current Margin" Principle.**
This is the **single most important control philosophy** in HVDC. Two operating points exist on the V–I characteristic:
- Rectifier: **Constant Current** at $I_{d,ref}$
- Inverter: **Constant Extinction Angle (CEA)** at fixed $\gamma$
- A small offset of about **0.1 pu (10%)** is intentionally kept between them — this is the **Current Margin ($\Delta I$)**, which guarantees the **two characteristics intersect at exactly one stable point**.

$$I_{d,ref}^{inv} = I_{d,ref}^{rec} - \Delta I_{margin}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Without the current margin, the two control curves would either be parallel (no defined operating point) or cross at multiple unstable points — system collapse.**

**Step 4 — Power Reversal is achieved by reversing $I_d$, NOT $V_d$.**
Modern HVDC systems use the **bipolar DC link with metallic return**; power flow direction is changed by **flipping the polarity of DC voltage** at one terminal while keeping the current direction same (or by using **DC switches / valve bypass** in LCC systems with **current reversal**, in newer VSC systems $I_d$ reverses polarity since $V_d$ polarity is fixed).

**Step 5 — Hierarchical decomposition of the control system.**

| Control Level | Function | Bandwidth | Time Constant |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Master Control** | Power dispatch, $P_{d,ref}$, $Q_{d,ref}$ between stations | 1–10 s | Slow |
| **Pole Control** | $I_{d,ref}$ allocation, current balancing in bipolar | 100 ms | Medium |
| **Valve Group Control** | $\alpha$-order, $\gamma$-order, voltage order to firing unit | 20–50 ms | Fast |
| **Valve Firing Control** | Individual thyristor gate pulses, equidistant pulse generation | < 1 ms | Very fast |

**Step 6 — Role of Tap Changer (OLTC).**
The converter transformer has an **On-Load Tap Changer** (typical ±15% range, 17–33 steps). Its job is to **keep $\alpha$ and $\gamma$ in their optimum range** (e.g., $12°$–$18°$) so that the **reactive power consumption stays low** and **harmonic currents stay balanced**. Tap changer operates in **seconds**, while the **firing angle is the fast control**.

**Step 7 — Reactive Power & AC Voltage Control.**
HVDC converters **consume** reactive power proportional to:

$$Q = P_d \, \tan(\phi) \quad \text{where} \quad \cos(\phi) \approx \frac{1}{2}(\cos\alpha + \cos(\alpha + \mu))$$

The AC voltage is held constant by switching **shunt capacitor banks, AC filters, and (in modern systems) STATCOMs**, coordinated with $\alpha$ adjustment.

**Step 8 — Ancillary / Modulation Functions.**
A modern HVDC link also provides:
- **Frequency control:** $P_d$ modulated by $\Delta f$ between AC areas.
- **Power swing damping:** $\Delta P_d = -K \cdot \Delta \omega$ (similar to PSS).
- **Sub-synchronous oscillation damping:** protects turbine-generator shafts.
- **Fast emergency power reduction / runback** in fault conditions.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important compilation** for solving Part B numericals. Memorize the symbols, NOT the LaTeX.

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{d} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{ac} \cos\alpha - \frac{3}{\pi} X_c I_d$ | Mean DC voltage at rectifier (overlap included) | kV |
| 2 | $V_{d} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{ac} \cos\gamma - \frac{3}{\pi} X_c I_d$ | Mean DC voltage at inverter | kV |
| 3 | $I_d = \dfrac{V_{d,rec} - V_{d,inv}}{R_{dc}}$ | DC current from V–I difference over line resistance | A or pu |
| 4 | $P_d = V_d \cdot I_d$ | DC power transmitted | MW |
| 5 | $Q_{conv} = P_d \tan\phi$ | Reactive power absorbed by converter | MVAR |
| 6 | $I_{d,margin} = 0.10 \text{ pu (typical)}$ | Current margin between rec CC and inv CEA | 0.1 pu |
| 7 | $\alpha_{min} = 5°$ (typical limit) | Minimum firing angle (forced) | degrees |
| 8 | $\gamma_{min} = 15°$–$18°$ (typical) | Minimum extinction angle | degrees |
| 9 | Overlap angle: $\mu = \gamma - \alpha$ (inverter relation) | Commutation overlap | degrees |
| 10 | $V_{d0} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{ac}$ (no-load) | Ideal no-load DC voltage | kV |
| 11 | $V_{d} \cdot I_{d} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{ac} \cos\phi \cdot I_d$ | Per-phase power balance | MW |
| 12 | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\cos\alpha + \cos(\alpha+\mu)\right)$ | Displacement power factor of converter | dimensionless |

> **LaTeX isolation note:** all absolute value / magnitude notations in the table have been rendered using $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid breaking the markdown pipe structure.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bulk power transmission > 800 km** — classic LCC-HVDC with the above control hierarchy is dominant.
- **Submarine cable interconnection** (e.g., Norway–UK North Sea Link) — relies on **fast $\alpha$–$\gamma$** control to handle long charging currents.
- **Asynchronous grid interconnection** (e.g., India–Sri Lanka, China–Philippines) — HVDC control **decouples the two grid frequencies**, which is impossible with AC ties.
- **Renewable integration** — VSC-HVDC offshore wind (e.g., BorWin, DolWin) uses **similar hierarchy but with IGBT valves** — control of $P$ and $Q$ is **independent and instantaneous**, since $\alpha$ no longer exists (replaced by PWM modulation index $M$).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — DC Voltage Equation with Commutation Overlap

We start from a 6-pulse converter where valves 1 and 3 are commutating. Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** across the commutating phases and averaging the DC-side voltage over one commutation period gives the classical HVDC equation.

**Step 1.** The instantaneous DC voltage during commutation of valve 1 to valve 3 (phase A to phase B) is:

$$v_d = \frac{v_a + v_b}{2} - v_c$$

**Step 2.** Substituting the standard three-phase voltages:
- $v_a = V_m \sin(\omega t)$
- $v_b = V_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$
- $v_c = V_m \sin(\omega t - 240°)$

and integrating over the conduction period from $\alpha$ to $\alpha + \mu$:

**Step 3.** Mean value over the conduction interval:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_d &= \frac{3}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\alpha+\mu} \frac{v_a + v_b}{2} \, d(\omega t) - \frac{3}{\pi} \int_{\alpha+\mu}^{\alpha+60°} v_c \, d(\omega t) \quad \text{[non-overlap]}\\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4.** Evaluating the integrals, the well-known result emerges:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_d &= \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{2\pi} V_{LL} \left[ \cos\alpha + \cos(\alpha + \mu) \right] \\
\end{aligned}$$

where $V_{LL}$ is the line-to-line RMS AC voltage.

**Step 5.** Introducing commutation reactance $X_c = \omega L_c$, the overlap angle $\mu$ satisfies:

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha+\mu) &= \frac{2 X_c I_d}{\sqrt{2}\, V_{LL}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6.** Combining (Step 4) and (Step 5) and simplifying — the **final compact HVDC voltage equation** used universally in KTU textbooks:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_d &= \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL} \cos\alpha \; - \; \frac{3 X_c}{\pi} I_d \\
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation of terms:**
> - First term: **no-load DC voltage** (proportional to $\cos\alpha$, the *controllable* part).
> - Second term: **voltage drop due to commutation reactance** (linear with $I_d$ — a *load-dependent* term).
> - The minus sign in the second term is **why the external V–I characteristic has a negative slope**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Current Margin Principle (Operating Point Stability)

**Step 1.** Write the rectifier characteristic (in $V_d$ – $I_d$ plane):

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{d,rec} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{ac,rec} \cos\alpha_{rec} - \frac{3 X_c}{\pi} I_d
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2.** Write the inverter characteristic (CEA mode, constant $\gamma$):

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{d,inv} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{ac,inv} \cos\gamma_{inv} - \frac{3 X_c}{\pi} I_d
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3.** Set $V_{d,rec} = V_{d,inv}$ and solve for $I_d$ (with no line resistance $R_{dc}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
I_d = \frac{V_{ac,rec} \cos\alpha_{rec} - V_{ac,inv} \cos\gamma_{inv}}{(2 X_c)/\pi}
\end{aligned}$$

This gives **one intersection point** — but in a system with two free variables ($\alpha_{rec}$ and $\gamma_{inv}$), we need a **constraint**.

**Step 4.** Apply the **Current Margin Constraint**:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{d,ref}^{inv} = I_{d,ref}^{rec} - \Delta I_m
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5.** The rectifier's current regulator reduces $\alpha$ (i.e., raises $V_{d,rec}$) to **push more current** until measured $I_d = I_{d,ref}^{rec}$. If $V_{ac,rec}$ drops suddenly, the regulator cannot push enough, and control **hands over** to the inverter's voltage (or CEA) regulator. This **graceful hand-off** is the foundation of HVDC reliability.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Control Modes and Their Selection Logic

| AC System State | Rectifier Mode | Inverter Mode | $\alpha$ range | $\gamma$ range | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Normal — power flow forward** | CC (constant current) | CEA (constant $\gamma$) | $12°$–$18°$ | $15°$–$18°$ | Standard, stable, low Q |
| **Voltage reduction in sending AC** | $\alpha_{min}$ (firing limit) | **CV (constant DC voltage)** | $\alpha = \alpha_{min}$ | varies | Rectifier loses control → inv takes over |
| **Inverter AC voltage dip** | CC | $\gamma_{min}$ limit | varies | $\gamma = \gamma_{min}$ | Risk of commutation failure |
| **Black start** | $\alpha$ fixed to max (90°) | Self-commutated or aux. source | manual | manual | Special start-up sequence |
| **Power reversal** | Current reference ramped to negative | Current reference ramped to negative | transition | transition | ~150 ms typical |
| **Reactive power standby** | $\alpha$ at high value (e.g., $40°$) | $\gamma$ at high value (e.g., $30°$) | low Q absorption | low Q absorption | Reduced DC voltage, low losses |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 HVDC Hierarchical Control Architecture

The following Mermaid block renders a **block-level functional architecture flow** of the four-tier control hierarchy. This is exactly the type of diagram examiners expect in 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1["LEVEL 1 MASTER CONTROL - Power and Frequency Dispatcher - Time Constant 1 to 10 s - Reference P_d_ref and Q_d_ref generation"]:::master
    L2["LEVEL 2 POLE CONTROL - I_d_ref allocation - Bipole balancing - Current margin enforcement - DC voltage regulation - Time constant 50 to 200 ms"]:::pole
    L3["LEVEL 3 VALVE GROUP CONTROL - Alpha order computation - Gamma order computation - Equidistant firing pulse - Time constant 5 to 20 ms"]:::valvegrp
    L4["LEVEL 4 VALVE FIRING UNIT - Individual thyristor gating - BOD and overvoltage protection - Firing pulse amplifier"]:::firing

    L1 -- P_d_ref and Q_d_ref --> L2
    L2 -- I_d_order and V_d_order --> L3
    L3 -- alpha_pulse and gamma_pulse --> L4
    L4 -- Gate pulses to thyristors --> VALVES["6 pulse or 12 pulse thyristor valves"]
    VALVES -- V_d and I_d feedback --> L2
    L1 -. Telemetry to opposite station .-> REMOTE["Remote station master control"]

    classDef master fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2a47,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef pole fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef valvegrp fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef firing fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#0b2a47,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 V–I Characteristic with Current Margin — Operating Point Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["I_d axis horizontal - DC Current in pu"]:::axis
    B["V_d axis vertical - DC Voltage in pu"]:::axis

    subgraph "Rectifier side"
        R1["Constant Current CC line - vertical at I_d = 1.0 pu"]:::rec
        R2["Alpha min limit line - vertical at I_d = 1.05 pu"]:::recLim
    end

    subgraph "Inverter side"
        I1["Constant Extinction Angle CEA - falling curve starting at I_d = 0.9 pu"]:::inv
        I2["Constant Voltage CV - vertical at V_d = 0.95 pu"]:::invLim
    end

    OP["Stable operating point - intersection at I_d = 1.0 pu - V_d = 0.98 pu"]:::op

    R1 --> OP
    I1 --> OP

    classDef axis fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#7f7f7f,color:#000000
    classDef rec fill:#deebf7,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#0b2a47
    classDef recLim fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c00000,color:#000000
    classDef inv fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235,color:#0b2a47
    classDef invLim fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#000000
    classDef op fill:#ffe699,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#000000,stroke-width:3px
```

### 4.3 Function-Trigger Flow — How Controls React to Disturbances

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["AC system disturbance detected - Change in V_ac or f_ac"]:::start
    A{"V_ac drop on rectifier AC bus?"}:::decision
    B{"I_d reference exceeded?"}:::decision
    C{"V_d below 0.9 pu at inverter?"}:::decision
    D["Rectifier: reduce alpha to minimum - Maximum V_d output"]:::act
    E["Rectifier CC controller - adjusts alpha - Maintains I_d_ref"]:::act
    F["Inverter CV controller - takes over DC voltage - Prevents overcurrent"]:::act
    G["Inverter CEA - increases gamma - Prevents commutation failure"]:::act
    H["Tap changer moves - Restores alpha to optimum range - seconds delay"]:::act
    R["System returns to normal operating point - Stable I_d and V_d"]:::result

    S --> A
    A -- yes --> D
    A -- no --> B
    B -- yes --> E
    B -- no --> C
    C -- yes --> F
    C -- no --> G
    D --> H
    F --> H
    G --> R
    E --> R
    H --> R

    classDef start fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2a47,color:#ffffff
    classDef decision fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#000000
    classDef act fill:#deebf7,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#0b2a47
    classDef result fill:#70ad47,stroke:#548235,color:#ffffff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
> *State any three primary control functions of an HVDC converter station.*

**Model Answer (3 points):**
1. **Constant Current (CC) control** at the rectifier — regulates the DC link current to a reference value $I_{d,ref}$ by adjusting the firing angle $\alpha$.
2. **Constant Extinction Angle (CEA) control** at the inverter — maintains a minimum $\gamma$ (typically $15°$–$18°$) to prevent commutation failure.
3. **Constant Voltage (CV) control** at the inverter — regulates the DC link voltage $V_d$ to within safe limits during disturbances.
*(Acceptable alternatives: $\alpha_{min}$ limit, tap changer control, reactive power control, frequency control.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
> *What is "current margin" in HVDC control? State its typical value.*

**Model Answer (3 points):**
- **Definition:** The current margin is the **intentional difference (offset)** maintained between the current order of the rectifier (CC mode) and the inverter (operating in CEA mode) on the V–I characteristic.
- **Purpose:** It guarantees a **single, stable, unique operating point** of intersection and enables a **smooth control hand-off** between rectifier and inverter during AC voltage disturbances.
- **Typical value:** **0.1 pu (10%)** of rated DC current.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **hierarchical control structure** of an HVDC link. State the bandwidth and main function of each level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **steady-state DC voltage equation** of a 6-pulse converter, including the effect of commutation overlap. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hierarchical Control Structure (7 marks):**

| Level | Block Name | Bandwidth / Time Constant | Main Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L1** | Master Control | 1 – 10 s | Generates $P_{d,ref}$ and $Q_{d,ref}$ based on operator / frequency dispatcher; coordinates with remote station via telecommunication. |
| **L2** | Pole Control | 50 – 200 ms | Converts power reference to current order; enforces **current margin** of 0.1 pu; handles **bipole balancing**; provides **DC voltage limiter**. |
| **L3** | Valve Group Control | 5 – 20 ms | Converts current order to **firing angle $\alpha$ order** and **extinction angle $\gamma$ order**; produces **equidistant firing pulses**; performs **valve selection logic**. |
| **L4** | Valve Firing (Individual) | < 1 ms | Generates isolated **gate pulses to thyristors**; includes BOD (Break-Over Diode) and **forward voltage monitoring** for safe firing. |

*Feedback loops:* $V_d$ and $I_d$ are measured by **DC transducers** and fed back to L2; AC voltage and frequency to L1.

**Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **time constants** of L1 and L2. **L1 is slow (seconds); L2 is fast (sub-second)** because the power reference cannot change abruptly, but the current must track within a few cycles for stability.

**(b) DC Voltage Equation with Overlap (7 marks):**

The derivation is the **same as Section 3.1** of these notes. Key steps to be presented in the answer:

1. **Assume 6-pulse Graetz bridge** with thyristor valves 1, 3, 5 (positive group) and 4, 6, 2 (negative group).
2. **Write instantaneous DC voltage** during commutation of valve 1 to 3:

$$v_d = v_a - \frac{v_a + v_b}{2} = \frac{v_a - v_b}{2}$$

3. **Average over one conduction period** $\frac{\pi}{3}$:

$$V_d = \frac{3}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\alpha+\mu} \frac{v_a - v_b}{2}\, d(\omega t)$$

4. **Substitute $v_a = V_m \sin(\omega t)$**, $v_b = V_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$, integrate:

$$V_d = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{2\pi} V_{LL}\left[\cos\alpha + \cos(\alpha + \mu)\right]$$

5. **Use the commutation relation** $V_m \sin\mu = 2 X_c I_d / \sqrt{3}$ to eliminate $\mu$:

$$\boxed{V_d = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL} \cos\alpha - \frac{3 X_c}{\pi} I_d}$$

6. **Interpretation:** First term is the **no-load controlled voltage** (depends on $\alpha$); second is the **commutation voltage drop** (linear in $I_d$, proportional to $X_c$).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain with a sketch the **V–I characteristics of a two-terminal HVDC link** showing the rectifier CC mode and inverter CEA mode. Indicate the **stable operating point** and the role of the **current margin**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A monopolar HVDC link operates with $V_{ac} = 220$ kV (line-to-line), $X_c = 12 \, \Omega$, and rectifier firing angle $\alpha = 15°$. Calculate (i) the **no-load DC voltage** $V_{d0}$, (ii) the **DC voltage at $I_d = 1000$ A**, and (iii) the **DC power** transmitted, neglecting line resistance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) V–I Characteristics and Operating Point (7 marks):**

The V–I plot has:
- **Horizontal axis:** $I_d$ (DC current, pu)
- **Vertical axis:** $V_d$ (DC voltage, pu)

Three characteristic lines are drawn:

1. **Rectifier CC line:** *Vertical* at $I_d = 1.0$ pu, extending from $V_d = 0$ upward.
2. **Inverter CEA line:** *Negatively sloped* line starting at $I_d = 0.9$ pu, $V_d = V_{d0,inv} \cos\gamma$.
3. **Inverter CV / $\alpha_{min}$ line:** *Vertical* at slightly higher $I_d$ (e.g., 1.05 pu).

The **stable operating point** is the intersection of the rectifier CC line and the inverter CEA line. **Current margin** is the horizontal gap of 0.1 pu between them — without it, the two vertical lines would never intersect (infinite current) or the system would oscillate between two unstable intersections. The margin ensures **single, deterministic, and stable operation**.

During AC voltage dip on the rectifier side, the CC line shifts left; the operating point slides along the CEA curve to a new stable intersection.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_{ac,LL} = 220$ kV, $X_c = 12 \, \Omega$, $\alpha = 15°$, $I_d = 1000$ A.

**(i) No-load DC voltage** (set $I_d = 0$):

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{d0} &= \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot V_{ac,LL} \cos\alpha \\
&= \frac{3 \times 1.4142}{3.1416} \cdot 220 \cdot \cos(15°) \\
&= 1.350 \cdot 220 \cdot 0.9659 \\
&= 1.350 \cdot 212.51 \\
&\approx 286.9 \text{ kV}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) DC voltage at $I_d = 1000$ A:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_d &= V_{d0} - \frac{3 X_c}{\pi} I_d \\
&= 286.9 \text{ kV} - \frac{3 \times 12}{3.1416} \times 1000 \\
&= 286.9 \text{ kV} - 11.46 \text{ kV} \\
&\approx 275.5 \text{ kV}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) DC power transmitted:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_d &= V_d \cdot I_d \\
&= 275.5 \times 10^3 \text{ V} \cdot 1000 \text{ A} \\
&= 2.755 \times 10^8 \text{ W} \\
&= 275.5 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 vs. 3 in the formula** — many students write $V_d = (\sqrt{2}/\pi) V_{ac} \cos\alpha$ which is **wrong** (that's the single-phase half-wave result). The 6-pulse bridge uses the **$3\sqrt{2}/\pi$** factor.
> 2. **Mixing up $V_{ac}$ (phase) and $V_{ac,LL}$ (line-to-line).** KTU 2024 problems almost always give **line-to-line voltage**. Substituting phase voltage silently is a **2-mark deduction** per question.
> 3. **Not converting $\alpha$ from degrees to radians in $\cos$** — modern calculators take degrees by default, but if you write `cos(15)` in code, you may get wrong answers. Use $\cos(15°)$ explicitly.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert the second term's units**: $X_c$ in $\Omega$ and $I_d$ in A give $X_c I_d$ in V, NOT kV. Add the unit conversion carefully.
> 5. **Not drawing the current margin arrow** in the V–I plot for part (a) — it's worth **1 mark** standalone.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-frequency points to revise before KTU ESE — bullet form:**

- ✅ The **four control levels**: Master → Pole → Valve Group → Valve Firing. (Time constants: s → 100 ms → 20 ms → <1 ms)
- ✅ **Rectifier** = CC control, **Inverter** = CEA control — this is the **default** configuration.
- ✅ **Current margin = 0.1 pu** (10%) — guarantees stable, single operating point.
- ✅ **Power reversal** in LCC = polarity reversal of $V_d$ (DC switches) OR current reversal in monopolar links; in VSC = $I_d$ reverses because $V_d$ polarity is fixed by IGBT body diodes.
- ✅ **DC voltage equation** (6-pulse): $V_d = (3\sqrt{2}/\pi) V_{LL} \cos\alpha - (3 X_c / \pi) I_d$.
- ✅ **HVDC converter ALWAYS absorbs reactive power** (operates at lagging pf) — must be supplied by filters / SVC / STATCOM.
- ✅ **Tap changer** operates in **seconds**; **firing angle** operates in **milliseconds** — they are coordinated.
- ✅ **$\alpha_{min} = 5°$** (rectifier lower limit) and **$\gamma_{min} = 15°$–$18°$** (inverter extinction angle limit).
- ✅ **Commutation failure** occurs when $\gamma$ becomes < 8°–10° — inverter protection raises $\gamma$ rapidly.
- ✅ **Modulation functions:** frequency, POD, SSD — modern HVDC links are *not* just power transfer devices; they are **grid-stabilizing assets**.
- ✅ **For 12-pulse converters** (two 6-pulse bridges in series with $30°$ phase shift), DC voltage is **doubled**, and characteristic harmonics $5, 7, 17, 19, \ldots$ are eliminated.
- ✅ **Master–Remote station communication** is via **PLC (Power Line Carrier) or dedicated OPGW fibre**; failure of telecom is a known root cause of HVDC blackouts.
- ✅ In KTU numericals, **always state units** in the final answer — boards deduct **0.5 mark per numerical** for missing units.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equivalent Circuit for a Two Terminal DC Link
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET862 - HVDC AND FACTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equivalent Circuit for a Two Terminal DC Link

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Two-Terminal HVDC Link** is a High-Voltage Direct Current transmission system that interconnects exactly **two** AC networks (or two points of the same AC grid) via a controlled DC interconnection. The system is electrically characterized by an **equivalent circuit** comprising two converter stations (one operating as a **rectifier** at the sending end and the other as an **inverter** at the receiving end), the DC transmission line, smoothing reactors, and the equivalent commutation reactances of the AC systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> The equivalent circuit of a two-terminal DC link is a lumped-parameter representation in which the rectifier and inverter are modeled as **controllable DC voltage sources** $V_{dr}$ and $V_{di}$ in series with **commutation resistances** $R_{cr}$ and $R_{ci}$, with the DC line modeled as lumped resistance $R_d$ (or a more detailed distributed-parameter line), and the entire loop carrying the common DC current $I_d$.

The governing loop equation of the two-terminal DC link is:

$$V_{dr} - V_{di} = (R_{cr} + R_d + R_{ci}) \, I_d$$

This single scalar equation captures the entire steady-state power-transfer relationship between the two AC systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine two water reservoirs at different elevations connected by a pipe. The **rectifier** is like a pump at the higher reservoir pushing water down, and the **inverter** is like another pump at the lower end that can either pull water (rectifier-to-inverter mode) or push back (regulating mode). The **DC line** is the pipe; its diameter and length determine how much resistance water flow encounters. The **DC current** $I_d$ is the water flow rate, and the **DC voltage difference** between the two converter stations is the "pressure difference" driving that flow.

If the rectifier increases its pressure (raises DC voltage by reducing firing angle), more water flows. If the inverter offers less back-pressure (raises its own voltage by reducing extinction angle), again more water flows. **Power flow is controlled entirely by the difference in DC voltages**, just as AC power flow is controlled by the angle difference between voltages.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** The DC link has *no* reactive power flow on the line itself, *no* frequency, and *no* synchronizing torque — only the difference in DC voltage magnitudes determines the magnitude and direction of power transfer. This is the single most important intuition for two-terminal DC link analysis.

## 1.3 Standard Constants and Reference Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Ideal DC voltage constant (3-pulse factor) | $3\sqrt{2}/\pi$ | **≈ 1.3505** (dimensionless) |
| Six-pulse bridge DC voltage factor | $3\sqrt{2}/\pi$ | **1.3505 × $V_{LL}$** |
| Commutation resistance factor | $3/\pi$ | **≈ 0.955** (per unit reactance) |
| Smoothing reactor | $L_d$ | typically **50–300 mH** |
| DC line resistance (overhead) | $R_d$ | typically **0.01–0.05 Ω/km** |
| Base DC voltage | $V_{d,base}$ | **±500 kV / ±800 kV** (modern UHVDC) |

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Two-Terminal DC Link (Block-by-Block Logic)

The two-terminal link is decomposed into **five** distinct, mathematically tractable blocks. Each block has a single, well-defined equivalent parameter.

### Step 1 — AC Source Equivalent (Thevenin Equivalent)
The sending-end AC system is reduced to a Thevenin equivalent:
- AC source EMF: $E_s \angle \delta_s$
- Equivalent system (commutation) reactance: $X_c$ (sourced from short-circuit level)

The receiving AC system is similarly reduced:
- AC source EMF: $E_r \angle \delta_r$
- Commutation reactance: $X_{cr}$ (or $X_c$ if symmetrical)

### Step 2 — Converter Bridge Representation
A **6-pulse Graetz bridge** when in continuous conduction and ignoring commutation overlap produces a DC side voltage:

$$V_{d0} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL}$$

where $V_{LL}$ is the AC line-to-line RMS voltage. This is the *no-load* DC voltage (ignoring overlap). With overlap $u$ (commutation angle), the actual terminal DC voltage becomes:

$$V_d = V_{d0} \cos\alpha - \frac{3}{\pi} X_c I_d \quad \text{(Rectifier, controlled by } \alpha \text{)}$$

$$V_d = V_{d0} \cos\gamma - \frac{3}{\pi} X_c I_d \quad \text{(Inverter, controlled by } \gamma \text{)}$$

The term $\frac{3}{\pi} X_c I_d$ is the **commutation voltage drop**. The constant $\frac{3}{\pi}$ is the **commutation resistance coefficient** $R_{c,equiv}$.

### Step 3 — Commutation Resistance
A mathematical artifact: the commutation voltage drop is proportional to $I_d$, hence it is modeled as an equivalent **resistance** (not a true resistor, just a linearization):

$$R_c = \frac{3 X_c}{\pi} \;\;[\Omega]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it a "resistance"?** Because in the DC loop equation, the term $\frac{3 X_c I_d}{\pi}$ looks identical to an $I \cdot R$ drop. This allows the entire converter to be replaced by a **Thevenin equivalent on the DC side**: a DC voltage source $V_{d0}\cos\alpha$ in series with resistance $R_c$.

### Step 4 — DC Transmission Line
The DC line may be modeled at increasing levels of fidelity:

| Model | Parameters | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Lumped $R$ | $R_d$ | Short lines (<50 km), steady-state, KTU module 1 |
| Nominal T | $R_d/2$, $L_d/2$, $C$, $R_d/2$ | Medium lines, transient studies |
| Nominal π | $R_d$, $L_d/2$, $C/2$, $L_d/2$, $R_d$ | Medium lines, surge analysis |
| Bergeron (lumped $RL$, distributed $LC$) | $Z_c$, travel time $\tau$ | Transients, surge studies |
| Frequency-dependent (line model) | Full modal data | EMTP/PSCAD studies |

For **KTU Module 1 steady-state analysis**, the **lumped R** model is canonical.

### Step 5 — Smoothing Reactor
A large series inductor $L_d$ (typically 50–300 mH) is placed on each side of the line. Its purpose is:
- Limit **di/dt** during DC faults
- Prevent **commutation failures** in the inverter
- Smooth **current ripple** from the 6-pulse bridge

In DC steady state ($dI_d/dt = 0$), the smoothing reactor drops **zero** voltage and is omitted from the equivalent circuit.

## 2.2 The Canonical Equivalent Circuit (KTU Board Standard)

```
   ┌──── Rectifier (Sending End) ────┐         ┌──── Inverter (Receiving End) ────┐
   │                                 │         │                                 │
   │  V_d0r·cos α ──[R_cr]──┬──[R_d]─┼──[R_ci]── V_d0i·cos γ                  │
   │         (+)            │        │        (-)        (+)                   │
   └────────────────────────┴────────┴──────────────────┘                    │
                              │                                               
                              I_d flows in loop                               
```

The single governing **loop equation** is:

$$V_{d0r} \cos\alpha - \frac{3 X_c}{\pi} I_d - R_d I_d - V_{d0i} \cos\gamma + \frac{3 X_c}{\pi} I_d = 0$$

In the **symmetrical** case ($X_{cr} = X_{ci} = X_c$):

$$I_d = \frac{V_{d0r}\cos\alpha - V_{d0i}\cos\gamma}{R_d + \frac{6 X_c}{\pi}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The factor of **6** in the denominator (not 3) is the most common error. It comes from commutation resistance at **both** ends. Students who write 3 lose marks.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{d0} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL}$ | No-load DC voltage (6-pulse bridge) | V |
| 2 | $V_{dr} = V_{d0}\cos\alpha - \dfrac{3 X_c}{\pi} I_d$ | Rectifier DC voltage with overlap | V |
| 3 | $V_{di} = V_{d0}\cos\gamma - \dfrac{3 X_c}{\pi} I_d$ | Inverter DC voltage with extinction angle | V |
| 4 | $R_c = \dfrac{3 X_c}{\pi}$ | Commutation equivalent resistance | Ω |
| 5 | $I_d = \dfrac{V_{dr} - V_{di}}{R_d}$ | Loop current (line drop only) | A |
| 6 | $I_d = \dfrac{V_{d0}\cos\alpha - V_{d0}\cos\gamma}{R_d + 2 R_c}$ | Loop current with both-end commutation | A |
| 7 | $P_{dc} = V_d I_d$ | DC power transmitted | W |
| 8 | $\cos\phi \approx \cos\alpha - \dfrac{X_c I_d}{\sqrt{2} V_{LL}}$ | Power factor at converter bus | – |
| 9 | $Q_{conv} = P_{dc} \tan\phi$ | Reactive power consumed by converter | VAr |
| 10 | $V_d = V_{d0} \cos\alpha - R_c I_d$ | Generalized DC terminal voltage | V |

**Boundary Conditions / KTU Constraints:**

- $0^{\circ} \leq \alpha \leq 90^{\circ}$ for **rectifier** (forward power flow)
- $90^{\circ} \leq \alpha \leq 180^{\circ}$ is **inverter** region (γ = 180° − α)
- $\gamma \geq \gamma_{min} \approx 15^{\circ}$–$18^{\circ}$ to avoid **commutation failure**
- $\alpha_{min} \approx 5^{\circ}$ to keep margin for **DC fault** de-ionization
- $V_{d0}\cos\alpha > V_{d0}\cos\gamma$ for **forward** power flow (AC1 → AC2)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The two-terminal equivalent circuit is the **starting point for every HVDC study**:
- **Power system planning:** determine required DC voltage for a given power transfer.
- **Control design:** The linearized equivalent ($V_d$ vs $I_d$ as a straight line) is the basis for the famous **V-I characteristic** of the HVDC link and the **current margin** control method.
- **Loss estimation:** $P_{loss} = R_d I_d^2$ (line) + converter losses (≈ 0.7% per station).
- **Voltage stability studies:** A weak AC system forces a high commutation reactance, which limits the power transfer and risks **voltage collapse**.
