In a **High Voltage Direct Current (HVDC)** transmission system employing **Line Commutated Converters (LCC)**, **Current Control at the Rectifier** refers to the strategy in which the sending-end converter (the *rectifier*) regulates the magnitude of the **direct current ($I_d$)** flowing through the DC transmission line by continuously adjusting its **firing angle ($\alpha$)**. The rectifier therefore operates on a **Constant Current (CC)** characteristic, in which the DC current is held at a reference value $I_{ref}$ (the *Current Order*) irrespective of fluctuations in the AC supply voltage or the DC line voltage.

Mathematically, the rectifier enforces:

$$I_d \approx I_{ref} = \text{const}$$

This is achieved by firing the thyristor valves at an angle $\alpha$ such that the DC voltage equation of the rectifier is satisfied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In a two-terminal HVDC link, the **rectifier controls current** (CC mode) and the **inverter controls voltage / extinction angle** (CEA mode). The stable operating point is the intersection of these two characteristics on the $V_d$–$I_d$ plane.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **water tap connected to a high-pressure pump** (the AC system) delivering water through a long pipe (the HVDC line) to a tank (the inverter end). The pump pressure can surge or dip, the pipe may slope up or down, and the tank level changes constantly. To keep the **flow rate** exactly constant, you need a **smart valve** at the pump end that opens a little more when pressure drops and closes a little when pressure surges. That smart valve is exactly what the **rectifier's current controller** does — it tweaks the **firing angle $\alpha$** (the valve opening) so that the *current* in the pipe stays fixed at the desired setpoint, even if the AC voltage or the DC line voltage tries to change it.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Nominal DC voltage ($V_d$):** Typically **±500 kV** or **±800 kV** for modern UHVDC systems.
- **Rated DC current ($I_d$):** Typically **2 kA to 5 kA**.
- **Firing angle ($\alpha$):** Operated in the range **5° ≤ α ≤ 20°** at the rectifier (to maintain small reactive power consumption and avoid mode crossover).
- **Overlap angle ($u$):** Typically **15° to 25°**.
- **Commutation reactance ($X_c$):** Per-phase, value depends on the converter transformer leakage reactance.
- **Current margin ($\Delta I$):** Usually **10% to 15%** of rated current.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Recap:**
> - **Current Order ($I_{ref}$):** The reference DC current commanded to the rectifier controller.
> - **Current Margin:** The intentional difference (≈ 10%) between the rectifier's current setting and the inverter's current setting, ensuring the two converters do not fight for control of the DC current.
> - **VDCOL (Voltage Dependent Current Order Limit):** A safety feature that reduces the current order when DC voltage falls, protecting the converter from operating at excessive angles.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Current is Controlled at the Rectifier (and not at the Inverter)

The assignment of control roles is **not arbitrary** — it follows directly from the physical behaviour of thyristor converters.

- A **thyristor** can only be turned **ON** by a gate pulse; it turns OFF only when the AC commutation voltage reverses polarity (natural commutation).
- At the **rectifier**, an increase in DC current $I_d$ causes a **larger voltage drop** across the commutation reactance. To *reduce* $I_d$, the controller must *decrease* the DC voltage, which is done by **increasing the firing angle $\alpha$**. This is achievable because $\alpha$ can be moved from a small value to a large value (e.g., 5° → 90°). So the rectifier can both **raise** and **lower** current — it has full bi-directional control authority.
- At the **inverter**, current control would require operating in the **rectifier region** (negative $\gamma$, i.e., $\alpha > 90°$), which is unstable and unsafe because it pushes the converter toward commutation failure.
- Hence, the **inverter is assigned the safer role of controlling the extinction angle $\gamma$** (CEA control), and the **rectifier takes charge of current**.

## 2.2 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Bulleted Reasoning)

- **Step 1:** Measure the actual DC current $I_d$ on the HVDC line using a **DC Current Transducer (DCCT)**.
- **Step 2:** Compare measured $I_d$ with the **Current Order** $I_{ref}$ (set by the operator / master controller).
- **Step 3:** Generate an **error signal** $\varepsilon = I_{ref} - I_d$.
- **Step 4:** Pass $\varepsilon$ through a **PI (Proportional-Integral) controller** to produce a desired firing angle $\alpha^*$.
- **Step 5:** Fire the thyristor valves at $\alpha = \alpha^*$ using the **Equidistant Firing Pulse Generator (EFPG)**.
- **Step 6:** The resulting DC voltage $V_d$ either rises (if $I_d < I_{ref}$) or falls (if $I_d > I_{ref}$), forcing $I_d$ back toward $I_{ref}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The rectifier is therefore a **negative feedback closed-loop controller** for the variable $I_d$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Variables | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|----------|---------|-----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | $V_{d0} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL}$ | Ideal no-load DC voltage of a 6-pulse bridge | $V_{LL}$ = line-to-line AC RMS voltage | 1.35 × $V_{LL}$ |
| 2 | $V_d = V_{d0} \cos\alpha - \dfrac{3}{\pi} X_c I_d$ | Rectifier DC voltage (with overlap) | $X_c$ = commutation reactance per phase | $\alpha$ = 5°–20° at rectifier |
| 3 | $V_d = V_{d0} \cos\gamma - \dfrac{3}{\pi} X_c I_d$ | Inverter DC voltage (in terms of $\gamma$) | $\gamma$ = extinction angle | $\gamma$ = 15°–18° at inverter |
| 4 | $I_d = \dfrac{V_{d0}(\cos\alpha - \cos\gamma)}{R_c + \dfrac{6}{\pi} X_c}$ | Steady-state DC current | $R_c$ = DC line resistance | Set by rectifier |
| 5 | $\cos\alpha = \cos\gamma + \dfrac{(R_c + \tfrac{6}{\pi}X_c)\, I_d}{V_{d0}}$ | $\alpha$ required for a given $I_d$ | — | 5°–20° |
| 6 | $P_d = V_d \cdot I_d$ | DC power transmitted | $P_d$ in **MW** | depends on rating |
| 7 | $Q_d = P_d \tan\phi$ | Reactive power drawn by converter | $\phi$ = displacement angle | rectifier absorbs $Q$ |
| 8 | $I_{margin} = I_{d,rect} - I_{d,inv}$ | Current margin (always positive) | — | 10%–15% of rated $I_d$ |
| 9 | $V_{d,\text{min}} = k \cdot V_{d,\text{rated}}$ | VDCOL threshold | $k \approx 0.4$ to 0.9 | activates at low $V_d$ |
| 10 | $u = \alpha - \cos^{-1}\!\left(\cos\alpha - \tfrac{2 X_c I_d}{\sqrt{2} V_{LL}}\right)$ | Overlap angle at rectifier | — | 15°–25° |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formulas above, all angles ($\alpha, \gamma, u$) are in **radians** when used in trigonometric functions, but the boundary values in the table are stated in **degrees** for ease of understanding. KTU exams typically accept either, but you must declare the unit explicitly.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Current control at the rectifier is **not a textbook abstraction** — it is the operational backbone of every HVDC installation worldwide:

- **CIGRÉ HVDC Benchmark System** uses the rectifier in CC mode as the textbook default.
- **Power swing damping:** When a disturbance occurs on the AC side (e.g., a fault), the rectifier's fast current controller maintains power flow, helping the interconnected AC grids ride through the disturbance.
- **Power flow scheduling:** Operators can ramp $I_{ref}$ up/down to schedule the exact amount of power flowing through the DC link, independently of the AC system's load.
- **DC line fault isolation:** When a DC fault occurs, the rectifier quickly pushes $\alpha$ to ~90° (inverter mode) to force $I_d \to 0$, allowing de-energisation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Rectifier DC Voltage Equation $V_d = V_{d0}\cos\alpha - \frac{3}{\pi}X_c I_d$

### Assumptions
- 6-pulse Graetz bridge.
- Balanced, sinusoidal, distortion-free AC supply.
- DC current $I_d$ is constant and ripple-free (large smoothing inductor $L_d$).
- Valve voltage drops and commutation overlap are partially neglected; only $X_c$ is retained.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The instantaneous phase-a AC voltage is:
$$v_a(t) = \sqrt{2} V_{ph} \sin(\omega t)$$

The instantaneous line-to-line voltage $v_{ab}(t)$ is:
$$v_{ab}(t) = \sqrt{2} V_{LL} \sin\!\left(\omega t + \frac{\pi}{6}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Average of an un-delayed conduction segment (no firing delay, $\alpha = 0$):**

$$V_{d0} = \frac{1}{\pi/3}\int_{-\pi/6}^{\pi/6} \sqrt{2} V_{LL} \cos\theta \, d\theta$$

Evaluating:
$$V_{d0} = \frac{3}{\pi}\sqrt{2}\, V_{LL} \cdot \Big[\sin\theta\Big]_{-\pi/6}^{\pi/6} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL} \cdot 1 = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL}$$

**Step 2 — Introduce firing delay $\alpha$:** The integration limits shift from $[-\pi/6, +\pi/6]$ to $[\alpha - \pi/6, \alpha + \pi/6]$. By symmetry the average becomes:

$$V_d^{\prime} = \frac{1}{\pi/3}\int_{\alpha - \pi/6}^{\alpha + \pi/6} \sqrt{2} V_{LL} \cos(\omega t - \alpha_0)\, d(\omega t)$$

This evaluates to:
$$V_d^{\prime} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL} \cos\alpha = V_{d0} \cos\alpha$$

**Step 3 — Account for commutation overlap $u$:** During overlap, two valves share conduction. The commutation voltage equation is:

$$\sqrt{2} V_{LL} \sin(\omega t) = X_c \frac{di}{dt}$$

Integrating over the overlap period gives a mean voltage drop:
$$\Delta V = \frac{3}{\pi} X_c I_d$$

**Step 4 — Combine:**
$$V_d = V_{d0}\cos\alpha - \frac{3}{\pi} X_c I_d$$

This is the canonical rectifier equation used in **every HVDC textbook and KTU problem**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Steady-State DC Line Current

The DC line is modelled as a series resistance $R_c$ between the rectifier terminal and the inverter terminal. KVL on the DC side:

$$V_{d,rec} - V_{d,inv} = R_c I_d$$

Substituting the rectifier and inverter voltage equations:

$$V_{d0,rec}\cos\alpha - \frac{3}{\pi} X_{c,rec} I_d \;-\; \left(V_{d0,inv}\cos\gamma - \frac{3}{\pi} X_{c,inv} I_d\right) = R_c I_d$$

If we lump the commutation reactances and assume $V_{d0,rec} = V_{d0,inv} = V_{d0}$:

$$V_{d0}(\cos\alpha - \cos\gamma) = R_c I_d + \frac{3}{\pi}(X_{c,rec} + X_{c,inv}) I_d$$

$$\boxed{\;I_d = \frac{V_{d0}(\cos\alpha - \cos\gamma)}{R_c + \frac{6}{\pi} X_c}\;}$$

This single equation shows that the **rectifier ($\alpha$) and the inverter ($\gamma$) jointly determine the current** — but in operation, the rectifier varies $\alpha$ to keep $I_d$ at $I_{ref}$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Rectifier vs Inverter Control

| Parameter | Rectifier (Sending End) | Inverter (Receiving End) |
|-----------|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| Control mode | **Constant Current (CC)** | **Constant Extinction Angle (CEA)** |
| Manipulated variable | Firing angle $\alpha$ | Extinction angle $\gamma$ |
| Typical operating angle | 5°–20° | 15°–18° |
| Can it regulate $I_d$? | **Yes** (full authority) | No (would risk commutation failure) |
| Can it regulate $V_d$? | Indirectly (via $I_d \cdot R_c$) | **Yes** (set by $\gamma$ and $V_{d0}$) |
| Reactive power | Absorbs $Q$ from AC grid | Absorbs $Q$ from AC grid |
| Back-up mode | Min-$\alpha$ (= uncontrolled rectifier) | Min-$\gamma$ / Current control |
| Failure mode response | Pushes $\alpha \to 90°$ → blocks | Inherently limits via $\gamma$ |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Condition for Stable CC–CEA Intersection

The rectifier characteristic is a **near-vertical line** at $I_d = I_{ref}$ (with a small slope to allow co-ordination). The inverter characteristic is:

$$V_{d,inv} = V_{d0}\cos\gamma - \frac{3}{\pi} X_c I_d$$

which is a **straight line with negative slope** in the $V_d$–$I_d$ plane. The stable operating point is the intersection. The **slope of the rectifier CC characteristic** is chosen to be slightly positive (a few percent) so that the intersection always lies on the active CC segment, never on the slope. This guarantees:

$$V_{d,rec}(I_{ref}) > V_{d,inv}(I_{ref})$$

ensuring a **positive current margin** $\Delta I = I_{d,rec} - I_{d,inv}$.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Given:**
- $V_{LL} = 220$ kV (AC side of rectifier transformer)
- $X_c = 12 \, \Omega$ per phase
- $R_c = 6 \, \Omega$ (total DC loop resistance)
- $\alpha = 15°$, $\gamma = 18°$

**Find:** $I_d$ and $P_d$.

**Step 1 — Compute $V_{d0}$:**
$$V_{d0} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \times 220\,\text{kV} = 1.35 \times 220 = 297\,\text{kV}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator:**
$$V_{d0}(\cos\alpha - \cos\gamma) = 297\,(\cos 15° - \cos 18°)$$
$$= 297\,(0.9659 - 0.9511) = 297 \times 0.0148 = 4.396\,\text{kV}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator:**
$$R_c + \frac{6}{\pi} X_c = 6 + \frac{6}{\pi}(12) = 6 + 22.92 = 28.92\,\Omega$$

**Step 4 — DC current:**
$$I_d = \frac{4396}{28.92} = 152.0\,\text{A}$$

**Step 5 — DC power at the rectifier terminals:**
$$P_d = V_{d,rec} \times I_d = \left(V_{d0}\cos\alpha - \frac{3}{\pi}X_c I_d\right) \times I_d$$
$$V_{d,rec} = 297(0.9659) - 1.91 \times 0.152\,\text{kV} = 286.8 - 0.291 = 286.5\,\text{kV}$$
$$P_d = 286.5 \times 0.152 = 43.55\,\text{MW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU exams usually present a 2-terminal system with the inverter end specified as $\gamma$ = const, and ask for $I_d$, $P_d$, $Q_d$, and reactive power exchange. Practice the above steps thoroughly.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Rectifier Current Controller (Closed-Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Grid Side] --> B[Converter Transformer]
    B --> C[Thyristor Valve Bridge 6-pulse]
    C --> D[Smoothing Reactor Ld]
    D --> E[DC Transmission Line]
    E --> F[Inverter End]
    F --> G[AC Grid Receiving End]

    H[Current Order Iref from Operator] --> I[Summing Junction]
    J[DCCT Measured Id] --> I
    I --> K[Error Signal epsilon]
    K --> L[PI Controller]
    L --> M[Alpha Reference Generator]
    M --> N[Equidistant Firing Pulse Generator EFPG]
    N --> C
    C -. feedback via DCCT .-> J
```

**Read-out of the diagram:**
- The DCCT measures the actual DC current and feeds it back.
- The summing junction computes the error $\varepsilon = I_{ref} - I_d$.
- The PI controller produces the required $\alpha^*$.
- The Equidistant Firing Pulse Generator (EFPG) ensures that the six pulses are exactly $60°$ apart regardless of AC frequency variations.

## 4.2 Vd-Id Characteristic — Rectifier & Inverter

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Vd_Id_Plane["V_d versus I_d Plane"]
        R["RECTIFIER: Constant Current (CC) Line --- I = I_ref, V_d varies"]
        I["INVERTER: Constant Extinction Angle (CEA) Line --- negative slope: V_d = V_d0 cos gamma minus 3 by pi X_c I_d"]
        M["Minimum alpha line --- V_d = V_d0 cos alpha_min --- backup"]
        OP["Operating Point --- Intersection of CC and CEA"]
    end

    R -. intersects .-> OP
    I -. intersects .-> OP
    M -. used when CC fails .-> R
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **CC line** is drawn with a slight positive slope (typically 3%–5%) rather than perfectly vertical — this small slope is what enables the two characteristics to cross at exactly one unique point. KTU frequently asks why the slope is non-zero.

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Master Control Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Master Controller - Power Order MW] --> B[Bipolar Power Allocator]
    B --> C1[Pole 1 Current Order]
    B --> C2[Pole 2 Current Order]
    C1 --> D1[Rectifier Pole 1 Current Controller]
    C2 --> D2[Rectifier Pole 2 Current Controller]
    D1 --> E1[Alpha Command 1]
    D2 --> E2[Alpha Command 2]
    E1 --> F1[Firing Pulses to Valves]
    E2 --> F2[Firing Pulses to Valves]

    G[VDCOL - Voltage Dependent Current Order Limit] -. overrides .-> D1
    G -. overrides .-> D2
```

**Read-out:**
- The **master controller** sets the *power* order (in MW), not the current.
- The power is divided between the two poles of a bipolar HVDC link.
- Each pole-level **current controller** at the rectifier end converts the MW order into an $I_{ref}$.
- The **VDCOL** sits between the master controller and the pole controllers, automatically *reducing* the current order when $V_d$ falls below a threshold.

## 4.4 Mode-Switching State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CC_Mode
    CC_Mode --> Min_Alpha_Mode : AC voltage drop / alpha hits lower limit
    Min_Alpha_Mode --> CC_Mode : AC voltage restored
    CC_Mode --> Inverter_Block : DC line fault detected alpha pushed to 90 plus degrees
    Inverter_Block --> CC_Mode : fault cleared and deionisation time elapsed
    CC_Mode --> VDCOL_Active : V_d below threshold
    VDCOL_Active --> CC_Mode : V_d recovered above threshold
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Why is current control assigned to the rectifier and not to the inverter in a two-terminal HVDC link?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The thyristor converter at the rectifier has full bi-directional authority over the firing angle $\alpha$ (can move $\alpha$ between ~5° and ~150°), which lets it *both raise and lower* the DC voltage, and hence *both raise and lower* the DC current. (1.5 marks)
2. At the inverter, controlling current would require operating the converter in the *rectifier region* (i.e., advancing the firing such that commutation margin is lost), which risks commutation failure. (1 mark)
3. Hence, the inverter is assigned the safer role of **Constant Extinction Angle (CEA)** control, leaving current control to the rectifier. (0.5 marks)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** What is the "current margin" in an HVDC system? Why is a 10% current margin maintained between the rectifier and inverter characteristics?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The current margin is the difference $\Delta I = I_{d,rec} - I_{d,inv}$ between the current orders of the rectifier and the inverter. (1 mark)
2. A positive $\Delta I$ (typically 10%) ensures that the rectifier's CC line and the inverter's CEA line **intersect on the rectifier's CC segment**, so that the *rectifier* always wins the right to control $I_d$. (1 mark)
3. If the margin is too small, AC voltage fluctuations may cause the intersection to drift onto the inverter's CC segment, leading to *mode-crossing* and unstable operation. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**A two-terminal monopolar HVDC link has the following data:**
- AC supply (rectifier side): $V_{LL} = 230$ kV
- Commutation reactance: $X_c = 10\,\Omega$ per phase
- DC line resistance (loop): $R_c = 5\,\Omega$
- Rectifier firing angle $\alpha = 12°$, inverter extinction angle $\gamma = 18°$

**(a)** Derive the steady-state DC current $I_d$ flowing through the link. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the rectifier's DC voltage, the DC power transmitted, and the reactive power absorbed by the rectifier. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:** $V_{LL} = 230$ kV, $X_c = 10\,\Omega$, $R_c = 5\,\Omega$, $\alpha = 12°$, $\gamma = 18°$.

**Step 1 — Compute $V_{d0}$:**
$$V_{d0} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi}\, V_{LL} = 1.35 \times 230 = 310.5\,\text{kV}$$

**Step 2 — Use the steady-state current equation:**
$$I_d = \frac{V_{d0}(\cos\alpha - \cos\gamma)}{R_c + \frac{6}{\pi} X_c}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**
$$V_{d0}(\cos 12° - \cos 18°) = 310.5 \times (0.9781 - 0.9511) = 310.5 \times 0.0270 = 8.38\,\text{kV}$$

**Step 4 — Denominator:**
$$R_c + \frac{6}{\pi} X_c = 5 + \frac{6 \times 10}{\pi} = 5 + 19.10 = 24.10\,\Omega$$

**Step 5 — DC current:**
$$\boxed{I_d = \frac{8380}{24.10} = 347.7\,\text{A}}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Rectifier DC voltage:**
$$V_{d,rec} = V_{d0}\cos\alpha - \frac{3}{\pi} X_c I_d$$
$$V_{d,rec} = 310.5 \times 0.9781 - 1.91 \times 0.3477$$
$$= 303.7 - 0.664 = 303.0\,\text{kV}$$

**Step 2 — DC power at the rectifier terminal:**
$$P_d = V_{d,rec} \times I_d = 303.0 \times 0.3477 = 105.4\,\text{MW}$$

**Step 3 — Displacement angle of the rectifier:**
$$\phi_{rec} \approx \alpha + \frac{u}{2} \approx 12° + 5° = 17° \;\;(\text{rough estimate})$$

A cleaner approach uses the formula:
$$\cos\phi \approx \frac{1}{2}(\cos\alpha + \cos(\alpha + u))$$

Assuming $u \approx 20°$, $\cos\phi = \frac{1}{2}(\cos 12° + \cos 32°) = \frac{1}{2}(0.9781 + 0.8480) = 0.9130$
$$\phi = \cos^{-1}(0.9130) = 24.0°$$

**Step 4 — Reactive power absorbed:**
$$Q_d = P_d \tan\phi = 105.4 \times \tan(24°) = 105.4 \times 0.4452 = 46.9\,\text{MVAR}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $I_d = 347.7$ A
- $V_{d,rec} = 303.0$ kV
- $P_d = 105.4$ MW
- $Q_d = 46.9$ MVAR (absorbed by rectifier from the AC grid)

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat labelled $V_d$–$I_d$ characteristic, explain how the rectifier and inverter characteristics intersect to give the operating point of an HVDC link. Show the role of the **current margin** in ensuring stable operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **VDCOL**? Why is it used? Sketch its characteristic and explain its effect on the rectifier current order during AC-side voltage dips. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **The two characteristics** (2 marks):
   - **Rectifier CC characteristic:** a near-vertical line at $I_d = I_{ref}$ with a small positive slope, indicating that the rectifier holds current constant.
   - **Inverter CEA characteristic:** $V_{d,inv} = V_{d0,inv}\cos\gamma - \frac{3}{\pi} X_{c,inv} I_d$, a straight line with negative slope in the $V_d$–$I_d$ plane.
2. **Intersection** (2 marks):
   - The intersection is the unique stable operating point. To the left, $V_{d,rec} > V_{d,inv}$, so $I_d$ rises; to the right, $I_d$ falls. The system always returns to the intersection.
3. **Current margin** (2 marks):
   - The CC line of the rectifier is offset to the right of the inverter's CC line by 10%. This guarantees the intersection lies on the rectifier's CC line — so the *rectifier* is in command of the current.
4. **Stability criterion** (1 mark):
   - The positive slope of the rectifier CC line (3%–5%) ensures the intersection cannot slide to the right end of the characteristic, preventing instability.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

1. **Definition of VDCOL (2 marks):** Voltage Dependent Current Order Limit is a protection function that *reduces* the current order $I_{ref}$ when the DC voltage $V_d$ falls below a pre-set threshold.
2. **Why it is used (2 marks):**
   - At low $V_d$, the converter would have to operate at large $\alpha$ or large $\gamma$, leading to excessive reactive power consumption and risk of commutation failure.
   - Lowering $I_d$ allows $V_d$ to recover.
3. **Sketch description (2 marks):**
   - For $V_d$ above the upper threshold (typically 0.9 pu), $I_{ref}$ is held at rated value.
   - For $V_d$ between upper and lower threshold (0.4–0.9 pu), $I_{ref}$ decreases linearly.
   - For $V_d$ below the lower threshold, $I_{ref}$ is held at a minimum.
4. **Effect during AC voltage dip (1 mark):**
   - When the AC voltage drops, $V_{d0}$ falls, $V_d$ falls, VDCOL kicks in, $I_{ref}$ reduces, the rectifier's $\alpha$ reduces, the converter consumes less reactive power, and the system stabilises.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\frac{3}{\pi}X_c I_d$ voltage drop term** in $V_d$. This term appears in *almost every HVDC problem* and is the single most common source of lost marks.
> 2. **Mixing up $\alpha$ and $\gamma$.** A firing angle $\alpha$ belongs to the rectifier; an extinction angle $\gamma$ belongs to the inverter. Do not use them interchangeably.
> 3. **Forgetting units in the final answer** (kV vs V, MW vs W, kA vs A). KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for this.
> 4. **Drawing the CC characteristic as a perfectly vertical line** — KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests whether you know that the CC line has a small positive slope to enable co-ordination.
> 5. **Not stating the numerical value of the current margin** (typically 10% of rated current) when the question asks "what is current margin?".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Rectifier = Current Controller; Inverter = Voltage ($\gamma$) Controller.** This is the fundamental *division of labour* in a 2-terminal LCC-HVDC system.
- The rectifier's job is to **measure $I_d$, compare with $I_{ref}$, and adjust $\alpha$** via a PI controller and the Equidistant Firing Pulse Generator (EFPG).
- **Key rectifier equation:** $V_{d,rec} = V_{d0}\cos\alpha - \frac{3}{\pi} X_c I_d$.
- **Key inverter equation:** $V_{d,inv} = V_{d0}\cos\gamma - \frac{3}{\pi} X_c I_d$.
- **Steady-state DC current:** $I_d = \dfrac{V_{d0}(\cos\alpha - \cos\gamma)}{R_c + \frac{6}{\pi}X_c}$.
- **No-load DC voltage:** $V_{d0} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi}V_{LL} = 1.35\,V_{LL}$.
- **Typical firing angle range** at rectifier: **5°–20°**. **Typical extinction angle** at inverter: **15°–18°**.
- **Current margin** $\Delta I$ = (rated $I_d$) × **10%** keeps the rectifier in command of $I_d$.
- The rectifier CC line has a **small positive slope** (3%–5%), not a perfectly vertical line.
- **VDCOL** automatically lowers $I_{ref}$ when $V_d$ falls — a critical protection feature.
- **Mode-switching:** Rectifier can fall back to *Min-$\alpha$* mode if AC voltage drops; can switch to *Inverter Block* ($\alpha \to 90°+$) for DC faults.
- The rectifier is a **negative-feedback closed-loop** controller with **DCCT $\to$ summing junction $\to$ PI $\to$ EFPG $\to$ thyristor valves**.
- **Reactive power:** Both rectifier and inverter *absorb* reactive power from their respective AC grids.
- **DC power:** $P_d = V_d \cdot I_d$.
- **For every KTU problem:** keep units clean (kV, kA, MW, MVAR, Ω) and remember to convert radians $\leftrightarrow$ degrees when stating angles.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inverter Extinction Angle Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET862 - HVDC AND FACTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inverter Extinction Angle Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inverter Extinction Angle Control (CEA Control)** is a fundamental control strategy employed at the **receiving-end inverter** of an HVDC link, in which the **firing delay angle (α)** is continuously adjusted so that the **extinction angle (γ)** — the angular interval between the end of commutation and the next zero-crossing of the commutating voltage — is maintained at, or above, a pre-defined **minimum extinction angle (γ_min)**. This ensures successful commutation of the thyristor valves and prevents **commutation failure** in the inverter.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme nomenclature, this is also referred to as the **Constant Extinction Angle (CEA)** control, and the operating point is typically marked on the steady-state **V-I characteristic** of the converter as the **γ = γ_min** locus.

## 1.2 Key Terminology Quick-Reference

| Symbol | Parameter | Typical Range (HVDC) |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| **α** | Firing (delay) angle of inverter | 90° ≤ α ≤ 180° |
| **γ** | Extinction angle | 15° ≤ γ ≤ 25° |
| **μ** | Overlap (commutation) angle | 15° ≤ μ ≤ 25° |
| **β** | Firing advance angle (β = 180° − α) | 35° ≤ β ≤ 90° |
| **δ** | Margin angle (δ = γ − μ) | 5° to 10° |
| **u** | Ignition (firing) advance angle (u = β − μ) | 5° to 10° |

> [!NOTE]
> **The cardinal relationship that governs every HVDC inverter is:**
> $$\gamma = \beta - \mu = 180° - \alpha - \mu$$
> This single equation links the extinction angle to the firing angle and the overlap angle, and is the foundation for all inverter control strategies.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Bridge Demolition" Picture

Imagine a **revolving drawbridge** that allows ships to pass. The bridge can only begin rotating open **after** the previous car has fully cleared it (this clearing time is analogous to the **overlap angle μ**). Once it has cleared, there must still be a **safety margin** of time (this is the **extinction angle γ**) before the next car attempts to enter — otherwise, a collision occurs. The bridge operator must therefore set the opening trigger early enough that, even if a ship is slow to leave (large μ), there is still safe clearance (γ ≥ γ_min). This is exactly what **Inverter Extinction Angle Control** does for thyristor valves: it ensures the valve has reliably regained its forward blocking capability before the next valve in the sequence applies forward voltage.

## 1.4 Why Is This Control Necessary?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A thyristor, once fired, **cannot be turned off by the gate** — it turns off only when the current through it falls to zero AND a sufficient **de-ionization (turn-off) time** elapses before forward voltage reapplies. If forward voltage reapplies too soon, the thyristor re-fires uncontrollably → **commutation failure**.

The extinction angle γ directly represents this de-ionization window. Hence, **inverter control must guarantee γ ≥ γ_min** at all operating points. This is the *prime* reason for the existence of CEA control.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Logical Flow

The Inverter Extinction Angle Control operates in a closed-loop feedback manner. The logical steps are:

1. **Measure** the actual extinction angle γ_actual from valve voltage/current waveforms (typically via the zero-crossing of the post-commutation valve voltage).
2. **Compare** γ_actual with the reference γ_ref (= γ_min for CEA operation).
3. **Generate an error signal**: $\varepsilon = \gamma_{ref} - \gamma_{actual}$.
4. **Adjust** the firing delay angle α (equivalently, β) through the **Gate Pulse Generator (GPG)**:
   - If γ_actual < γ_ref → **increase α** (delay the firing more) to give the outgoing valve more turn-off time.
   - If γ_actual > γ_ref → **decrease α** (fire earlier) to maintain desired DC voltage.
5. The inverter naturally operates at the intersection of the **γ = γ_min locus** and the **constant-current (CC) locus** of the rectifier.

> [!NOTE]
> Because γ is **not a directly measured electrical quantity** (it is computed from the valve voltage waveform), the controller is implemented using either **Individual Phase Control (IPC)** with α-min/γ-min transition or **Equal Pulse-Width Control (EPWC)** in modern HVDC systems.

## 2.2 The α − γ − μ Triangle

```
              β (firing advance angle)
        <--------------------------->
        |                          |
        |   overlap   |  extinction |
        |     μ       |      γ      |
        |             |             |
   ─────|─────────────|─────────────|─────>  180° axis
        0            β-μ          180°-α
                  ←────→  ←────→
                   μ        γ
```

- **β − μ = u** → the *ignition advance angle*
- **180° − α = β** → fundamental converter identity
- **γ = β − μ** → extinction angle definition

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam-grade** and are directly applicable to numerical problems in KTU ESE.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Conditions |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $\gamma = 180° - \alpha - \mu$ | Fundamental inverter relation | Universal |
| 2 | $V_{d} = V_{d0}\cos\alpha - R_{c}I_{d}$ | Rectifier DC voltage | Steady state |
| 3 | $V_{d} = V_{d0}\cos\gamma - R_{c}I_{d}$ | Inverter DC voltage (CEA form) | Steady state |
| 4 | $V_{d0} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL}$ | Ideal no-load DC voltage | 6-pulse bridge |
| 5 | $\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha+\mu) = \dfrac{2 I_{d} X_{c}}{\sqrt{2} V_{LL}}$ | Overlap relation (rectifier) | Constant current source |
| 6 | $\cos\gamma - \cos(\gamma+\mu) = \dfrac{2 I_{d} X_{c}}{\sqrt{2} V_{LL}}$ | Overlap relation (inverter) | Constant current source |
| 7 | $\mu = \cos^{-1}\!\left[\cos\gamma - \dfrac{\sqrt{2} I_{d} X_{c}}{V_{LL}}\right] - \gamma$ | μ as function of γ, Id | CEA control |
| 8 | $P_{d} = V_{d} \cdot I_{d}$ | DC power transmitted | Universal |
| 9 | $\delta = \gamma - \mu$ | Margin (turn-off safety) angle | Must be > 0 |
| 10 | $\gamma_{min} \approx 15°\text{ to }18°$ | Minimum extinction angle | Modern thyristors |

> [!NOTE]
> In all the formulas above, $X_{c} = \omega L_{c}$ is the per-phase **commutation reactance**, $R_{c}$ is the per-phase **commutation resistance**, and $V_{LL}$ is the line-to-line RMS AC voltage on the converter bus.

## 2.4 Inverter V-I Characteristic (Steady State)

The complete **steady-state V-I characteristic** of the inverter under CEA control is composed of three segments:

1. **Segment AB — CEA segment (γ = γ_min, fixed):** The DC voltage varies with current according to the commutation overlap. Voltage *decreases* as current increases.
2. **Segment BC — Constant Current (CC) segment:** The inverter operates as a constant-current source, holding α constant.
3. **Segment CD — α-min segment:** Voltage drops sharply if current drops too low (risk of $\alpha$ hitting its lower limit).

The intersection of the rectifier CC line and the inverter CEA locus determines the **steady-state operating point**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Commutation failure prevention** in 800 kV UHVDC links (e.g., Champa-Kurukshetra, Xiangjiaba-Shanghai).
- **Power swing damping** during AC-side disturbances.
- **Recovery dynamics** after AC faults — fast γ-loop controllers (response ~ 1–2 ms).
- **Integration with renewable-rich weak AC networks** where voltage fluctuations threaten commutation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Fundamental Identity γ = 180° − α − μ

**Starting Premise:** A thyristor valve is fired at angular instant $\omega t = (\pi - \alpha)$ in the AC cycle and conducts until the commutation from it to the next valve completes. The commutation itself occupies an angular interval $\mu$. After commutation ends, the valve is exposed to negative voltage for a duration $\gamma$ before the next valve is fired.

**Step 1 — Firing instant of incoming valve:**
$$\omega t_{f,incoming} = \pi - \alpha$$

**Step 2 — End of commutation (when outgoing valve current reaches zero):**
$$\omega t_{end,comm} = \pi - \alpha + \mu$$

**Step 3 — Firing instant of the *next* valve in sequence (which would re-apply forward voltage to the outgoing valve):**
$$\omega t_{f,next} = 2\pi - \alpha$$

**Step 4 — Extinction angle is defined as the angular gap between the end of commutation and the next firing instant:**
$$\gamma = \omega t_{f,next} - \omega t_{end,comm} = (2\pi - \alpha) - (\pi - \alpha + \mu)$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**
$$\gamma = \pi - \mu = 180° - \mu \quad \text{(in degrees)}$$

**Wait — correction:** The above assumes the next valve in the SAME pair. In a 6-pulse bridge, the next firing is **60° away** for the *next* valve, but the relevant next forward voltage to the outgoing valve comes from the valve that is **fired 60° later** in the firing sequence of that commutation group. The correct universal definition is:

$$\gamma = 180° - \alpha - \mu$$

**Step 6 — Verification with numerical example (KTU textbook standard):**
- Take $\alpha = 140°$ and $\mu = 20°$
- Then $\gamma = 180° - 140° - 20° = 20°$ ✓ (a typical safe inverter value)

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — DC Voltage Across the Inverter (CEA form)

The general expression for average DC voltage of a 6-pulse bridge with overlap is:
$$V_{d} = \dfrac{V_{d0}}{2}\bigl[\cos\alpha + \cos(\alpha + \mu)\bigr]$$

**Step 1 — Substitute** $(\alpha + \mu) = (180° - \gamma)$ because $\alpha + \mu + \gamma = 180°$ ⇒ $\alpha + \mu = 180° - \gamma$.

**Step 2 — Apply trigonometric identity:** $\cos(180° - \gamma) = -\cos\gamma$.

**Step 3 — Plug into V_d equation:**
$$V_{d} = \dfrac{V_{d0}}{2}\bigl[\cos\alpha - \cos\gamma\bigr]$$

**Step 4 — Using commutation relation:** $\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha+\mu) = \dfrac{2 I_{d} X_{c}}{\sqrt{2} V_{LL}}$, and noting $V_{d0} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi}V_{LL}$:

$$\cos\alpha - \cos\gamma = \dfrac{2 I_{d} X_{c}}{\sqrt{2} V_{LL}} = \dfrac{I_{d} X_{c} \pi}{3 V_{LL}} \cdot \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back:**
$$V_{d} = \dfrac{V_{d0}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{I_{d} X_{c} \pi}{3 V_{LL}}$$

**Step 6 — Realizable, clean CEA form** (after including commutation resistance drop):
$$\boxed{\,V_{d} = V_{d0}\cos\gamma - \dfrac{3 X_{c}}{\pi} I_{d}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical CEA control equation** of an HVDC inverter, and is the equation whose V-I plot defines the γ-min locus on the converter characteristic.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Overlap μ as a Function of γ and I_d (for Numerical Problems)

**Starting equation (commutation relation in CEA form):**
$$\cos\gamma - \cos(\gamma + \mu) = \dfrac{2 I_{d} X_{c}}{\sqrt{2}\, V_{LL}}$$

**Step 1 — Let** $k = \dfrac{2 I_{d} X_{c}}{\sqrt{2}\, V_{LL}}$

**Step 2 — Then:** $\cos(\gamma + \mu) = \cos\gamma - k$

**Step 3 — Take inverse cosine on both sides:**
$$\gamma + \mu = \cos^{-1}\bigl[\cos\gamma - k\bigr]$$

**Step 4 — Solve for μ:**
$$\boxed{\,\mu = \cos^{-1}\!\left[\cos\gamma - \dfrac{2 I_{d} X_{c}}{\sqrt{2}\, V_{LL}}\right] - \gamma\,}$$

**Numerical Worked Example (KTU standard 7-mark style):**
*Given:* $\gamma = 18°$, $V_{LL} = 220$ kV, $I_d = 2000$ A, $X_c = 18$ Ω.

**Step 1 — Compute k:**
$$k = \dfrac{2 \times 2000 \times 18}{\sqrt{2} \times 220{,}000} = \dfrac{72{,}000}{311{,}127} = 0.2314$$

**Step 2 — Compute the bracket:**
$$\cos\gamma - k = \cos 18° - 0.2314 = 0.9511 - 0.2314 = 0.7197$$

**Step 3 — Inverse cosine:**
$$\cos^{-1}(0.7197) = 44.03°$$

**Step 4 — Subtract γ:**
$$\mu = 44.03° - 18° = 26.03°$$

**Step 5 — Margin (turn-off safety) angle:**
$$\delta = \gamma - \mu = 18° - 26.03° = -8.03°$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The negative margin angle above indicates commutation failure.** This shows why γ_min is set to about **15°–18°** for modern thyristors — to ensure δ remains positive under all operating conditions.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Control Type | Controlled Variable | Operating Region | Risk |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---|
| Rectifier — Constant Current (CC) | $\alpha$ | $\alpha_{min} \le \alpha \le \alpha_{max}$ | α hitting limits |
| Inverter — Constant Extinction Angle (CEA) | $\gamma$ | $\gamma = \gamma_{min}$ | Commutation failure if γ < γ_min |
| Inverter — Constant Current (CC) | $\alpha$ | $\alpha_{min} \le \alpha$ | Mode change with rectifier |
| Inverter — Constant Power (CP) | $\alpha, \gamma$ | Adaptive | Stability issues |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of CEA Control Loop (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Voltage v_ac at Inverter Bus] --> B[Valve Voltage/Current Sensors]
    C[DC Current I_d] --> B
    B --> D[Extinction Angle Calculator gamma_meas]
    D --> E{gamma_meas less than gamma_ref ?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Increase Firing Delay alpha]
    E -- No --> G[Decrease Firing Delay alpha]
    F --> H[Gate Pulse Generator GPG]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Thyristor Valve Firing Pulses]
    I --> J[Inverter Bridge 6-pulse]
    J --> K[DC Link V_d, I_d]
    K --> A
    K --> C
    L[gamma_ref = gamma_min setpoint] --> E
```

## 4.2 Steady-State V-I Characteristic of the HVDC Link

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REC[Rectifier End]
        R1[Constant Current CC] --- R2[alpha-min Limit]
    end
    subgraph INV[Inverter End]
        I1[Constant Extinction Angle CEA] --- I2[Constant Current CC] --- I3[alpha-min Limit]
    end
    REC -- DC Link --> INV
    OP((Steady State
    Operating Point)) -.-> R1
    OP -.-> I1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The steady-state operating point is the **intersection of the Rectifier CC line and the Inverter CEA locus**. The Current Margin (typically 10–15% of rated current) is the horizontal gap between these two lines.

## 4.3 Waveform Schematic — Valve Voltage with γ Highlighted

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph W1[Commutation Interval]
        A1[Valve 3 Conducts] -->|firing of valve 5| A2[Overlap mu]
        A2 -->|end of commutation| A3[Valve 5 fully ON]
    end
    subgraph W2[Extinction Window]
        A3 -->|gamma interval| A4[Valve 3 reverse biased]
        A4 -->|firing of valve 1| A5[Valve 3 forward biased again]
    end
```

## 4.4 Mode Transition Diagram — α-min / CEA / CC

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CEA: Normal operation
    CEA --> CC_inv: I_d decreases below margin
    CC_inv --> alpha_min: I_d drops further
    alpha_min --> CEA: I_d recovers
    CC_inv --> CEA: Disturbance cleared
    CEA --> [*]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define **extinction angle γ** of an HVDC inverter. Why must it always be maintained above a minimum value?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The extinction angle γ is the angular interval, expressed in electrical degrees, between the **end of commutation** (when the outgoing thyristor current falls to zero) and the **next zero-crossing of the anode-cathode voltage** of that valve (when forward voltage reappears). In an inverter, it represents the de-ionization time available for the outgoing thyristor to regain forward blocking capability. If γ becomes less than the **turn-off time (t_q)** of the thyristor (converted to electrical angle), the valve re-ignites unintentionally, leading to **commutation failure** — a severe disturbance that can collapse the DC link. Hence γ is always held at or above γ_min (typically 15°–18° for modern HVDC thyristors). **[3 marks]**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** State and explain the relationship between the **firing angle α**, **overlap angle μ**, and **extinction angle γ** of an HVDC inverter.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three angles are related by the **fundamental identity** of an HVDC inverter:
$$\alpha + \mu + \gamma = 180°$$

**Derivation logic:** A valve is fired at $(180° - \alpha)$, conducts through overlap μ, and then must wait a further angular interval γ before the next valve in the same commutating group is fired (re-applying forward voltage to it). Since the next firing occurs $180° - \alpha$ later in the cycle, the total angular budget of $180°$ is split into overlap μ and extinction γ. This single equation is the **foundation of every inverter control equation**, since rearranging gives $\gamma = 180° - \alpha - \mu$. **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the principle of **Constant Extinction Angle (CEA) control** of an HVDC inverter. Derive the steady-state V-I characteristic of the inverter under CEA control. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 500 kV, 2000 A, 6-pulse HVDC inverter operates under CEA control with $\gamma_{min} = 18°$. The AC line-to-line voltage at the inverter bus is 220 kV, and the commutation reactance per phase is 18 Ω. Compute **(i)** the no-load DC voltage $V_{d0}$, **(ii)** the overlap angle μ, and **(iii)** the margin (turn-off safety) angle δ. State clearly whether the operation is safe. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

#### (a) — Principle and V-I Characteristic (7 marks)

**Principle (3 marks):**
The CEA control continuously adjusts the inverter firing angle α such that the extinction angle γ is maintained at a pre-defined minimum value γ_min. This is achieved by a closed-loop feedback controller:
- A **γ-measuring circuit** computes γ from the valve voltage waveform.
- The **error** $(\gamma_{ref} - \gamma_{meas})$ drives the **gate pulse generator** to advance or retard α.
- Since γ cannot be reduced below the safety threshold, the inverter's V-I curve becomes a **fixed γ = γ_min locus**.

**Steady-state V-I relation derivation (4 marks):**
Average DC voltage of a 6-pulse bridge with overlap is:
$$V_{d} = \dfrac{V_{d0}}{2}\bigl[\cos\alpha + \cos(\alpha+\mu)\bigr]$$
Substituting $\alpha + \mu = 180° - \gamma$ (from $\alpha + \mu + \gamma = 180°$):
$$V_{d} = \dfrac{V_{d0}}{2}\bigl[\cos\alpha - \cos\gamma\bigr]$$
Using the commutation overlap relation $\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha+\mu) = \dfrac{2 X_{c} I_{d}}{\sqrt{2} V_{LL}}$, and noting $V_{d0} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL}$:
$$\cos\alpha - \cos\gamma = \dfrac{\pi X_{c} I_{d}}{3 V_{LL}}$$
Substituting back:
$$V_{d} = \dfrac{V_{d0}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\pi X_{c} I_{d}}{3 V_{LL}} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\pi X_{c} I_{d}}{3 V_{LL}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2} X_{c} I_{d}}{2}$$
But more comprehensively, including the $V_{d0}\cos\gamma$ term:
$$\boxed{\,V_{d} = V_{d0}\cos\gamma - \dfrac{3 X_{c}}{\pi} I_{d}\,}$$
This equation, with γ fixed at γ_min, is the V-I characteristic of the CEA-controlled inverter — a straight line of slope $-\dfrac{3 X_{c}}{\pi}$ and intercept $V_{d0}\cos\gamma_{min}$.

#### (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** $V_{LL} = 220$ kV, $I_d = 2000$ A, $X_c = 18$ Ω, $\gamma_{min} = 18°$, 6-pulse bridge.

**(i) No-load DC voltage $V_{d0}$ (2 marks):**
$$V_{d0} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL} = \dfrac{3 \times 1.4142}{3.1416} \times 220{,}000$$
$$V_{d0} = 1.350 \times 220{,}000 = 297{,}025 \text{ V} \approx 297.03 \text{ kV}$$

**(ii) Overlap angle μ (3 marks):**
Using the CEA commutation relation:
$$\cos\gamma - \cos(\gamma+\mu) = \dfrac{2 I_{d} X_{c}}{\sqrt{2} V_{LL}}$$
Compute RHS:
$$\text{RHS} = \dfrac{2 \times 2000 \times 18}{\sqrt{2} \times 220{,}000} = \dfrac{72{,}000}{311{,}127} = 0.2314$$
Then:
$$\cos(\gamma+\mu) = \cos 18° - 0.2314 = 0.9511 - 0.2314 = 0.7197$$
$$\gamma + \mu = \cos^{-1}(0.7197) = 44.03°$$
$$\mu = 44.03° - 18° = 26.03°$$

**(iii) Margin angle δ (2 marks):**
$$\delta = \gamma - \mu = 18° - 26.03° = -8.03°$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Result is unsafe — commutation failure will occur.** δ < 0 means the outgoing valve has *not* yet regained forward blocking capability when forward voltage reappears. **Conclusion: Either reduce $I_d$ (current limit) or increase γ_min.** To make δ = +5° (safe), we need μ = 13°, requiring a lower current. KTU expects students to **explicitly state this conclusion** for full marks.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model]`

**(a)** Compare **Constant Current (CC) control** and **Constant Extinction Angle (CEA) control** for HVDC converters. Discuss how these two control strategies are coordinated at the two ends of a bipole HVDC link to define the steady-state operating point. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An HVDC inverter is operating with $\alpha = 140°$, $\mu = 22°$, and DC current $I_d = 1500$ A. The per-phase commutation reactance is $X_c = 12$ Ω and the AC line voltage is 200 kV. Determine: **(i)** the extinction angle γ, **(ii)** the margin angle δ, **(iii)** the DC voltage $V_d$, and **(iv)** the DC power $P_d$ transmitted. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

#### (a) — Comparison and Coordination (7 marks)

| Aspect | CC Control (Rectifier) | CEA Control (Inverter) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Controlled variable** | DC current $I_d$ | Extinction angle γ |
| **Adjusts** | Firing angle α | Firing angle α |
| **Region** | $\alpha_{min} \le \alpha \le \alpha_{max}$ | γ held at γ_min |
| **Slope of V-I curve** | Vertical (constant I) | Negative (voltage drops with I) |
| **Primary purpose** | Limit fault current | Prevent commutation failure |
| **Risk if uncontrolled** | Overcurrent | Commutation failure |

**Coordination (4 marks):** In a 2-terminal HVDC link, the **rectifier** is set to **CC mode** (current order from dispatch centre) and the **inverter** is set to **CEA mode** (γ = γ_min). The two V-I curves intersect at the **steady-state operating point**, but they do NOT intersect directly — the inverter's CEA line sits at a **Current Margin** of about 10–15% of rated current *below* the rectifier CC line. Thus the actual operating point lies on:
- The **rectifier CC line** (for normal voltage variations), and
- The **inverter CEA locus** (when the rectifier voltage drops, the inverter takes over current control, switching to CC mode at α_min).

This **current margin** ensures that the current is always set by the rectifier, while the inverter is responsible for **defining the DC voltage level** of the link.

#### (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** $\alpha = 140°$, $\mu = 22°$, $I_d = 1500$ A, $X_c = 12$ Ω, $V_{LL} = 200$ kV.

**(i) Extinction angle γ (2 marks):**
$$\gamma = 180° - \alpha - \mu = 180° - 140° - 22° = 18°$$

**(ii) Margin angle δ (1 mark):**
$$\delta = \gamma - \mu = 18° - 22° = -4°$$

> [!WARNING]
> δ < 0 — operation is **UNSAFE**; commutation failure risk. KTU students must state this explicitly.

**(iii) DC voltage $V_d$ (2 marks):**
$$V_{d0} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \times 200{,}000 = 1.350 \times 200{,}000 = 270{,}050 \text{ V}$$
$$V_{d} = V_{d0}\cos\gamma - \dfrac{3 X_{c}}{\pi} I_{d}$$
$$V_{d} = 270{,}050 \times \cos 18° - \dfrac{3 \times 12}{3.1416} \times 1500$$
$$V_{d} = 270{,}050 \times 0.9511 - 11.459 \times 1500$$
$$V_{d} = 256{,}830 - 17{,}188 = 239{,}642 \text{ V} \approx 239.64 \text{ kV}$$

**(iv) DC power $P_d$ (2 marks):**
$$P_{d} = V_{d} \times I_{d} = 239{,}642 \times 1500 = 359{,}463{,}000 \text{ W} \approx 359.46 \text{ MW}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Most common error (–2 marks):** Writing $V_d = V_{d0}\cos\alpha$ for an inverter. The correct expression uses **γ**, not α, when the inverter is in CEA mode. (Rectifier uses α; inverter in CEA uses γ.)
> 2. **Second most common error (–2 marks):** Forgetting to check the **margin angle δ = γ − μ**. A negative δ means commutation failure — students routinely compute μ but forget to verify safety.
> 3. **Third common error (–1 mark):** Using $V_{LL}$ (line-to-line) directly in the per-phase overlap formula. The correct form is $\dfrac{2 I_d X_c}{\sqrt{2} V_{LL}}$ — note the $V_{LL}$ is the *inverter bus* line-to-line RMS voltage.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency (–1 mark):** Mixing kV and V within the same calculation. Standardize: convert $V_{LL}$ to V before computing $V_{d0}$, then express final $V_d$ in kV.
> 5. **Sketch missing in Part (a) of ESE (–2 marks):** A V-I characteristic diagram is **compulsory** for 7-mark questions on CEA control.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Inverter Extinction Angle Control**

- ⚡ **Core identity:** $\alpha + \mu + \gamma = 180°$ → memorize this on Day 1.
- ⚡ **CEA objective:** Hold γ ≥ γ_min (≈ 15°–18°) at all times to prevent commutation failure.
- ⚡ **DC voltage equation (CEA form):** $V_d = V_{d0}\cos\gamma - \dfrac{3 X_c}{\pi} I_d$.
- ⚡ **Overlap relation (CEA form):** $\cos\gamma - \cos(\gamma+\mu) = \dfrac{2 I_d X_c}{\sqrt{2} V_{LL}}$.
- ⚡ **Margin angle:** $\delta = \gamma - \mu$ — must be **> 0** (typically ≥ 5°–10°) for safe operation.
- ⚡ **Firing advance angle:** $\beta = 180° - \alpha$; **ignition advance:** $u = \beta - \mu$.
- ⚡ **Coordination:** Rectifier runs in **CC mode**, Inverter runs in **CEA mode**; current margin (10–15%) separates them.
- ⚡ **Control loop:** γ-measured → compared with γ_ref → error drives GPG → α adjusted.
- ⚡ **Failure mode:** AC-side voltage dip → μ increases → γ decreases → if γ < γ_min, commutation failure.
- ⚡ **6-pulse bridge:** $V_{d0} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL} \approx 1.35\, V_{LL}$.
- ⚡ **State transitions in inverter:** CEA (normal) ⇄ CC (after rectifier voltage dip) ⇄ α-min (low current).
- ⚡ **Modern refinement:** EPWC (Equal Pulse Width Control) replaces individual phase control in new HVDC systems to eliminate **negative-sequence coupling** during inverter operation.
- ⚡ **Exam tip:** Always draw the **V-I characteristic** showing both CC line and CEA locus whenever a CEA question appears in ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hierarchy of Controls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET862 - HVDC AND FACTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hierarchy of Controls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy of Controls in HVDC**: The *Hierarchy of Controls* in an HVDC transmission system is a **multi-level, decentralized control architecture** in which the operation of the complete HVDC link is governed by a stack of controllers operating at progressively faster time scales. The hierarchy typically consists of **four nested levels** — (i) Master/System Controller, (ii) Pole/Bipole Controller, (iii) Converter/Valve-Group Controller, and (iv) Valve (Firing-Pulse) Controller — each layer issuing references to the layer below it and receiving feedback status signals from below.

The HVDC link, although electrically a single DC circuit, is controlled as if it were a stack of nested control loops, similar to a **military command chain**, where a supreme commander gives strategic orders, field commanders translate them into tactical moves, and soldiers physically execute them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The 2024 KTU scheme explicitly lists "Hierarchy of Controls" as a key sub-topic under "HVDC System — General Background and Principles." Examiners frequently ask 3-mark questions asking students to *list* and *briefly describe* the levels.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Power Grid as a Modern Airplane Cockpit"

Imagine flying a commercial **Boeing 737** as a passenger airline pilot:

- **Captain (Autopilot Master Computer)** — sets the *destination, altitude, and cruise speed* (strategic). This is your **Master Controller**, operating in **seconds**.
- **First Officer (Flight Management System)** — translates "go to 35,000 ft" into elevator, throttle, and rudder commands. This is the **Pole Controller**, operating in **tens of milliseconds**.
- **Flight Control Computer (Surface Actuators)** — actually moves the flaps and elevators. This is the **Valve-Group / Firing Controller**, operating in **less than 1 millisecond**.

Similarly, in HVDC, the *top* decides how much power to send; the *bottom* decides exactly which thyristor to fire and when. The hierarchy ensures **stability, redundancy, and rapid response** to disturbances (like commutation failure or DC line faults).

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Power handling of a bipole | P | 1000 – 12000 **MW** |
| DC line voltage | V_d | ± 500 kV to ± 800 **kV** |
| Rated DC current | I_d | 1000 – 4500 **A** |
| AC system frequency | f | 50 / 60 **Hz** |
| Master control response time | T_master | 1 – 100 **s** |
| Pole control response time | T_pole | 1 – 100 **ms** |
| Valve (firing) control time | T_valve | 1 – 10 **ms** (one sub-cycle) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **inverse relationship between control level and response speed** is a defining property of HVDC control hierarchy. The slower the level, the *more strategic* and *more supervisory* it is.

---

### 1.4 Necessity of a Hierarchical Structure

An HVDC link cannot be governed by a single monolithic controller because:

1. **Time-scale separation** — Power dispatch decisions change every few seconds, while individual thyristor firing must occur every 1.67 ms (for 50 Hz). One controller cannot service both.
2. **Geographic dispersion** — The two converter stations may be 1000+ km apart; distributed intelligence is mandatory.
3. **Redundancy & security** — Failure of one level must not collapse the entire system; lower levels can keep the link running in degraded mode.
4. **Modularity** — New converter stations (e.g., multi-terminal HVDC) can be added with minimal redesign.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Levels of the HVDC Control Hierarchy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU examiner expects the student to list, in **descending order from slowest to fastest**, the four levels and identify *one* key function of each.

---

#### **Level 1 — Master / System Controller (SCADA Level)**

- **Function:** Sets the **active power order (P_order)** and **DC voltage reference (V_d_ref)** for the complete HVDC link, based on the load dispatch centre's economic dispatch.
- **Inputs:** Power demand from the regional grid operator, frequency, and tie-line power flows.
- **Outputs:** Power reference to each Pole Controller.
- **Time scale:** **1 to 100 seconds.**
- **Location:** Control room of the AC system operator (remote).
- **Communication:** Telemetry channels, **SCADA**, **PLCC** (Power Line Carrier Communication), or **fibre-optic** links.

> **Why needed?** Optimizes *economic* dispatch; coordinates the HVDC link with neighbouring AC grids.

---

#### **Level 2 — Pole (Bipole) Controller**

- **Function:** Translates the master power order into **DC current orders (I_d_order)** for the rectifier and into **DC voltage / extinction angle orders (γ_order / V_d_order)** for the inverter. Implements the **current–margin (γ-margin) control strategy**.
- **Inputs:** P_order from master, measured V_d, I_d, AC bus voltage, commutation voltage.
- **Outputs:** α-order (firing-angle order) to the converter controller; γ-order to the inverter controller.
- **Time scale:** **1 to 100 milliseconds.**
- **Key role:** Maintains the **current margin** (ΔI = 0.1 p.u.) between rectifier and inverter characteristics to prevent simultaneous operation at the same mode.

> **Why needed?** Ensures *steady-state* operating point lies on the **rectifier constant-current** mode and the **inverter constant-extinction-angle (CEA)** mode, with a defined margin.

---

#### **Level 3 — Converter (Valve-Group) Controller**

- **Function:** Converts the α-order from the pole controller into a **valve-firing reference angle** synchronized to the AC commutation voltage. Performs **equidistant pulse firing control (EPFC)**.
- **Inputs:** α-order, AC bus voltage zero-crossings, valve voltages.
- **Outputs:** Firing pulses to the thyristor valves.
- **Time scale:** **3.33 ms (one-sixth of a 50 Hz cycle) per valve**.
- **Key block:** The **Voltage-Dependent Current Order Limiter (VDCOL)** often sits at this level to protect valves during AC faults.

> **Why needed?** Decouples the *electrical* α-order from the *physical* pulse timing; provides **firing symmetry** to eliminate non-characteristic harmonics.

---

#### **Level 4 — Valve (Individual Thyristor) Controller / Firing-Pulse Generator**

- **Function:** Generates the **gate-firing pulse** to the individual thyristor level, with the necessary pulse amplitude, width, and **re-ignition / de-ionization protection** logic.
- **Inputs:** Firing command from Level 3, thyristor status (BOD – Breakover Diode protection), valve voltage.
- **Outputs:** **Gate pulse to the thyristor** through the **Thyristor Firing Electronics (TFE)** card via the **Valve Base Electronics (VBE)**.
- **Time scale:** **< 1 ms** (pulse rise time of order microseconds).
- **Protection features:** BOD firing, forward-voltage-breakover trigger, and **re-firing prevention** during the de-ionization interval.

> **Why needed?** The final physical link to the power semiconductor. Failure here is the most localized and least catastrophic.

---

### 2.2 Information Flow Diagram (Vertical Command Chain)

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ LEVEL 1: MASTER CONTROLLER (SCADA / Load Dispatch)       │
│   Inputs : P_demand, f_grid, tie-line power              │
│   Output : P_order, V_d_ref                              │
└────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┘
                         │  [seconds]
┌────────────────────────▼─────────────────────────────────┐
│ LEVEL 2: POLE CONTROLLER                                 │
│   Inputs : P_order, V_d, I_d, V_ac                       │
│   Output : I_d_order, α_order, γ_order                   │
│   [Current-Margin Control, VDCOL]                        │
└────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┘
                         │  [10–100 ms]
┌────────────────────────▼─────────────────────────────────┐
│ LEVEL 3: CONVERTER (VALVE-GROUP) CONTROLLER              │
│   Inputs : α_order, AC bus voltage zero-crossings         │
│   Output : Firing pulse train (equidistant)              │
│   [EPFC, Phase-Locked Oscillator]                        │
└────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┘
                         │  [1–3 ms]
┌────────────────────────▼─────────────────────────────────┐
│ LEVEL 4: VALVE / THYRISTOR CONTROLLER (TFE)              │
│   Inputs : Pulse from Level 3, valve voltage, BOD        │
│   Output : Gate pulse to individual thyristor            │
│   [VBE – Valve Base Electronics]                         │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **most-asked** formulas in KTU End-Semester Examinations for Module 1.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Used At Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{d0} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi}\,V_{LL}$ | No-load ideal DC voltage (per bridge) | Level 2 / 3 |
| 2 | $V_{d,\text{rect}} = V_{d0}\cos\alpha - \dfrac{3 X_c}{\pi} I_d$ | Rectifier DC voltage (overlap included) | Level 2 |
| 3 | $V_{d,\text{inv}} = V_{d0}\cos\gamma - \dfrac{3 X_c}{\pi} I_d$ | Inverter DC voltage (with extinction angle γ) | Level 2 |
| 4 | $V_{d,\text{inv}} = V_{d0}\cos\beta + \dfrac{3 X_c}{\pi} I_d$ | Alternative inverter formula in terms of β = π − α | Level 2 |
| 5 | $P_d = V_d \cdot I_d$ | DC power transmitted | Level 1 |
| 6 | $I_{d,\text{margin}} = I_{d,\text{rect}} - I_{d,\text{inv}} \approx 0.1\ \text{p.u.}$ | Current margin between rectifier and inverter | Level 2 |
| 7 | $\mu = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\cos\alpha - \dfrac{\sqrt{2}\,X_c I_d}{V_{LL}}\right) - \alpha$ | Commutation overlap angle | Level 3 |
| 8 | $\gamma = \pi - (\alpha + \mu)$ at inverter | Extinction angle relationship | Level 2 / 3 |
| 9 | $f_{\text{step}} = 6 f_s$ (for 6-pulse), 12 f_s (12-pulse) | Firing pulse repetition rate | Level 3 |
| 10 | $\Delta t_{\text{firing}} = \dfrac{T_s}{6}$ | Time between consecutive valve firings (50 Hz ⇒ 3.33 ms) | Level 4 |

> **Notation used:** $V_{LL}$ = line-to-line AC RMS voltage, $X_c$ = commutation reactance per phase, $f_s$ = AC system frequency, $\alpha$ = firing delay angle, $\beta$ = firing advance angle, $\gamma$ = extinction angle, $\mu$ = overlap angle.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | How Hierarchy Helps |
|---|---|
| **Long-distance bulk power transmission** (e.g., Rihand–Delhi ±500 kV, 814 km) | Master controller balances thermal/hydro generation mix; pole controller stabilizes the DC link during AC disturbances. |
| **Asynchronous grid interconnection** (e.g., Chandrapur–Padghe back-to-back) | Each level handles independent AC networks with different frequencies and phase angles. |
| **Offshore wind HVDC** (e.g., North Sea links) | Level 2 implements variable-speed power order; Level 3 mitigates sub-synchronous oscillations. |
| **Multi-terminal HVDC (MTDC)** | The hierarchy allows plug-and-play addition of converter stations. |
| **FACTS controllers (STATCOM/SVC)** | Same hierarchical philosophy — system → functional → internal → gate — borrowed from HVDC. |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State DC Voltage at Rectifier and Inverter (Level 2 Reference)

**Assumption:** 6-pulse Graetz bridge, constant DC current $I_d$, no AC system unbalance.

**Step 1: Average DC Voltage of an Ideal 6-Pulse Bridge (No Overlap, $\mu = 0$)**

The instantaneous DC output of a 6-pulse bridge fed by a sinusoidal AC source of RMS line voltage $V_{LL}$ is:
$$v_d(t) = \sqrt{2}\,V_{LL}\,\sin(\omega t) \quad \text{(per commutation interval of 60°)}$$

The average over one full cycle (6 such intervals of duration $\pi/3$ each) is:

$$V_{d0} = \frac{3}{\pi}\int_{-\pi/6}^{+\pi/6}\sqrt{2}\,V_{LL}\,\cos\theta\,d\theta$$

Expanding the integral:

$$V_{d0} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}\,V_{LL}}{\pi}\left[\sin\theta\right]_{-\pi/6}^{+\pi/6}$$

Evaluating the sine at the limits:

$$V_{d0} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}\,V_{LL}}{\pi}\!\left[\sin\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{6}\right) - \sin\!\left(-\tfrac{\pi}{6}\right)\right]$$

Since $\sin(\pi/6) = 1/2$ and $\sin(-\pi/6) = -1/2$:

$$V_{d0} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}\,V_{LL}}{\pi}\!\left[\tfrac{1}{2} - \left(-\tfrac{1}{2}\right)\right] = \frac{3\sqrt{2}\,V_{LL}}{\pi}\cdot 1$$

> $$\boxed{\,V_{d0} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi}\,V_{LL} \approx 1.35\,V_{LL}\,}$$

This is the **no-load DC voltage of one 6-pulse bridge**. For a 12-pulse system (two bridges in series), the value is doubled: $V_{d0}^{12} = 2V_{d0}^{6}$.

---

**Step 2: DC Voltage with Firing Angle $\alpha$ (Rectifier Mode)**

When the firing is delayed by angle $\alpha$ from the natural commutation point, the integration limits shift from $-\pi/6$ to $(\alpha - \pi/6)$:

$$V_d = \frac{3\sqrt{2}\,V_{LL}}{\pi}\!\int_{\alpha-\pi/6}^{\alpha+\pi/6}\!\cos\theta\,d\theta$$

$$V_d = \frac{3\sqrt{2}\,V_{LL}}{\pi}\!\left[\sin\!\left(\alpha+\tfrac{\pi}{6}\right) - \sin\!\left(\alpha-\tfrac{\pi}{6}\right)\right]$$

Applying the identity $\sin(A+B) - \sin(A-B) = 2\cos A \sin B$:

$$V_d = \frac{3\sqrt{2}\,V_{LL}}{\pi}\cdot 2\cos\alpha\cdot\sin\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{6}\right) = \frac{3\sqrt{2}\,V_{LL}}{\pi}\cdot 2\cos\alpha\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}$$

> $$\boxed{\,V_d = V_{d0}\cos\alpha \quad \text{(rectifier, no overlap)}\,}$$

---

**Step 3: Effect of Commutation Reactance (Overlap $\mu > 0$)**

During commutation, the DC current is being transferred from the outgoing valve to the incoming valve through the commutation reactance $X_c$. This causes a *voltage drop*:

$$\Delta V_d = \frac{3 X_c}{\pi}\,I_d$$

Subtracting this from the ideal expression:

> $$\boxed{\,V_{d,\text{rect}} = V_{d0}\cos\alpha - \frac{3 X_c}{\pi}\,I_d\,}$$

This is the equation used by the **Pole Controller (Level 2)** to compute the actual DC voltage from the ordered firing angle.

---

**Step 4: Inverter Equation in Terms of Extinction Angle $\gamma$**

For the inverter, the relevant delay is from the end of commutation to the next commutation's start, defined as the **extinction angle $\gamma$**. Substituting $\alpha = \pi - \gamma + \mu$ into the rectifier equation and simplifying yields:

$$V_{d,\text{inv}} = V_{d0}\cos\gamma - \frac{3 X_c}{\pi}\,I_d$$

> $$\boxed{\,V_{d,\text{inv}} = V_{d0}\cos\gamma - \frac{3 X_c}{\pi}\,I_d\,}$$

This is the **control equation of the inverter (Level 2)** when operating in **Constant Extinction Angle (CEA)** mode.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Current Margin and the Operating Point (Level 2 Logic)

The DC link is governed by two equations simultaneously:

$$V_{d,\text{rect}} = V_{d0}\cos\alpha - R_c I_d$$
$$V_{d,\text{inv}} = V_{d0}\cos\gamma - R_c I_d$$
where $R_c = 3X_c/\pi$.

The DC current is set by the difference of the two voltages divided by the DC line resistance $R_d$:

$$I_d = \frac{V_{d,\text{rect}} - V_{d,\text{inv}}}{R_d}$$

**Operating Strategy:**

1. The **rectifier** operates in **Constant Current (CC)** mode, controlling $I_d$ via $\alpha$.
2. The **inverter** operates in **Constant Extinction Angle (CEA)** mode, holding $\gamma$ = $\gamma_{\min}$ (typically 15°–18°).
3. A **current margin** $\Delta I = I_{d,\text{rect}} - I_{d,\text{inv}} = 0.1$ p.u. is maintained so that if the rectifier voltage drops, the inverter takes over in CC mode.

**Step-by-step logical proof:**

* At steady state, $\alpha$ on the rectifier is small (≈ 12°–18°) so the rectifier characteristic is nearly vertical (constant $I_d$).
* On the inverter side, $\gamma$ is fixed, so the characteristic is also nearly vertical but at a *lower current* by $\Delta I$.
* The intersection of these two vertical lines determines the **system operating point**.

The pole controller (Level 2) continuously monitors $I_d$ and adjusts $\alpha$ such that the actual current matches $I_{d,\text{order}} - \Delta I/2$ at the rectifier and $I_{d,\text{order}} + \Delta I/2$ at the inverter.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Firing-Pulse Timing (Level 3 Logic)

For a 6-pulse bridge, each thyristor conducts for 120°. The firing pulses are **60° apart** (one-sixth of a cycle). For a 50 Hz system:

$$T_s = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{50} = 20\,\text{ms}$$

The time between consecutive valve firings:

$$\Delta t_{\text{firing}} = \frac{T_s}{6} = \frac{20\,\text{ms}}{6} \approx 3.33\,\text{ms}$$

> $$\boxed{\,\Delta t_{\text{firing}} = \frac{1}{6 f_s} = 3.33\ \text{ms for 50 Hz,}\ \ 2.78\ \text{ms for 60 Hz}\,}$$

The Level 3 controller uses a **Phase-Locked Oscillator (PLO)** synchronized to the AC bus voltage to generate these equidistant firing instants. Any jitter or asymmetry would inject non-characteristic harmonics, which is why *equidistant pulse firing control (EPFC)* is preferred over the older *individual phase control (IPC)*.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Frequency Response of Each Level

| Level | Bandwidth | Typical Time Constant | Dominant Physical Phenomenon |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 — Master | 0.01 – 1 Hz | 1 – 100 s | Economic dispatch, AGC, load-frequency control |
| 2 — Pole | 1 – 100 Hz | 10 – 100 ms | DC current dynamics, mode switching |
| 3 — Converter | 100 – 1000 Hz | 1 – 10 ms | Valve firing synchronization, EPFC |
| 4 — Valve | > 1 kHz | < 1 ms | Gate pulse rise, plasma spread, snubber response |

> **Key Insight:** Each level is roughly **two decades faster** than the one above it. This clean separation allows **independent tuning** of each loop without interference — a hallmark of *cascaded control system* design.

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete HVDC Control Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["LEVEL 1 MASTER CONTROLLER SCADA LOAD DISPATCH"]
    L2["LEVEL 2 POLE CONTROLLER CURRENT MARGIN VDCOL"]
    L3["LEVEL 3 CONVERTER CONTROLLER EPFC PLO"]
    L4["LEVEL 4 VALVE CONTROLLER TFE VBE"]

    L1 -- Porder Vdref seconds --> L2
    L2 -- Iorder alphaorder gammaorder ms --> L3
    L3 -- Firing pulses microseconds --> L4
    L4 -- Gate pulse to thyristor --> THY["THYRISTOR VALVE BRIDGE"]

    L2 -. Iorder measured Vd .-> L2
    L3 -. Alpha feedback .-> L3
    L4 -. BOD status .-> L4

    THY -. AC bus commutation voltage .-> L3
    THY -. DC voltage Vd .-> L2
    THY -. DC current Id .-> L2
    THY -. Active power Pd .-> L1
```

sequenceDiagram

    O->>M: New Porder (e.g. +200 MW)
    Note over M: Time scale: seconds
    M->>P: Porder, Vdref

    P->>P: Compute new Iorder = Porder / Vd
    P->>C: alphaorder (firing angle)
    Note over C: Time scale: sub-cycle

    C->>V: Firing pulse (equidistant)

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Mode Switching in Pole Controller

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StartUp
    StartUp --> RectifierCC: alpha small
    RectifierCC --> InverterCEA: current margin established
    InverterCEA --> RectifierCC: Vdrect drops
    RectifierCC --> InverterCC: AC fault on rectifier side
    InverterCEA --> InverterCC: Vdref violated
    InverterCC --> RectifierCC: fault cleared
    InverterCC --> VDCOL_Limit: Vd < 0.3 pu
    VDCOL_Limit --> RectifierCC: Vd recovered
    InverterCEA --> Shutdown: emergency
    Shutdown --> [*]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Series A]`
> **List the four levels of the HVDC control hierarchy in descending order and state the time scale of operation of each level.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four levels of HVDC control hierarchy from slowest to fastest are:

1. **Master / System Controller** — operates on a time scale of **1 to 100 seconds**; sets active power order based on load dispatch.
2. **Pole / Bipole Controller** — operates on a time scale of **1 to 100 milliseconds**; generates current, alpha, and gamma orders using current-margin logic.
3. **Converter / Valve-Group Controller** — operates on a time scale of **1 to 10 milliseconds**; implements equidistant pulse firing and PLL synchronisation.
4. **Valve / Individual Thyristor Controller** — operates on a time scale of **< 1 millisecond**; produces the actual gate pulse to the thyristor via the VBE.

*(Examiner's note: Students who write "seconds, ms, μs" without naming the levels lose 1 mark.)*

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Series B]`
> **What is the role of the Master Controller in an HVDC link?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Master Controller, also called the **System Controller** or **SCADA-level controller**, performs the following functions:

1. Receives the **active power demand** from the regional load dispatch centre.
2. Computes the **optimal power order (P_order)** for each pole of the HVDC link based on **economic dispatch, frequency bias, and tie-line loading**.
3. Issues the **P_order and DC voltage reference (V_d_ref)** to the Pole Controllers of both converter stations.
4. Operates at the **slowest time scale (seconds)**, since economic decisions are updated only every few dispatch intervals.
5. Provides **supervisory control, monitoring, and alarm** functions.

---

### **PART B — Full-Question Solutions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)**

---

#### ✅ **QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)**

> **Q3(A).** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]`
> **(a)** Draw the block diagram of the HVDC control hierarchy and explain the function of each level. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Derive the steady-state DC voltage equation of a 6-pulse converter bridge (a) without overlap and (b) with commutation overlap, stating clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

The control hierarchy consists of four nested levels:

1. **Master Controller (Level 1):** Generates the **active power order (P_order)** for the entire HVDC link from the load dispatch centre. Time scale: **seconds**.

2. **Pole Controller (Level 2):** Translates P_order into **DC current order (I_d_order)**, **firing-angle order (α_order)** for the rectifier, and **extinction-angle order (γ_order)** for the inverter. Implements **current-margin control** and the **Voltage-Dependent Current Order Limiter (VDCOL)**. Time scale: **10–100 ms**.

3. **Converter Controller (Level 3):** Converts α-order into **equidistant firing pulses** synchronized to the AC bus voltage using a **Phase-Locked Oscillator (PLO)**. Time scale: **1–3 ms**.

4. **Valve Controller (Level 4):** Provides the **gate pulse** to the individual thyristor through the **Valve Base Electronics (VBE)**, with **BOD protection** and **re-firing prevention**. Time scale: **< 1 ms**.

The block diagram is the same as Section 4.1 above (draw a four-block cascaded diagram with feedback lines).

**Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

See the **full derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes (Derivation 1).

- **No overlap ($\mu = 0$):** $V_d = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL} \cos\alpha = V_{d0}\cos\alpha$  …(7.1)
- **With overlap ($\mu > 0$):** $V_d = V_{d0}\cos\alpha - \dfrac{3X_c}{\pi} I_d$  …(7.2)

**Assumptions:**
- AC supply is balanced, sinusoidal, and of constant magnitude.
- DC current $I_d$ is smooth and constant (large smoothing reactor).
- Thyristors are ideal switches.
- 6-pulse operation with 60° conduction.

---

#### ✅ **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Alternative)**

> **Q3(B).** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`
> **(a)** Explain the current-margin control strategy used in HVDC systems. Illustrate with a V_d – I_d characteristic. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A 6-pulse rectifier bridge is fed from a 220 kV (line), 50 Hz AC system. The commutation reactance is 8 Ω per phase. If the firing angle is 18° and overlap angle is 12°, compute (i) no-load DC voltage, (ii) DC voltage with overlap, (iii) DC current, and (iv) DC power, assuming the load resistance is 20 Ω. **(7 marks)**

**Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

The **current-margin method** maintains a small difference (≈ 0.1 p.u.) between the rectifier and inverter current settings, ensuring that:

- The **rectifier** operates in **Constant Current (CC)** mode by adjusting $\alpha$ between 5° and ~20°.
- The **inverter** operates in **Constant Extinction Angle (CEA)** mode by holding $\gamma$ = $\gamma_{\min}$ (15°–18°).
- If the rectifier's AC voltage sags, the **inverter automatically switches to CC mode** and vice versa, preventing collapse of the DC link.

**V_d – I_d characteristic:**

```
   V_d ↑
        |        Rectifier (CC mode)
        |        |
   V_d0 |────────●
        |        |  ← α decreases to maintain I_d
        |        |
        |        ●  Inverter (CEA mode, V_d = V_d0 cosγ)
        |         \
        |          \  ← β increases if I_d tries to rise
   ─────┼───────────●───────────●────→ I_d
   I_d_inv          I_d_margin  I_d_rect
                   (ΔI ≈ 0.1 pu)
```

**Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

Given: $V_{LL} = 220\ \text{kV}$, $f_s = 50\ \text{Hz}$, $X_c = 8\ \Omega$, $\alpha = 18°$, $\mu = 12°$, $R_{\text{load}} = 20\ \Omega$.

**(i) No-load DC voltage per bridge:**

$$V_{d0} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi}\,V_{LL} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi}\times 220\,\text{kV}$$

$$V_{d0} = \frac{3 \times 1.4142 \times 220}{\pi}\,\text{kV} = \frac{933.07}{\pi}\,\text{kV} \approx 297.0\,\text{kV}$$

**(ii) DC voltage with overlap:**

$$V_d = V_{d0}\cos\alpha - \frac{3X_c}{\pi}\,I_d$$

We need $I_d$ first. Using the relation for a rectifier with both $\alpha$ and $\mu$:

$$V_d = \frac{3\sqrt{2}\,V_{LL}}{\pi}\left[\cos\alpha + \cos(\alpha+\mu)\right]\cdot\frac{1}{2}$$

Applying the identity for average with overlap:

$$V_d = V_{d0}\left[\cos\alpha + \cos(\alpha+\mu)\right]/2$$

(Using the standard form: $V_d = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}\,V_{LL}}{2\pi}\left[\cos\alpha + \cos(\alpha+\mu)\right]$)

Compute: $\alpha + \mu = 18° + 12° = 30°$.

$$\cos 18° = 0.9511,\quad \cos 30° = 0.8660$$

$$V_d = \frac{3\sqrt{2}\times 220\,\text{kV}}{2\pi}\,(0.9511 + 0.8660)$$

$$V_d = \frac{933.07\,\text{kV}}{2\pi}\times 1.8171 = 148.5\,\text{kV}\times 1.8171 \approx 269.8\,\text{kV}$$

**(iii) DC current:**

$$I_d = \frac{V_d}{R_{\text{load}}} = \frac{269.8\,\text{kV}}{20\,\Omega} = 13.49\,\text{kA} = 13\,490\,\text{A}$$

**(iv) DC power:**

$$P_d = V_d \cdot I_d = 269.8\,\text{kV} \times 13.49\,\text{kA} \approx 3.64\,\text{GW}$$

Or equivalently $P_d = I_d^2 R_{\text{load}} = (13.49)^2 \times 20 \approx 3.64\,\text{GW}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $V_{LL}$ properly:** If the question gives *line-to-neutral* voltage, divide by $\sqrt{3}$ first. Most mistakes here cost **2 marks**.
> 2. **Using $V_d = V_{d0}\cos\alpha$ even with $\mu > 0$:** This omits the overlap voltage drop. Examiner deducts **1 mark** in Part (b) calculations.
> 3. **Mixing up α, β, γ:** Remember: $\beta = \pi - \alpha$ and $\gamma = \pi - (\alpha + \mu)$. Drawing them as separate angles without a clear relationship is a common error.
