> 4. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the 12-pulse configuration:** For 12-pulse (two 6-pulse bridges in series), $V_{d0}^{12} = 2 V_{d0}^{6}$ and $I_d$ is the same. Examiner will ask a follow-up "what if it were 12-pulse?" in 14-mark questions.
> 5. **Confusing VDCOL with the master controller:** VDCOL is implemented at the **Pole Controller level (Level 2)**, not at Level 1. This is a frequently-tested distinction.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The HVDC control hierarchy has **four levels**: Master → Pole → Converter → Valve.
- ✅ The time scale **decreases by ~2 decades** at each successive level: 100 s → 100 ms → 1 ms → < 1 ms.
- ✅ **Master Controller** issues **P_order**; **Pole Controller** issues **I_order, α_order, γ_order**; **Converter Controller** issues **firing pulses**; **Valve Controller** issues **gate pulse**.
- ✅ **Current-margin control** maintains a 0.1 p.u. gap between rectifier and inverter current orders.
- ✅ **VDCOL** sits at Level 2 and reduces $I_{d,\text{order}}$ when $V_d$ falls below ~0.3 p.u.
- ✅ **EPFC** (Equidistant Pulse Firing Control) is the modern standard, replacing IPC, for harmonic reduction.
- ✅ **Key formula:** $V_{d0} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL} \approx 1.35\,V_{LL}$ per 6-pulse bridge.
- ✅ **Key formula:** $V_{d,\text{rect}} = V_{d0}\cos\alpha - \dfrac{3X_c}{\pi}I_d$
- ✅ **Key formula:** $V_{d,\text{inv}} = V_{d0}\cos\gamma - \dfrac{3X_c}{\pi}I_d$
- ✅ **Key timing:** $\Delta t_{\text{firing}} = \dfrac{1}{6f_s} = 3.33$ ms (50 Hz) or 2.78 ms (60 Hz).
- ✅ **Feedback** is always upward; **commands** are always downward. The hierarchy is **strictly vertical** in information flow.
- ✅ For a **12-pulse** system, double the $V_{d0}$; characteristic harmonics are $12k \pm 1$ (11th, 13th, 23rd, 25th, …).
- ✅ The **PLO** (Phase-Locked Oscillator) at Level 3 is the heart of modern firing control — its bandwidth must be wider than the AC system frequency deviation (~±0.5 Hz).

> 🎯 **Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain the hierarchy," always pair each level with **(a) its function, (b) its time scale, and (c) its key input/output signal**. A 7-mark answer that includes all three for all four levels will always score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Flow in Power Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET862 - HVDC AND FACTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Power Flow in Power Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Power Flow Analysis** (also termed Load Flow Analysis) is the steady-state computational procedure used to determine the real power (P), reactive power (Q), voltage magnitude (|V|), and voltage phase angle (δ) at every bus in an interconnected power network under balanced three-phase sinusoidal operating conditions. In the context of **HVDC and FACTS**, power flow establishes the fundamental relationship between sending-end and receiving-end quantities, forming the mathematical foundation upon which reactive power compensation, series compensation, and phase-shifting controllers are designed to enhance the **Steady-State Power Transfer Capability** (P_max) and **Transient Stability Margin** of transmission corridors.

The classical formulation relies on solving the **AC Power Flow Equations**:

$$P_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \vert Y_{ij} \vert \cos(\theta_{ij} - \delta_i + \delta_j)$$

$$Q_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \vert Y_{ij} \vert \sin(\theta_{ij} - \delta_i + \delta_j)$$

where the standard bus classification in KTU board problems is:
- **Slack / Swing Bus** (V, δ specified)
- **PQ Bus / Load Bus** (P, Q specified)
- **PV Bus / Generator Bus** (P, |V| specified)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Within HVDC and FACTS, the focus is NOT on iterative numerical methods (Gauss-Seidel, Newton-Raphson), but rather on the **analytical two-bus power transfer model** that underpins compensation theory.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Pipeline Model"

Imagine two large water reservoirs connected by a single pipe. The water that flows from Reservoir A to Reservoir B depends on:
1. **The pressure difference** between the two reservoirs (analogous to the voltage phase angle δ).
2. **The diameter of the pipe** (analogous to the reciprocal of the transfer reactance 1/X).
3. **The height (pressure) at each reservoir** (analogous to |V₁| and |V₂|).

If the pipe is narrow (high X), very little water flows. To increase the flow, you can either:
- Increase the pressure difference (raise δ — limited by stability),
- Insert a **booster pump** midway (a **Series Compensator** like TCSC — reduces effective X), or
- Add a **secondary feed pipe** that injects pressurized water (a **Shunt Compensator** like SVC/STATCOM — supports |V|).

This is exactly the role of **FACTS controllers** in real power systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - Nominal frequency: **f = 50 Hz** (India), ω = **2πf = 314.159 rad/s**
> - Base apparent power in Indian grid studies: **S_base = 100 MVA** (common)
> - Per-unit (p.u.) system: preferred over absolute units for analysis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Bus System Model — The Heart of FACTS Theory

Every FACTS controller in KTU Module 2 is analyzed using a simplified **two-machine, two-bus equivalent** of the transmission system. Consider a generator at the sending end (Bus 1) supplying a load at the receiving end (Bus 2) through a transmission line of total series reactance **X** and resistance **R** (assumed negligible for high-voltage EHV lines, R << X).

The classical **real power transfer equation** is derived as:

$$P_{12} = \frac{\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert}{X} \sin(\delta_1 - \delta_2) = \frac{\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert}{X} \sin\delta$$

The corresponding **reactive power** at the receiving end:

$$Q_{12} = \frac{\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert}{X} \cos\delta - \frac{\vert V_2 \vert^{2}}{X}$$

### 2.2 The Five Pillars of Power Flow Manipulation in HVDC/FACTS

**Pillar 1 — Power Angle (δ) Control**
- Real power depends on sin(δ).
- Maximum transfer occurs at δ = 90°, P_max = |V₁||V₂|/X.
- In practice, δ is restricted to ~30°–45° for stability, limiting natural transfer.

**Pillar 2 — Voltage Magnitude (|V|) Control**
- Shunt FACTS devices (SVC, STATCOM) regulate |V| at intermediate or receiving buses.
- Boosting |V| increases P_max quadratically (proportional to |V₁||V₂|).

**Pillar 3 — Series Reactance (X) Control**
- Series FACTS devices (TCSC, SSSC, TCSR) insert a controllable voltage in series.
- Effective reactance becomes X_eff = X − X_c (for compensation).
- P_max increases by a factor of 1/(1 − k), where k = X_c/X.

**Pillar 4 — Phase Angle Shifting**
- Phase-shifting transformers and UPFC's series branch shift δ artificially.
- Allows power redirection in meshed networks.

**Pillar 5 — Active/Reactive Decoupling via HVDC**
- An HVDC link decouples the AC systems at its two terminals.
- DC power is controlled independently: P_dc = V_dc · I_dc.

### 2.3 The Compensation Compensation Factor (K-Factor)

When a series compensator of reactance **X_c** is inserted, the **degree of series compensation** is:

$$k_s = \frac{X_c}{X}$$

The effective transfer reactance becomes:

$$X_{eff} = X(1 - k_s)$$

The new maximum power transfer:

$$P_{max}^{comp} = \frac{\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert}{X(1 - k_s)} = \frac{P_{max}^{uncomp}}{1 - k_s}$$

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Quantity | Expression | Use Case | Typical Units |
|---|---------------------|------------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | Real power transfer | $P = \dfrac{\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert}{X}\sin\delta$ | Base power flow | p.u. or MW |
| 2 | Reactive power at receiving end | $Q_2 = \dfrac{\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert}{X}\cos\delta - \dfrac{\vert V_2 \vert^{2}}{X}$ | Voltage support | p.u. or MVAR |
| 3 | Maximum power transfer | $P_{max} = \dfrac{\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert}{X}$ | Stability limit | p.u. or MW |
| 4 | Power loss in line | $P_{loss} = \dfrac{P^{2} + Q^{2}}{\vert V \vert^{2}} R$ | Efficiency analysis | MW |
| 5 | Surge Impedance Loading (SIL) | $SIL = \dfrac{\vert V \vert^{2}}{Z_c}$ | Line natural loading | MW |
| 6 | Compensated reactance | $X_{eff} = X(1 - k_s)$ | Series compensation | Ω or p.u. |
| 7 | Compensated P_max | $P_{max}^{c} = \dfrac{\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert}{X(1 - k_s)}$ | Series FACTS | p.u. or MW |
| 8 | Power transfer gain | $\dfrac{P_{comp}}{P_{uncomp}} = \dfrac{1}{1 - k_s}$ | Compensation ratio | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Mid-point voltage (uncompensated) | $V_m = \vert V_1 \vert e^{-j\delta/2}$ | Voltage profile | p.u. |
| 10 | Mid-point voltage (shunt comp) | $V_m \approx \sqrt{\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert}$ | SVC/STATCOM application | p.u. |
| 11 | Reactive power balance at bus i | $\sum Q_{gen} - \sum Q_{load} - \sum Q_{loss} = 0$ | KVL/KCL constraint | MVAR |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Power flow analysis is the cornerstone for planning grid expansion, designing FACTS controllers, evaluating HVDC link placements, and performing security-constrained dispatch in modern Indian smart-grid operations managed by **POSOCO (Power System Operation Corporation)** and **CEA (Central Electricity Authority)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Real Power Transfer Equation

**Given:** A two-bus system where Bus 1 (sending end) has phasor voltage $\bar{V}_1 = \vert V_1 \vert \angle \delta_1$ and Bus 2 (receiving end) has phasor voltage $\bar{V}_2 = \vert V_2 \angle \delta_2$. They are interconnected through a purely inductive transmission line of series reactance $X$ (resistance R is neglected for EHV systems, as $R \ll X$).

**Step 1 — Write the line admittance.**

The line is modeled as a pure reactance, hence its impedance is $\bar{Z} = jX$. The corresponding admittance is:

$$\bar{Y} = \frac{1}{jX} = -j\frac{1}{X}$$

Thus the admittance magnitude and angle are:

$$\vert Y \vert = \frac{1}{X}, \quad \theta = -90^{\circ}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the general AC power flow equation from Bus 1 to Bus 2.**

For a two-bus system, the complex power flowing from Bus 1 to Bus 2 is:

$$\bar{S}_{12} = \bar{V}_1 \bar{I}_{12}^{*}$$

The line current from Bus 1 to Bus 2 is:

$$\bar{I}_{12} = (\bar{V}_1 - \bar{V}_2) \bar{Y} = (\bar{V}_1 - \bar{V}_2) \left(-j\frac{1}{X}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Express the current phasor in magnitude-angle form.**

Writing the voltages explicitly:

$$\bar{V}_1 = \vert V_1 \vert (\cos\delta_1 + j\sin\delta_1)$$

$$\bar{V}_2 = \vert V_2 \vert (\cos\delta_2 + j\sin\delta_2)$$

The voltage difference becomes:

$$\bar{V}_1 - \bar{V}_2 = (\vert V_1 \vert \cos\delta_1 - \vert V_2 \vert \cos\delta_2) + j(\vert V_1 \vert \sin\delta_1 - \vert V_2 \vert \sin\delta_2)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the complex conjugate of the current.**

$$\bar{I}_{12}^{*} = (\bar{V}_1^{*} - \bar{V}_2^{*}) \left(+j\frac{1}{X}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$\bar{S}_{12} = \bar{V}_1 \cdot (\bar{V}_1^{*} - \bar{V}_2^{*}) \cdot \frac{j}{X}$$

**Step 5 — Expand the product using Euler's identity.**

Multiplying through:

$$\bar{S}_{12} = \frac{j}{X}\left[\vert V_1 \vert^{2} - \bar{V}_1 \bar{V}_2^{*}\right]$$

Now substitute $\bar{V}_1 = \vert V_1 \vert e^{j\delta_1}$ and $\bar{V}_2^{*} = \vert V_2 \vert e^{-j\delta_2}$:

$$\bar{V}_1 \bar{V}_2^{*} = \vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert e^{j(\delta_1 - \delta_2)}$$

**Step 6 — Let δ = δ₁ − δ₂ and apply the exponential expansion.**

$$\bar{V}_1 \bar{V}_2^{*} = \vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert (\cos\delta + j\sin\delta)$$

Insert into the $\bar{S}_{12}$ expression:

$$\bar{S}_{12} = \frac{j}{X}\left[\vert V_1 \vert^{2} - \vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert \cos\delta - j\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert \sin\delta\right]$$

**Step 7 — Distribute the j/X factor across the bracket.**

$$\bar{S}_{12} = \frac{j\vert V_1 \vert^{2}}{X} - \frac{j\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert \cos\delta}{X} + \frac{\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert \sin\delta}{X}$$

**Step 8 — Separate the real part (P) and imaginary part (Q).**

The real part (active power) is:

$$P_{12} = \frac{\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert \sin\delta}{X}$$

The imaginary part (reactive power) is:

$$Q_{12} = \frac{\vert V_1 \vert^{2} - \vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert \cos\delta}{X}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **fundamental power-angle equation** used in every KTU HVDC/FACTS numerical. The maximum theoretical value $P_{max} = \vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert / X$ occurs at $\delta = 90^{\circ}$. However, in real systems the **steady-state stability limit** is operationally capped at $\delta_{max} \approx 30^{\circ}$ to $\delta_{max} \approx 45^{\circ}$ depending on system damping and protection settings.

### 3.2 Derivation of Reactive Power at the Receiving End

**Step 1 — Start from the current flowing into Bus 2.**

$$\bar{I}_{21} = (\bar{V}_2 - \bar{V}_1) \left(-j\frac{1}{X}\right) = \frac{j(\bar{V}_1 - \bar{V}_2)}{X}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the complex power absorbed at Bus 2.**

$$\bar{S}_{21} = \bar{V}_2 \bar{I}_{21}^{*} = \bar{V}_2 \cdot \frac{-j(\bar{V}_1^{*} - \bar{V}_2^{*})}{X}$$

**Step 3 — Expand and separate.**

$$\bar{S}_{21} = \frac{-j}{X}\left[\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert (\cos\delta - j\sin\delta) - \vert V_2 \vert^{2}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Take the real and imaginary components.**

$$P_{21} = \frac{\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert \sin\delta}{X} \quad \text{(identical to } P_{12} \text{ — lossless line)}$$

$$Q_{21} = \frac{\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert \cos\delta - \vert V_2 \vert^{2}}{X}$$

This sign convention indicates that **Q is being consumed** at the receiving end when the bus voltage is below the natural profile — motivating the use of **shunt reactors/compensators**.

### 3.3 Derivation of Compensated Power Transfer

**Step 1 — Insert a series compensator of reactance $X_c$ at the midpoint.**

The line now has a total reactance of $X - X_c = X(1 - k_s)$, where $k_s = X_c / X$ is the compensation level.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the P–δ equation.**

$$P_{12}^{comp} = \frac{\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert}{X(1 - k_s)} \sin\delta$$

**Step 3 — For a fixed load angle δ, the power increases by the compensation gain factor:**

$$\text{Gain} = \frac{P_{12}^{comp}}{P_{12}^{uncomp}} = \frac{1}{1 - k_s}$$

For $k_s = 0.5$ (50% compensation), the power transfer doubles at the same angle. This is the **primary justification** for series compensation in long EHV lines in India (e.g., the 400 kV Rourkela–Raipur line).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Two-Bus Equivalent System with Possible Compensation Points

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Gen["Generator / Sending End\n|V1| angle delta1\nSlack or PV Bus"] -->|Series X| MidNode
    MidNode{"Mid-Point\nV_m\n(FACTS Installation)"}
    MidNode -->|Series X_c| ShuntBus["Shunt Compensator\nSVC or STATCOM\nQ_shunt"]
    MidNode -->|Series X| Load["Load / Receiving End\n|V2| angle delta2\nPQ Bus"]
    ShuntBus -. Reactive Injection .-> MidNode
    StyleCheck["delta = delta1 - delta2\nP_max = |V1||V2|/X"]:::meta
    MidNode -.- StyleCheck
    classDef meta fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff8f00,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Power Flow Architecture within an Integrated HVDC–FACTS Network

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Generating Station\nBus 1, Slack] --> B[Step-Up Transformer]
    B --> C[EHV AC Transmission Line\nReactance X]
    C --> D{Intermediate Bus\nFACTS Installation Hub}
    D --> E[Series Compensator\nTCSC or SSSC]
    D --> F[Shunt Compensator\nSVC or STATCOM]
    D --> G[Unified Power Flow Controller\nUPFC combined]
    D --> H[Step-Down Transformer]
    H --> I[AC Load Centre\nBus 2, PQ]
    D -.->|Parallel Path| J[HVDC Converter Station 1\nRectifier]
    J -->|DC Line| K[HVDC Converter Station 2\nInverter]
    K --> I
    classDef facts fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hvdc fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class E,F,G facts
    class J,K hvdc
```

### 4.3 P-δ Power Angle Curve: Compensated vs Uncompensated

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Uncompensated["Uncompensated System - Lower Pmax"]
        UC["Pmax_uc = |V1||V2|/X"]
    end
    subgraph PartComp["Partially Compensated - ks = 0.3"]
        PC["Pmax_pc = Pmax_uc / 0.7"]
    end
    subgraph FullComp["Heavily Compensated - ks = 0.7"]
        FC["Pmax_fc = Pmax_uc / 0.3"]
    end
    Uncompensated --> PartComp --> FullComp
    note["As ks increases towards 1,\nPmax increases dramatically\nbut subsynchronous resonance risk\nand over-voltage protection\nbecome critical"]:::warn
    FullComp -.- note
    classDef warn fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Reactive Power Compensation Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([System Voltage Detected\nat Critical Bus]) --> Check{V_m less than V_ref?}
    Check -- Yes --> InjectQ[Inject Reactive Power\nvia STATCOM or SVC]
    Check -- No --> AbsorbQ[Absorb Reactive Power\nvia Shunt Reactor]
    InjectQ --> Update[Update V_m]
    AbsorbQ --> Update
    Update --> Check
    classDef action fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    class InjectQ,AbsorbQ action
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Surge Impedance Loading (SIL) of a transmission line and explain its significance in power flow studies.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** Surge Impedance Loading (SIL) is the power delivered by a transmission line when it is terminated by a load equal to its surge impedance $Z_c = \sqrt{L/C}$. The mathematical expression is $SIL = \vert V \vert^{2} / Z_c$, where V is the rated line-to-line voltage.
- **Physical Significance (1 Mark):** At SIL loading, the line consumes no net reactive power — i.e., $Q_{generated} = Q_{consumed}$ by the line's distributed inductance and capacitance.
- **Engineering Importance (1 Mark):** Lines loaded below SIL exhibit Ferranti effect (receiving-end voltage rises), requiring shunt reactors. Lines loaded above SIL absorb reactive power, requiring shunt capacitors or STATCOM support. SIL is therefore the reference benchmark for designing FACTS compensation.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State and explain the steady-state power-angle equation of a two-machine system.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Statement (1 Mark):** $P = \dfrac{\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert}{X} \sin\delta$, where $V_1$ and $V_2$ are the sending- and receiving-end voltage magnitudes, X is the total transfer reactance, and δ is the angle between them.
- **Explanation (1 Mark):** Real power transfer is directly proportional to the sine of the load angle and inversely proportional to the line reactance. Maximum transfer $P_{max} = \vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert / X$ occurs at δ = 90°.
- **Practical Constraint (1 Mark):** The steady-state stability limit restricts δ to about 30°–45°, far below the theoretical 90°, leaving substantial unutilized capacity that FACTS devices recover by reducing effective X or boosting voltages.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units or p.u. notation** in the final numerical answer — deducts up to **0.5 marks**.
> 2. **Sign error in Q equation** — the convention $Q = \dfrac{\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert \cos\delta - \vert V_2 \vert^{2}}{X}$ is frequently inverted by students. Marks lost: **1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing "compensation degree k_s" with "compensation percentage"** — k_s = 0.4 means 40%, not 0.4%. Loss: **0.5 marks**.
> 4. **Not drawing the equivalent circuit** before solving — mandatory for full marks in KTU valuation. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Using δ in degrees** in the equation instead of radians when intermediate calculus is required — loss: **0.5 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Derive the real and reactive power transfer equations for a two-bus lossless system connected through a pure reactance X. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Assumptions (1 Mark):**
- Three-phase balanced system.
- Transmission line represented by lumped pure reactance X (R = 0, G = 0, B_shunt negligible).
- Voltage magnitudes and angles remain constant during the transfer.

**Step 2 — Circuit Model (1 Mark):** A two-bus system with $\bar{V}_1 = \vert V_1 \vert \angle \delta_1$ and $\bar{V}_2 = \vert V_2 \vert \angle \delta_2$ connected via $jX$.

**Step 3 — Current Expression (1 Mark):**

$$\bar{I}_{12} = \frac{\bar{V}_1 - \bar{V}_2}{jX} = -j\frac{(\bar{V}_1 - \bar{V}_2)}{X}$$

**Step 4 — Complex Power at Sending End (2 Marks):**

$$\bar{S}_{12} = \bar{V}_1 \bar{I}_{12}^{*} = \frac{j}{X}\left[\vert V_1 \vert^{2} - \vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert e^{j\delta}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Separating Real and Imaginary Parts (2 Marks):**

$$P_{12} = \frac{\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert}{X} \sin\delta, \quad Q_{12} = \frac{\vert V_1 \vert^{2} - \vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert \cos\delta}{X}$$

**Final boxed result.**

**(b)** A 400 kV, 400 km transmission line has a series reactance of 0.4 p.u. and negligible resistance. The sending-end and receiving-end voltages are 1.05 p.u. and 1.00 p.u. respectively. If a 50% series compensation is applied, calculate: (i) the original maximum power transfer, (ii) the compensated maximum power transfer, and (iii) the real power transferred at a load angle of 30° before and after compensation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given Data (1 Mark):**
- $\vert V_1 \vert = 1.05$ p.u., $\vert V_2 \vert = 1.00$ p.u., $X = 0.4$ p.u.
- Compensation level: $k_s = 0.5$, so $X_{eff} = X(1 - k_s) = 0.4 \times 0.5 = 0.2$ p.u.
- Load angle: $\delta = 30^{\circ}$, $\sin 30^{\circ} = 0.5$.

**Step 2 — Original P_max (1 Mark):**

$$P_{max}^{uc} = \frac{1.05 \times 1.00}{0.4} = 2.625 \text{ p.u.}$$

**Step 3 — Compensated P_max (1 Mark):**

$$P_{max}^{c} = \frac{1.05 \times 1.00}{0.2} = 5.25 \text{ p.u.}$$

**Step 4 — Real Power at δ = 30° Uncompensated (1 Mark):**

$$P_{uc} = \frac{1.05 \times 1.00}{0.4} \times 0.5 = 1.3125 \text{ p.u.}$$

**Step 5 — Real Power at δ = 30° Compensated (1 Mark):**

$$P_{c} = \frac{1.05 \times 1.00}{0.2} \times 0.5 = 2.625 \text{ p.u.}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion and Interpretation (2 Marks):**
- Compensation **doubles** both P_max and the power at any given δ.
- Practical note: At 50% compensation, the line is approaching critical series resonance, so protection coordination and SSR (Sub-Synchronous Resonance) studies become essential.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** With the help of the two-bus equivalent circuit, derive the expression for real and reactive power flow when a midpoint shunt compensator (SVC/STATCOM) is installed. Discuss how this affects the steady-state stability limit. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Diagram of Compensated System (1 Mark):** Two-bus system with a shunt compensator injecting reactive current $I_{sh} = jB_{sh} V_m$ at the midpoint.

**Step 2 — Voltage at Midpoint (1 Mark):** Without compensation, the mid-point voltage lies on a circle of diameter $\overline{V_1 V_2}$. With a compensator holding the mid-point voltage at $V_m \approx \sqrt{\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert}$ (geometric mean), the voltage profile is flattened.

**Step 3 — Modified P-δ Equation (2 Marks):** The effective transfer reactance is halved (since the line is split into two equal X/2 sections), and the power transfer becomes:

$$P = \frac{2 \vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert}{X} \sin\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right) \cos\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right) = \frac{\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert}{X} \sin\delta$$

However, the *virtual* maximum transfer doubles in practice because each half-line now sees a higher effective driving voltage, and reactive injection prevents voltage collapse.

**Step 4 — Reactive Power Injected (1 Mark):**

$$Q_{sh} = \frac{4 \vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert}{X}\left[1 - \cos\delta\right] - \frac{2(\vert V_1 \vert^{2} + \vert V_2 \vert^{2})}{X}$$

**Step 5 — Stability Impact (2 Marks):** The shunt compensator holds $V_m$ constant, effectively doubling the steady-state stability margin. The critical clearing angle increases, enabling higher transmitted power without losing synchronism — the basis of **SVC-based stability enhancement** deployed across Indian grid substations.

**(b)** A 220 kV, 250 km line has $X = 0.32$ p.u. and is initially uncompensated. The sending-end voltage is 1.04 p.u. and receiving-end voltage is 0.96 p.u. A TCSC provides 40% series compensation. Compute: (i) the uncompensated and compensated P_max, (ii) the reactive power consumed at the receiving end at δ = 20° in both cases, and (iii) the percentage increase in P_max. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Data (1 Mark):** $\vert V_1 \vert = 1.04$, $\vert V_2 \vert = 0.96$, $X = 0.32$ p.u., $k_s = 0.4$, $\delta = 20^{\circ}$.

**Step 2 — Effective Reactance (0.5 Mark):** $X_{eff} = 0.32 \times (1 - 0.4) = 0.192$ p.u.

**Step 3 — P_max Comparison (1 Mark):**

$$P_{max}^{uc} = \frac{1.04 \times 0.96}{0.32} = 3.12 \text{ p.u.}$$

$$P_{max}^{c} = \frac{1.04 \times 0.96}{0.192} = 5.20 \text{ p.u.}$$

**Step 4 — Reactive Power at δ = 20° (1.5 Marks):**
- $\cos 20^{\circ} = 0.9397$.
- Uncompensated:

$$Q_{2,uc} = \frac{1.04 \times 0.96 \times 0.9397 - 0.96^{2}}{0.32} = \frac{0.9381 - 0.9216}{0.32} = 0.0516 \text{ p.u.}$$

- Compensated:

$$Q_{2,c} = \frac{1.04 \times 0.96 \times 0.9397 - 0.96^{2}}{0.192} = \frac{0.0165}{0.192} = 0.0860 \text{ p.u.}$$

**Step 5 — Percentage Increase in P_max (1 Mark):**

$$\%\text{ Increase} = \frac{5.20 - 3.12}{3.12} \times 100\% = 66.67\%$$

**Step 6 — Inference (2 Marks):** Series compensation not only increases P_max by ~67% but also raises reactive power consumption at the receiving end, suggesting a need for **coordinated shunt compensation** (hybrid SVC + TCSC) in long EHV corridors.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Power Flow in Power Systems**

- The **fundamental power-angle equation** is $P = \dfrac{\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert}{X}\sin\delta$. Memorize its derivation — it appears in nearly every KTU HVDC/FACTS problem.
- **P_max = |V₁||V₂|/X** is the absolute stability ceiling; the practical steady-state limit is at δ ≈ 30°–45°.
- **Series compensation** modifies X → X(1 − k_s); the **gain factor** in P_max is 1/(1 − k_s).
- **Shunt compensation** supports voltage magnitude; SVC and STATCOM are the primary devices in KTU syllabus.
- **HVDC links** decouple AC systems; DC power is set independently of AC δ, providing **asynchronous power transfer**.
- **Surge Impedance Loading (SIL) = |V|²/Z_c** is the natural loading benchmark — at SIL, Q_net = 0.
- **Ferranti effect** (voltage rise at no-load) appears below SIL and is countered by **shunt reactors**.
- For every reactive power problem, always write Q as $Q = \dfrac{\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert \cos\delta - \vert V_2 \vert^{2}}{X}$ (not the inverse).
- **Per-unit system** is mandatory in KTU numericals; never mix absolute SI units with p.u. in the same equation.
- The **two-bus equivalent** is the universal building block — every FACTS controller in Module 2 is analyzed using it.
- **Compensation level k_s above 0.7** introduces risks: subsynchronous resonance, over-voltage during load rejection, and MOV (Metal Oxide Varistor) stress — these are frequently asked as short notes in Part A.
- **FACTS classification:** Shunt (SVC, STATCOM), Series (TCSC, SSSC, TCSR), Combined (UPFC, IPFC).
- Always **draw the equivalent circuit** in the first step of any 14-mark numerical — this alone earns a 1-mark credit in KTU valuation.
- For HVDC: $P_{dc} = V_{dc} \cdot I_{dc}$, and the **rectifier operates at α (firing angle delay)**, the **inverter operates at γ (extinction angle)** or δ for voltage control.
- **Power Circle Diagram** (P–Q locus) for a two-bus system is a circle of radius $\vert V_1 \vert \vert V_2 \vert / X$ centered at $(0, \vert V_1 \vert^{2}/X)$ — used to identify operating limits before compensation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage Regulation and Reactive Power Flow Control in Power Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET862 - HVDC AND FACTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Voltage Regulation and Reactive Power Flow Control in Power Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚡ Voltage Regulation and Reactive Power Flow Control in Power Systems

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Voltage Regulation (VR)** is defined as the change in magnitude of the receiving-end voltage at a particular bus between no-load and full-load conditions, expressed as a percentage of the full-load (rated) voltage. Mathematically, it is the normalized voltage deviation that occurs across a transmission element or at a load bus due to internal impedance drops, load current variation, and reactive power imbalances.

$$VR\,\% = \frac{\vert V_{NL} - V_{FL} \vert}{V_{FL}} \times 100$$

**Reactive Power Flow Control** is the systematic regulation of the reactive component of power (measured in **Volt-Ampere Reactive, VAR** or **MVAR**) that flows through transmission networks. Unlike real power (P, measured in **Watts (W)** or **MW**), reactive power (Q) does no useful work but is essential for establishing and sustaining magnetic and electric fields in inductive loads, transmission lines, transformers, and rotating machines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Voltage regulation and reactive power flow control form the **foundational prerequisite** for understanding **FACTS (Flexible AC Transmission Systems)** devices such as SVC, STATCOM, TCSC, and UPFC, which are covered in the subsequent modules of PEEET862.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Reactive Power Cannot Be Transmitted Efficiently Over Long Distances:** Unlike real power (P) which is transmitted economically over hundreds of kilometers, reactive power (Q) suffers from $I^2X$ losses that scale with the square of the line length. This is why voltage regulation at the load center requires **local or nearby reactive compensation** rather than bulk transfer from remote generating stations.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Water Pressure in a Pipe System"

Imagine a city water distribution network:

- **Real Power (P)** is the *actual water flow* that reaches homes and performs useful work (drinking, washing, cooking). It is consumed and dissipated.
- **Reactive Power (Q)** is the *internal pressure* maintained inside the pipe walls. It does not flow out of the taps, but without adequate pressure, the water cannot be delivered against gravity or friction.
- **Voltage** is analogous to *water pressure* at the consumer end. If the pressure (voltage) drops too low, the faucets deliver only a trickle.
- **Voltage Regulation** is like a *pressure regulator valve* placed near the consumer, which automatically adjusts to maintain constant pressure regardless of how many taps are open (load variation).
- **FACTS Devices** (SVC, STATCOM) are the *electronic pressure regulators* — they inject or absorb reactive power locally to hold voltage steady without mechanical switching.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Just as a long thin pipe loses pressure over distance due to friction, a long transmission line loses voltage due to its series reactance. The "friction" in AC power is the inductive reactance **X = ωL = 2πfL** (measured in **Ohms (Ω)**), and the only way to maintain voltage at the far end is to **inject reactive power** along the route or at the receiving end.

---

## 1.3 The Fundamental Coupling: Voltage ↔ Reactive Power

The single most important relationship in this module is that **voltage magnitude and reactive power are tightly coupled** at any bus in the power system. This coupling is expressed by the **Q-V sensitivity equation**:

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial Q} \approx \frac{1}{\frac{\partial Q}{\partial V}}$$

This means:
- If you **inject reactive power** (+ΔQ) at a bus, the **voltage rises** (+ΔV).
- If you **absorb reactive power** (-ΔQ) at a bus, the **voltage falls** (-ΔV).

This bi-directional coupling is the operating principle behind every shunt-connected compensator (SVC, STATCOM, synchronous condenser).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:** Reactive power is NOT "wasted" power in the same sense as real power losses. It is a *necessary by-product* of supporting the AC magnetic field. The penalty for transmitting reactive power is the *additional I²R and I²X losses* it causes in the system, not the reactive power itself being "lost."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Voltage Drop Across a Transmission Line

Consider a short transmission line modelled as a series impedance $\mathbf{Z} = R + jX$ between sending end (S) and receiving end (R). Taking $\mathbf{V_R} = V_R \angle 0°$ as the reference phasor and assuming a lagging load with power factor angle $\phi$:

The current flowing through the line is:

$$\mathbf{I} = I \angle{-\phi}$$

The sending-end voltage is:

$$\mathbf{V_S} = \mathbf{V_R} + \mathbf{I} \cdot \mathbf{Z}$$

Expanding using rectangular coordinates:

$$\mathbf{V_S} = V_R \cos 0 + jV_R \sin 0 + I(R + jX)(\cos\phi - j\sin\phi)$$

Performing the multiplication $(R + jX)(\cos\phi - j\sin\phi)$:

$$\mathbf{V_S} = V_R + I(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi) + j\,I(X\cos\phi - R\sin\phi)$$

For a transmission line where $X \gg R$ (a valid approximation for EHV lines where $X/R \geq 10$), the **approximate magnitude of voltage drop** per phase simplifies to:

$$\Delta V \approx I \cdot X \cdot \sin\phi \quad \text{(dominant reactive term)}$$

The component $I \cdot R \cdot \cos\phi$ contributes to real power losses, while $I \cdot X \cdot \sin\phi$ governs the **reactive voltage drop** that must be compensated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because $\Delta V$ is directly proportional to $\sin\phi$ (the reactive component of the load current), a heavily lagging (inductive) load causes the **largest voltage sag** at the receiving end. This is precisely why industrial loads with low power factors (e.g., induction motors, arc furnaces) experience the worst voltage regulation problems.

---

## 2.2 Real and Reactive Power Flow Equations (Lossless Line)

For a lossless transmission line ($\mathbf{Z} = jX$, $\mathbf{Y} = jB$, where $B$ is the line susceptance), with $\mathbf{V_S} = V_S \angle \delta$ and $\mathbf{V_R} = V_R \angle 0°$, the **power flow equations** at the receiving end are:

$$P_R = \frac{V_S \cdot V_R}{X} \sin\delta$$

$$Q_R = \frac{V_S \cdot V_R}{X} \cos\delta - \frac{V_R^2}{X}$$

The real power transfer $P_R$ depends on the **load angle $\delta$** (the angular displacement between the two end voltages), while the reactive power $Q_R$ depends on the **voltage magnitude difference**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Observation for KTU Examinations:** Notice that real power $P_R$ is **independent of the absolute voltage magnitudes** to first order, but **highly dependent on the angle $\delta$**. Conversely, reactive power $Q_R$ is **independent of $\delta$** to first order but **highly dependent on the voltage magnitudes**. This decoupling is the theoretical justification for using **angle-controlling devices (e.g., PST, UPFC series branch)** to regulate P, and **voltage-controlling devices (e.g., SVC, STATCOM shunt branch)** to regulate Q.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates **all essential equations** that KTU 2024 scheme examiners have historically tested under Module 2 of PEEET862 / similar power system electives:

| # | Formula | Description / Physical Meaning | Standard Units |
|:-:|:--------|:-------------------------------|:---------------|
| 1 | $VR\,\% = \dfrac{\vert V_{NL} - V_{FL} \vert}{V_{FL}} \times 100$ | Percentage voltage regulation of a line or bus | Dimensionless (\%) |
| 2 | $\Delta V \approx I(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$ | Approximate magnitude of voltage drop per phase | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $P_R = \dfrac{V_S V_R}{X}\sin\delta$ | Real power flow at receiving end (lossless line) | Watts (W) or MW |
| 4 | $Q_R = \dfrac{V_S V_R}{X}\cos\delta - \dfrac{V_R^2}{X}$ | Reactive power flow at receiving end | VAR or MVAR |
| 5 | $P_S = \dfrac{V_S V_R}{X}\sin\delta$ | Real power flow at sending end (lossless, same as $P_R$) | Watts (W) |
| 6 | $Q_S = \dfrac{V_S^2}{X} - \dfrac{V_S V_R}{X}\cos\delta$ | Reactive power injected at sending end | VAR or MVAR |
| 7 | $Q_{line} = \dfrac{V_S^2 + V_R^2 - 2V_S V_R\cos\delta}{X}$ | Net reactive power absorbed by line inductance | VAR or MVAR |
| 8 | $Z_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | Surge (Characteristic) Impedance of a line | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 9 | $SIL = \dfrac{V_{LL}^2}{Z_c}$ | Surge Impedance Loading (Natural loading of line) | Watts (W) or MW |
| 10 | $Q_{cap} = V^2 \cdot \omega C$ | Reactive power generated by shunt capacitor bank | VAR or MVAR |
| 11 | $Q_{ind} = \dfrac{V^2}{\omega L}$ | Reactive power absorbed by shunt reactor | VAR or MVAR |
| 12 | $\dfrac{dQ}{dV} \approx \dfrac{2V}{X}$ (no-load case) | Q-V sensitivity at a bus, governs voltage stability | Siemens (S) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Tip:** Questions in the 14-mark Part B slot often test the derivation of equations (3) and (4) starting from the basic line model, OR a numerical problem where equation (2) is used to compute the kVAR rating of a shunt compensator. Memorize the **sign conventions**: positive $Q$ denotes reactive power *absorbed* (inductive), negative $Q$ denotes reactive power *generated* (capacitive).

---

## 2.4 Why Reactive Power Control Matters — Engineering Drivers

Real-world utilities invest heavily in reactive power management for **four critical reasons**:

1. **Voltage Profile Maintenance:** All customers (residential, commercial, industrial) expect voltage within $\pm 5\%$ of nominal. Without reactive support, far-end voltages collapse under heavy load.
2. **Loss Minimization:** Line current $I = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}/V$ increases with Q. Since losses are $I^2R$, transmitting reactive power inflates thermal losses and reduces transfer capacity.
3. **Transfer Capacity Enhancement:** The maximum real power that can be transmitted on a line is $P_{max} = V_S V_R / X$ (the *steady-state stability limit* reached when $\delta = 90°$). By holding $V_R$ high, this ceiling is preserved.
4. **Voltage Stability & Collapse Prevention:** Following a contingency (line trip, generator outage), the system Q-V curve may become non-feasible, causing a runaway voltage collapse (e.g., the **2003 Northeastern US blackout** and **2012 India blackout** were both aggravated by inadequate reactive reserves).

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Practice:** Modern grid codes (e.g., the Indian Electricity Grid Code 2023, IEEE 1547-2018) mandate that large generators and wind/solar farms must provide reactive power capability of at least $\pm 0.95$ power factor at the point of common coupling (PCC). This is enforced using FACTS devices like **STATCOMs** at the renewable plant's switchyard.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔬 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Real and Reactive Power Flow Across a Lossless Line

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU university examinations on this topic. We derive equations (3) and (4) from the line equivalent circuit.

**Given:**
- Lossless transmission line with pure series reactance $\mathbf{Z} = jX$
- Sending end voltage phasor: $\mathbf{V_S} = V_S \angle \delta$
- Receiving end voltage phasor: $\mathbf{V_R} = V_R \angle 0°$ (chosen as reference)

**Step 1 — Write the line current using Ohm's law on the series branch:**

$$\mathbf{I} = \frac{\mathbf{V_S} - \mathbf{V_R}}{jX}$$

Convert $jX$ to polar form: $jX = X \angle 90°$, so $\dfrac{1}{jX} = \dfrac{1}{X} \angle -90°$.

**Step 2 — Convert the phasors to rectangular form:**

$$\mathbf{V_S} = V_S \cos\delta + j\,V_S \sin\delta$$

$$\mathbf{V_R} = V_R + j\,0$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator $\mathbf{V_S} - \mathbf{V_R}$:**

$$\mathbf{V_S} - \mathbf{V_R} = (V_S \cos\delta - V_R) + j(V_S \sin\delta)$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $jX$ (i.e., multiply by $-j/X$):**

$$\mathbf{I} = \frac{1}{X}\left[(V_S \cos\delta - V_R) + j(V_S \sin\delta)\right] \cdot \frac{1}{j}$$

Using the identity $\dfrac{1}{j} = -j$:

$$\mathbf{I} = -\frac{j}{X}\left[(V_S \cos\delta - V_R) + j(V_S \sin\delta)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Distribute the $-j$ across both terms:**

$$\mathbf{I} = \frac{-j(V_S \cos\delta - V_R)}{X} + \frac{-j \cdot j(V_S \sin\delta)}{X}$$

Using $-j \cdot j = -j^2 = +1$:

$$\mathbf{I} = \frac{-j(V_S \cos\delta - V_R) + (V_S \sin\delta)}{X}$$

**Step 6 — Regroup into real (in-phase with $V_R$) and imaginary (quadrature) components:**

$$\mathbf{I} = \frac{V_S \sin\delta}{X} + j\,\frac{V_R - V_S \cos\delta}{X}$$

**Step 7 — Identify the real part of $\mathbf{I}$ (in-phase component, $I_P$):**

$$I_P = \frac{V_S \sin\delta}{X}$$

**Step 8 — Identify the imaginary part of $\mathbf{I}$ (quadrature component, $I_Q$):**

$$I_Q = \frac{V_R - V_S \cos\delta}{X}$$

**Step 9 — Compute complex power at the receiving end $\mathbf{S_R} = \mathbf{V_R} \cdot \mathbf{I}^\*$:**

Since $\mathbf{V_R} = V_R \angle 0°$ is real, and $\mathbf{I}^\*$ is the complex conjugate of $\mathbf{I}$:

$$\mathbf{I}^* = \frac{V_S \sin\delta}{X} - j\,\frac{V_R - V_S \cos\delta}{X}$$

Multiplying by $V_R$:

$$\mathbf{S_R} = V_R \left[\frac{V_S \sin\delta}{X} - j\,\frac{V_R - V_S \cos\delta}{X}\right]$$

**Step 10 — Separate real and imaginary parts of $\mathbf{S_R}$:**

$$P_R = \frac{V_S V_R}{X} \sin\delta$$

$$Q_R = \frac{V_R^2 - V_S V_R \cos\delta}{X} = \frac{V_S V_R}{X}\cos\delta - \frac{V_R^2}{X}$$

> [$\blacksquare$ — Q.E.D. — The two fundamental power flow equations are derived.]

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Approximate Voltage Drop Formula

This derivation justifies the approximate formula $\Delta V \approx IR\cos\phi + IX\sin\phi$ used for short-line voltage regulation studies.

**Given:** Phasor diagram with $\mathbf{V_S} = \mathbf{V_R} + \mathbf{I}\mathbf{Z}$, where $\mathbf{V_R} = V_R \angle 0°$ and $\mathbf{I} = I \angle -\phi$.

**Step 1 — Write the voltage drop phasor $\Delta\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{I} \cdot \mathbf{Z}$:**

$$\Delta\mathbf{V} = I \angle -\phi \cdot (R + jX)$$

**Step 2 — Expand using Euler's formula for the angle:**

$$\Delta\mathbf{V} = I(\cos\phi - j\sin\phi)(R + jX)$$

**Step 3 — Distribute:**

$$\Delta\mathbf{V} = IR\cos\phi - jIR\sin\phi + jIX\cos\phi - j^2 IX\sin\phi$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $j^2 = -1$:**

$$\Delta\mathbf{V} = (IR\cos\phi + IX\sin\phi) + j(IX\cos\phi - IR\sin\phi)$$

**Step 5 — Add $\mathbf{V_R} = V_R + j0$ to obtain $\mathbf{V_S}$:**

$$\mathbf{V_S} = \left[V_R + IR\cos\phi + IX\sin\phi\right] + j\left[IX\cos\phi - IR\sin\phi\right]$$

**Step 6 — Define the in-phase component (resistive drop):**

$$\Delta V_d = IR\cos\phi + IX\sin\phi \quad \text{(the component along } V_R\text{)}$$

**Step 7 — Define the quadrature component (reactive drop):**

$$\delta V_q = IX\cos\phi - IR\sin\phi \quad \text{(the component at 90° to } V_R\text{)}$$

**Step 8 — Approximate the magnitude of voltage drop for small $\delta$:**

For practical transmission lines, the angle between $\mathbf{V_S}$ and $\mathbf{V_R}$ (the load angle $\delta$) is small (typically $< 25°$). Under this assumption:

$$\vert \mathbf{V_S} \vert \approx V_R + \Delta V_d = V_R + IR\cos\phi + IX\sin\phi$$

**Step 9 — Therefore the approximate voltage drop is:**

$$\Delta V \approx IR\cos\phi + IX\sin\phi$$

> [$\blacksquare$ — Q.E.D. — Approximate voltage drop formula derived from the phasor diagram.]

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Exam Style

**Problem:** A **33 kV, 50 Hz**, three-phase transmission line delivers a load of **10 MW** at **0.8 power factor lagging** at the receiving end. The line has total impedance of $(6 + j8)\,\Omega$ per phase. Calculate: (a) the sending-end voltage per phase, (b) the voltage regulation, and (c) the reactive power that must be injected at the receiving end to bring the voltage regulation to **zero**.

**Solution — Step 1: Compute the receiving-end phase voltage and line current:**

$$V_R = \frac{33\,000}{\sqrt{3}} = 19{,}052.56 \text{ V per phase}$$

$$I = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3}\,V_{LL}\cos\phi} = \frac{10 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 33{,}000 \times 0.8} = 218.69 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Compute the voltage drop per phase using the approximate formula:**

$$\phi = \cos^{-1}(0.8) = 36.87°, \quad \sin\phi = 0.6$$

$$\Delta V = I(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi) = 218.69 \times (6 \times 0.8 + 8 \times 0.6)$$

$$\Delta V = 218.69 \times (4.8 + 4.8) = 218.69 \times 9.6 = 2{,}099.4 \text{ V per phase}$$

**Step 3: Sending-end phase voltage:**

$$V_S = V_R + \Delta V = 19{,}052.56 + 2{,}099.4 = 21{,}151.96 \text{ V per phase}$$

Sending-end line voltage: $V_{S,LL} = 21{,}151.96 \times \sqrt{3} = 36{,}636.4 \text{ V} \approx 36.64 \text{ kV}$

**Step 4: Voltage regulation:**

$$VR\,\% = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{21{,}151.96 - 19{,}052.56}{19{,}052.56} \times 100 \approx 11.02\%$$

**Step 5: Reactive power needed at receiving end to achieve VR = 0:**

For zero voltage regulation, the magnitude $\vert \mathbf{V_S} \vert = \vert \mathbf{V_R} \vert$. This requires the quadrature component $\delta V_q$ to vanish by reactive compensation, OR the in-phase drop $\Delta V_d$ to be cancelled. The compensation is implemented by a shunt capacitor that supplies:

$$Q_{cap} = V_R^2 \cdot B_{cap} = V_R^2 \cdot \omega C$$

Alternatively, the line's reactive consumption at full load is $Q_{line} = I^2 X = (218.69)^2 \times 8 = 382{,}617 \text{ VAR} = 0.383 \text{ MVAR}$.

To bring $V_S$ down to equal $V_R$, the capacitor must supply approximately:

$$Q_{cap} \approx \frac{V_R \cdot \Delta V}{X} = \frac{19{,}052.56 \times 2{,}099.4}{8} = 4{,}999{,}973 \text{ VAR} \approx 5.0 \text{ MVAR}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Notice that the capacitor rating (5 MVAR) is much larger than the line's reactive consumption (0.38 MVAR). This is because we are not just compensating the line's reactive drop — we are *boosting* the receiving end voltage back up to the no-load value. The 5 MVAR figure represents the *full voltage support* requirement.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reactive Power Control Architecture in a Modern Power System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how voltage regulation and reactive power control are orchestrated in a contemporary grid using conventional equipment and FACTS devices.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Gen[Synchronous Generator with AVR] -->|P and Q| StepUp[Step Up Transformer]
    StepUp -->|Bulk transmission| HVLine[EHV Transmission Line 400kV]
    HVLine -->|Voltage drop ΔV occurs| StepDown[Step Down Transformer at Substation]
    StepDown -->|Distribution 11kV or 33kV| DistBus[Distribution Bus]
    DistBus -->|Industrial load| IndLoad[Induction Motors Lagging PF 0.8]
    DistBus -->|Domestic load| DomLoad[Residential Mixed Load]

    HVLine -. Reactive compensation .-> ShuntReactor1[Shunt Reactor at sending end absorbs Q]
    HVLine -. Reactive compensation .-> ShuntReactor2[Shunt Reactor at midpoint absorbs Q]
    DistBus -. Dynamic compensation .-> SVC[Static Var Compensator SVC TCR plus TSC]
    DistBus -. Dynamic compensation .-> STATCOM[STATCOM Voltage Source Converter]
    DistBus -. Series compensation .-> TCSC[Thyristor Controlled Series Capacitor]
    StepUp -. Excitation control .-> Gen
    StepDown -. On Load Tap Changer OLTC .-> StepDown
    IndLoad -. Power factor correction .-> ShuntCap[Shunt Capacitor Bank at load bus]

    classDef facts fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef conv fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class SVC,STATCOM,TCSC facts
    class Gen,StepUp,StepDown,OLTC,ShuntReactor1,ShuntReactor2,ShuntCap conv
    class IndLoad,DomLoad,DistBus,HVLine load
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The solid arrows (→) represent the **flow of real and reactive power** from generation to load. The dotted arrows (·->) represent the **control / compensation action** that injects or absorbs reactive power at strategic locations. The blue-shaded FACTS devices (SVC, STATCOM, TCSC) provide *fast, dynamic* control (response time 1–2 cycles), while the green-shaded conventional equipment (reactor, capacitor, OLTC) provide *slower, coarser* control (response time seconds to minutes).

## 4.2 Q-V Characteristics of a Load Bus with Shunt Compensation

The following Mermaid chart shows the conceptual relationship between the **Q-V curve** of a load bus, the **Q-source characteristic** of a shunt compensator, and the resulting **equilibrium operating point**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph QVplot["Q-V Plane at Load Bus"]
        QV1[Q-V curve of network: Q = V² - V·Vth cos δth / Xth]:::curveStyle
        QV2[Q-V curve of load: Qload = constant]:::loadStyle
        QV3[Operating point before compensation P0]:::pointStyle
        QV4[Operating point after SVC/STATCOM P1 with higher V]:::pointStyle
    end

    classDef curveStyle fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px
    classDef loadStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:2px
    classDef pointStyle fill:#28a745,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
```

**Interpretation:**

- The **yellow curve** is the network's intrinsic Q-V relationship — it represents the reactive power that the network *can supply* to the bus as a function of bus voltage magnitude.
- The **pink line** is the load's reactive demand — typically modeled as constant Q or constant impedance.
- The **green points** are the equilibrium intersections. Compensation (SVC/STATCOM) **shifts the load's effective Q-V line upward** (by adding capacitive VARs), moving the operating point from P0 to P1 with a higher, more stable voltage.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering rule of thumb for KTU viva:** The Q-V curve's **nose point** (the minimum voltage beyond which no feasible solution exists) defines the **voltage stability limit**. The closer the operating point is to the nose, the more vulnerable the system is to voltage collapse following any contingency.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

**Q: Define percentage voltage regulation of a transmission line. Why is it undesirable to have a high value of voltage regulation in practical power systems?**

**Model Answer:**

Percentage voltage regulation of a transmission line is defined as the change in magnitude of the receiving-end voltage between no-load and full-load conditions, expressed as a percentage of the full-load receiving-end voltage.

$$VR\,\% = \frac{\vert V_{NL} - V_{FL} \vert}{V_{FL}} \times 100$$

High voltage regulation is undesirable for three reasons:
1. **Customer equipment damage/malfunction:** Appliances rated at 230 V may receive voltages as low as 180 V or as high as 260 V, causing premature failure of motors, electronics, and lighting.
2. **Reduced system efficiency:** Large voltage drops imply large line currents, which produce $I^2R$ losses proportional to the square of the current.
3. **Poor transient stability margin:** A depressed receiving-end voltage reduces the maximum transferable real power $P_{max} = V_S V_R / X$, making the system prone to angle instability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking scheme:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for any one valid reason. Total: 3 marks.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Q: Distinguish between shunt and series compensation in transmission systems. Give one example of a FACTS device used for each type.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Shunt Compensation | Series Compensation |
|:--------|:-------------------|:--------------------|
| **Connection** | Connected in parallel (line-to-neutral) at a bus | Inserted in series with the line conductor |
| **Primary effect** | Controls bus voltage magnitude by injecting/absorbing reactive current | Controls line current and effective transfer reactance |
| **Equivalent** | Acts like a variable reactive admittance $B_{sh}$ | Acts like a variable series reactance $X_{se}$ |
| **Application** | Voltage regulation, voltage stability, reactive balance | Power flow control, damping oscillations, increasing transfer capability |
| **FACTS device example** | **SVC** (Static Var Compensator) or **STATCOM** | **TCSC** (Thyristor Controlled Series Capacitor) or **SSSC** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking scheme:** 1 mark for shunt definition + example, 1 mark for series definition + example, 1 mark for the comparison summary. Total: 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A [14 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expressions for real power (P) and reactive power (Q) transmitted over a short transmission line represented by a lumped series reactance $X$, with sending-end voltage $V_S \angle \delta$ and receiving-end voltage $V_R \angle 0°$. Clearly state the assumptions made.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **132 kV, 50 Hz**, three-phase transmission line has a series impedance of $(20 + j60)\,\Omega$ per phase. It delivers a load of **50 MVA** at **0.85 power factor lagging**. Compute:
1. The percentage voltage regulation of the line.
2. The reactive power that a shunt capacitor bank at the receiving end must supply to improve the power factor to **0.95 lagging**.
3. The new percentage voltage regulation after power factor correction.

---

### 🔹 Question B [14 Marks, Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a phasor diagram, explain the approximate formula for the voltage drop across a short transmission line: $\Delta V \approx IR\cos\phi + IX\sin\phi$. Identify which term dominates in (i) low-voltage distribution lines and (ii) extra-high-voltage (EHV) transmission lines, and justify your answer with the typical X/R ratios.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 220 kV, 50 Hz, three-phase transmission line delivers a load of **100 MW** at **0.9 p.f. lagging**. The line parameters are $R = 0.1\,\Omega/\text{km}$ and $X = 0.5\,\Omega/\text{km}$, with total length of **150 km**. Calculate the (i) sending-end voltage magnitude, (ii) sending-end power factor, and (iii) percentage voltage regulation. Use the approximate voltage drop formula.

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions for Part B

### ✅ Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of P and Q Flow Equations

**Assumptions:**
1. The transmission line is short enough to be represented by a lumped series reactance $X$ (neglecting shunt capacitance).
2. The line is lossless, i.e., $R = 0$, so $\mathbf{Z} = jX$.
3. The receiving-end voltage $\mathbf{V_R} = V_R \angle 0°$ is the reference phasor.
4. The sending-end voltage leads by a load angle $\delta$, i.e., $\mathbf{V_S} = V_S \angle \delta$.

**Derivation:**

The current flowing from sending end to receiving end is:

$$\mathbf{I} = \frac{\mathbf{V_S} - \mathbf{V_R}}{jX}$$

Substituting the phasors in rectangular form:

$$\mathbf{I} = \frac{(V_S \cos\delta - V_R) + j(V_S \sin\delta)}{jX}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $-j$:

$$\mathbf{I} = \frac{V_S \sin\delta}{X} - j\,\frac{V_S \cos\delta - V_R}{X}$$

Separating real and imaginary parts:

$$\mathbf{I} = I_P - jI_Q, \quad \text{where } I_P = \frac{V_S \sin\delta}{X}, \quad I_Q = \frac{V_S \cos\delta - V_R}{X}$$

The complex power at the receiving end is $\mathbf{S_R} = \mathbf{V_R} \cdot \mathbf{I}^\*$. Since $\mathbf{V_R}$ is real:

$$\mathbf{S_R} = V_R \cdot \left(\frac{V_S \sin\delta}{X} + j\,\frac{V_S \cos\delta - V_R}{X}\right)$$

Equating real and imaginary parts:

$$P_R = \frac{V_S V_R}{X}\sin\delta$$

$$Q_R = \frac{V_S V_R \cos\delta - V_R^2}{X} = \frac{V_S V_R}{X}\cos\delta - \frac{V_R^2}{X}$$

> [$\blacksquare$ — Both equations derived from first principles.]

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Step 1: Compute receiving-end phase voltage and line current.**

$$V_R = \frac{132{,}000}{\sqrt{3}} = 76{,}210.2 \text{ V per phase}$$

$$I = \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL}} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 132{,}000} = 218.69 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Compute voltage drop per phase.**

$\cos\phi = 0.85$, $\sin\phi = \sqrt{1 - 0.85^2} = 0.5268$

$$\Delta V = I(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi) = 218.69 \times (20 \times 0.85 + 60 \times 0.5268)$$

$$\Delta V = 218.69 \times (17 + 31.61) = 218.69 \times 48.61 = 10{,}629.5 \text{ V per phase}$$

**Step 3: Sending-end phase and line voltages.**

$$V_S = V_R + \Delta V = 76{,}210.2 + 10{,}629.5 = 86{,}839.7 \text{ V per phase}$$

$$V_{S,LL} = 86{,}839.7 \times \sqrt{3} = 150{,}411 \text{ V} \approx 150.4 \text{ kV}$$

**Step 4: Percentage voltage regulation (original).**

$$VR_1\,\% = \frac{10{,}629.5}{76{,}210.2} \times 100 \approx 13.95\%$$

**Step 5: Reactive power supplied by load and capacitor sizing for p.f. correction.**

Original reactive power consumed by the load:

$$Q_1 = S \sin\phi_1 = 50 \times 0.5268 = 26.34 \text{ MVAR}$$

Target reactive power after p.f. correction to 0.95 lagging:

$$\cos\phi_2 = 0.95, \quad \sin\phi_2 = \sqrt{1 - 0.95^2} = 0.3122$$

$$Q_2 = P \tan\phi_2 = 50 \times \tan(\cos^{-1}(0.95)) = 50 \times 0.3287 = 16.43 \text{ MVAR}$$

Reactive power to be supplied by the shunt capacitor bank:

$$Q_{cap} = Q_1 - Q_2 = 26.34 - 16.43 = 9.91 \text{ MVAR}$$

**Step 6: New line current after p.f. correction.**

$$I_{new} = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} V_{LL} \cos\phi_2} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 132{,}000 \times 0.95} = 230.16 \text{ A}$$

**Step 7: New voltage drop and regulation.**

$$\Delta V_{new} = I_{new}(R\cos\phi_2 + X\sin\phi_2) = 230.16 \times (20 \times 0.95 + 60 \times 0.3122)$$

$$\Delta V_{new} = 230.16 \times (19 + 18.73) = 230.16 \times 37.73 = 8{,}683.9 \text{ V per phase}$$

$$VR_2\,\% = \frac{8{,}683.9}{76{,}210.2} \times 100 \approx 11.40\%$$

> **Summary of Part (b) Results:**
> 1. Original voltage regulation: **13.95%**
> 2. Required capacitor bank: **9.91 MVAR** (≈ 10 MVAR)
> 3. Improved voltage regulation: **11.40%** (a reduction of 2.55 percentage points)

---

### ✅ Solution to Question B (Alternative)

#### Part (a) — Phasor Diagram Explanation (See SECTION 4.1 for the Q-V architecture)

The approximate voltage drop formula is derived by treating the voltage drop phasor $\Delta\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{IZ}$ and projecting it onto the receiving-end voltage axis (in-phase component $\Delta V_d$) and the quadrature axis ($\delta V_q$). The in-phase component that lies along $\mathbf{V_R}$ is:

$$\Delta V_d = IR\cos\phi + IX\sin\phi$$

For practical small load angles ($\delta < 25°$), the magnitude of the sending-end voltage approximates as:

$$V_S \approx V_R + IR\cos\phi + IX\sin\phi$$

**Which term dominates?**

- **(i) Low-voltage distribution lines (e.g., 11 kV, 33 kV):** Typical X/R ratio is **0.5 to 2**. The resistance R is comparable to or larger than X. Therefore, the **$IR\cos\phi$ term dominates** and voltage drop is primarily resistive.
- **(ii) Extra-high-voltage (EHV) lines (e.g., 400 kV, 765 kV):** Typical X/R ratio is **8 to 15**. The reactance X far exceeds R. Therefore, the **$IX\sin\phi$ term dominates**, and voltage drop is primarily reactive. This is why EHV lines *must* be compensated by shunt reactors under light load and series capacitors under heavy load.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation for Question B

**Step 1: Total line impedance.**

$$R_{total} = 0.1 \times 150 = 15\,\Omega, \quad X_{total} = 0.5 \times 150 = 75\,\Omega$$

**Step 2: Receiving-end phase voltage and line current.**

$$V_R = \frac{220{,}000}{\sqrt{3}} = 127{,}017 \text{ V per phase}$$

$$\cos\phi = 0.9, \quad \sin\phi = 0.4359$$

$$I = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3}\,V_{LL}\cos\phi} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 220{,}000 \times 0.9} = 291.6 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Approximate voltage drop per phase.**

$$\Delta V = I(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi) = 291.6 \times (15 \times 0.9 + 75 \times 0.4359)$$

$$\Delta V = 291.6 \times (13.5 + 32.69) = 291.6 \times 46.19 = 13{,}468.4 \text{ V per phase}$$

**Step 4: Sending-end phase voltage and line voltage.**

$$V_S = V_R + \Delta V = 127{,}017 + 13{,}468.4 = 140{,}485.4 \text{ V per phase}$$

$$V_{S,LL} = 140{,}485.4 \times \sqrt{3} = 243{,}328 \text{ V} \approx 243.33 \text{ kV}$$

**Step 5: Sending-end power factor.**

Real power at receiving end: $P = 100$ MW
Reactive power at receiving end: $Q_R = P \tan\phi = 100 \times 0.4843 = 48.43$ MVAR
Line reactive consumption: $Q_{line} = I^2 X = (291.6)^2 \times 75 = 6{,}377{,}000$ VAR $= 6.38$ MVAR
Total reactive power at sending end: $Q_S = Q_R + Q_{line} = 48.43 + 6.38 = 54.81$ MVAR
Sending-end power factor angle: $\tan\phi_S = Q_S/P = 54.81/100 = 0.5481$, so $\phi_S = 28.7°$, $\cos\phi_S \approx 0.877$

**Step 6: Percentage voltage regulation.**

$$VR\,\% = \frac{\Delta V}{V_R} \times 100 = \frac{13{,}468.4}{127{,}017} \times 100 \approx 10.60\%$$

> **Summary of Part (b) Results for Question B:**
> 1. Sending-end line voltage: **243.33 kV**
> 2. Sending-end power factor: **0.877 lagging**
> 3. Percentage voltage regulation: **10.60%**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors (–2 marks):** Forgetting that the receiving-end phase voltage is $V_{LL}/\sqrt{3}$ for star-connected systems. Always divide the line-to-line voltage by $\sqrt{3}$ before applying single-phase equivalent circuits.
> 2. **Quadrant Errors in Phasor Diagrams (–2 marks):** Drawing the current phasor on the wrong side of $V_R$ for leading vs. lagging power factors. **Lagging pf → current drawn below $V_R$ on phasor diagram; leading pf → above.**
> 3. **Quadrature Component Ignored (–1 mark):** Using only $\Delta V \approx IR$ instead of the full $IR\cos\phi + IX\sin\phi$. The reactive component is often the dominant term in EHV systems and examiners specifically test for it.
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency (–1 mark):** Mixing up kV with V or MW with W in the final numerical answer. Always express the final voltage regulation in **percentage** (not per-unit) unless the question asks otherwise.
> 5. **No Explanation of Assumption (–1 mark):** In derivation problems, failing to state the assumption "$R \ll X$" or "lossless line" explicitly. Always enumerate your assumptions *before* starting the derivation — this is worth at least 1 mark on its own.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ High-Density Revision Bullet List for Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep:**

- ✅ **Voltage Regulation Definition:** $VR\% = (\vert V_{NL} - V_{FL} \vert / V_{FL}) \times 100$. Lower is better; ideally < 5% at consumer terminals.
- ✅ **Voltage Drop Formula (Short Line):** $\Delta V \approx IR\cos\phi + IX\sin\phi$. The $IX\sin\phi$ term dominates in EHV; the $IR\cos\phi$ term dominates in LV distribution.
- ✅ **Real Power Flow:** $P_R = (V_S V_R / X)\sin\delta$ — controlled by **load angle $\delta$**, not voltage magnitude.
- ✅ **Reactive Power Flow:** $Q_R = (V_S V_R / X)\cos\delta - V_R^2 / X$ — controlled by **voltage magnitude difference**, not $\delta$.
- ✅ **Surge Impedance Loading (SIL):** $SIL = V_{LL}^2 / Z_c$, where $Z_c = \sqrt{L/C}$. When a line is loaded at SIL, it is "self-regulating" — no net reactive exchange.
- ✅ **Voltage ↔ Reactive Power Coupling:** $\partial V / \partial Q > 0$ always. Inject Q → V rises; absorb Q → V falls. This is the foundation of all shunt compensation.
- ✅ **Shunt Compensation Devices:** Shunt capacitors (lead, generate Q), shunt reactors (lag, absorb Q), SVC, STATCOM.
- ✅ **Series Compensation Devices:** Series capacitors (reduce effective X), TCSC, SSSC.
- ✅ **Combined Shunt + Series:** UPFC (Unified Power Flow Controller) — the most versatile FACTS device, covered in Module 3.
- ✅ **Capacitor Sizing Formula:** $Q_{cap} = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ to improve power factor from $\cos\phi_1$ to $\cos\phi_2$.
- ✅ **Voltage Stability Indicator:** dQ/dV < 0 at the operating point → stable. If dQ/dV = 0, the system is at the **nose point** of the Q-V curve and a small disturbance can cause voltage collapse.
- ✅ **Steady-State Stability Limit:** $P_{max} = V_S V_R / X$ — occurs at $\delta = 90°$ for a lossless line. Reducing X (series compensation) or boosting V (shunt compensation) raises this ceiling.
- ✅ **Common Industrial Limits:** Voltage must be maintained within $\pm 5\%$ of nominal at all consumer terminals (per IE Rules 1956 / CEA Technical Standards).
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Frequently Tested Concepts:** (i) Derivation of P-Q equations, (ii) Numerical on voltage regulation, (iii) Numerical on capacitor bank sizing for p.f. correction, (iv) Comparison of shunt vs. series compensation with FACTS examples, (v) Q-V curve and voltage stability explanation.
- ✅ **Units to Memorize:** Real Power → **W, kW, MW**; Reactive Power → **VAR, kVAR, MVAR**; Apparent Power → **VA, kVA, MVA**; Voltage → **V, kV**; Reactance → **Ω (Ohms)**; Frequency → **Hz**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Flow Control - Constraints of Maximum Transmission Line Loading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET862 - HVDC AND FACTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Power Flow Control - Constraints of Maximum Transmission Line Loading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Power Flow Control** in an electrical transmission system refers to the deliberate management and regulation of real and reactive power flow through transmission corridors using series compensators, shunt compensators, phase angle regulators, and HVDC links. 

**Constraints of Maximum Transmission Line Loading** are the physical, electrical, and stability-based boundaries that limit the maximum amount of real power that can be transmitted through an AC overhead transmission line or cable. These constraints collectively define the **Line Loadability Envelope**, classically represented as the "Canyon" or "Staircase" curve, where maximum transferable power is plotted against transmission line length.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The maximum power that can be transmitted over an AC line is fundamentally limited by four primary constraints: **(1) Thermal Limit, (2) Voltage Drop / Voltage Stability Limit, (3) Steady-State Angular Stability Limit, and (4) Transient / Dynamic Stability Limit.** FACTS and HVDC technologies are designed specifically to relax these constraints.

## 1.2 The Power Angle Equation — The Heart of the Matter

For a lossless AC transmission line modeled as a series reactance $X$, the real power transferred between the sending end voltage $V_S$ and receiving end voltage $V_R$ across a load angle $\delta$ is:

$$P = \frac{V_S \cdot V_R}{X} \sin(\delta)$$

This single equation governs the entire constraint philosophy, because **$X$, $V_S$, $V_R$, and $\delta$** are the only four levers we can manipulate — and each one is bounded by a physical limit.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Garden Hose with Crimps" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine trying to push water through a long, thin garden hose with three intentional crimps:
>
> - **Thermal limit** = The hose material will melt if you push too much water (current heating of conductor).
> - **Voltage drop limit** = The pressure at the far end becomes too weak to operate the sprinkler (sag, voltage collapse).
> - **Stability limit** = The crimp gets so tight that the hose kinks and water-flow oscillates wildly (swinging of rotors, angular instability).
>
> **The Engineer's Goal:** Use pumps (shunt compensation), wider sections (series compensation), and variable valves (FACTS) to push MORE water (power) through the same hose. HVDC is essentially **replacing the hose entirely with a high-pressure pipeline** that has none of these crimps.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Surge Impedance (SIL Base):** Approximately **$400 \, \Omega$** for a typical 400 kV line, giving SIL ≈ **$400$ MW** per circuit.
- **Thermal limit of ACSR conductors:** Roughly **$1000$ to $4000$ A** depending on cross-section.
- **Acceptable voltage deviation:** Typically **$\pm 5\%$** of nominal at the load end.
- **Steady-state stability margin:** $\delta_{max} \leq 30^\circ$ to $45^\circ$ for normal operation.
- **Critical clearing time:** Order of **$100$ ms to $500$ ms** for transient stability.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Transmission Line Loading Constraints

A transmission line is never rated by a single number. Its maximum loadability is determined by whichever constraint becomes active **first** as we increase power transfer. As line length increases, the dominant constraint transitions in a predictable sequence.

### 2.1.1 Pillar 1 — Thermal Limit (Conductor Heating)

Every conductor has a maximum continuous current rating $I_{max}$ determined by:
- Conductor material (ACSR, AAAC, ACAR)
- Cross-sectional area
- Ambient temperature
- Wind speed and solar radiation
- Maximum allowable sag temperature

The thermal power limit is:

$$P_{thermal} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_{max} \cdot pf$$

where $V_L$ is the line-to-line voltage and $pf$ is the operating power factor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal limit is INDEPENDENT of line length.** A short line and a long line of the same conductor type have the same thermal rating. It is a pure conductor constraint.

### 2.1.2 Pillar 2 — Voltage Drop / Voltage Stability Limit

The receiving end voltage must remain within acceptable bounds (typically $\pm 5\%$). For a line of reactance $X$ transferring power $P$ and reactive power $Q$:

$$V_R = V_S - \frac{PR + QX}{V_S} - j\frac{PX - QR}{V_S}$$

The voltage drop magnitude is dominated by the $PX$ term:

$$\Delta V \approx \frac{PX}{V_S}$$

**Voltage collapse** occurs when the system reaches the "nose point" of the PV curve, beyond which no steady-state operating point exists. The distance to voltage collapse is often called the **Voltage Stability Margin**.

### 2.1.3 Pillar 3 — Steady-State Angular Stability Limit

From the power-angle equation, maximum theoretical power transfer occurs at $\delta = 90^\circ$:

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_S \cdot V_R}{X}$$

However, **practical steady-state operation requires $\delta \ll 90^\circ$** because:
- The system must remain stable under small disturbances.
- Operators typically enforce a **steady-state stability margin of 20–30%**, meaning $\delta_{operating} \leq 30^\circ$ to $45^\circ$.

The practical limit is:

$$P_{ss} = \frac{V_S \cdot V_R}{X} \sin(\delta_{max}) \approx (0.5 \text{ to } 0.7) \cdot \frac{V_S V_R}{X}$$

### 2.1.4 Pillar 4 — Transient / Dynamic Stability Limit

When a fault occurs on the line, the rotor angles of synchronous generators accelerate, then swing after fault clearing. The system must be capable of maintaining synchronism. The **Equal Area Criterion** dictates:

$$A_{accelerating} \leq A_{decelerating}$$

The transient stability limit is usually more restrictive than steady-state, often limiting the first swing $\delta$ to $\leq 80^\circ$ to $100^\circ$ for a properly designed system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why all four matter for KTU:** Each constraint is dominant in a different length regime. Understanding this is the entire point of the "staircase curve" (Section 4 diagram).

## 2.2 Surge Impedance Loading (SIL) — The Natural Benchmark

The **Surge Impedance** $Z_s$ of a transmission line is the characteristic impedance under lossless conditions:

$$Z_s = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

The **Surge Impedance Loading (SIL)** is the natural power at which the line is "flat" — no net reactive power absorbed or generated:

$$SIL = \frac{V^2}{Z_s} = \frac{V_{LL}^2}{Z_s}$$

For a typical 400 kV line: $Z_s \approx 400 \, \Omega$, hence $SIL \approx 400$ MW.

| Loading Condition | Behavior | Reactive Power |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $P < SIL$ | Line behaves as a **capacitor** (Ferranti effect) | Line **generates** $Q$ |
| $P = SIL$ | Line is "flat" (natural loading) | $Q \approx 0$ |
| $P > SIL$ | Line behaves as an **inductor** | Line **absorbs** $Q$ |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All mathematical absolute-value and dividers are rendered using `\vert` and `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Real Power Transfer | $P = \dfrac{V_S V_R}{X} \sin(\delta)$ | MW | Governing equation of AC transmission |
| 2 | Maximum Theoretical Power | $P_{max} = \dfrac{V_S V_R}{X}$ | MW (at $\delta = 90^\circ$) | Theoretical ceiling, never used in practice |
| 3 | Surge Impedance | $Z_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | $\Omega$ (typically $250$–$400$) | Natural line parameter |
| 4 | Surge Impedance Loading | $SIL = \dfrac{V_{LL}^2}{Z_s}$ | MW | Reference for loading assessment |
| 5 | Thermal Limit | $P_{th} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_{max} \cdot pf$ | MW | Conductor heating bound |
| 6 | Voltage Drop (approx.) | $\Delta V \approx \dfrac{PX}{V_S}$ | per unit / kV | Voltage stability bound |
| 7 | Practical Steady-State Limit | $P_{ss} \approx 0.7 \cdot P_{max}$ | MW (at $\delta \approx 45^\circ$) | Operating stability margin |
| 8 | Practical Transient Limit | $P_{tr} \approx 0.85 \cdot P_{max}$ | MW (at $\delta \approx 60^\circ$) | Post-fault stability margin |
| 9 | Compensated Reactance | $X_{eff} = X(1-k_s)$ | $\Omega$ (with series comp. ratio $k_s$) | Series compensation effect |
| 10 | DC Line Power | $P_{DC} = V_{DC} \cdot I_{DC}$ | MW | HVDC fundamental — no $X$, no $\delta$ |

## 2.4 Why These Constraints Exist — The "Why" Behind the Equations

- **Why thermal limit?** Conductors physically heat. Excessive temperature anneals aluminum, reduces tensile strength, and increases sag dangerously.
- **Why voltage limit?** Customers' equipment is rated for specific voltage (e.g., $230 \pm 5\%$ V). Motors stall, electronics fail, and transformers saturate outside this band.
- **Why steady-state stability limit?** A power system is a network of synchronously rotating masses. The torque-angle curve is sinusoidal, with a peak at $90^\circ$. Beyond that, synchronism is lost.
- **Why transient limit?** Faults cause rotor acceleration. If the system cannot shed enough kinetic energy by decelerating after fault clearance, the generators lose step.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power System Planning:** Determines the required number of parallel circuits for a given load.
- **FACTS Sizing:** Series capacitors are sized to push $P_{max}$ up by reducing effective $X$.
- **HVDC Justification:** For submarine cables and very long overhead lines, the DC option has **no $X$ and no $\delta$**, so it bypasses three of the four constraints at once.
- **Renewable Integration:** Wind and solar farms at remote locations require long transmission distances — exactly the regime where AC constraints bite hardest.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Power-Angle Equation (from First Principles)

### Setup

Consider a simplified two-machine system: Generator at sending end with internal EMF $E_S$ behind transient reactance $X_d'$, transformer reactance $X_T$, and transmission line reactance $X_L$, with an infinite bus (or large system) at the receiving end with voltage $V_R$.

Total series reactance:

$$X = X_d' + X_T + \frac{X_L}{2}$$

(In the case of a line of reactance $X_L$ with transformers at both ends and generator transient reactance at one end.)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the sending end and receiving end phasors.

$$\vec{V_S} = V_S \angle \delta \quad , \quad \vec{V_R} = V_R \angle 0^\circ$$

**Step 2:** Write the current flowing from sending to receiving end through series reactance $X$.

$$\vec{I} = \frac{\vec{V_S} - \vec{V_R}}{jX} = \frac{V_S \angle \delta - V_R \angle 0^\circ}{jX}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the complex power at the sending end.

$$\vec{S_S} = \vec{V_S} \cdot \vec{I}^* = V_S \angle \delta \cdot \left[ \frac{V_S \angle -\delta - V_R \angle 0^\circ}{-jX} \right]$$

**Step 4:** Simplify using Euler's identity. Note that $\dfrac{1}{-j} = j$.

$$\vec{S_S} = \frac{V_S}{X} \angle \delta \cdot j \cdot \left( V_S \angle -\delta - V_R \angle 0^\circ \right)$$

**Step 5:** Distribute the multiplication.

$$\vec{S_S} = \frac{jV_S^2}{X} - \frac{jV_S V_R}{X} \angle \delta$$

**Step 6:** Expand the second term using $j e^{j\delta} = e^{j(\delta + \pi/2)}$, then separate real and imaginary parts.

$$\vec{S_S} = \frac{V_S^2}{X} \angle 90^\circ - \frac{V_S V_R}{X} \angle (\delta + 90^\circ)$$

**Step 7:** Extract the real power component.

$$P_S = \text{Re}\{\vec{S_S}\} = \frac{V_S V_R}{X} \sin(\delta)$$

**Step 8:** By symmetry of a lossless network, real power in equals real power out, so:

$$\boxed{P = \frac{V_S \cdot V_R}{X} \sin(\delta)}$$

This is the **governing equation of AC power transmission**. Every single constraint on maximum loading ultimately comes back to a restriction on this equation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Surge Impedance Loading

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Consider a lossless distributed parameter line with inductance $L$ (H/m) and capacitance $C$ (F/m) per unit length. The propagation constant and characteristic impedance are:

$$\gamma = j\omega \sqrt{LC} \quad , \quad Z_s = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

**Step 2:** At SIL, the line operates as a flat voltage profile — no reflection. The line draws only real current from the source.

**Step 3:** The receiving end current at SIL is:

$$I_{SIL} = \frac{V_R}{Z_s}$$

**Step 4:** The real power delivered to the load is therefore:

$$P_{SIL} = V_R \cdot I_{SIL} = \frac{V_R^2}{Z_s}$$

**Step 5:** Using line-to-line voltage $V_{LL} = \sqrt{3} \, V_R$ (for a three-phase line, with $V_R$ being phase voltage):

$$\boxed{SIL = \frac{V_{LL}^2}{Z_s}}$$

**Step 6:** Numerical example for a 400 kV line with $Z_s = 400 \, \Omega$:

$$SIL = \frac{(400 \text{ kV})^2}{400 \, \Omega} = \frac{160{,}000}{400} = 400 \text{ MW}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The "Canyon Curve" Critical Equation

The **length-dependent limit** for a transmission line is governed by the steady-state stability constraint, which becomes dominant beyond a certain length. Setting $V_S = V_R = V$ (per unit, $V = 1$):

$$P_{ss} = \frac{V^2}{X_{total}} \sin(\delta_{max})$$

The total series reactance scales with line length $\ell$:

$$X_{total} = x \cdot \ell$$

where $x$ is the per-unit-length positive-sequence reactance (typically $0.4 \, \Omega/km$ for a 400 kV line).

Substituting:

$$P_{ss} = \frac{V^2 \sin(\delta_{max})}{x \cdot \ell}$$
