This shows that **maximum transferable power decreases as $1/\ell$** — halving the distance doubles the power transfer capability. This is the origin of the downward slope of the "canyon" curve for long lines.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Loading Constraint vs. Line Length

| Length Regime | Approx. Distance | Dominant Constraint | Mitigation Strategy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Short lines | $\ell < 80$ km | **Thermal limit** of conductor | Use larger conductor, parallel circuits |
| Medium lines | $80 \leq \ell < 300$ km | **Voltage drop / voltage stability** | Shunt reactors/capacitors, SVC, STATCOM |
| Long lines | $300 \leq \ell < 800$ km | **Steady-state angular stability** | Series capacitors, TCSC, phase shifters |
| Very long lines | $\ell \geq 800$ km | **Transient stability** | HVDC link, UPFC, fast fault clearing |
| Submarine cables | All lengths | **Cable charging current** (Ferranti) | HVDC is mandatory beyond $\sim 50$ km |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice the boundaries. The Indian power grid operates primarily in the **80–800 km** regime. The country's HVDC backbones (e.g., Raigarh–Pugalur 800 kV, $\sim 1800$ km) sit firmly in the "very long" regime where AC is infeasible and HVDC becomes the only technical option.

## 3.5 Mathematical Analysis — Effect of Series Compensation

If a series compensator (TCSC or fixed series capacitor) reduces effective line reactance by a factor $k_s$ (compensation ratio, $0 < k_s < 1$):

$$X_{eff} = X(1 - k_s)$$

The new maximum power is:

$$P_{max}^{comp} = \frac{V_S V_R}{X(1 - k_s)} = \frac{P_{max}^{uncomp}}{1 - k_s}$$

For $k_s = 0.5$ (50% compensation), power transfer capability **doubles**. For $k_s = 0.75$ (used in the Raipur–Pugalur 800 kV lines, equivalent to TCSC at lower voltages), it **quadruples**. This single equation is the mathematical justification for the entire FACTS industry.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Classical "Canyon" or "Staircase" Loadability Curve

The famous plot of maximum transmittable power versus transmission line length shows the four constraint regimes. We render this as a functional block-flow showing the dominance transitions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Maximum Line Loading vs Length] --> B[Region 1: Short Lines Below 80 km]
    A --> C[Region 2: Medium Lines 80 to 300 km]
    A --> D[Region 3: Long Lines 300 to 800 km]
    A --> E[Region 4: Very Long Lines Above 800 km]

    B --> B1[Constraint: Thermal Limit]
    B1 --> B2[Flat Plateau: Independent of Length]
    B2 --> B3[Mitigation: Larger Conductor or Parallel Circuit]

    C --> C1[Constraint: Voltage Drop Limit]
    C1 --> C2[Line Absorbs Reactive Power]
    C2 --> C3[Mitigation: Shunt Reactor or SVC]

    D --> D1[Constraint: Steady State Stability]
    D1 --> D2[Pmax Decreases as 1 divided by Length]
    D2 --> D3[Mitigation: Series Capacitor or TCSC]

    E --> E1[Constraint: Transient Stability]
    E1 --> E2[AC Line Practically Infeasible]
    E2 --> E3[Mitigation: HVDC Link or UPFC]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Constraint Hierarchy of an AC Line

```mermaid
graph TD
    Source[Source: P equals Vs Vr by X times sin delta] --> Const1
    Source --> Const2
    Source --> Const3
    Source --> Const4

    Const1[Constraint 1: Thermal Limit] --> L1[Independent of line length]
    Const1 --> M1[Action: Bundle conductors]

    Const2[Constraint 2: Voltage Stability] --> L2[Dominates at medium length]
    Const2 --> M2[Action: Shunt compensation SVC STATCOM]

    Const3[Constraint 3: Steady State Stability] --> L3[Delta max below 45 degrees]
    Const3 --> M3[Action: Series capacitor TCSC]

    Const4[Constraint 4: Transient Stability] --> L4[Equal Area Criterion]
    Const4 --> M4[Action: Fast breakers HVDC UPFC]

    M1 --> Final[Maximized AC Transfer]
    M2 --> Final
    M3 --> Final
    M4 --> Final
```

## 4.3 Power-Angle Operational Zones (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Paxis[Real Power P axis] --> Zone1
    Paxis --> Zone2
    Paxis --> Zone3

    Zone1[Zone A: P below 0.5 Pmax] --> Safe[Safe Steady State Operation]
    Zone1 --> Op1[Delta below 30 degrees]
    Op1 --> Dev1[Generator loads normal]

    Zone2[Zone B: P between 0.5 and 0.7 Pmax] --> Margin[Reduced Stability Margin]
    Zone2 --> Op2[Delta between 30 and 45 degrees]
    Op2 --> Dev2[Operator alert zone]

    Zone3[Zone C: P above 0.7 Pmax] --> Critical[Critical Transient Risk]
    Zone3 --> Op3[Delta above 45 degrees]
    Op3 --> Dev3[Approaching steady state limit]

    Safe --> Outcome1[Acceptable for all disturbances]
    Margin --> Outcome2[Acceptable for small disturbances only]
    Critical --> Outcome3[Unacceptable for moderate faults]
```

## 4.4 HVDC vs AC Constraint Comparison (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    AC[AC Line Constraints] --> AC1[Thermal Limit: Exists]
    AC --> AC2[Voltage Drop: Exists]
    AC --> AC3[Stability Limit: Dominant at long length]
    AC --> AC4[Reactive Power: Required]

    HVDC[HVDC Line Constraints] --> DC1[Thermal Limit: Exists]
    HVDC --> DC2[Voltage Drop: Minimal]
    HVDC --> DC3[Stability Limit: None]
    HVDC --> DC4[Reactive Power: None Required]

    AC1 --> Compare[Constraint Comparison]
    AC2 --> Compare
    AC3 --> Compare
    AC4 --> Compare
    DC1 --> Compare
    DC2 --> Compare
    DC3 --> Compare
    DC4 --> Compare
    Compare --> Result[HVDC Eliminates 3 of 4 AC Constraints]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams Instead of Physical Drawings:** The "staircase curve" requires a physical $P$ vs $\ell$ graph with non-linear envelopes. Mermaid is a flowchart tool, not a graphing tool. The block diagrams above capture the **functional logic** of constraint dominance transitions, which is what KTU examiners test in module questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Model Question Paper Pattern:** Two questions, 3 marks each, answer in 4–6 sentences.

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List and briefly explain the four major constraints that limit the maximum power transfer capability of an AC transmission line.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four major constraints on maximum transmission line loading are:

1. **Thermal Limit (1 mark):** The conductor can carry only a finite continuous current before its temperature causes excessive sag or annealing of the metal. This is independent of line length.

2. **Voltage Drop / Voltage Stability Limit (1 mark):** The receiving end voltage must remain within $\pm 5\%$ of nominal. Heavy loading causes the $PX$ voltage drop to push the system toward voltage collapse.

3. **Steady-State Angular Stability Limit (0.5 mark):** From $P = (V_S V_R / X)\sin\delta$, the practical operating angle must remain below $45^\circ$ to maintain a stability margin.

4. **Transient / Dynamic Stability Limit (0.5 mark):** Following a fault, rotor angles must not exceed the critical swing angle dictated by the Equal Area Criterion.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Define Surge Impedance Loading (SIL) of a transmission line. What is its significance in power system operation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Surge Impedance Loading is the natural loading of a transmission line at which it absorbs no net reactive power. It is given by $SIL = V_{LL}^2 / Z_s$, where $Z_s = \sqrt{L/C}$ is the surge impedance of the line. For a 400 kV line, $Z_s \approx 400 \, \Omega$, so $SIL \approx 400$ MW.

**Significance (1.5 marks):**
- At $P = SIL$, the line operates at its natural voltage profile (no Ferranti rise, no voltage drop).
- At $P < SIL$, the line generates reactive power (capacitive behavior).
- At $P > SIL$, the line absorbs reactive power (inductive behavior), requiring shunt compensation.
- SIL serves as the **benchmark** for classifying line loading as "lightly loaded" or "heavily loaded" and is the standard reference in loadability curves.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Two sub-parts, usually 7 marks each. Examiner provides internal choice between two full questions. Solutions below are exhaustive.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the power-angle equation of a lossless transmission line, starting from the basic phasor representation. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 400 kV, 50 Hz, three-phase transmission line is 400 km long. The line has a positive-sequence series reactance of $0.4 \, \Omega$/km per phase and negligible resistance. The sending end and receiving end voltages are both maintained at 400 kV. Calculate: **(i)** the total series reactance, **(ii)** the maximum theoretical power transfer, **(iii)** the practical steady-state power limit at $\delta = 30^\circ$, and **(iv)** the Surge Impedance Loading if $Z_s = 400 \, \Omega$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the assumptions.
- Lossless line ($R = 0$, $G = 0$).
- Purely inductive series reactance $X$ between sending and receiving buses.
- Both voltages are sinusoidal at the same frequency, displaced by angle $\delta$.
- System is in steady state.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Define the phasors.
Let $\vec{V_S} = V_S \angle \delta$ and $\vec{V_R} = V_R \angle 0^\circ$.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Write the current phasor.
$$\vec{I} = \frac{\vec{V_S} - \vec{V_R}}{jX} = \frac{V_S \angle \delta - V_R \angle 0^\circ}{jX}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Compute complex power at sending end.
$$\vec{S_S} = \vec{V_S} \vec{I}^*$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Apply Euler expansion and take the real part.
$$P = \frac{V_S V_R}{X} \sin(\delta)$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** State the final equation and its physical meaning.
This equation is the **fundamental power-angle characteristic**. Real power depends sinusoidally on $\delta$, peaks at $\delta = 90^\circ$, and is directly proportional to $V_S V_R$ and inversely proportional to $X$.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Given Data:**
- $V_{LL} = 400$ kV, $\ell = 400$ km, $x = 0.4 \, \Omega$/km, $R = 0$
- $V_S = V_R = 400$ kV, $Z_s = 400 \, \Omega$

**(i) Total series reactance (1.5 marks):**
$$X = x \cdot \ell = 0.4 \times 400 = 160 \, \Omega$$

**(ii) Maximum theoretical power (2 marks):**
$$P_{max} = \frac{V_S V_R}{X} = \frac{(400)(400)}{160} = 1000 \text{ MW}$$

**(iii) Practical limit at $\delta = 30^\circ$ (2 marks):**
$$P_{ss} = \frac{V_S V_R}{X} \sin(30^\circ) = 1000 \times 0.5 = 500 \text{ MW}$$

**(iv) Surge Impedance Loading (1.5 marks):**
$$SIL = \frac{V_{LL}^2}{Z_s} = \frac{(400)^2}{400} = 400 \text{ MW}$$

**Conclusion:** The line can theoretically transfer 1000 MW, but the practical steady-state limit at a $30^\circ$ angle is only **500 MW** — half the theoretical maximum. Operating below this margin protects the system from steady-state instability.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a suitable sketch, explain the various constraints that limit the maximum loading of a transmission line as a function of its length. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 250 km, 220 kV transmission line has a series reactance of $0.4 \, \Omega$/km and a Surge Impedance of $400 \, \Omega$. A series capacitor with compensation ratio $k_s = 0.4$ is installed. Calculate: **(i)** the uncompensated and compensated series reactance, **(ii)** the percentage increase in maximum power transfer capability, and **(iii)** the new SIL of the line. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Sketch description (2 marks):** A plot of $P_{max}$ (y-axis) versus line length $\ell$ (x-axis) showing four distinct regions:
- **Region 1** (Short line, $\ell < 80$ km): Flat plateau at thermal limit. Constant $P_{max}$.
- **Region 2** (Medium line, $80 < \ell < 300$ km): Voltage stability limit becomes dominant. Curve bends downward.
- **Region 3** (Long line, $300 < \ell < 800$ km): Steady-state stability limit dominates. Curve falls as $1/\ell$.
- **Region 4** (Very long line, $\ell > 800$ km): Transient stability limit. Steep decline — AC becomes impractical.

**Explanation (5 marks — 1.25 marks each):**

1. **Thermal Limit:** Set by conductor's maximum current. The $I^2R$ heating limits continuous current, and hence power. Independent of length.

2. **Voltage Drop Limit:** Voltage drop $\Delta V \approx PX/V_S$ increases with loading. Beyond a critical loading, the receiving end voltage collapses. Dominates in the 80–300 km range.

3. **Steady-State Stability:** From $P = (V^2 / X)\sin\delta$, since $X \propto \ell$, maximum power decreases as $1/\ell$. Operating at $\delta = 45^\circ$ gives $P_{ss} = 0.707 \cdot V^2 / X$.

4. **Transient Stability:** Equal Area Criterion limits the first swing of rotor angles after a fault. The maximum power that can be transferred without losing synchronism decreases sharply with length, beyond which HVDC becomes the only feasible option.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Given Data:**
- $\ell = 250$ km, $V_{LL} = 220$ kV, $x = 0.4 \, \Omega$/km, $Z_s = 400 \, \Omega$, $k_s = 0.4$

**(i) Uncompensated and compensated reactance (2.5 marks):**

Uncompensated:
$$X = x \cdot \ell = 0.4 \times 250 = 100 \, \Omega$$

Compensated (with $k_s = 0.4$):
$$X_{eff} = X(1 - k_s) = 100 \times (1 - 0.4) = 60 \, \Omega$$

**(ii) Percentage increase in maximum power (2.5 marks):**

$$P_{max}^{uncomp} = \frac{V^2}{X} = \frac{(220)^2}{100} = 484 \text{ MW}$$

$$P_{max}^{comp} = \frac{V^2}{X_{eff}} = \frac{(220)^2}{60} = 806.67 \text{ MW}$$

Percentage increase:
$$\% \text{ Increase} = \frac{806.67 - 484}{484} \times 100 = 66.67\%$$

**(iii) New SIL (2 marks):**

$$SIL = \frac{V_{LL}^2}{Z_s} = \frac{(220)^2}{400} = 121 \text{ MW}$$

**Note:** SIL is **unchanged** by series compensation because $Z_s$ is a property of the unaltered line geometry. Series compensation increases *transferable* power without changing the *natural* loading. This is a common conceptual point in KTU viva questions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical problems:** If line length is in km and reactance is in $\Omega$/km, students often write $X = 0.4$ without multiplying by $\ell$. **Loss: 1–2 marks per error.**
>
> 2. **Confusing $V_L$ (line voltage) and $V_{ph}$ (phase voltage):** The power equation uses phase voltage. SIL formula typically uses line voltage. Mixing them yields wrong answers by a factor of $\sqrt{3}$.
>
> 3. **Stating "thermal limit dominates for long lines":** This is the **opposite** of the truth. Thermal limit dominates for **short** lines. Stability dominates for long lines. Examiners specifically test this misconception.
>
> 4. **Omitting the steady-state stability margin:** Writing $P_{max} = V_S V_R / X$ without mentioning that real operation is at $0.5$ to $0.7 \cdot P_{max}$ loses 1 mark in any descriptive question.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to state assumptions in derivations:** In Part B derivations, "lossless," "purely reactive," and "sinusoidal steady state" are mandatory preamble statements. Omission costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> **Last-Module-Revision Summary — Memorize Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- [ ] **Power-angle equation:** $P = (V_S V_R / X) \sin\delta$ — memorize and understand its derivation cold.
- [ ] **Maximum power** occurs at $\delta = 90^\circ$: $P_{max} = V_S V_R / X$.
- [ ] **Practical operating angle** is $\leq 30^\circ$ to $45^\circ$, giving $P_{ss} \approx 0.5$ to $0.7 \cdot P_{max}$.
- [ ] **Four constraints:** Thermal (short lines) → Voltage (medium) → Steady-state (long) → Transient (very long).
- [ ] **Surge Impedance:** $Z_s = \sqrt{L/C}$.
- [ ] **SIL:** $SIL = V_{LL}^2 / Z_s$; typical 400 kV value is **400 MW**.
- [ ] **At $P < SIL$:** Line generates reactive power (Ferranti effect).
- [ ] **At $P > SIL$:** Line absorbs reactive power (needs shunt reactors).
- [ ] **Series compensation effect:** $X_{eff} = X(1-k_s)$ → Power increases by factor $1/(1-k_s)$.
- [ ] **HVDC advantage:** Eliminates $X$ and $\delta$ constraints — three of four AC constraints vanish.
- [ ] **Numerical memory anchors:**
  - 400 kV line, $Z_s = 400 \, \Omega$ → SIL = **400 MW**.
  - Standard line reactance: $x \approx 0.4 \, \Omega$/km.
  - 50% series compensation **doubles** the power transfer limit.
- [ ] **Equal Area Criterion:** $A_{accel} = A_{decel}$ — the foundation of transient stability.
- [ ] **Canyon Curve:** The graph of $P_{max}$ vs $\ell$ — the single most important visual for this topic.
- [ ] **Submarine cables:** Require HVDC beyond $\sim 50$ km because of charging current.
- [ ] **Real-world examples:** Talcher–Kolar, Raigarh–Pugalur, and Rihand–Delhi HVDC links in India all exist precisely because AC line constraints become binding at those distances.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Needs and Emergence of FACTS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET862 - HVDC AND FACTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Needs and Emergence of FACTS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flexible AC Transmission Systems (FACTS)** is a concept based on the use of power electronic controllers — both static and dynamic — to enhance controllability and increase power transfer capability of AC transmission systems. The term was formally introduced by **Dr. Narain G. Hingorani** of the **Electric Power Research Institute (EPRI)** in **1988**.

The IEEE definition (IEEE PES Task Force) describes FACTS as:

> "Alternating current transmission systems incorporating power electronic-based and other static controllers to enhance controllability and increase power transfer capability."

FACTS controllers are essentially **solid-state, high-power, fast-acting** devices that can regulate one or more of the three fundamental transmission parameters: **voltage magnitude, line impedance, and phase angle**.

### 1.2 The Three Control Parameters of AC Power Flow

The complex power transmitted between two buses is governed by:

$$P = \frac{V_1 \cdot V_2}{X} \sin(\delta)$$

$$Q = \frac{V_1^2}{X} - \frac{V_1 \cdot V_2}{X} \cos(\delta)$$

Where:
- $V_1, V_2$ = sending and receiving end voltage magnitudes
- $\delta = \delta_1 - \delta_2$ = power angle (phase difference)
- $X$ = series line reactance

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Levers of Control** in AC transmission:
> 1. **Voltage Magnitude** $\rightarrow$ Controlled by **Shunt** devices
> 2. **Line Impedance (X)** $\rightarrow$ Controlled by **Series** devices
> 3. **Phase Angle ($\delta$)** $\rightarrow$ Controlled by **Phase-shifting** devices

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Highway Traffic Analogy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Smart Highway**
>
> Imagine a national highway (the transmission line) connecting two cities. Traditionally, traffic (power) flows in one direction, and congestion is solved only by building new highways. **FACTS** is the equivalent of installing:
> - **Smart traffic signals** at on-ramps (Shunt compensation → controls voltage at the bus)
> - **Variable speed lanes** that open/close dynamically (Series compensation → controls line impedance)
> - **Rotating interchanges** that redirect traffic flow (Phase-shifting transformers → controls power angle)
>
> Just as modern traffic management can **double or triple the throughput** of an existing highway without laying a single meter of new asphalt, FACTS devices can **multiply the usable capacity** of an existing transmission corridor without new right-of-way.

### 1.4 Why "Emergence" of FACTS? — The Context

The FACTS concept emerged from a **convergence of crises** in the global power sector during the **1980s**:

| Driving Force | Description |
|---|---|
| **Rights-of-Way Crisis** | New transmission lines face enormous legal, environmental, and social barriers |
| **Deregulation** | Open-access transmission demanded by FERC Orders 888/889 (1996) required *controllable* power paths |
| **Stability Bottlenecks** | Many lines were limited by transient/dynamic stability rather than thermal capacity |
| **Environmental Concern** | Public opposition to new high-voltage corridors, EMFs, and visual pollution |
| **Technology Maturity** | High-power GTO, IGBT, IGCT switches and VSC topologies became commercially viable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Insight that Sparked FACTS**: Transmission lines are not thermally limited for most of their life — they are **dynamically limited**. By replacing slow, mechanical, discrete controls (tap changers, switched capacitors) with **fast, continuous, solid-state controls**, the *dynamic* limit can be raised close to the *thermal* limit, recovering vast amounts of stranded capacity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Transmission Constraint

A transmission line has three distinct thermal, voltage, and stability limits. FACTS addresses primarily the **stability** and **voltage** limits, not the thermal limit.

#### 2.1.1 Thermal Limit
Set by the maximum permissible conductor temperature (typically **75 °C – 85 °C** for ACSR conductors).

$$I_{thermal} = \sqrt{\frac{q_c(\theta_c - \theta_a)}{R_{ac}}}$$

Where $q_c$ is the convective heat loss, $\theta_c$ is max conductor temperature, $\theta_a$ is ambient, $R_{ac}$ is AC resistance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermal limit is a physical conductor limit — FACTS cannot change it.** It is the *upper bound* on all FACTS-enhanced operation.

#### 2.1.2 Voltage Drop / Voltage Stability Limit
For a radial line, the receiving-end voltage under load is:

$$V_2 = \sqrt{V_1^2 - 2PR - 2QX + (I \cdot X)^2}$$

Voltage collapse occurs when $dQ/dV = 0$ at the receiving bus — the **nose point** of the PV curve.

#### 2.1.3 Steady-State Stability Limit (Angle Stability)
The theoretical steady-state power limit of a 2-machine system is:

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_1 \cdot V_2}{X}$$

Actual operating point is held well below this (typically **30% – 50%** of $P_{max}$) for transient stability margin.

#### 2.1.4 Dynamic / Transient Stability Limit
Set by the **Equal Area Criterion** — the area under the P–δ curve available for deceleration must equal the accelerating area during the fault:

$$A_{accel} = \int_{\delta_0}^{\delta_c} (P_m - P_e^{fault}) \, d\delta$$
$$A_{decel} = \int_{\delta_c}^{\delta_{max}} (P_e^{post} - P_m) \, d\delta$$

### 2.2 Surge Impedance Loading (SIL) — The Natural Power

For a lossless line, the **Surge Impedance** is:

$$Z_s = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

And the **Surge Impedance Loading** is the natural power at which the line is self-regulating (no net reactive power exchange with the system):

$$\text{SIL} = \frac{V^2}{Z_s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At loading = SIL: the line **absorbs no reactive power** and voltage profile is flat.
> At loading > SIL: line **consumes** reactive power → voltage sag at receiving end.
> At loading < SIL: line **generates** reactive power (Ferranti effect) → voltage rise.

For a **400 kV, 50 Hz** typical line: $Z_s \approx 250 - 300\ \Omega$ → SIL ≈ **400 – 600 MW**.
Most 400 kV lines in India are loaded at **2–4 × SIL**, meaning they are **heavy reactive power consumers** — exactly the regime where shunt FACTS shines.

### 2.3 Compensation Principles

#### 2.3.1 Series Compensation
Inserting a capacitor $X_C$ in series with line reactance $X_L$ reduces the effective transfer reactance:

$$X_{eff} = X_L - X_C = X_L(1 - k_s)$$

Where $k_s = X_C / X_L$ is the **degree of series compensation** (typically 25% – 75%).

Effect on power transfer:

$$P_{max}^{comp} = \frac{V_1 V_2}{X_L(1 - k_s)} = \frac{P_{max}^{uncomp}}{1 - k_s}$$

#### 2.3.2 Shunt Compensation
A shunt-connected reactive element (inductor or capacitor) at the midpoint or receiving bus maintains voltage magnitude. The injected reactive current $\pm I_Q$ keeps $V_2$ constant.

#### 2.3.3 Phase-Angle Compensation
A phase-shifting transformer (or UPFC phase shifter) introduces a controlled quadrature voltage $\Delta V$ that modifies $\delta$:

$$P \approx \frac{V_1 V_2}{X} \sin(\delta + \phi)$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Real Power Transfer | $P = \frac{V_1 V_2}{X}\sin(\delta)$ | Per unit or MW |
| 2 | Reactive Power Transfer | $Q = \frac{V_1^2}{X} - \frac{V_1 V_2}{X}\cos(\delta)$ | MVAR |
| 3 | Steady-State Stability Limit | $P_{max} = \frac{V_1 V_2}{X}$ | MW |
| 4 | Surge Impedance | $Z_s = \sqrt{L/C}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 5 | Surge Impedance Loading | $\text{SIL} = V^2 / Z_s$ | MW (3-phase) |
| 6 | Compensated Reactance | $X_{eff} = X_L(1 - k_s)$ | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | Series-Compensated $P_{max}$ | $P_{max,c} = P_{max,u} / (1 - k_s)$ | MW |
| 8 | Line Voltage Drop | $\Delta V \approx \frac{PR + QX}{V}$ | Approximation |
| 9 | Characteristic Impedance of Cable | $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ | Same form as line |
| 10 | Thermal Limit Current | $I_{th} = \sqrt{(T_c - T_a) / (R_{ac} \cdot R_{th})}$ | A |

### 2.5 Why FACTS Over Conventional Controls?

| Conventional Method | Limitation | FACTS Replacement |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanically switched capacitors** | Step changes, slow, wear | SVC / STATCOM (continuous, ms response) |
| **Tap-changing transformers** | Slow (seconds), discrete steps | Thyristor-controlled transformer |
| **Fixed series capacitors** | Risk of sub-synchronous resonance | **TCSC** — adaptive, SSR-mitigating |
| **Phase-shifting transformers** | Slow, mechanical | **UPFC** — solid-state, dynamic |
| **Reactor switching** | Discrete | **TCR / TSR** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility**: A **TCSC** on a 400 kV line in India (e.g., the Raipur-Rourkela line) increased power transfer from ~250 MW to ~430 MW — a **70% increase** in the same corridor, at a cost far below building a new line.

### 2.6 The Hierarchical Classification (IEEE / CIGRE)

FACTS controllers are classified into **three generations** based on the switch technology:

1. **First Generation (Thyristor-based)**: SVC, TCSC, TCR, TSR — semi-controllable (latch-on devices)
2. **Second Generation (Self-commutated VSC)**: STATCOM, SSSC, UPFC, IPFC — fully controllable
3. **Third Generation (Interline / Multi-function)**: IUPFC, GUPFC, IPFC — coordinated multi-line controllers

This classification is examinable in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Power Transfer Equation from First Principles

**Statement**: For a lossless transmission line of reactance $X$ connecting two buses with voltages $V_1 \angle \delta_1$ and $V_2 \angle \delta_2$, derive the real and reactive power transfer.

#### Step 1: Model the System
A two-bus system with sending end $S$ (bus 1) and receiving end $R$ (bus 2), connected through a pure reactance $X$ (resistance neglected for simplicity).

#### Step 2: Express the Line Current
By Ohm's law applied to the series reactance:

$$I = \frac{V_1 \angle \delta_1 - V_2 \angle \delta_2}{jX}$$

#### Step 3: Conjugate the Current
To compute complex power $S_1 = V_1 I^*$, we need the complex conjugate of the current:

$$I^* = \frac{V_1 \angle -\delta_1 - V_2 \angle -\delta_2}{-jX} = \frac{V_2 \angle -\delta_2 - V_1 \angle -\delta_1}{jX}$$

Using $1/(-j) = j$:

$$I^* = j \cdot \frac{V_1 \angle -\delta_1 - V_2 \angle -\delta_2}{X}$$

#### Step 4: Compute Sending-End Complex Power
$$S_1 = V_1 \angle \delta_1 \cdot I^* = V_1 \angle \delta_1 \cdot j \cdot \frac{V_1 \angle -\delta_1 - V_2 \angle -\delta_2}{X}$$

$$S_1 = \frac{j}{X}\left[V_1^2 - V_1 V_2 \angle(\delta_1 - \delta_2)\right]$$

Letting $\delta = \delta_1 - \delta_2$:

$$S_1 = \frac{j}{X}\left[V_1^2 - V_1 V_2 (\cos\delta + j\sin\delta)\right]$$

$$S_1 = \frac{jV_1^2}{X} - \frac{jV_1 V_2 \cos\delta}{X} + \frac{V_1 V_2 \sin\delta}{X}$$

#### Step 5: Separate Real and Imaginary Parts
$$P_1 = \frac{V_1 V_2 \sin\delta}{X}$$

$$Q_1 = \frac{V_1^2 - V_1 V_2 \cos\delta}{X}$$

$$\boxed{\,P = \frac{V_1 V_2}{X} \sin\delta, \qquad Q = \frac{V_1^2}{X} - \frac{V_1 V_2}{X}\cos\delta\,}$$

This is the **fundamental AC transmission equation** and forms the **conceptual basis for every FACTS controller**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Power Transfer and Steady-State Stability Margin

**Statement**: Show that the maximum transferable power occurs at $\delta = 90°$ for a lossless line.

#### Step 1: Differentiate the Power-Angle Equation

$$P(\delta) = \frac{V_1 V_2}{X} \sin\delta$$

$$\frac{dP}{d\delta} = \frac{V_1 V_2}{X} \cos\delta$$

#### Step 2: Set the Derivative to Zero

$$\frac{dP}{d\delta} = 0 \Rightarrow \cos\delta = 0 \Rightarrow \delta = 90°$$

#### Step 3: Confirm Maximum via Second Derivative

$$\frac{d^2P}{d\delta^2} = -\frac{V_1 V_2}{X} \sin\delta$$

At $\delta = 90°$: $\frac{d^2P}{d\delta^2} = -\frac{V_1 V_2}{X} < 0$ → confirms maximum.

#### Step 4: Substitute Back to Obtain $P_{max}$

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_1 V_2}{X} \sin(90°) = \frac{V_1 V_2}{X}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_{max} = \frac{V_1 V_2}{X}\,}$$

#### Step 5: Steady-State Stability Margin (in %)

$$\text{Stability Margin} = \frac{P_{max} - P_{op}}{P_{max}} \times 100\%$$

A typical operational margin is **30% – 40%** to account for dynamic and transient excursions.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Surge Impedance Loading (SIL) of a Lossless Line

**Statement**: Show that a lossless line loaded at SIL exhibits a flat voltage profile with no net reactive power exchange.

#### Step 1: Telegrapher's Equations (Lossless)
For a lossless line, $R = 0$ and $G = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = -L \frac{\partial I}{\partial t}, \qquad \frac{\partial I}{\partial x} = -C \frac{\partial V}{\partial t}$$

#### Step 2: Wave Equation in Phasor Form
Assuming sinusoidal steady state with propagation constant $\beta$:

$$V(x) = V^+ e^{-j\beta x} + V^- e^{+j\beta x}$$

$$I(x) = \frac{1}{Z_c}\left[V^+ e^{-j\beta x} - V^- e^{+j\beta x}\right]$$

Where the **characteristic impedance** is:

$$Z_c = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

#### Step 3: Terminated Line Condition
For an infinitely long line or a finite line terminated in $Z_c$, the reflected wave $V^- = 0$. The line behaves as a **pure resistance** $Z_c$.

#### Step 4: Power Delivered to a Matched Termination
For a 3-phase line-to-line voltage $V_{LL}$:

$$\text{SIL} = \frac{V_{LL}^2}{Z_c}$$

#### Step 5: Reactive Power Balance
Since the termination is purely resistive, no reactive power is drawn, and the distributed capacitance of the line exactly supplies the reactive needs of the distributed inductance at every point → **flat voltage profile**.

$$\boxed{\,\text{SIL} = \frac{V_{LL}^2}{Z_c} = \frac{V_{LL}^2}{\sqrt{L/C}}\,}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Compensation on $P_{max}$

| Compensation Type | Effective Quantity Modified | $P_{max}$ after compensation | Increase factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **None** | — | $P_{max} = V_1 V_2 / X$ | 1.0 |
| **Series $X_C = k_s X$** | $X_{eff} = (1-k_s)X$ | $P_{max} = \dfrac{V_1 V_2}{X(1-k_s)}$ | $\dfrac{1}{1-k_s}$ |
| **Shunt at midpoint** (fixed) | $V_1$ or $V_2$ held constant | $P_{max} \approx 2 \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{X}$ (for $V_1 = V_2 = V$) | Up to 2.0 |
| **Phase shifter** (angle $\phi$) | $\delta \rightarrow \delta + \phi$ | $P_{max} = \dfrac{V_1 V_2}{X}\sin(\delta+\phi)$ | Depends on $\phi$ |
| **Combined Series + Shunt** (UPFC) | All three | Multiplicative | $> 2.0$ achievable |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU**: A 50% series compensation ($k_s = 0.5$) **doubles** the maximum power transfer on a line — this is why **TCSC** and **fixed series capacitors** are the most economically attractive FACTS family.

### 3.5 Derivation 4: Effect of Series Compensation on Transmission Angle

**Statement**: For a given power $P_0$ transmitted, find the new power angle $\delta_c$ after series compensation of degree $k_s$.

#### Step 1: Uncompensated Power-Angle Relationship

$$P_0 = \frac{V_1 V_2}{X} \sin\delta_0 \Rightarrow \sin\delta_0 = \frac{P_0 X}{V_1 V_2}$$

#### Step 2: Compensated Power-Angle Relationship

$$P_0 = \frac{V_1 V_2}{X(1-k_s)} \sin\delta_c \Rightarrow \sin\delta_c = \frac{P_0 X (1-k_s)}{V_1 V_2}$$

#### Step 3: Ratio of Sines

$$\frac{\sin\delta_c}{\sin\delta_0} = 1 - k_s$$

$$\boxed{\,\sin\delta_c = (1 - k_s)\sin\delta_0\,}$$

**Physical meaning**: For the *same* transmitted power, the new angle is **smaller**, giving a **larger stability margin**:

$$\Delta\text{Margin} = \sin\delta_c - \sin\delta_0 = -k_s \sin\delta_0$$

The stability margin increases by a factor of $k_s \sin\delta_0$ (when $\delta_0 < 90°$).
