## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Define FACTS. List any four major drivers for the emergence of FACTS technology.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark)**: Flexible AC Transmission Systems (FACTS) refer to a family of power-electronic-based controllers used to enhance or provide controllability of AC transmission systems, formally defined by IEEE as *"alternating current transmission systems incorporating power electronic-based and other static controllers to enhance controllability and increase power transfer capability."*

**Drivers (2 Marks)**:

1. **Rights-of-Way Crisis**: Difficulty in obtaining new transmission corridors due to environmental, legal, and social opposition.
2. **Stability Bottlenecks**: Many lines are dynamically limited well below their thermal limit, leaving stranded capacity.
3. **Deregulation & Open Access**: Restructured markets require controllable, dispatchable transmission paths.
4. **Maturity of High-Power Semiconductors**: Availability of GTO, IGBT, IGCT made VSC-based FACTS commercially viable.

*(Other acceptable drivers: reactive power management, power quality demands, environmental concerns, increasing transmission distances.)*

---

**Q2. State the fundamental equation governing real power transfer in an AC line. Identify the three controllable parameters that FACTS devices manipulate.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Equation (1.5 Marks):** For a lossless line of reactance $X$ between two buses with voltages $V_1$ and $V_2$ separated by power angle $\delta$:

$$P = \frac{V_1 \cdot V_2}{X}\sin\delta$$

**Three Controllable Parameters (1.5 Marks):**

| # | Parameter | FACTS Device Family |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Voltage Magnitude ($V_1$ or $V_2$) | Shunt devices — SVC, STATCOM |
| 2 | Line Impedance ($X$) | Series devices — TCSC, SSSC |
| 3 | Phase Angle ($\delta$) | Phase shifters — UPFC, PST |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]`

**(a)** Derive the power-angle equation for a two-bus system connected by a lossless transmission line, clearly stating all assumptions. Explain how the three fundamental transmission parameters can be controlled. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 220 kV, 50 Hz, 200 km three-phase transmission line has per-phase parameters: $r = 0.05\ \Omega/\text{km}$, $x = 0.4\ \Omega/\text{km}$, $y = 2.8 \times 10^{-6}$ S/km. The sending-end voltage is 240 kV and receiving-end voltage is 215 kV. Compute (using the approximate $\pi$-model): (i) Sending-end current, (ii) Real and reactive power at sending end, (iii) Power angle, and (iv) Surge Impedance Loading. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Assumptions:**
- Lossless line (R = 0) for the simplified derivation.
- Sending-end and receiving-end voltages are sinusoidal at the same frequency.
- Shunt admittance of the line is initially neglected.
- Both voltages are balanced, positive-sequence, three-phase quantities.

**Step 1**: Let $V_1 = V_1 \angle \delta_1$ and $V_2 = V_2 \angle \delta_2$, with $\delta = \delta_1 - \delta_2$. The line current is:

$$I = \frac{V_1 \angle \delta_1 - V_2 \angle \delta_2}{jX}$$

**Step 2**: Complex power at sending end $S_1 = V_1 I^*$. Substituting and simplifying (full algebra as in Section 3.1 above):

$$P_1 = \frac{V_1 V_2}{X}\sin\delta, \quad Q_1 = \frac{V_1^2 - V_1 V_2 \cos\delta}{X}$$

**Step 3**: The three parameters controllable by FACTS are $V_1$, $V_2$, and $X$ (or $\delta$ via a phase-shifting device). Voltage is controlled by shunt compensation (SVC/STATCOM); reactance by series compensation (TCSC/SSSC); phase angle by phase-shifting controllers (UPFC/PST).

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Total Line Parameters**

$$R = 0.05 \times 200 = 10\ \Omega$$
$$X = 0.4 \times 200 = 80\ \Omega$$
$$Y = j 2.8 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 = j 5.6 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{S}$$

**Step 2: Approximate π-Model**
Series impedance: $Z = R + jX = 10 + j80\ \Omega$

Shunt admittance is split equally: $Y/2 = j 2.8 \times 10^{-4}$ S at each end.

**Step 3: Receiving-End Quantities (per phase, line-to-neutral)**

$$V_2 = \frac{215 \angle 0°}{\sqrt{3}} = 124.13 \angle 0°\ \text{kV}$$

**Step 4: Current through Series Branch**

$$I_{series} = \frac{V_S - V_R}{Z}$$

Sending end line-to-neutral: $V_S = 240 / \sqrt{3} = 138.56$ kV

$$I_{series} = \frac{138.56 \angle \delta_1 - 124.13 \angle 0°}{10 + j80}$$

Taking the receiving-end voltage as reference and computing the angle iteratively or using the standard ABCD parameters:

$$I_R = \frac{V_S}{Z} - V_R\left(\frac{1}{Z} + \frac{Y}{2}\right)$$

$$I_R = \frac{138564 \angle \delta_1}{10+j80} - 124130 \left(\frac{1}{10+j80} + j2.8\times 10^{-4}\right)$$

After computing (with $\delta_1 \approx 7.5°$):

$$I_R \approx 175.6 \angle -28.4°\ \text{A (per phase)}$$

**Step 5: Sending-End Current**

$$I_S = I_R + V_S \cdot \frac{Y}{2} = 175.6 \angle -28.4° + 138564 \angle 7.5° \times j 2.8 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$I_S \approx 174.2 \angle -27.9°\ \text{A (per phase)}$$

**Step 6: Sending-End Real and Reactive Power**

$$S_S = 3 \times V_S \times I_S^* = 3 \times 138564 \times 174.2 \angle (7.5° - (-27.9°))$$

$$S_S \approx 72.4 \angle 35.4°\ \text{MVA}$$

$$P_S \approx 59.0\ \text{MW}, \quad Q_S \approx 42.0\ \text{MVAR}$$

**Step 7: Power Angle**

$$\delta_1 = 7.5° \approx \text{power angle (rounded)}$$

**Step 8: Surge Impedance Loading**

$$Z_c = \sqrt{\frac{X}{B}} = \sqrt{\frac{80}{5.6 \times 10^{-4}}} \approx 378\ \Omega$$

$$\text{SIL} = \frac{V_{LL}^2}{Z_c} = \frac{(220 \times 10^3)^2}{378} \approx 128\ \text{MW}$$

The line is lightly loaded (P < SIL), so the line generates reactive power.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]`

**(a)** Explain with neat block diagrams the three control philosophies used in FACTS controllers. Discuss how the choice of control philosophy depends on the system objective. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 400 kV, 50 Hz, 250 km transmission line has $L = 1.0$ mH/km and $C = 11$ nF/km. Calculate: (i) Surge impedance, (ii) Surge Impedance Loading, (iii) Velocity of propagation, and (iv) Wavelength. Comment on whether this line is operating above or below SIL when loaded to 800 MW. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The three control philosophies of FACTS controllers are:

1. **Shunt Compensation** — A current-source or voltage-source connected in parallel (shunt) with the transmission line. The injected reactive current maintains voltage magnitude. Used for voltage regulation, reactive power support, and oscillation damping. **Devices**: SVC, STATCOM.

2. **Series Compensation** — A voltage source or impedance inserted in series with the line. The injected voltage modifies the effective line impedance. Used for power flow control, stability enhancement, and SSR mitigation. **Devices**: TCSC, SSSC, Fixed Series Capacitor.

3. **Combined Series-Shunt (Phase Angle / Unified Power Flow Controller)** — A combination of series and shunt voltage sources sharing a common DC link, capable of independently controlling voltage, impedance, and phase angle. Used for the most demanding applications — full power flow management. **Devices**: UPFC, IPFC.

**Block Diagrams**: Refer to Section 4.4 above. (In an exam, sketch: a transmission line with a shunt-connected compensator on the receiving end, a series-connected compensator in the line, and a UPFC having both series and shunt branches sharing a DC link capacitor.)

**System-Objective Dependence**:

- If the objective is **voltage support** at a weak bus → use **shunt** compensation.
- If the objective is to **redistribute power flow** between parallel corridors → use **series** compensation.
- If the objective is **simultaneous voltage and power flow control** in a stressed network → use **UPFC** (most powerful but costliest).

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Total Line Parameters**

$$L_{total} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 250 = 0.25\ \text{H}$$
$$C_{total} = 11 \times 10^{-9} \times 250 = 2.75 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{F}$$

**Step 2: Surge Impedance**

$$Z_c = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{11 \times 10^{-9}}} = \sqrt{9.09 \times 10^{4}} \approx 301.5\ \Omega$$

**Step 3: Surge Impedance Loading (3-phase, line-to-line)**

$$\text{SIL} = \frac{V_{LL}^2}{Z_c} = \frac{(400 \times 10^3)^2}{301.5} \approx 530.7\ \text{MW}$$

**Step 4: Velocity of Propagation**

$$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 11 \times 10^{-9}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.1 \times 10^{-11}}}$$

$$v = \frac{1}{3.317 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 3.015 \times 10^{5}\ \text{km/s}$$

**Step 5: Wavelength at 50 Hz**

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f} = \frac{3.015 \times 10^{5}}{50} = 6030\ \text{km}$$

**Step 6: Comment on Loading**
Given loading = 800 MW; SIL = 530.7 MW.
Since 800 MW > SIL, **the line is operating ABOVE SIL** → it consumes net reactive power → voltage drop along the line will be significant → **shunt compensation is needed** at the receiving end.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert line-to-line to line-to-neutral** when computing per-phase current or power. This is the single most common mistake — losing 1 to 2 marks.
> 2. **Sign of reactive power**: A line operating *above* SIL absorbs Q (Q is positive into the line); a line below SIL generates Q. Students often swap the sign.
> 3. **Not specifying the per-phase or three-phase nature** of $Z_c$ and SIL in the answer.
> 4. **Confusing transient stability** (equal-area criterion) with **steady-state stability** (P-δ curve). FACTS improves *both* but through different mechanisms.
> 5. **In derivation questions**, skipping the assumption stage or the complex conjugate step loses a mark even if the final answer is correct.
> 6. **Units mismatch**: SIL must be in MW (not W) and $Z_c$ in ohms — verify dimensional consistency.
> 7. **Confusing $X_L$ (inductive reactance)** with **$\omega L$** in writing $Z_c = \sqrt{L/C}$ — the formula uses $L$ and $C$ in their SI base units, **not** $X_L$ and $B_C$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Needs and Emergence of FACTS"**
>
> ✅ **FACTS** = Flexible AC Transmission Systems — concept by **Hingorani (EPRI, 1988)**.
>
> ✅ The **fundamental AC power equation** is $P = \dfrac{V_1 V_2}{X}\sin\delta$; the three controllable parameters are **$V$, $X$, and $\delta$**.
>
> ✅ Transmission lines are limited by **four limits**: thermal, voltage drop, steady-state stability, and transient stability. FACTS primarily addresses the **stability and voltage** limits.
>
> ✅ **Surge Impedance Loading (SIL)** is the natural loading of a line: $\text{SIL} = V^2 / Z_c$, where $Z_c = \sqrt{L/C}$. Above SIL → line absorbs Q; below SIL → line generates Q (Ferranti region).
>
> ✅ **Series compensation** of degree $k_s$ **doubles** $P_{max}$ at $k_s = 0.5$; **shunt compensation** keeps voltage flat; **phase-shifting** changes the effective angle.
>
> ✅ FACTS generation: **1st gen = thyristor (SVC, TCSC)**; **2nd gen = VSC (STATCOM, SSSC, UPFC)**; **3rd gen = multi-line (IPFC, GUPFC)**.
>
> ✅ Conventional controls (tap changers, switched capacitors) are **slow, mechanical, and discrete**; FACTS is **fast (ms), static, and continuous**.
>
> ✅ Key driving forces: **rights-of-way crisis, deregulation, stability bottlenecks, environmental concerns, VSC technology maturity**.
>
> ✅ Velocity of propagation on overhead line: $v \approx 3 \times 10^5$ km/s; wavelength at 50 Hz $\approx 6000$ km.
>
> ✅ For KTU: Always state **assumptions** in derivations; always **convert L-L to L-N** for per-phase calculations; always **label axes** of any P-δ diagram.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of FACTS Controllers - Advantages and Disadvantages, Transmission Line Compensation - Uncompensated Line, Shunt Compensation, Series Compensation, Phase Angle Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET862 - HVDC AND FACTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of FACTS Controllers - Advantages and Disadvantages, Transmission Line Compensation - Uncompensated Line, Shunt Compensation, Series Compensation, Phase Angle Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are FACTS Controllers?

A **FACTS (Flexible AC Transmission System) Controller** is a power electronics-based system, plus associated static equipment, that provides control of one or more AC transmission system parameters to enhance controllability and increase power transfer capability of the system. The IEEE defines FACTS as: *"Alternating current transmission systems incorporating power-electronics-based and other static controllers to enhance controllability, increase power transfer capability, and improve stability of the system."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Directly Exam-Aligned):**
> FACTS controllers are static power-electronic devices that can control voltage, impedance, or phase angle of an AC transmission line dynamically. They are classified based on their **connection topology** into **Series, Shunt, and Combined (Series-Series / Shunt-Series) Controllers**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Garden Hose Model:**
> Imagine transmitting power through a transmission line is like pushing water through a long, narrow garden hose.
> - **Series Compensation** = Inserting a *narrow nozzle booster* along the hose — it pushes harder by reducing the flow resistance (reactance).
> - **Shunt Compensation** = Adding a *side reservoir tank* that supplies or absorbs water (reactive power) at the midpoint, controlling the pressure (voltage).
> - **Phase Angle Control** = Twisting the hose outlet direction — the water is pushed at an angle, redirecting the flow.
> Without compensation, the hose (line) cannot carry its maximum theoretical flow (SIL) efficiently.

### 1.3 Classification of FACTS Controllers

Based on **connection type**, FACTS controllers are classified into four main families:

| Category | Function | Examples | 
|---|---|---|
| **Series Controllers** | Inject voltage in series with the line | SSSC, TCSC, TCSR, TSC |
| **Shunt Controllers** | Inject current into the line at a node | SVC, STATCOM |
| **Combined Series-Series** | Equivalent of two series controllers | IPFC |
| **Combined Shunt-Series** | Unified operation of shunt + series | UPFC, Phasor Measurement based |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction — LCC vs VSC Based FACTS:**
> The first-generation FACTS (SVC, TCSC) use **Thyristor (LCC-based)** switching. Modern FACTS (STATCOM, SSSC, UPFC) use **VSC (Voltage Source Converter)** technology with **GTO/IGBT/IGCT** switches, offering faster response and independent reactive power control.

### 1.4 Advantages and Disadvantages of FACTS Controllers

**Advantages:**
- Increase power transfer capability (up to **Surge Impedance Loading** limit).
- Improve transient and dynamic stability.
- Damp power oscillations.
- Regulate voltage profile along the line.
- Reduce loop flows and optimize power dispatch.
- Defer investment on new transmission lines.

**Disadvantages:**
- High initial capital cost.
- Complexity in control algorithms.
- Generation of **harmonics** (mitigated by filters).
- Possible **subsynchronous resonance** issues (especially with TCSC).
- Requires trained personnel for operation and maintenance.

### 1.5 Transmission Line Compensation — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compensation** means the use of reactive elements (inductors/capacitors) or FACTS devices to **neutralize part of the line's natural reactance**, thereby improving voltage profile, increasing power transfer, and ensuring system stability.

The four major compensation strategies studied in KTU Module 2 are:
1. **Uncompensated Line** (baseline — no compensation)
2. **Shunt Compensation**
3. **Series Compensation**
4. **Phase Angle Control**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Uncompensated Transmission Line

For a short transmission line represented by its **series reactance** $X_L$ connecting two voltages $\vec{V_S}$ (sending end) and $\vec{V_R}$ (receiving end) with a phase angle $\delta$ between them:

**Sending End Voltage Phasor:**
$$V_S \angle \delta = V_R \angle 0 + jX_L \cdot I$$

**Active Power Transfer (Uncompensated):**
$$P = \frac{V_S V_R}{X_L} \sin \delta$$

**Reactive Power Transfer (Uncompensated):**
$$Q = \frac{V_S^2 - V_S V_R \cos \delta}{X_L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:**
> - Maximum power transfer occurs at $\delta = 90°$ → $P_{max} = V_S V_R / X_L$. This is the **steady-state stability limit**.
> - Without compensation, $\delta$ rises linearly with load, leading to a steep angle increase and risk of instability.
> - The line has a natural **Surge Impedance Loading (SIL)** equal to $V^2 / Z_S$, where $Z_S$ is the surge impedance. Below SIL, the line generates vars; above SIL, it absorbs vars.

### 2.2 Shunt Compensation

A shunt-connected **variable reactive source** (SVC/STATCOM) is placed at the **midpoint** (or any bus) of the line. It injects/absorbs reactive current $I_q$ such that the midpoint voltage $V_M$ is regulated.

**Midpoint Shunt Compensation Power Equation:**
$$P = \frac{V^2}{X_L / 2} \sin \frac{\delta}{2} = \frac{2V^2}{X_L} \sin \frac{\delta}{2}$$

When compared to uncompensated:
$$P_{shunt,max} = 2 \times P_{uncomp,max} \quad \text{(at } \delta/2 = 90° \text{, i.e., } \delta = 180°)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examinations:**
> Shunt compensation at the **midpoint** doubles the transient stability margin. The reactive current injected is $I_q = I \sin\phi$ where $\phi$ is the power factor angle.

**Voltage Support Equation (Reactive Power Injected):**
$$\Delta V \approx \frac{Q_{shunt} \cdot X_L}{2V}$$

### 2.3 Series Compensation

A **series capacitor** of reactance $X_C$ is inserted in series with the line. The effective line reactance becomes:

$$X_{eff} = X_L - X_C = X_L(1 - k)$$

where $k$ is the **degree of series compensation**:
$$k = \frac{X_C}{X_L}, \quad 0 \le k \le 1$$

**Power Transfer with Series Compensation:**
$$P_{series} = \frac{V_S V_R}{X_L (1 - k)} \sin \delta$$

**Compensation Factor Effect:**
- At $k = 0.5$ (50% compensation) → Power transfer doubles for the same $\delta$.
- At $k = 0.7$ (70% compensation — practical limit to avoid SSR).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Series Compensation Increases Power Transfer:**
> The capacitor compensates part of the line's inductive reactance. This reduces the effective impedance, allowing more current to flow for the same voltage difference, hence more active power transfer. It also reduces the voltage drop across the line.

**Series Compensation and Voltage Regulation:**
$$\Delta V \approx \frac{P X_L (1 - k)}{V} \quad \text{(reduced as } k \text{ increases)}$$

### 2.4 Phase Angle Control

A **phase-shifting transformer (PST)** or **UPFC** adjusts the phase angle between sending and receiving ends by $\sigma$ (the phase shift angle), while keeping voltage magnitudes constant.

**Power Transfer Equation with Phase Angle Control:**
$$P_{PAC} = \frac{V_S V_R}{X_L} \sin(\delta - \sigma)$$

For **loop flow control** or **directional power flow**:
- If $\sigma$ is positive, power flows from bus S to R.
- If $\sigma$ is negative, power flow is reduced or reversed.

**Phase Shifter Rating:**
$$S_{PST} = V \cdot I \cdot 2\sin(\sigma/2)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategic Advantage of Phase Angle Control:**
> It enables controlled power routing in meshed networks, mitigates loop flows, and supports **inter-area oscillation damping** — a high-value KTU exam topic.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uncompensated Real Power | $P = \frac{V_S V_R}{X_L}\sin\delta$ | $\delta$ = torque angle | MW |
| 2 | Surge Impedance Loading | $SIL = \frac{V_{LL}^2}{Z_S}$ | $Z_S = \sqrt{L/C}$ | MW |
| 3 | Effective Line Reactance | $X_{eff} = X_L (1 - k)$ | $k = X_C/X_L$ | $\Omega$ |
| 4 | Series Compensated Power | $P_{series} = \frac{V_S V_R}{X_L(1-k)}\sin\delta$ | $0 \le k \le 1$ | MW |
| 5 | Midpoint Shunt Comp. Power | $P_{shunt} = \frac{2V^2}{X_L}\sin(\delta/2)$ | Doubles $P_{max}$ | MW |
| 6 | Phase Angle Power | $P_{PAC} = \frac{V_S V_R}{X_L}\sin(\delta - \sigma)$ | $\sigma$ = phase shift | MW |
| 7 | Reactive Power Compensation | $Q_C = \omega C V^2$ | Capacitor var generation | MVAR |
| 8 | Voltage Regulation | $\%VR = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$ | Quoted in % | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> These equations form the foundation of **load flow analysis**, **transient stability studies**, and **P-V and Q-V curve plotting** in power system software like **PSCAD, ETAP, PSS/E, and DIgSILENT PowerFactory**. FACTS devices are modeled using these exact expressions in simulation studies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Uncompensated Power Transfer Equation

**Starting Assumption:** A short transmission line modeled as a pure reactance $X_L$ between two voltage sources $V_S \angle \delta$ (sending) and $V_R \angle 0$ (receiving).

**Step 1: Express the line current**

The current flowing from sending end to receiving end is given by Ohm's law for AC circuits:

$$I = \frac{V_S \angle \delta - V_R \angle 0}{j X_L}$$

**Step 2: Convert to rectangular form using Euler's expansion**

Writing $V_S = V_S (\cos\delta + j\sin\delta)$ and $V_R = V_R$ (real), the numerator becomes:

$$V_S \cos\delta - V_R + j V_S \sin\delta$$

Dividing by $jX_L$ (which is equivalent to multiplying by $-j/X_L$):

$$I = \frac{1}{X_L}\left[-j(V_S\cos\delta - V_R) + V_S \sin\delta\right]$$

$$I = \frac{V_S \sin\delta}{X_L} - j\frac{V_S \cos\delta - V_R}{X_L}$$

**Step 3: Identify real and imaginary components of current**

$$I_{real} = \frac{V_S \sin\delta}{X_L}, \quad I_{imag} = \frac{V_S\cos\delta - V_R}{X_L}$$

**Step 4: Compute complex power at sending end**

The complex power at the sending end is $\vec{S} = \vec{V_S} \cdot \vec{I}^*$. Multiplying $V_S \angle \delta$ by the conjugate of the current:

$$S = V_S \angle \delta \cdot I^* = V_S \angle \delta \cdot \left[\frac{V_S \sin\delta}{X_L} + j\frac{V_S \cos\delta - V_R}{X_L}\right]$$

**Step 5: Multiply and separate real and imaginary parts**

After expansion and simplification (using $V_S \angle \delta = V_S\cos\delta + jV_S\sin\delta$):

$$P = \frac{V_S V_R}{X_L} \sin\delta$$

$$Q = \frac{V_S^2 - V_S V_R \cos\delta}{X_L}$$

**Conclusion:** This is the **fundamental power-angle equation** of any AC transmission system. All FACTS controller equations are derived as modifications of this base form.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Midpoint Shunt Compensation Power

**Step 1: Conceptual setup**

Place a controllable reactive source $Q_{sh}$ at the midpoint $M$ of the line of total reactance $X_L$. The line is split into two halves of reactance $X_L/2$ each.

**Step 2: Voltage at midpoint**

The current at the midpoint is determined by the load demand. The midpoint voltage $V_M$ is sustained by reactive injection. With perfect regulation, $V_M = V_S = V_R = V$.

**Step 3: Power flowing through the two half-segments**

The active power through the first half-line (from $V_S$ to $V_M$) is:

$$P = \frac{V \cdot V}{X_L/2} \sin(\delta/2) = \frac{2V^2}{X_L}\sin(\delta/2)$$

This same power flows to the receiving end (no active power loss in pure reactance model).

**Step 4: Maximum power condition**

For maximum power transfer: $\sin(\delta/2) = 1 \Rightarrow \delta = 180°$.

$$P_{shunt,max} = \frac{2V^2}{X_L} = 2 \times \frac{V^2}{X_L}$$

**Step 5: Comparison with uncompensated case**

Without compensation, the maximum transferable power (at $\delta = 90°$) is:

$$P_{uncomp,max} = \frac{V^2}{X_L}$$

**Conclusion:** Midpoint shunt compensation **doubles the transient stability limit** by allowing the rotor angle to swing up to 180° instead of 90° before instability occurs.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Series Compensation Effective Reactance

**Step 1: Original line model**

A transmission line of series reactance $X_L$ in series with the line resistance (negligible in this analysis). The impedance is:

$$Z_{line} = jX_L$$

**Step 2: Insert series capacitor**

Insert a capacitor of reactance $X_C = 1/(\omega C)$ in series:

$$Z_{total} = jX_L + (-jX_C) = j(X_L - X_C)$$

**Step 3: Define compensation factor**

$$k = \frac{X_C}{X_L}$$

Therefore:
$$X_{eff} = X_L(1 - k)$$

**Step 4: Power transfer with series compensation**

Substituting $X_{eff}$ into the basic power transfer equation:

$$P_{series} = \frac{V_S V_R}{X_{eff}} \sin\delta = \frac{V_S V_R}{X_L(1 - k)} \sin\delta$$

**Step 5: Improvement ratio**

The ratio of compensated to uncompensated power transfer at the same $\delta$ is:

$$\frac{P_{series}}{P_{uncomp}} = \frac{1}{1 - k}$$

For $k = 0.5$, this gives a factor of **2.0** (100% increase). For $k = 0.7$, the factor is **3.33** (233% increase).

**Step 6: Practical limit consideration**

For $k \ge 0.7$, the system becomes susceptible to **Subsynchronous Resonance (SSR)**. Practical compensation levels are limited to 30-70% depending on system studies.

---

### 3.4 Derivation — Phase Angle Control Power

**Step 1: Conceptual setup**

A phase-shifting transformer introduces a complex voltage $V_{ps} \angle \sigma$ in series. The effective phase difference between sending and receiving ends becomes $(\delta - \sigma)$.

**Step 2: Modified power equation**

$$P_{PAC} = \frac{V_S V_R}{X_L} \sin(\delta - \sigma)$$

**Step 3: Derivative for stability analysis**

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial \delta} = \frac{V_S V_R}{X_L} \cos(\delta - \sigma)$$

This is the **synchronizing torque coefficient**. A larger $\sigma$ allows the system to operate at a smaller effective $\delta$, providing a margin for stability enhancement.

**Step 4: Phase shifter control logic**

- For $\sigma > 0$ → effective angle reduced → increased power flow.
- For $\sigma < 0$ → effective angle increased → reduced power flow.

**Step 5: MVA rating of phase shifter**

The series-injected voltage magnitude is $2V\sin(\sigma/2)$. The current through the line is $I = P/(V\cos\phi)$. Therefore:

$$S_{PST} = V_{injected} \cdot I = 2V\sin(\sigma/2) \cdot \frac{P}{V\cos\phi} = \frac{2P\sin(\sigma/2)}{\cos\phi}$$

---

### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix of Compensation Techniques

| Parameter | Uncompensated | Shunt Compensated | Series Compensated | Phase Angle |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Steady-state stability limit ($P_{max}$) | $V^2 / X_L$ at $\delta = 90°$ | $2V^2 / X_L$ at $\delta = 180°$ | $\frac{V^2}{X_L(1-k)}$ at $\delta = 90°$ | $V^2 / X_L$ at $\delta - \sigma = 90°$ |
| Voltage regulation control | Poor | Excellent (midpoint support) | Moderate (reduces drop) | Limited |
| Reactive power exchange | Forced by line vars | Locally compensated | Indirectly via current | None |
| SSR risk | None | None | High ($k > 0.7$) | None |
| Typical FACTS device | — | SVC, STATCOM | TCSC, SSSC | UPFC, PST |
| Best application | Baseline | Voltage stability, VAR control | Power transfer boost, stability | Loop flow control, dispatch |
| Cost (relative) | Lowest | Moderate | Moderate | High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:**
> In actual 400 kV and 765 kV Indian transmission systems, **TCSC (Thyristor Controlled Series Compensator)** at Kanpur-Ballabhgarh and **STATCOMs** at various substations are real-world deployments of these compensation principles. The 1200 kV UHVAC Bina and Wardha lines in India also employ **shunt reactor compensation** based on the principles discussed above.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification of FACTS Controllers (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[FACTS Controllers] --> rootB[Series Controllers]
    rootA --> rootC[Shunt Controllers]
    rootA --> rootD[Combined Controllers]

    rootB --> nodeB1[SSSC Static Synchronous Series Compensator]
    rootB --> nodeB2[TCSC Thyristor Controlled Series Capacitor]
    rootB --> nodeB3[TCSR Thyristor Controlled Series Reactor]
    rootB --> nodeB4[TSC Thyristor Switched Capacitor]

    rootC --> nodeC1[SVC Static VAR Compensator]
    rootC --> nodeC2[STATCOM Static Synchronous Compensator]

    rootD --> nodeD1[IPFC Interline Power Flow Controller Series Series]
    rootD --> nodeD2[UPFC Unified Power Flow Controller Shunt Series]

    nodeB1 --> nodeB1a[Voltage Source Converter Based]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB2a[Thyristor LCC Based]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1a[Thyristor Switched Reactor Capacitor]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2a[Voltage Source Converter Based]
    nodeD2 --> nodeD2a[Most Versatile Single Device]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:**
> The UPFC is considered the most versatile FACTS controller as it can independently control voltage magnitude, line impedance, and phase angle simultaneously by combining a STATCOM (shunt) and SSSC (series) sharing a common DC bus.

---

### 4.2 Transmission Line Compensation Strategies (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subA[Uncompensated Line] --> subA1[Pure Reactance X-L]
    subA1 --> subA2[Power P equals V-S V-R sin delta divided by X-L]
    subA2 --> subA3[Max P at delta equals 90 deg]

    subB[Shunt Compensation] --> subB1[Reactive Source at Midpoint]
    subB1 --> subB2[Midpoint Voltage Regulated]
    subB2 --> subB3[Max P at delta equals 180 deg]

    subC[Series Compensation] --> subC1[Capacitor X-C Inserted in Series]
    subC1 --> subC2[Effective Reactance X-L times 1 minus k]
    subC2 --> subC3[Max P at delta equals 90 deg higher magnitude]

    subD[Phase Angle Control] --> subD1[Series Voltage Injected at Angle sigma]
    subD1 --> subD2[Effective Angle becomes delta minus sigma]
    subD2 --> subD3[Power Routed Directionally]
```

---

### 4.3 Functional Flow — Shunt Compensation at Midpoint

```mermaid
graph TD
    srcStep1[Sending End Bus V-S angle delta] --> stepA[Line Reactance X-L over 2]
    stepA --> midStep1[Midpoint Bus M with Shunt Device]
    midStep1 --> stepB[Shunt Reactor or Capacitor Bank]
    midStep1 --> midStep2[Voltage V-M regulated to nominal]
    stepB --> midStep1
    midStep2 --> stepC[Line Reactance X-L over 2]
    stepC --> rcpStep1[Receiving End Bus V-R angle 0]

    stepD[Control Logic] --> stepB
    stepE[Voltage Measurement at M] --> stepD
    midStep2 --> stepE
```

---

### 4.4 Functional Flow — Series Compensation with TCSC

```mermaid
graph TD
    srcStep2[Sending End V-S] --> lineStepA[Transmission Line Reactance X-L]
    lineStepA --> tcscStep1[TCSC Module Inserted in Series]
    tcscStep1 --> tcscStep2[Thyristor Switched Capacitor TSC branch]
    tcscStep1 --> tcscStep3[Thyristor Controlled Reactor TCR branch]
    tcscStep2 --> tcscStep4[Variable Reactance X-C effective]
    tcscStep3 --> tcscStep4
    tcscStep4 --> lineStepB[Remaining Line Reactance]
    lineStepB --> rcpStep2[Receiving End V-R]

    ctrlStep1[Impedance Reference] --> tcscStep5[Firing Angle Controller]
    tcscStep5 --> tcscStep2
    tcscStep5 --> tcscStep3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Block Diagram Note:**
> The block diagrams above are adapted to **functional architecture** representation as the physical circuit drawing of FACTS devices involves complex three-phase schematics. The block-level representation clearly shows the **interactions and signal flow** that the KTU examiner expects in 14-mark descriptive questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style]**
*"List and briefly explain any three types of FACTS controllers. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**1. SVC (Static VAR Compensator):** A shunt-connected thyristor-based device (combination of TCR and TSC) that provides dynamic reactive power support.
- *Advantage:* Fast dynamic response (~1 cycle), improves voltage stability.
- *Disadvantage:* Generates harmonic currents, requires filter banks.

**2. STATCOM (Static Synchronous Compensator):** A shunt-connected VSC-based device that injects/absorbs reactive current independently of system voltage.
- *Advantage:* Better low-voltage performance than SVC, smaller footprint.
- *Disadvantage:* Higher cost due to VSC and complex control.

**3. TCSC (Thyristor Controlled Series Capacitor):** A series-connected device that varies the effective line reactance dynamically.
- *Advantage:* Increases power transfer, damps SSR and power oscillations.
- *Disadvantage:* Risk of subsynchronous resonance, complex protection.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]**
*"Compare shunt compensation and series compensation of a transmission line. State the maximum power transfer condition for each."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Shunt Compensation | Series Compensation |
|---|---|---|
| Connection Point | Midpoint of line (or any bus) | In series with the line |
| Function | Voltage regulation, VAR support | Reduces effective reactance |
| Device | SVC / STATCOM | TCSC / SSSC |
| Max Power | $2V^2 / X_L$ at $\delta = 180°$ | $V^2 / (X_L(1-k))$ at $\delta = 90°$ |
| Stability Impact | Improves voltage stability | Improves both voltage and angle stability |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Expected Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** With neat block diagram, explain the classification of FACTS controllers. List advantages and disadvantages of FACTS technology. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **FACTS controller** is a power-electronic-based static device used to control AC transmission parameters dynamically. They are classified into four main categories:

**(i) Series Controllers:** Inject a voltage in series with the line. Examples: SSSC, TCSC, TCSR, TSC.
- Function: vary line impedance or voltage magnitude.
- Device: SSSC (VSC-based) or TCSC (LCC-based).

**(ii) Shunt Controllers:** Inject a current at a node to provide reactive power. Examples: SVC, STATCOM.
- Function: voltage regulation, reactive power support.
- Connected at midpoint of long lines for best effectiveness.

**(iii) Combined Series-Series Controllers:** Multiple series controllers on different lines sharing a common DC link. Example: IPFC.
- Function: independent control of multiple transmission lines.

**(iv) Combined Shunt-Series Controllers:** One shunt and one series controller sharing common DC link. Example: UPFC.
- Function: simultaneous control of voltage magnitude, impedance, and phase angle — most versatile FACTS device.

**Advantages:**
1. Increase power transfer capability up to **Surge Impedance Loading (SIL)**.
2. Improve transient and dynamic stability margins.
3. Damp low-frequency power oscillations.
4. Regulate voltage along the line reactively.
5. Reduce loop flows and optimize power dispatch.
6. Defer investment in new transmission corridors.

**Disadvantages:**
1. High capital expenditure (CAPEX).
2. Complex control algorithms requiring specialized expertise.
3. Generation of harmonics (need filter banks).
4. Possible **Subsynchronous Resonance (SSR)** issues with TCSC.
5. Possible failure modes requiring robust protection schemes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students often write only the **list of devices** without explaining their **function** and **connection type**. The examiner specifically looks for:
> (i) Connection topology (series/shunt/combined)
> (ii) Specific device name with technology (LCC/VSC)
> (iii) At least 3 distinct advantages and 2 disadvantages
> A bullet list with no explanation loses up to 2 marks per category.

---

**Q. (b)** Derive the expression for real power transfer of an uncompensated transmission line. Hence explain how shunt compensation at midpoint modifies the power transfer equation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Consider a short transmission line of reactance $X_L$ connecting sending end voltage $V_S \angle \delta$ and receiving end voltage $V_R \angle 0$.

**Step 2:** The line current is:

$$I = \frac{V_S \angle \delta - V_R \angle 0}{jX_L}$$

**Step 3:** Separating real and imaginary parts:

$$I = \frac{V_S \sin\delta}{X_L} - j\frac{V_S \cos\delta - V_R}{X_L}$$

**Step 4:** Complex power at sending end:

$$S_S = V_S \angle \delta \cdot I^* = V_S \angle \delta \cdot \left[\frac{V_S \sin\delta}{X_L} + j\frac{V_S \cos\delta - V_R}{X_L}\right]$$

**Step 5:** Separating real part (active power):

$$P = \frac{V_S V_R}{X_L} \sin\delta$$

**Maximum power** occurs at $\delta = 90°$:

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_S V_R}{X_L}$$

**Step 6: Effect of Shunt Compensation at Midpoint**

The line is split into two equal reactances of $X_L/2$ each. A controllable reactive source regulates the midpoint voltage $V_M \approx V$.

Active power through first half:

$$P = \frac{V \cdot V}{X_L/2} \sin(\delta/2) = \frac{2V^2}{X_L}\sin(\delta/2)$$

**Step 7: Maximum power with midpoint shunt compensation**

At $\delta = 180°$ (the new stability limit):

$$P_{shunt,max} = \frac{2V^2}{X_L} = 2 \times P_{uncomp,max}$$

**Conclusion:** Midpoint shunt compensation **doubles the transient stability limit**, providing a critical enhancement for long-distance bulk power transmission.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake to Avoid:**
> Do not write the shunt compensated equation as $P = V^2 / X_L \cdot \sin(\delta/2)$. The correct form is $P = 2V^2/X_L \cdot \sin(\delta/2)$ because each half-line has reactance $X_L/2$. This subtle factor-of-2 error is heavily penalized.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Expected Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** Explain series compensation of a transmission line with a phasor diagram. Derive the expression for the effective line reactance and the new power transfer equation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Concept**

In series compensation, a capacitor of reactance $X_C = 1/(\omega C)$ is inserted in series with the transmission line. The capacitor compensates for part of the line's inductive reactance, reducing the effective reactance and hence the voltage drop.

**Step 2: Phasor Diagram Description**

The line current $I$ lags the receiving end voltage $V_R$ by angle $\phi$ (load power factor). The voltage drop across the original line reactance is $I X_L$ (leading $I$ by 90°). After series compensation, the voltage drop is reduced to $I X_{eff} = I(X_L - X_C)$, leading to a smaller angular difference and improved voltage profile at the receiving end.

**Step 3: Effective Reactance**

The total series reactance is:

$$X_{eff} = X_L - X_C = X_L\left(1 - \frac{X_C}{X_L}\right) = X_L(1 - k)$$

where $k = X_C / X_L$ is the **degree of series compensation** ($0 < k < 1$).

**Step 4: Power Transfer with Series Compensation**

$$P_{series} = \frac{V_S V_R}{X_{eff}} \sin\delta = \frac{V_S V_R}{X_L(1-k)} \sin\delta$$

**Step 5: Interpretation**

For $k = 0.5$, the effective reactance becomes $X_L/2$, and the power transfer capability at the same torque angle $\delta$ is **doubled**. For $k = 0.7$, the power transfer capability is tripled.

**Step 6: Practical Limits**

- $k < 0.3$ → minimal benefit.
- $0.3 \le k \le 0.7$ → optimal range.
- $k > 0.7$ → SSR risk, protection issues.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:**
> Students sometimes write $X_{eff} = X_L + X_C$ instead of $X_L - X_C$. Remember: capacitive reactance has **opposite sign** to inductive reactance. Always write $X_{eff} = X_L - X_C$ and ensure $X_C < X_L$ for stable operation.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the principle of phase angle control of a transmission line. Derive the power transfer equation and state its applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Concept**

Phase angle control uses a **phase-shifting transformer (PST)** or the series branch of a **UPFC** to inject a voltage that changes the **relative phase angle** between the two ends of the line, without changing the voltage magnitudes.

**Step 2: Mathematical Model**

If the phase shift introduced is $\sigma$, the effective phase angle becomes $(\delta - \sigma)$. The active power transferred is:

$$P_{PAC} = \frac{V_S V_R}{X_L} \sin(\delta - \sigma)$$

**Step 3: Directional Power Flow**

- For $\sigma > 0$ (positive phase shift), the effective angle reduces, increasing power flow from S to R.
- For $\sigma < 0$, power flow decreases or reverses.

**Step 4: Synchronizing Torque Coefficient**

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial \delta} = \frac{V_S V_R}{X_L} \cos(\delta - \sigma)$$

A controlled $\sigma$ allows operation at higher synchronizing torque, improving transient stability.

**Step 5: MVA Rating of Phase Shifter**

The injected voltage magnitude is $V_{inj} = 2V\sin(\sigma/2)$. With line current $I$:

$$S_{PST} = 2V \cdot I \cdot \sin(\sigma/2)$$

**Step 6: Applications**

1. **Loop flow control** in meshed networks.
2. **Inter-area oscillation damping**.
3. **Power flow redirection** during contingencies.
4. **Relief of overloaded lines**.
5. **Economic dispatch optimization** in deregulated systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:**
> Examiners expect the **derived equation** with proper notation, not just a sentence describing the concept. Marks are awarded for:
> (i) Correct formula for $P_{PAC}$ (2 marks)
> (ii) Explanation of $\sigma$ sign convention (2 marks)
> (iii) At least 3 valid applications (2 marks)
> (iv) Block diagram or phasor description (1 mark)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2:**

- **FACTS Definition (IEEE):** Power-electronic-based static controllers enhancing AC transmission controllability.
- **Four Categories:** Series, Shunt, Combined Series-Series, Combined Shunt-Series.
- **Key Devices:** SVC, STATCOM, TCSC, SSSC, UPFC, IPFC.
- **Uncompensated Power Equation:** $P = (V_S V_R / X_L) \sin\delta$, max at $\delta = 90°$.
- **Surge Impedance Loading:** $SIL = V_{LL}^2 / Z_S$, where $Z_S = \sqrt{L/C}$.
- **Shunt Compensation:** Midpoint placement, doubles $P_{max}$ at $\delta = 180°$.
- **Series Compensation:** $X_{eff} = X_L(1 - k)$, $k = X_C / X_L$, practical $k \le 0.7$.
- **Series Compensated Power:** $P = V_S V_R / [X_L(1-k)] \cdot \sin\delta$.
- **Phase Angle Control:** $P = (V_S V_R / X_L) \sin(\delta - \sigma)$, controls loop flow.
- **Subsynchronous Resonance (SSR):** Major risk in TCSC; limits $k$ to ≤ 0.7.
- **UPFC:** Most versatile — combines STATCOM and SSSC on a common DC bus.
- **STATCOM vs SVC:** STATCOM provides full reactive current even at low voltage; SVC becomes a fixed capacitor at low V.
- **KTU Key Numerical Values:** For a 400 kV, 250 km line, typical $X_L \approx 50\ \Omega$, $k = 0.5$ doubles transfer.
- **Real-World Indian Examples:** Kanpur-Ballabhgarh (TCSC), Raichur-Sholapur (Series), various STATCOMs at 400 kV substations.
- **High-Frequency 14-Mark Topics:** (i) Derivation of $P-\delta$ curves with and without compensation, (ii) UPFC operation, (iii) SVC vs STATCOM comparison.
- **Low-Frequency 3-Mark Topics:** (i) FACTS device classification, (ii) Advantages/disadvantages list, (iii) Define SIL and compensation factor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static Shunt Compensator - Objectives of Shunt Compensations - Variable Impedance Type VAR Generators - TCR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET862 - HVDC AND FACTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Static Shunt Compensator - Objectives of Shunt Compensations - Variable Impedance Type VAR Generators - TCR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Static Shunt Compensator (SSC) — Formal Definition

A **Static Shunt Compensator (SSC)** is a shunt-connected, static (i.e., without rotating mass) reactive power compensation device used in power transmission and distribution systems. It is realized using power-electronic switching devices (typically thyristors, GTOs, or IGBTs) and is connected in **shunt (parallel)** with the power system network. Its primary function is to **absorb or generate reactive power dynamically**, thereby controlling the bus voltage magnitude, improving power factor, and enhancing system stability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A Shunt Compensator is functionally a *controllable reactive current source* $I_q$ drawn from or injected into the bus. The reactive power exchanged is $Q = V \cdot I_q$ (per phase), where $V$ is the system bus voltage (line-to-neutral RMS).

## 1.2 Classification of Shunt Compensators

Shunt compensators are broadly classified into two families based on the underlying technology:

| Family | Operating Principle | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Variable Impedance Type** | Uses thyristor-controlled switching of reactors/capacitors | **TCR**, TSC, SR (Switched Reactor) |
| **Switching Converter Type** | Uses VSC with DC link (self-commutated) | **STATCOM (STATic COMpensator)**, SVC-light |

The topic of this module focuses entirely on the **Variable Impedance Type VAR Generators**, with deep emphasis on the **TCR**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Intuition

Imagine a long pipe (transmission line) supplying a factory (load). The pressure at the end (voltage) drops when the factory draws too much current. To compensate, a **water tank with a controllable valve** is connected to the pipe at the load end.

- **Open the valve more** → water flows in → pressure rises (analogous to injecting reactive power / **capacitive mode**).
- **Close the valve partially** → restrict inflow → pressure falls (analogous to absorbing reactive power / **inductive mode**).

A **Static Shunt Compensator is exactly this controllable valve**, but for reactive current instead of water. The **TCR** is one of the simplest mechanical implementations of this valve: a reactor (inductor) whose effective reactance is *electronically modulated* by firing-angle control of a thyristor pair.

## 1.4 Objectives of Shunt Compensation (KTU Syllabus Mandate)

The principal objectives of connecting a shunt compensator to a transmission system are listed below. These are the **headline reasons** examiners test.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 — Core Objectives of Shunt Compensation:**
> 1. **Reactive Power Compensation** — To supply or absorb VARs as demanded by the load/system.
> 2. **Voltage Regulation / Voltage Profile Improvement** — Maintain the bus voltage magnitude close to its nominal value under varying load.
> 3. **Power Factor Correction** — Bring the displacement power factor closer to unity.
> 4. **Improvement of Steady-State Power Transfer Limit** — $P = \dfrac{V_1 V_2}{X} \sin \delta$ is enhanced because $V_1$ and $V_2$ are held closer to nominal.
> 5. **Enhancement of Transient Stability Margin** — During a fault, the compensator maintains voltage, increasing the area under the power-angle curve.
> 6. **Damping of Power Oscillations** — By modulating reactive power in synchronism with rotor oscillation frequency.
> 7. **Load Balancing & Symmetry** (in unbalanced loads) — Distribute VARs among phases to reduce unbalance.

## 1.5 Placement of the Shunt Compensator

The shunt compensator is connected at the **mid-point of a transmission line** (for maximum stability benefit) or at the **load bus** (for voltage regulation and power-factor correction). The connection is via a coupling transformer to step down the EHV level to the device voltage rating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reactive Power Exchange Rule:** If the bus voltage $V$ falls below the reference $V_{ref}$, the SSC must **inject** reactive power (capacitive mode). If $V > V_{ref}$, the SSC must **absorb** reactive power (inductive mode). This is the fundamental $V$–$I$ droop characteristic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Reactive Power Control

In a power system, reactive power flows from higher voltage magnitude to lower voltage magnitude (similar to real power flowing from higher phase angle to lower). A shunt compensator sitting at a bus behaves like a **controllable reactive current source $I_q$**.

The reactive power injected by the compensator is:

$$Q_c = V \cdot I_q \;\; (\text{per phase})$$

For a 3-phase system:

$$Q_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_q$$

where $V_L$ is the line-to-line RMS voltage and $I_q$ is the reactive RMS current.

The system is **steady-state stable** when $Q_c + Q_{line} = Q_{load}$, and the bus voltage settles to the new equilibrium.

## 2.2 Variable Impedance Type VAR Generators

A **Variable Impedance Type VAR Generator** achieves reactive power control not by generating/absorbing reactive power from a DC source, but by **changing the effective impedance** of a passive reactive element (L or C) using thyristor-based switching.

Two principal members of this family are:

### 2.2.1 Thyristor Controlled Reactor (TCR)
- A **fixed reactor** $L$ is connected in series with a **bidirectional thyristor switch (anti-parallel pair)**.
- The effective reactance is varied by **phase-angle firing control** of the thyristor pair.
- Operates in the **absorbing (inductive)** region only; cannot supply VARs on its own.
- For full inductive operation: $\alpha = 0°$ (thyristors conduct from voltage zero crossing).
- For full blocking: $\alpha = 90°$ (no conduction).
- Continuous control range: $90° \leq \alpha \leq 180°$.

### 2.2.2 Thyristor Switched Capacitor (TSC)
- A **fixed capacitor** is switched in/out of the circuit in integer multiples.
- Discontinuous control: the capacitor is either fully connected or fully disconnected.
- Operates in the **generating (capacitive)** region.

> [!NOTE]
> A complete Static VAR Compensator (SVC) is typically formed by a **parallel combination of TCR and TSC**, allowing full-range bidirectional VAR control (from fully inductive to fully capacitive).

## 2.3 TCR — Circuit Configuration

A single-phase TCR consists of:

1. A **linear reactor** of inductance $L$ (or reactance $X_L = \omega L$).
2. A **bidirectional thyristor valve** — two thyristors $T_1$ and $T_2$ connected in anti-parallel (or a single triac in low-voltage applications).
3. A **firing/control unit** that issues gate pulses at a controlled firing angle $\alpha$ measured from the voltage zero crossing.

For a three-phase TCR, **delta-connection** of three single-phase units is the most common (delta eliminates triplen harmonics from the line currents).

## 2.4 TCR Operating Principle (Step-by-Step)

Let the bus voltage be $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.

1. The thyristor pair is fired at angle $\alpha$ after the voltage zero crossing (where $\alpha = 0$ corresponds to the zero crossing of $v$).
2. With **ideal thyristors** (no overlap), each thyristor conducts from $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\pi$ (positive half-cycle) for $T_1$, and from $\pi + \alpha$ to $2\pi$ (negative half-cycle) for $T_2$.
3. The conduction interval within each half-cycle is:

$$\sigma = \pi - \alpha \;\; (\text{conduction angle in radians})$$

4. The current is therefore a **non-sinusoidal, chopped sinusoid** that is symmetric about the peak of the voltage half-cycle.
5. The RMS value of this chopped current depends on $\alpha$. As $\alpha$ increases from $0$ to $90°$, the current RMS decreases continuously from its maximum to zero.
6. The fundamental component of this current is $I_1(\alpha)$, which determines the **equivalent reactance** at fundamental frequency.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the KTU Board Examiner's go-to reference for TCR. Memorize every row.**

| Quantity | Expression | Range / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Fundamental RMS reactor current | $I_{LR}(\alpha) = \dfrac{V}{\omega L} \left(1 - \dfrac{2\alpha}{\pi} - \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{\pi}\right)^{1/2}$ | Amps (A) |
| Equivalent susceptance | $B_{LR}(\alpha) = \dfrac{1}{\omega L} \left(1 - \dfrac{2\alpha}{\pi} - \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{\pi}\right)$ | Siemens (S) |
| Equivalent reactance | $X_{LR}(\alpha) = \dfrac{1}{B_{LR}(\alpha)} = \dfrac{\pi \omega L}{\pi - 2\alpha - \sin 2\alpha}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| Reactive power absorbed | $Q_{TCR} = V^2 \cdot B_{LR}(\alpha)$ | VAR |
| Firing angle range | $0° \leq \alpha \leq 90°$ | Degrees |
| Conduction angle | $\sigma = \pi - \alpha$ | Radians |
| Maximum current (full conduction) | $I_{max} = \dfrac{V}{\omega L}$ at $\alpha = 0$ | A |
| Zero current (blocking) | $I = 0$ at $\alpha = 90°$ | A |
| Dominant harmonic order | $n = 2k+1$ (odd harmonics: 3rd, 5th, 7th, …) | — |
| Amplitude of $n^{th}$ harmonic (per-unit of $I_{max}$) | $\dfrac{I_n}{I_{max}} = \dfrac{4}{\pi} \left[\dfrac{\sin\left(n\alpha\right)}{n\left(n^2-1\right)}\right] \cos\alpha$ (for odd $n$) | pu |
| Characteristic harmonic amplitude envelope | Decreases with $n$ and with $\alpha$ | — |

**Key Insight:** The control variable $\alpha$ effectively multiplies the natural susceptance $B_L = 1/(\omega L)$ by a function $f(\alpha)$:

$$B_{LR}(\alpha) = B_L \cdot f(\alpha) \quad \text{where} \quad f(\alpha) = 1 - \frac{2\alpha}{\pi} - \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{\pi}$$

$f(\alpha)$ is the **normalized control characteristic**; it is **1 at $\alpha = 0$** and **0 at $\alpha = \pi/2$**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

TCRs are deployed extensively in:

- **Steel mills, arc furnaces, and mine hoists** — for flicker mitigation and power factor correction.
- **HVDC converter stations** — for reactive power support of the AC side of LCC-HVDC links.
- **EHV transmission systems** — as part of SVCs to damp subsynchronous resonance, improve voltage stability, and increase transfer capability.
- **Wind farm collection grids** — to support fluctuating reactive power demand.

> [!NOTE]
> A TCR alone is an *absorber* of reactive power. To deliver both VAR absorption and generation, it is paired with a TSC (Thyristor Switched Capacitor) bank. The combination is called an **SVC (Static VAR Compensator)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Current $I_1(\alpha)$ in a TCR

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let the supply voltage be:

$$v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$

The reactor has inductance $L$ such that:

$$v(t) = L \frac{di}{dt} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; i(t) = \frac{1}{L} \int v(t)\, dt + C$$

where $C$ is determined by the initial condition (current at the firing instant).

### 3.1.2 Current During Conduction (Thyristor $T_1$, Positive Half-Cycle)

$T_1$ is fired at $\omega t = \alpha$ when the voltage is rising through zero. The current starts from zero (initial condition $i(\alpha/\omega) = 0$). The current is:

$$i(t) = \frac{1}{L} \int_{\alpha/\omega}^{t} V_m \sin(\omega \tau)\, d\tau$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$i(t) = \frac{V_m}{\omega L} \left[ -\cos(\omega \tau) \right]_{\alpha/\omega}^{t}$$

$$i(t) = \frac{V_m}{\omega L} \left[ -\cos(\omega t) + \cos\alpha \right]$$

$$\boxed{i(t) = \frac{V_m}{\omega L} \left[ \cos\alpha - \cos(\omega t) \right]}$$

### 3.1.3 Current Extinction

$T_1$ naturally turns off at $\omega t = \pi - \alpha$ (the current returns to zero by symmetry of the reactor's purely inductive nature). The current pulse is therefore symmetric about the voltage peak ($\omega t = \pi/2$).

### 3.1.4 Full-Wave Expression

Combining positive and negative half-cycles (with $T_2$ firing at $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$):

$$i(t) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{V_m}{\omega L} \left[\cos\alpha - \cos(\omega t)\right], & \alpha \leq \omega t \leq \pi - \alpha \\[2mm] 0, & \pi - \alpha \leq \omega t \leq \pi + \alpha \\[2mm] \dfrac{V_m}{\omega L} \left[\cos\alpha + \cos(\omega t)\right], & \pi + \alpha \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi - \alpha \\[2mm] 0, & 2\pi - \alpha \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi + \alpha \end{cases}$$

### 3.1.5 Fourier Series Expansion

The current waveform is an **odd half-wave symmetric** function (only odd harmonics, no DC, no cosine terms). The Fourier sine series is:

$$i(t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} I_n \sin(n \omega t)$$

where the amplitude of the $n^{th}$ harmonic is given by:

$$I_n = \frac{2}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi - \alpha} \frac{V_m}{\omega L} \left[\cos\alpha - \cos(\omega t)\right] \sin(n \omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 1 — Split the integral:**

$$I_n = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi \omega L} \left\{ \cos\alpha \int_{\alpha}^{\pi - \alpha} \sin(n\omega t)\, d(\omega t) - \int_{\alpha}^{\pi - \alpha} \cos(\omega t) \sin(n\omega t)\, d(\omega t) \right\}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the first integral:**

$$A_1 = \int_{\alpha}^{\pi - \alpha} \sin(n\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \left[ -\frac{\cos(n\omega t)}{n} \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi - \alpha} = \frac{1}{n}\left[\cos(n\alpha) - \cos(n\pi - n\alpha)\right]$$

Using $\cos(n\pi - x) = \cos(n\pi)\cos(x) + \sin(n\pi)\sin(x) = (-1)^n \cos(x)$ for integer $n$:

$$A_1 = \frac{1}{n}\left[\cos(n\alpha) - (-1)^n \cos(n\alpha)\right] = \frac{\cos(n\alpha)}{n}\left[1 - (-1)^n\right]$$

For odd $n$: $1 - (-1)^n = 2$, so $A_1 = \dfrac{2\cos(n\alpha)}{n}$.
For even $n$: $1 - (-1)^n = 0$, so $A_1 = 0$ (no even harmonics — expected).

**Step 3 — Evaluate the second integral** using the product-to-sum identity $\cos A \sin B = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[\sin(A+B) - \sin(A-B)\right]$:

$$A_2 = \int_{\alpha}^{\pi - \alpha} \cos(\omega t) \sin(n\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{1}{2} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi - \alpha} \left[\sin((n+1)\omega t) + \sin((n-1)\omega t)\right] d(\omega t)$$

(Note: $\sin(A+B) = \sin((n+1)\omega t)$ since $B - A = (n-1)\omega t$ here.)

Evaluating:

$$A_2 = \frac{1}{2} \left[ -\frac{\cos((n+1)\omega t)}{n+1} - \frac{\cos((n-1)\omega t)}{n-1} \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi - \alpha}$$

After expansion (this is a long but standard manipulation; result for odd $n$):

$$A_2 = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \frac{2\cos((n+1)\alpha)}{n+1} + \frac{2\cos((n-1)\alpha)}{n-1} \right] = \frac{\cos((n+1)\alpha)}{n+1} + \frac{\cos((n-1)\alpha)}{n-1}$$

**Step 4 — Assemble and simplify** for odd $n$ (using $\cos((n+1)\alpha) = (-1)^{(n-1)/2}\cos\alpha$ when $n$ is odd, etc., after long trigonometric reduction):

The classical result, after the full algebra, is:

$$I_n = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi \omega L} \cdot \frac{2 \sin\alpha \cos(n\alpha) - n \cos\alpha \sin(n\alpha)}{n(n^2-1)}$$

Combining the sin and cos terms, the amplitude can be written compactly as:

$$\boxed{I_n = \frac{4 V_m}{\pi \omega L} \cdot \frac{\sin\alpha \cos(n\alpha) - n \cos\alpha \sin(n\alpha) \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}}{n(n^2-1)}}$$

A more practical (and standard) compact form found in textbooks is:

$$I_n = \frac{4 V_m}{\pi \omega L} \left[ \frac{\sin\alpha \cos(n\alpha) - n \cos\alpha \sin(n\alpha)}{n(n^2-1)} \right] \quad \text{(odd } n \text{)}$$

**Step 5 — Fundamental component ($n=1$):**

Setting $n=1$ and simplifying:

$$I_1 = \frac{V_m}{\omega L} \left[ 1 - \frac{2\alpha}{\pi} - \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{\pi} \right]^{1/2}$$

This is the **fundamental RMS equivalent current**.

### 3.1.6 Equivalent Susceptance

The fundamental component leads the voltage by $90°$ (purely inductive). Hence the equivalent admittance is purely susceptive:

$$Y_{eq} = -j B_{LR}(\alpha), \quad \text{where} \quad B_{LR}(\alpha) = \frac{1}{\omega L} \left[ 1 - \frac{2\alpha}{\pi} - \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{\pi} \right]$$

The reactive power absorbed is:

$$Q_{TCR} = \frac{V^2}{X_{LR}(\alpha)} = V^2 B_{LR}(\alpha) \quad (\text{inductive, positive value})$$

## 3.2 Numerical Example — Worked-Out Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Typical):** A single-phase TCR is connected to a $400\text{ V, 50 Hz}$ bus. The reactor has $L = 10\text{ mH}$. The firing angle is $\alpha = 30°$. Compute:
> (a) The fundamental RMS current.
> (b) The equivalent susceptance and reactive power.
> (c) The amplitude of the 3rd harmonic current.

**Given:**

- $V_{RMS} = 400\text{ V}$, $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot 400 = 565.69\text{ V}$
- $f = 50\text{ Hz}$, $\omega = 2\pi f = 314.159\text{ rad/s}$
- $L = 10\text{ mH} = 0.01\text{ H}$
- $\alpha = 30° = \pi/6\text{ rad}$

**Step (a) — Maximum current:**

$$I_{max} = \frac{V_m}{\omega L} = \frac{565.69}{314.159 \times 0.01} = \frac{565.69}{3.14159} = 180.06\text{ A (peak)}$$

**Step (b) — Control function $f(\alpha)$:**

$$f(\alpha) = 1 - \frac{2\alpha}{\pi} - \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{\pi} = 1 - \frac{2 \cdot 0.5236}{3.1416} - \frac{\sin(60°)}{3.1416}$$

$$f(30°) = 1 - 0.3333 - \frac{0.8660}{3.1416} = 1 - 0.3333 - 0.2757 = 0.3910$$

**Fundamental RMS current:**

$$I_1 = I_{max} \cdot \sqrt{f(\alpha)} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \quad \text{(the $\sqrt{f}$ gives RMS already from peak; the $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ is implicit)}$$

A cleaner form using RMS base:

$$B_L = \frac{1}{\omega L} = \frac{1}{3.14159} = 0.3183\text{ S}$$

$$B_{LR} = B_L \cdot f(\alpha) = 0.3183 \times 0.3910 = 0.1244\text{ S}$$

$$I_1 = V_{RMS} \cdot B_{LR} = 400 \times 0.1244 = 49.78\text{ A (RMS, fundamental)}$$

**Reactive power (single-phase):**

$$Q_{TCR} = V^2 B_{LR} = 400^2 \times 0.1244 = 19{,}904\text{ VAR} \approx 19.9\text{ kVAR (inductive)}$$

**Step (c) — 3rd harmonic amplitude:**

Using the formula for odd $n = 3$:

$$I_3 = \frac{4 V_m}{\pi \omega L} \left[ \frac{\sin\alpha \cos(3\alpha) - 3\cos\alpha \sin(3\alpha)}{3(3^2-1)} \right]$$

Numerator: $\sin 30° \cos 90° - 3 \cos 30° \sin 90° = 0.5 \times 0 - 3 \times 0.8660 \times 1 = -2.598$

Denominator: $3 \times 8 = 24$

$$I_3 = \frac{4 \times 180.06}{3.1416} \times \frac{-2.598}{24} = 229.18 \times (-0.1083) = -24.82\text{ A (peak)}$$

$$|I_3| \approx 24.8\text{ A (peak)} \approx 17.55\text{ A (RMS)}$$

This is a **significant** harmonic — the very reason for delta-connection and harmonic filters in practical TCR installations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Shunt Compensator Classification — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Shunt Compensator Family] --> B[Variable Impedance Type]
    A --> C[Switching Converter Type]
    B --> D[TCR Thyristor Controlled Reactor]
    B --> E[TSC Thyristor Switched Capacitor]
    B --> F[SR Switched Reactor]
    B --> G[SVC Static VAR Compensator]
    G --> H[TCR plus TSC Bank]
    C --> I[STATCOM]
    C --> J[SVG Static VAR Generator using VSC]
    D --> D1[Absorbs VARs Inductive]
    E --> E1[Supplies VARs Capacitive]
    I --> I1[Self commutated Bidirectional]
```

## 4.2 TCR — Single-Phase Circuit Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    AC[AC Bus Voltage v t] --> TR[Coupling Transformer Optional]
    TR --> L[Linear Reactor L]
    L --> TH[T1 T2 Anti parallel Thyristor Pair]
    TH --> N[Return Neutral]
    CTRL[Gate Firing Unit] -- Gate pulses alpha --> TH
    CTRL -- Voltage sensing --> AC
    CTRL -- Current sensing --> TH
```

## 4.3 TCR — Three-Phase Delta-Connected Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    BUS[Three Phase HV Bus] --> T1A[Phase A]
    BUS --> T1B[Phase B]
    BUS --> T1C[Phase C]
    T1A --> UNIT1[TCR Unit 1 Reactor L1 plus Thyristors]
    T1B --> UNIT2[TCR Unit 2 Reactor L2 plus Thyristors]
    T1C --> UNIT3[TCR Unit 3 Reactor L3 plus Thyristors]
    UNIT1 -.Delta.- UNIT2
    UNIT2 -.Delta.- UNIT3
    UNIT3 -.Delta.- UNIT1
    CTRL[Master Firing Controller] --> UNIT1
    CTRL --> UNIT2
    CTRL --> UNIT3
```

## 4.4 TCR — V-I Operating Characteristic Curve (Droop)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph DROOP[TCR V I Operating Curve]
    P1[Point 1 Vmax, Imin Inductive Max VAR] --> P2[Point 2 Vref, Zero Current]
    P2 --> P3[Point 3 Vmin, Imax Full Conduction]
    end
    note1[Slope equals Xslope sub S Setting] -.- DROOP
    CTRL[Voltage Regulator] --> DROOP
```

## 4.5 TCR — Firing Angle to Susceptance Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Alpha increases from 0 to 90 deg] --> B[f alpha decreases from 1 to 0]
    B --> C[BLR alpha decreases from BL to 0]
    C --> D[QTCR decreases from Qmax to 0]
    A2[Alpha decreases from 90 to 0 deg] --> B2[f alpha increases from 0 to 1]
    B2 --> C2[BLR alpha increases from 0 to BL]
    C2 --> D2[QTCR increases from 0 to Qmax]
```

## 4.6 TCR — Harmonic Spectrum Summary

```mermaid
graph TD
    H1[Fundamental n equals 1 Dominant] --> H3
    H3[3rd Harmonic Largest Unwanted]
    H3 --> H5[5th Harmonic Significant]
    H5 --> H7[7th Harmonic Moderate]
    H7 --> H9[9th Harmonic Small]
    H9 --> H11[11th Harmonic Smaller]
    FILT[Harmonic Filter Bank Tunes out 5th, 7th] --> H5
    FILT --> H7
    DELTA[Delta Connection Eliminates 3rd and Triplens] --> H3
    DELTA --> H9
```
