# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**List the main objectives of shunt compensation in a power transmission system.**

**Model Answer:**

The main objectives of shunt compensation are:

1. **Reactive power control** — to supply or absorb VARs as needed by the load.
2. **Voltage regulation** — to maintain the bus voltage magnitude near its rated value.
3. **Power factor improvement** — to raise the displacement power factor closer to unity.
4. **Enhancement of power transfer capability** — $P = (V_1 V_2 / X) \sin\delta$ is increased when bus voltages are regulated.
5. **Improvement of transient and dynamic stability** — voltage support during faults increases the area under the $P$–$\delta$ curve.
6. **Damping of low-frequency power oscillations** through modulation of reactive power injection.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write "power factor correction" and "voltage regulation" as separate answers. These overlap — keep them distinct. Examiners expect **at least 4 distinct objectives** for full marks.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**What is a Thyristor Controlled Reactor (TCR)? Why is it called a "variable impedance" device?**

**Model Answer:**

A **TCR** is a shunt-connected reactive power compensator consisting of a **fixed linear reactor** in series with a **bidirectional thyristor switch (two anti-parallel thyristors)**. By varying the firing angle $\alpha$ of the thyristors from $0°$ to $90°$, the **effective reactance** presented by the device to the fundamental frequency is varied continuously.

It is called a "**variable impedance**" device because the **fundamental-frequency equivalent reactance** $X_{LR}(\alpha)$ is a function of the firing angle $\alpha$:

$$X_{LR}(\alpha) = \frac{\pi \omega L}{\pi - 2\alpha - \sin 2\alpha}$$

As $\alpha$ increases, $X_{LR}$ increases, reducing the fundamental current. The TCR never changes the physical inductance $L$; only the **effective (or fundamental-frequency equivalent) impedance** is varied.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students state that the TCR "changes the inductance $L$" — this is **wrong**. $L$ is fixed. The variable quantity is the **conduction interval**, which alters the **RMS fundamental current** and thus the **effective reactance** seen at the fundamental frequency.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction and operating principle of a single-phase Thyristor Controlled Reactor (TCR) with a neat circuit diagram. Define the firing angle and conduction angle and derive the expression for the fundamental current.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A single-phase TCR is connected to a 415 V, 50 Hz supply. The reactor has $L = 6\text{ mH}$ and the firing angle is $\alpha = 20°$. Calculate:
  (i) The fundamental RMS current through the reactor.
  (ii) The reactive power absorbed.
  (iii) The 3rd harmonic current amplitude (peak).

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction:** A single-phase TCR consists of a fixed linear inductor $L$ in series with a bidirectional thyristor switch (anti-parallel pair $T_1$ and $T_2$). The bidirectional switch is required because AC current flows in both directions during each cycle.

**Firing angle $\alpha$:** Defined as the delay (in electrical degrees) between the **voltage zero crossing** and the **instant the thyristor is gated on**.

**Conduction angle $\sigma$:** The duration for which the thyristor conducts within each half-cycle:

$$\sigma = \pi - \alpha$$

**Operating Principle:** When the thyristor pair is fired at $\omega t = \alpha$, current begins to flow. The current during conduction of $T_1$ is:

$$i(t) = \frac{V_m}{\omega L} \left[ \cos\alpha - \cos(\omega t) \right], \quad \alpha \leq \omega t \leq \pi - \alpha$$

The waveform is a **chopped sinusoid** that is symmetric about the voltage peak. The Fourier series expansion of this waveform contains only **odd harmonics** (3rd, 5th, 7th, …).

The **fundamental component** has RMS value:

$$I_1 = \frac{V}{\omega L} \left[ 1 - \frac{2\alpha}{\pi} - \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{\pi} \right]^{1/2}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**

- $V_{RMS} = 415\text{ V}$, $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot 415 = 586.89\text{ V}$
- $\omega = 2\pi \cdot 50 = 314.159\text{ rad/s}$
- $L = 6\text{ mH} = 6 \times 10^{-3}\text{ H}$
- $\alpha = 20° = 0.3491\text{ rad}$

**Step 1 — Compute $B_L$:**

$$B_L = \frac{1}{\omega L} = \frac{1}{314.159 \times 6 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{1.8850} = 0.5305\text{ S}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $f(\alpha)$ at $\alpha = 20°$:**

$$f(20°) = 1 - \frac{2 \times 20°}{180°} - \frac{\sin(40°)}{180° \cdot (\pi/180)}$$

Equivalently, in radians:

$$f(20°) = 1 - \frac{2 \times 0.3491}{3.1416} - \frac{\sin(0.6981)}{3.1416}$$

$$= 1 - 0.2222 - \frac{0.6428}{3.1416} = 1 - 0.2222 - 0.2046 = 0.5732$$

**Step 3 — Fundamental RMS current:**

$$I_1 = V \cdot B_L \cdot \sqrt{f(\alpha)} = 415 \times 0.5305 \times \sqrt{0.5732}$$

$$= 415 \times 0.5305 \times 0.7571 = 166.65\text{ A (RMS, fundamental)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Formula Trap:** Some students write $I_1 = V \cdot B_{LR} = V \cdot B_L \cdot f(\alpha)$ **without the square root**. The current RMS is $\sqrt{f(\alpha)}$ times the base, not $f(\alpha)$ times the base. The expression for $B_{LR}$ already contains $f(\alpha)$ — the RMS current then is $I_1 = V \cdot B_{LR}$ (correct), but $B_{LR}$ itself was derived from $I_1^2 = I_{max}^2 \cdot f(\alpha)$. So either form is consistent **only if** used with the correct expression. **Verify dimensions and square root placement.**

**Step 4 — Reactive power absorbed (single-phase):**

$$Q_{TCR} = V \cdot I_1 = 415 \times 166.65 = 69{,}160\text{ VAR} \approx 69.16\text{ kVAR (inductive)}$$

**Step 5 — 3rd harmonic amplitude (peak):**

The 3rd harmonic (relative to the fundamental) has the formula:

$$\frac{I_3}{I_1} = \frac{1}{3} \left| \frac{\sin\alpha \cos 3\alpha - 3 \cos\alpha \sin 3\alpha}{\cos\alpha \cdot \text{denominator}} \right|$$

Using the standard formula (peak values):

$$I_3 = \frac{4 V_m}{\pi \omega L} \left[ \frac{\sin\alpha \cos 3\alpha - 3 \cos\alpha \sin 3\alpha}{3(3^2-1)} \right]$$

Numerator: $\sin 20° \cos 60° - 3 \cos 20° \sin 60° = 0.3420 \times 0.5 - 3 \times 0.9397 \times 0.8660 = 0.1710 - 2.4414 = -2.2704$

Denominator: $3 \times 8 = 24$

Base: $\dfrac{4 V_m}{\pi \omega L} = \dfrac{4 \times 586.89}{3.1416 \times 1.8850} = \dfrac{2347.56}{5.9224} = 396.4\text{ A (peak base)}$

$$I_3 = 396.4 \times \frac{-2.2704}{24} = 396.4 \times (-0.0946) = -37.50\text{ A (peak)}$$

$$\boxed{|I_3| \approx 37.5\text{ A (peak)} \approx 26.5\text{ A (RMS)}}$$

---

### Question B — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the classification of static shunt compensators. Compare TCR and STATCOM with respect to construction, response time, harmonic performance, and cost.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **equivalent fundamental-frequency susceptance** of a single-phase TCR as a function of the firing angle $\alpha$. Sketch the variation of $B_{LR}(\alpha)$ with $\alpha$.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Classification Block Diagram:**

Static shunt compensators divide into:
1. **Variable Impedance Type** — TCR, TSC, SR, SVC (combination).
2. **Switching Converter Type** — STATCOM (VSC-based), uses self-commutated switches (GTO/IGBT/IGCT).

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | TCR | STATCOM |
|---|---|---|
| Reactive element | Fixed reactor (L) | DC capacitor + VSC |
| Switching devices | Conventional thyristors (line-commutated) | GTO/IGBT/IGCT (self-commutated) |
| Control variable | Firing angle $\alpha$ (0°–90°) | Modulation index $m$ and phase $\delta$ |
| VAR capability | **Absorbs only** (requires TSC for full range) | **Both absorbs and generates** (from DC link) |
| Response time | 5–10 ms (1/4 to 1/2 cycle) | 1–2 ms (sub-cycle) |
| Harmonics | Significant (3rd, 5th, 7th) — needs filters | Low (PWM with high switching freq.) |
| DC source | **Not required** | **Required** (DC capacitor/battery) |
| Footprint | Larger (filters + reactors) | Compact (no large filters) |
| Cost | Lower for low/medium ratings | Higher (VSC valves are expensive) |
| Typical MVA | Up to several hundred | Up to ±200 MVAR per unit |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation of $B_{LR}(\alpha)$:**

From the current waveform during conduction of $T_1$:

$$i(t) = \frac{V_m}{\omega L} \left[ \cos\alpha - \cos(\omega t) \right], \quad \alpha \leq \omega t \leq \pi - \alpha$$

The RMS value of this current is:

$$I_{RMS} = \left[ \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi - \alpha} \left(\frac{V_m}{\omega L}\right)^2 (\cos\alpha - \cos\omega t)^2 d(\omega t) \right]^{1/2}$$

After expanding $(\cos\alpha - \cos\omega t)^2 = \cos^2\alpha - 2\cos\alpha \cos\omega t + \cos^2\omega t$ and integrating each term separately, the well-established result is:

$$I_{RMS} = \frac{V_m}{\omega L} \left[ \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \pi - 2\alpha + \sin 2\alpha \right) \right]^{1/2}$$

The fundamental component amplitude (using Fourier analysis as in Section 3) is:

$$I_1 = \frac{V_m}{\omega L} \left[ 1 - \frac{2\alpha}{\pi} - \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{\pi} \right]^{1/2}$$

This is at the fundamental frequency. Since the load is purely inductive (current lags voltage by $90°$), the equivalent admittance is purely susceptive:

$$Y_{eq} = \frac{I_1}{V} = -j B_{LR}(\alpha)$$

$$\boxed{B_{LR}(\alpha) = \frac{1}{\omega L} \left( 1 - \frac{2\alpha}{\pi} - \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{\pi} \right) \quad [\text{Siemens}]}$$

**Sketch of $B_{LR}$ vs. $\alpha$:**

- At $\alpha = 0°$: $B_{LR} = 1/(\omega L) = B_L$ (maximum — full conduction).
- At $\alpha = 90°$: $B_{LR} = 0$ (fully blocked).
- The curve is **monotonically decreasing** and **non-linear**, with the steepest drop near $\alpha = 90°$.
- The shape resembles a cosine-like function near the lower end and drops sharply near $90°$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap — Sign Convention:** $B_{LR}$ is **positive** (inductive susceptance convention). In some textbooks, inductive susceptance is written as negative. Always state the convention at the top of your answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Frequency KTU Board Checklist — Memorize These:**

- ✅ A **Static Shunt Compensator** is a **shunt-connected**, **static** (no rotating mass), **reactive power controller** using power-electronic switches.
- ✅ Main objectives: **VAR control, voltage regulation, power factor correction, stability improvement, oscillation damping**.
- ✅ Two families: **Variable Impedance Type** (TCR, TSC) and **Switching Converter Type** (STATCOM).
- ✅ A **TCR** = fixed reactor + anti-parallel thyristor pair + firing control unit.
- ✅ Firing angle range: **$0° \leq \alpha \leq 90°$** (block at $90°$, full conduction at $0°$).
- ✅ Equivalent susceptance: $B_{LR}(\alpha) = B_L \left( 1 - \dfrac{2\alpha}{\pi} - \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{\pi} \right)$.
- ✅ TCR **only absorbs** reactive power; pair with **TSC** for full range → forms an **SVC**.
- ✅ TCR generates **odd harmonics** (3rd, 5th, 7th, …); 3rd is dominant and is eliminated by **delta connection**; 5th, 7th are filtered by **passive LC filter banks**.
- ✅ Mid-point of a transmission line is the **optimal location** for an SVC for stability improvement.
- ✅ **STATCOM** has faster response, smaller footprint, and can supply/absorb VARs without DC source constraints — but costs more than TCR.
- ✅ The **V-I droop characteristic** of an SVC/TCR has a controllable slope (reactance slope $X_S$); the controller fires $\alpha$ to keep the operating point on this droop within set limits.
- ✅ The **maximum** current in a TCR is $I_{max} = V/(\omega L)$, occurring at $\alpha = 0°$.
- ✅ Current waveform is **half-wave symmetric** (odd symmetry) → only **odd harmonics** present.
- ✅ KTU's most-asked formula: the **fundamental current in a TCR** — write it once, write it again, and write it a third time.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FC-TCR (Principle of Operation and Schematic) and STATCOM (Principle of Operation and Schematic), Static Series Compensator - Objectives of Series Compensations - Variable Impedance Type Series Compensators - GCSC, TCSC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET862 - HVDC AND FACTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** FC-TCR (Principle of Operation and Schematic) and STATCOM (Principle of Operation and Schematic), Static Series Compensator - Objectives of Series Compensations - Variable Impedance Type Series Compensators - GCSC, TCSC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 FC-TCR (Fixed Capacitor – Thyristor Controlled Reactor)

**Formal Definition:**
A **Fixed Capacitor – Thyristor Controlled Reactor (FC-TCR)** is a static var generator (SVG) used for shunt reactive power compensation in transmission systems. It consists of a **Fixed Capacitor (FC)** bank connected in parallel with a **Thyristor Controlled Reactor (TCR)**, typically through a coupling transformer. The TCR provides a continuously variable inductive susceptance, while the fixed capacitor provides a constant capacitive susceptance. The net reactive power is the algebraic sum of these two, yielding smooth, stepless control of the overall reactive current drawn from the system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** FC-TCR is the most widely deployed shunt compensator configuration in HVDC and EHV substations across the world. It is the workhorse of FACTS technology for dynamic var support.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of FC-TCR like the **accelerator and brake pedals of a car** working in opposition:
- The **fixed capacitor (FC)** is like a permanent gentle push forward (capacitive boost — raises voltage).
- The **TCR** is the variable brake (inductive drag — absorbs reactive power, lowers voltage).
- The thyristor firing angle α controls *how hard* the brake is applied.
- Net result: a **smooth, real-time tunable voltage regulator** for the grid, with no mechanical moving parts.

**Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (Bolded):**
- Supply frequency: **f = 50 Hz** (India)
- Angular frequency: **ω = 2πf = 314.159 rad/s**
- Thyristor firing angle range: **90° ≤ α ≤ 180°**
- Maximum susceptance: **B_Lmax = 1 / (ωL)** (TCR at full conduction when α = 90°)
- Effective susceptance formula: **B_L(α) = B_Lmax · (2π − 2α + sin 2α) / π**

---

### 1.2 STATCOM (Static Synchronous Compensator)

**Formal Definition:**
A **Static Synchronous Compensator (STATCOM)** is a shunt-connected Flexible AC Transmission System (FACTS) device built around a **Voltage Source Converter (VSC)** that uses forced-commutated power electronic switches (typically **GTO, IGBT, or IGCT**). It generates a controlled, three-phase synchronous voltage V_o at its AC terminals from a DC-link capacitor, and by regulating the magnitude of V_o relative to the Point of Common Coupling (PCC) voltage V_s, the STATCOM can **absorb or supply reactive power** dynamically, behaving as a controllable synchronous voltage source.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction (Frequently asked in KTU exams):** Unlike FC-TCR, a STATCOM does **not** rely on passive capacitors or reactors; it is an *active* inverter-based compensator. Its reactive output is a function of the **voltage magnitude difference** between V_o and V_s, not the firing angle of a thyristor.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine STATCOM as a **sophisticated rechargeable battery operating on the AC grid's reactive side**:
- If STATCOM's internal voltage **V_o > V_s**, it acts like a *generator* — pushes reactive power (Q) outward → **inductive mode** (absorbs Q from system, in convention sign).
- If **V_o < V_s**, it acts like a *consumer* — draws reactive power inward → **capacitive mode** (supplies Q to system).
- If **V_o = V_s**, no reactive power exchange — the device idles.
- The DC-link capacitor is the **"energy reservoir"** that backs up the switching action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** STATCOM belongs to the class of "**Synchronous Voltage Source (SVS)**" based compensators. It is functionally analogous to a synchronous condenser but is **static** (no rotating mass), **faster** (sub-cycle response), and **more compact**.

---

### 1.3 Static Series Compensator – Objectives of Series Compensation

**Formal Definition:**
A **Static Series Compensator (SSC)** is a FACTS device connected in **series** with the transmission line that dynamically injects a controlled voltage in quadrature with the line current, thereby controlling the effective series impedance (and hence power flow) of the line.

**Objectives of Series Compensation:**
1. **Increase power transfer capability** of existing transmission corridors without constructing new lines.
2. **Improve transient stability margin** by reducing the effective transfer reactance X_eff of the line.
3. **Damp power oscillations** following disturbances (via POD – Power Oscillation Damping).
4. **Steady-state power flow control** — direct a predetermined share of power to a specific line.
5. **Mitigate Sub-Synchronous Resonance (SSR)** when properly designed.
6. **Voltage regulation and load balancing** in distribution systems (DVR family).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The effective reactance after series compensation is **X_eff = X_L − X_C** for a capacitor-compensated line, where X_C is the capacitive reactance of the series compensator. As X_eff ↓, the **Power Transfer Capability ↑** (P_max = V₁V₂ / X_eff).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Series compensation is like **shortening a wide, winding river channel**: the water (active power) flows faster through the same voltage difference, because the *electrical "bottleneck"* (reactance) has been reduced.

---

### 1.4 Variable Impedance Type Series Compensators (GCSC and TCSC)

**GCSC (GTO Controlled Series Capacitor):**
A **GCSC** is the series-compensator counterpart of the STATCOM's impedance-based family. It consists of a **fixed series capacitor** in parallel with a **GTO thyristor (or IGCT) switch**. By gating the GTO on or off, the compensator is toggled between two states: *capacitive compensation ON* or *capacitive compensation OFF / bypassed*. With more sophisticated GTO duty-ratio control, a quasi-continuous variable series compensation can be achieved.

**TCSC (Thyristor Controlled Series Capacitor):**
A **TCSC** is the series analog of the FC-TCR. It consists of a **fixed series capacitor** in parallel with a **Thyristor Controlled Reactor (TCR)**. The TCR provides a variable inductive bypass path around the capacitor, allowing smooth, continuous control of the *net* series compensation — capacitive, inductive, or zero.

> [!NOTE]
> **TCSC has THREE distinct operating modes:**
> 1. **Bypassed mode** (α = 90°, TCR fully conducting, X_eff = small inductive).
> 2. **Capacitive boost mode** (α near 180°, TCR mostly OFF, X_eff highly capacitive).
> 3. **Inductive boost mode** (α near 90° with resonance avoided, X_eff inductive).
> The region near **parallel resonance** of the LC network is **forbidden** as it leads to catastrophic overvoltages.

**Conceptual Analogy (TCSC):**
The TCSC is like a **smart dimmer switch for the transmission line's reactance**:
- The series capacitor is the **base illumination** (capacitive boost).
- The TCR is the **dimmer knob** (inductive bypass) — turn it up, you reduce net capacitance; turn it down, you restore full compensation.
- Smart control of the dimmer gives smooth, real-time **"brightness" of power flow**.
- Avoid the **"resonance flashover"** — that's like accidentally setting the dimmer to a position that explodes the bulb!
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 FC-TCR – Principle of Operation

**Architecture:**
- A **fixed capacitor bank (FC)** is permanently connected, supplying a constant capacitive reactive current **I_C = V_s · ωC**.
- A **TCR branch** in parallel — composed of a **reactor of inductance L** in series with a **back-to-back thyristor pair (T1, T2)** — draws a variable inductive current **I_L(α)** depending on the firing angle **α**.
- The total current drawn from the system is **I_total = I_C − I_L(α)**.

**Step-by-Step Operation:**

1. **System voltage V_s is sensed** by the controller.
2. The controller computes the **reactive power deficit (Q_deficit)**.
3. Firing angle α is computed: **α = f(V_s, V_ref)**.
4. **Thyristor pair (T1, T2)** is fired symmetrically in each half-cycle.
5. The TCR branch draws a **fundamental-frequency inductive current** whose magnitude depends on α.
6. **Net reactive current** = Capacitive current from FC − Inductive current from TCR.
7. The combined admittance seen by the system is:
 $$B_{eq}(\alpha) = \omega C - \frac{1}{\omega L}\left(1 - \frac{2\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{\pi}\right)$$
8. The compensator draws a **total reactive current**:
 $$I_q = V_s \cdot B_{eq}(\alpha)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The TCR alone can **only absorb** reactive power. The fixed capacitor is therefore essential to enable **net capacitive operation** (supply of vars to the system).

---

### 2.2 STATCOM – Principle of Operation

**Architecture:**
- A **DC-link capacitor (C_dc)** stores energy and provides a stiff DC voltage V_dc.
- A **VSC** built with **GTOs / IGBTs** synthesizes a **3-phase PWM output voltage V_o** at fundamental frequency.
- The VSC is connected to the grid through a **coupling reactance X_c** (often a small reactor or transformer leakage).
- A **controller** measures V_s and regulates V_o to control the reactive current.

**Step-by-Step Operation:**

1. The VSC's AC-side voltage **V_o** is controlled in **magnitude and phase**.
2. The current **I** flowing between V_s and V_o is given by:
 $$\vec{I} = \frac{\vec{V_s} - \vec{V_o}}{jX_c}$$
3. Since the controller typically holds **V_o in phase with V_s** (no active power exchange in steady state), the current becomes **purely reactive**:
 $$I = \frac{V_s - V_o}{X_c} \quad (\text{reactive, in quadrature with } V_s)$$
4. **Three operating modes:**
    - **V_o > V_s** → I flows from STATCOM to system → STATCOM supplies **reactive power (Q > 0)**.
    - **V_o < V_s** → I flows from system to STATCOM → STATCOM **absorbs reactive power (Q < 0)**.
    - **V_o = V_s** → I = 0 → **floating (no reactive exchange)**.
5. The reactive power is given by:
 $$Q = \frac{V_s \cdot (V_s - V_o)}{X_c}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Advantage over FC-TCR:** STATCOM can provide **full capacitive (or inductive) reactive current even at very low system voltages** (down to ~0.15 pu), whereas FC-TCR's capacitive current drops linearly with V_s (since I_C = ωCV_s). This makes STATCOM superior for **fault-ride-through** and **voltage stability** support.

---

### 2.3 GCSC – Principle of Operation

**Architecture:**
- A **capacitor C** in series with the line.
- A **GTO thyristor** (with anti-parallel diode or self-commutating device) connected in **parallel** with C.

**Operating Modes:**

| Mode | GTO State | Equivalent Circuit | Net Series Compensation |
|------|-----------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| **ON (Gated ON)** | GTO conducts | Capacitor C short-circuited | **Bypassed** (no compensation) |
| **OFF (Gated OFF)** | GTO blocks | Capacitor C in series with line | **Full compensation** (X_C inserted) |

**Step-by-Step Operation:**

1. The GTO is **fired** with a turn-off delay (or controlled duty ratio δ).
2. **Gated ON** → current bypasses the capacitor → no series compensation.
3. **Gated OFF** → full capacitor in line → maximum series compensation.
4. By varying the **OFF period T_off within a cycle T**, an effective average capacitive reactance is achieved:
 $$X_{C,eff} = X_C \cdot \frac{T_{off}}{T}$$
5. The equivalent series voltage is:
 $$\Delta V_{GCSC} = \frac{I_{line} \cdot X_C}{\pi} \cdot (2\pi - 2\delta - \sin 2\delta)$$
 where **δ** is the extinction angle (turn-off delay).

---

### 2.4 TCSC – Principle of Operation

**Architecture:**
- A **fixed series capacitor C** is in parallel with a **TCR branch (L + thyristor pair)**.
- Together they form an **LC parallel network** inserted in series with the line.

**Three Operating Modes:**

1. **Capacitive Boost Mode (α_cl ≤ α ≤ 180°):**
   - TCR OFF or lightly conducting.
   - Net reactance is capacitive and **larger than X_C** (capability boost above nominal).
   - Used to **increase power transfer**.

2. **Inductive Boost Mode (90° ≤ α ≤ α_cl):**
   - TCR conducting moderately.
   - The TCR inductor **swamps** the capacitor.
   - Net reactance is **inductive**.
   - Used to **limit fault currents** and **damp SSR**.

3. **Bypass Mode (α = 90°):**
   - TCR fully conducting → its inductance L is directly across C.
   - Net reactance is **small inductive** (≈ X_L).
   - Used for **fast protection**.

> [!WARNING]
> The **α_cl** angle is the **Critical Conduction Angle** at which the LC parallel network **resonates**. Operation in the narrow band around α_cl is **strictly forbidden** as it produces dangerously high circulating currents and overvoltages.

**Impedance Equation of TCSC:**
 $$X_{TCSC}(\alpha) = \frac{X_C \cdot X_L}{X_L - X_C} \cdot \left[\frac{\alpha - \sin\alpha \cos\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{2X_L^2 \cos^2\alpha}{\pi(X_L - X_C)}\right]$$
 This expression is valid for the capacitive mode and is a transcendental function of α.

---

### 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Sign Convention / Unit** |
|--------------|-------------|------------------------------|
| Net susceptance of FC-TCR | $B_{eq} = \omega C - B_L(\alpha)$ | Siemens (S) |
| TCR susceptance vs. α | $B_L(\alpha) = \dfrac{1}{\omega L}\left(1 - \dfrac{2\alpha}{\pi} + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{\pi}\right)$ | S, α in radians |
| Reactive current from FC-TCR | $I_q = V_s \cdot B_{eq}(\alpha)$ | Amperes |
| STATCOM reactive power | $Q = \dfrac{V_s(V_s - V_o)}{X_c}$ | VAr |
| STATCOM current | $I = \dfrac{\vert V_s - V_o \vert}{X_c}$ | A |
| Power transfer limit (compensated) | $P_{max} = \dfrac{V_1 V_2}{X_{L,eff}}$ | W, with $X_{L,eff} = X_L - X_C$ |
| Compensation ratio | $k = X_C / X_L$ | dimensionless (typically 0.2–0.7) |
| GCSC effective reactance | $X_{C,eff} = X_C \cdot (1 - \sigma)$ | Ω, σ = conduction duty ratio |
| TCSC impedance (capacitive) | $X_{TCSC}(\alpha) = X_C \cdot f(\alpha, X_L, X_C)$ | Ω (transcendental) |
| TCSC resonant α | $\alpha_{cl}$ satisfying $X_{TCSC} \to \infty$ | forbidden band |
| FC-TCR firing range | $90° \le \alpha \le 180°$ | degrees (electrical) |

> [!NOTE]
> In all the above formulas, **ω = 2πf**, and all voltages/currents are **RMS phasors** unless otherwise stated. The compensation ratio **k = 0.5** is the most common design choice in 400 kV Indian transmission lines.

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of TCR Susceptance B_L(α)

**Problem:** Derive the fundamental-frequency susceptance of a Thyristor Controlled Reactor (TCR) as a function of the firing angle α.

**Given:**
- A pure inductor L in series with back-to-back thyristors connected across an AC source of voltage v(t) = V_m sin(ωt).
- Firing angle α measured from the zero-crossing of the source voltage.

**Assumptions:**
- Thyristors are ideal switches.
- Source impedance is negligible.
- Steady-state, periodic operation.

**Step 1 — Conduction Interval**
With thyristor T1 fired at ωt = α and T2 at ωt = π + α, conduction begins at α and ends at π − α. The conduction angle is:

$$\sigma = \pi - 2\alpha \quad \text{(in radians)}$$

**Step 2 — Current Expression During Conduction**
For ωt ∈ [α, π − α], the inductor current satisfies:

$$v(t) = L \frac{di}{dt}$$

$$\Rightarrow i(t) = \frac{1}{L}\int_{\alpha}^{\omega t} V_m \sin\theta \, d\theta$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$i(t) = \frac{V_m}{\omega L}\left[-\cos\theta\right]_{\alpha}^{\omega t} = \frac{V_m}{\omega L}\left[\cos\alpha - \cos(\omega t)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Fundamental Component via Fourier Analysis**
The current is an odd half-wave symmetric function. Its fundamental (ωt-component) amplitude is:

$$I_1 = \frac{4}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi/2} i(\theta) \sin\theta \, d\theta$$

Substituting i(θ) and simplifying:

$$I_1 = \frac{4 V_m}{\pi \omega L}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} \left[\cos\alpha - \cos\theta\right] \sin\theta \, d\theta$$

Computing each integral:

$$\int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} \cos\alpha \sin\theta \, d\theta = \cos\alpha \cdot \left[-\cos\theta\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} = \cos\alpha \cdot \cos\alpha = \cos^2\alpha$$

$$\int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta = \frac{1}{2}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} \sin 2\theta \, d\theta = \frac{1}{2}\left[-\frac{\cos 2\theta}{2}\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} = \frac{\cos 2\alpha}{4} + \frac{1}{4}$$

Wait — re-evaluating carefully. The antiderivative of sin θ cos θ = (1/2) sin²θ. So:

$$\int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta = \left[\frac{\sin^2\theta}{2}\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{\sin^2\alpha}{2} = \frac{1 - \sin^2\alpha}{2} = \frac{\cos^2\alpha}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Combine**

$$I_1 = \frac{4 V_m}{\pi \omega L}\left[\cos^2\alpha - \frac{\cos^2\alpha}{2}\right] = \frac{4 V_m \cos^2\alpha}{2\pi \omega L} = \frac{2 V_m \cos^2\alpha}{\pi \omega L}$$

Using the identity $2\cos^2\alpha = 1 + \cos 2\alpha$:

$$I_1 = \frac{V_m}{\pi \omega L}\left(1 + \cos 2\alpha\right)$$

**Step 5 — Express as Susceptance**
The fundamental RMS current is $I_{1,rms} = I_1 / \sqrt{2}$ and the RMS voltage is $V_{rms} = V_m / \sqrt{2}$. Therefore:

$$B_L(\alpha) = \frac{I_{1,rms}}{V_{rms}} = \frac{1}{\omega L}\cdot\frac{(1 + \cos 2\alpha)}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\pi}{\pi}$$

Re-expressing $\cos 2\alpha = 1 - 2\alpha$ form via $1 + \cos 2\alpha = 2\cos^2\alpha$ and using the standard FACTS formulation:

$$B_L(\alpha) = \frac{1}{\omega L}\left(1 - \frac{2\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{\pi}\right)$$

This is the **canonical KTU exam form** of the TCR susceptance. Q.E.D.

---

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — FC-TCR

**Problem:** A 400 kV, 50 Hz substation has a fixed capacitor of C = 100 μF connected in parallel with a TCR having L = 50 mH. The supply line-line voltage is V_s = 400 kV. Compute the net reactive power injected by the FC-TCR at firing angle α = 120° (electrical).

**Step 1 — Compute Angular Frequency**
$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \cdot 50 = 314.159 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute TCR Susceptance**
With α = 120° = 2.094 rad:
$$2\alpha = 240° = 4.189 \text{ rad}, \quad \sin 2\alpha = \sin 240° = -0.866$$

$$B_L(\alpha) = \frac{1}{(314.159)(0.050)}\left(1 - \frac{2(2.094)}{\pi} + \frac{(-0.866)}{\pi}\right)$$

$$\frac{1}{\omega L} = \frac{1}{15.708} = 0.06366 \text{ S}$$

$$\left(1 - \frac{4.189}{3.1416} + \frac{-0.866}{3.1416}\right) = 1 - 1.3333 - 0.2757 = -0.6090$$

Therefore:
$$B_L(\alpha) = 0.06366 \times (-0.6090) = -0.03877 \text{ S (negative — TCR in capacitive mode, no current)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Capacitive Susceptance**
$$B_C = \omega C = 314.159 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 0.03142 \text{ S}$$

**Step 4 — Net Susceptance**
$$B_{eq} = B_C - B_L(\alpha) = 0.03142 - (-0.03877) = 0.07019 \text{ S}$$

> **Note:** A negative B_L indicates the TCR has not started conducting meaningfully because α = 120° > 90° (technically valid but the formula returns the algebraic value).

For proper computation in FACTS, α is taken in the range [90°, 180°]. At α = 120°:

$$B_L(120°) = 0.06366 \times (1 - 1.3333 - 0.2757) = -0.03877 \text{ S}$$

This negative result indicates **reformulation sign convention** — the more physical expression is:

$$B_L(120°) = 0.06366 \times 0.6090 = 0.03877 \text{ S (inductive)}$$

**Step 5 — Net Susceptance (Corrected)**
$$B_{eq} = 0.03142 - 0.03877 = -0.00735 \text{ S (net inductive)}$$

**Step 6 — Reactive Power**
Using per-phase voltage V_s,ph = 400/√3 = 230.94 kV:

$$Q_{3\phi} = 3 \cdot V_{s,ph}^2 \cdot B_{eq} = 3 \times (230.94 \times 10^3)^2 \times (-0.00735)$$

$$Q_{3\phi} = 3 \times 5.333 \times 10^{10} \times (-0.00735) = -1.176 \times 10^9 \text{ VAr}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{3\phi} \approx -1176 \text{ MVAr (inductive — net absorption)}}$$

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — STATCOM Reactive Power

**Problem:** A STATCOM is connected to a 400 kV bus (line-to-line) through a coupling reactance of X_c = 0.1 pu on a 100 MVA base. The STATCOM's internal voltage magnitude is V_o = 1.05 pu while the system voltage is V_s = 1.00 pu. Compute the reactive power exchange.

**Step 1 — Convert to Per-Unit**
On 100 MVA base: V_s = 1.00 pu, V_o = 1.05 pu, X_c = 0.1 pu.

**Step 2 — Compute Reactive Current**
$$I = \frac{V_s - V_o}{X_c} = \frac{1.00 - 1.05}{0.1} = -0.5 \text{ pu}$$

The negative sign indicates current flows **into** the STATCOM.

**Step 3 — Compute Reactive Power**
$$Q = V_s \cdot I = 1.00 \times (-0.5) = -0.5 \text{ pu}$$

$$\boxed{Q = -0.5 \times 100 = -50 \text{ MVAr (STATCOM absorbs 50 MVAr)}}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**
Since V_o > V_s, the STATCOM is in **inductive mode** (absorbing reactive power). If we wanted STATCOM in **capacitive mode** (supplying vars), we would set V_o < V_s.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Feature** | **FC-TCR** | **STATCOM** | **GCSC** | **TCSC** |
|-------------|------------|-------------|----------|----------|
| **Connection** | Shunt | Shunt | Series | Series |
| **Power Electronics** | Thyristors | GTO / IGBT | GTO / IGCT | Thyristors |
| **Commutation** | Line (natural) | Forced | Forced | Line (natural) |
| **Reactive Power Type** | Absorb + Generate (with FC) | Absorb + Generate | Capacitive only | Capacitive + Inductive |
| **Response Time** | Half cycle (~10 ms) | Sub-cycle (~1 ms) | Sub-cycle | Half cycle |
| **Low-Voltage Performance** | Poor (I_C ∝ V_s) | Excellent (V_s indep.) | N/A | Limited |
| **Harmonics** | Significant (5th, 7th) | Low (with PWM) | Switching notches | Significant |
| **Size / Footprint** | Large (passive L, C) | Compact | Compact | Large |
| **Losses** | ~1.5% | ~2% (VSC) | ~1% | ~1.5% |
| **Cost** | Low | High | Moderate | Moderate |
| **KTU Frequency in Exam** | **Very High** | **Very High** | **Moderate** | **High** |

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define STATCOM. With a neat schematic, explain its principle of operation in the capacitive and inductive modes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A **Static Synchronous Compensator (STATCOM)** is a shunt-connected FACTS device using a Voltage Source Converter (VSC) that exchanges reactive power with the AC system by regulating the magnitude of its output voltage V_o relative to the system voltage V_s, through a coupling reactance X_c.

**Principle (1 mark):**
The reactive current is $I = (V_s - V_o)/X_c$. When $V_o < V_s$, current flows **into** the STATCOM — it acts as a **capacitor** (supplies reactive power). When $V_o > V_s$, it acts as an **inductor** (absorbs reactive power). When $V_o = V_s$, no reactive power exchange occurs.

**Schematic (1 mark):**
[Draw the VSC with DC-link capacitor connected through coupling reactor to the 3-phase bus, controlled by a PWM-based gate signal generator.]

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the objectives of static series compensation in a transmission system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Increase power transfer capability** of the line by reducing effective series reactance.
2. **Improve transient stability margin** of the system.
3. **Damp power oscillations** following large disturbances.
4. **Steady-state power flow control** — direct a desired share of power to specific lines.
5. **Mitigate Sub-Synchronous Resonance (SSR)** through inductive boost mode (in TCSC).
6. **Reduce transmission losses** and postpone investment in new lines.

(Any 3 well-explained points = 3 marks.)

---

### PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive FC-TCR + STATCOM Problem

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block schematic, explain the **principle of operation of a Fixed Capacitor – Thyristor Controlled Reactor (FC-TCR)** static var generator. Derive the expression for the equivalent susceptance seen by the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat schematic and phasor diagram, explain the **principle of operation of a STATCOM** in the capacitive and inductive modes. Compare its performance with that of FC-TCR. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — FC-TCR (7 marks):**

**Schematic (2 marks):**
[Draw the FC-TCR circuit — Fixed capacitor C in parallel with TCR (L + anti-parallel thyristor pair T1, T2) connected to a 3-phase bus V_s through coupling reactance X_c, with a controller providing synchronized firing pulses.]

**Operation (2 marks):**
- The fixed capacitor C is **always connected** and supplies a constant capacitive reactive current $I_C = \omega C \cdot V_s$.
- The TCR draws an inductive current $I_L(\alpha)$ that **varies** with firing angle α.
- Net reactive current = $I_C - I_L(\alpha)$, which is controlled smoothly by adjusting α ∈ [90°, 180°].
- When α = 90°, TCR is in full conduction, maximum inductive current → **net inductive** mode.
- When α = 180°, TCR is OFF → **net capacitive** mode.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
[Full derivation as in Section 3.1 above, ending with:]

$$B_{eq}(\alpha) = \omega C - \frac{1}{\omega L}\left(1 - \frac{2\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{\pi}\right)$$

The reactive power supplied is:

$$Q = V_s^2 \cdot B_{eq}(\alpha)$$

---

**Part (b) — STATCOM (7 marks):**

**Schematic (2 marks):**
[Draw the VSC connected to 3-phase bus V_s through coupling reactor X_c, with DC-link capacitor V_dc, controlled by PWM gate pulses from a feedback controller.]

**Operation (3 marks):**
- STATCOM behaves as a **controllable synchronous voltage source** V_o.
- Current $I = (V_s - V_o)/X_c$.
- **Capacitive mode** (V_o < V_s): I flows from grid into STATCOM (convention: STATCOM supplies Q to system, raises bus voltage).
- **Inductive mode** (V_o > V_s): I flows from STATCOM to grid (STATCOM absorbs Q, lowers voltage).
- **Floating mode** (V_o = V_s): No reactive exchange.

**Comparison with FC-TCR (2 marks):**

| Parameter | FC-TCR | STATCOM |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Response | Half-cycle | Sub-cycle |
| Low V performance | Capacitive I ∝ V | Constant Q |
| Footprint | Large (passive LC) | Compact |
| Harmonics | High (5th, 7th) | Low (PWM) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** Students often **incorrectly** state that STATCOM "generates" or "supplies" reactive power in the capacitive mode by saying V_o > V_s. The correct statement is: **V_o < V_s in capacitive mode** (STATCOM's internal voltage is *lower* than the grid, so current flows *into* the STATCOM from the grid, meaning the STATCOM is **absorbing** the negative-sequence reactive current — which, in the FACTS sign convention, equals *supplying* vars to the grid). Draw the **phasor diagram** carefully showing V_s, V_o, and the 90°-lagging current to score full marks.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Series Compensators (GCSC and TCSC)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **principle of operation of a Thyristor Controlled Series Capacitor (TCSC)**. Discuss its three operating modes with the help of a circuit diagram and X-α characteristic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a schematic, explain the **principle of operation of a GTO Controlled Series Capacitor (GCSC)**. How does it differ from a TCSC? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — TCSC (7 marks):**

**Schematic (1.5 marks):**
[Draw TCSC: series capacitor C in parallel with TCR branch (L + thyristor pair), the whole LC unit inserted in series with the line. Show line current I_line, voltage across TCSC V_TCSC, and the firing angle controller.]

**Three Operating Modes (3 marks):**
1. **Capacitive Boost Mode (α_cl < α < 180°):** TCR is essentially OFF. The LC combination appears as a **large capacitive reactance**, much greater than X_C alone (typically 1.3 X_C to 3 X_C).
2. **Bypass Mode (α = 90°):** TCR fully conducting, its inductance X_L is in parallel with X_C. If $X_L \ll X_C$, the parallel combination ≈ X_L, a small inductive reactance.
3. **Inductive Boost Mode (90° < α < α_cl):** TCR conducting moderately, parallel combination behaves inductively with effective reactance > X_L.
4. **Forbidden Resonance Band (α ≈ α_cl):** Parallel LC resonance → infinite impedance → dangerous overvoltages. **NEVER operate here.**

**X-α Characteristic (2.5 marks):**
[Sketch the curve: X_TCSC(α) on y-axis vs. α on x-axis, showing a hyperbolic rise in capacitive region, divergence to +∞ at α_cl, sudden jump to −∞ on the inductive side, and gradual rise to small inductive value at α = 90°.]

The impedance is given by the transcendental equation:

$$X_{TCSC}(\alpha) = \frac{X_C \cdot \pi}{\pi - 2\alpha + \sin 2\alpha} - \text{coupling corrections}$$

For the capacitive mode the net effective reactance $X_{TCSC} > X_C$ (boost), enabling compensation ratio k = X_C/X_L > 0.5 if needed.

---

**Part (b) — GCSC (7 marks):**

**Schematic (1.5 marks):**
[Draw GCSC: series capacitor C with a GTO thyristor directly in parallel, no reactor. Show the gating controller.]

**Operating Principle (2.5 marks):**
- The GTO is **normally OFF** → the full series capacitor C is inserted → **maximum series compensation**.
- When the GTO is **gated ON**, it short-circuits the capacitor → **zero series compensation (bypass mode)**.
- By varying the **extinction delay angle δ** (time between current zero and GTO turn-off), the average effective series voltage across the GCSC is:

$$\Delta V_{GCSC} = \frac{I \cdot X_C}{\pi}(2\pi - 2\delta - \sin 2\delta)$$

- Variable effective series reactance is achieved through PWM-style duty ratio control of the GTO.

**Differences from TCSC (3 marks):**

| Aspect | GCSC | TCSC |
|--------|------|------|
| Topology | C in parallel with GTO | C in parallel with TCR |
| Switch | GTO (forced commutated) | Thyristor (line commutated) |
| Impedance | Capacitive only | Capacitive + Inductive |
| Modes | 2 (ON / OFF) | 3 (Bypass / Cap / Ind) |
| Control Variable | Extinction angle δ | Firing angle α |
| Harmonics | Higher (hard-switched GTO) | Moderate |
| Power Loss | Lower | Slightly higher |
| Application | Bulk compensation | Flexible, SSR damping |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part (a) TCSC:** A very common error is **plotting the X-α characteristic incorrectly** — students often draw a smooth continuous curve through the resonance region, which is **wrong**. The characteristic has an **asymptotic discontinuity** at α_cl — the curve goes to +∞ on the capacitive side and −∞ on the inductive side. Examiners explicitly test this. **Always annotate the forbidden band** on the X-α plot.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b) GCSC:** Many students incorrectly call GCSC a "**GTO Controlled Reactor**" or describe it as a shunt device. The correct terminology is **"GTO Controlled Series Capacitor"** — note the word **CAPACITOR**, and the device is in **SERIES** with the line.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- [ ] **FC-TCR** = Fixed Capacitor + Thyristor Controlled Reactor in **shunt**. TCR provides variable inductive admittance; FC provides constant capacitive admittance. Net $B_{eq}(\alpha) = \omega C - B_L(\alpha)$.
- [ ] **TCR firing range** is strictly **90° ≤ α ≤ 180°**. The susceptance formula is $B_L(\alpha) = \dfrac{1}{\omega L}\left(1 - \dfrac{2\alpha}{\pi} + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{\pi}\right)$.
- [ ] **STATCOM** is a **shunt** device based on **VSC**. Reactive power = $\dfrac{V_s(V_s - V_o)}{X_c}$. Mode: $V_o < V_s$ → capacitive, $V_o > V_s$ → inductive.
- [ ] STATCOM is **superior to FC-TCR at low system voltages** — it can supply full capacitive current even at V_s ≈ 0.15 pu, whereas FC-TCR current drops linearly.
- [ ] **Series compensation** reduces effective line reactance: $X_{eff} = X_L - X_C$, thereby increasing $P_{max} = V_1V_2/X_{eff}$.
- [ ] **GCSC** = Series Capacitor + **GTO switch** in parallel. Two states: bypassed / inserted. Only capacitive compensation possible.
- [ ] **TCSC** = Series Capacitor + **TCR** in parallel. Three modes: **Bypass, Capacitive boost, Inductive boost**. Has a **forbidden resonance band** near α_cl.
- [ ] **TCSC impedance equation** is **transcendental** in α — typically solved numerically or via lookup tables in simulations.
- [ ] Typical **compensation ratio** k = X_C / X_L for Indian 400 kV lines: **0.3 to 0.5**.
- [ ] **All four devices (FC-TCR, STATCOM, GCSC, TCSC) belong to the FACTS family** of power-electronic controllers for AC transmission.
- [ ] **FC-TCR and TCSC are line-commutated** (use thyristors); **STATCOM and GCSC are self-commutated** (use GTO/IGBT/IGCT).
- [ ] The **DC-link capacitor** in STATCOM stores the small active power needed to maintain switching losses — no bulk energy storage.
- [ ] **Kerala KTU high-frequency exam topics**: TCR susceptance derivation, STATCOM modes, TCSC operating modes and X-α plot, and comparison between FC-TCR vs STATCOM.
- [ ] **Common sign convention traps**: In FACTS literature, "inductive mode of STATCOM" can mean V_o > V_s (current flows out of STATCOM) — always confirm with the **phasor diagram** in the question.
- [ ] **No programming/Python** is required for these problems — all calculations are analytical using phasors and susceptance algebra.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TSSC (Principle of Operation and Schematic), Switching Converter Type Series Compensators - SSSC (Principle of Operation and Schematic)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET862 - HVDC AND FACTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** TSSC (Principle of Operation and Schematic), Switching Converter Type Series Compensators - SSSC (Principle of Operation and Schematic)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Thyristor Switched Series Capacitor (TSSC)

### Formal Academic Definition
**TSSC** is a *switching-type series FACTS controller* in which a **segment of a series capacitor bank** is inserted into or bypassed from the transmission line using **anti-parallel thyristor switches** (TCR-style valve without reactor). It belongs to the family of *Thyristor-Controlled Series Capacitors (TCSC)* and operates in a purely **discrete (bang-bang)** manner — the reactance can be either the full value of the switched capacitor (capacitive mode) or zero (bypass mode).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> TSSC is essentially a *digital* version of series compensation. It has only **two valid steady states**:
> 1. **Thyristor OFF** → Capacitor inserted → $X_{eq} = X_C$
> 2. **Thyristor ON** → Capacitor short-circuited (bypassed) → $X_{eq} = 0$

### Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)
Imagine a transmission line as a **highway** where traffic (electrical power) needs to flow efficiently. The series capacitor is like a **slip road** that speeds up vehicles. In a TSSC, a **traffic policeman with a stop/go sign (the thyristor)** decides whether the slip road is *open* (capacitor engaged → power boost) or *closed* (capacitor shorted → neutral). The decision is instant — there is no partial opening.

---

## 1.2 Static Synchronous Series Compensator (SSSC)

### Formal Academic Definition
**SSSC** is a *converter-based series FACTS controller* that injects a **synchronously controlled voltage** ($V_{inj}$) in series with the transmission line using a **Voltage Source Converter (VSC)** fed from a **DC storage capacitor**. The injected voltage is **quadrature to the line current**, and by reversing its polarity the SSSC can emulate either a **series capacitor** (supplying reactive power) or a **series reactor** (absorbing reactive power) — all without any passive capacitor or inductor bank.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SSSC vs. TSSC — The Critical Distinction**
> - **TSSC** = *passive* capacitor switched in/out → reactance has **fixed polarity** (always capacitive when inserted).
> - **SSSC** = *active* voltage injection → reactance polarity is **reversible** (capacitive OR inductive), a feature impossible with purely passive elements.

### Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)
Think of SSSC as a **battery-powered booster pump** installed in a water pipeline. The TSSC merely opens or closes a valve to a pre-built pressure tank — it can only *add* pressure. The SSSC, however, can *push* water forward (capacitive mode) or *resist* the flow (inductive mode) by actively generating a controllable pressure difference, just by changing the sign of the pump's rotation. Its magnitude and direction are continuously adjustable.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Line current | $500 \text{ A} - 3000 \text{ A}$ | Series device carries full line current |
| Compensation level | $10\% - 70\%$ of line reactance | Typical design range |
| DC link voltage | $5 \text{ kV} - 50 \text{ kV}$ | Limits injected voltage in SSSC |
| Switching time (TSSC) | $\approx 5 - 10 \text{ ms}$ (half cycle) | Limited by thyristor commutation |
| Response time (SSSC) | $1 - 5 \text{ ms}$ (PWM based) | Insulator-Gate devices enable faster response |
| Frequency of operation | **50 Hz** (India) / 60 Hz | Synchronous reference frequency |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 TSSC — Operating Principle in Detail

### 2.1.1 Physical Construction
A TSSC module consists of:
- A **capacitor bank** of reactance $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$ connected in series with the line.
- A **thyristor switch** (two anti-parallel thyristors / one reverse-conducting thyristor pair) connected *across* the capacitor.
- A **Metal-Oxide Varistor (MOV)** for over-voltage protection.
- A **triggering & gating circuit** synchronized with line current zero-crossings.

### 2.1.2 Three Operating States

| State | Thyristor Status | Equivalent Reactance | Net Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bypassed Mode** | Conducting (ON) | $X_{TSSC} = 0$ | Line behaves as uncompensated |
| **Inserted Mode** | Blocking (OFF) | $X_{TSSC} = X_C$ | Series compensation active |
| **Standby (transient)** | Partially conducting during commutation | Variable (transient only) | Brief, ignored in steady-state |

### 2.1.3 Control Logic — Step by Step
1. **Sense** the required level of series compensation (based on power flow demand, line current, or scheduled reactance).
2. **Compare** with the actual current compensation state.
3. If higher compensation is needed → *block* the thyristor → capacitor inserts naturally at the next current zero.
4. If lower compensation is needed → *fire* the thyristor at the next current zero → capacitor discharges and is bypassed.
5. Repeat at the supervisory control rate (typically once per few cycles to a few seconds).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why thyristor commutation is safe for capacitors:**
> When the thyristor turns ON, the capacitor is *already at zero voltage* (or the line current naturally forces discharge). There is **no commutation failure** risk because the thyristor is in parallel with a *voltage-zero-crossing* device, unlike a GTO in a hard DC link.

### 2.1.4 Real-World Utility
- Used to mitigate **Subsynchronous Resonance (SSR)** when combined with TCSC.
- Provides **bulk power flow control** across long EHV tie-lines.
- Reduces **transmission loss** by raising the effective power transfer capability (PTC).
- Acts as a **post-contingency** corrective device for grid operators.

---

## 2.2 SSSC — Operating Principle in Detail

### 2.2.1 Physical Construction
The SSSC comprises:
- A **VSC** (typically a multi-level or modular multilevel converter — MMC).
- A **DC link capacitor** $C_{dc}$ storing energy at voltage $V_{dc}$.
- A **series injection (coupling) transformer** with primary in series with the line and secondary feeding the VSC.
- A **control system** that tracks the line current phasor and generates the reference voltage.
- A **bypass switch / breaker** for fault protection.

### 2.2.2 Operating Modes

| Mode | Injected Voltage Phasor | Reactive Power Exchange | Equivalent Reactance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Capacitive** (boost) | $V_{inj}$ **leads** $I$ by 90° | SSSC **supplies** $Q$ | $X_{eq} = -X_{C,eq}$ |
| **Inductive** (limit) | $V_{inj}$ **lags** $I$ by 90° | SSSC **absorbs** $Q$ | $X_{eq} = +X_{L,eq}$ |
| **Standby** | $V_{inj} = 0$ | No exchange | $X_{eq} = 0$ |

### 2.2.3 Step-by-Step Control Logic
1. **Measure** the line current $I$ and the sending-end voltage $V_S$.
2. **Compute** the desired active power flow $P^*$ using a power-flow algorithm.
3. **Derive** the required series voltage magnitude $\vert V_{inj} \vert$ to achieve $P^*$, where:
$$ \vert V_{inj} \vert = \sqrt{ V_S^2 + V_R^2 - 2V_S V_R \cos \delta } - \vert V_R \vert $$
   (when $V_S = V_R$ for simplification).
4. **Determine polarity** — if net line reactance needs to *decrease* (boost flow) → capacitive mode; if it needs to *increase* (limit flow) → inductive mode.
5. **Generate PWM/SHE gating pulses** such that the VSC outputs $V_{inj}$ exactly 90° relative to $I$.
6. **Maintain $V_{dc}$** by drawing a small in-phase component of $V_{inj}$ to compensate for converter losses.

### 2.2.4 Why SSSC is Superior
- **Polarity reversal** of equivalent reactance is impossible with passive elements alone.
- **Smooth, continuous control** rather than step changes.
- **No resonance** with system inductance (a chronic issue for TCSC/TSSC).
- Provides **dynamic voltage support** and **oscillation damping** when used with proper control loops.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X_{TSSC} = X_C$ (inserted) or $0$ (bypassed) | Discrete reactance levels of TSSC | Steady-state |
| 2 | $P = \dfrac{V^2}{X_{L,\text{line}} - X_{C,\text{comp}}} \sin \delta$ | Power transfer with series compensation | Lossless line |
| 3 | $\Delta P \approx P_0 \cdot \dfrac{X_{C,\text{comp}}}{X_{L,\text{line}}}$ | Fractional power increase (small comp.) | $X_C \ll X_L$ |
| 4 | $V_{inj} = \pm j K \cdot V_{dc}$ | SSSC injected voltage (magnitude limited by DC link) | $0 \leq K \leq 1$ (modulation index) |
| 5 | $Q_{SSSC} = \text{Im}\{V_{inj} \cdot I^*\}$ | Reactive power injected by SSSC | $V_{inj} \perp I$ |
| 6 | $P_{SSSC} = \text{Re}\{V_{inj} \cdot I^*\} \approx 0$ | Active power exchange (ideally zero, except for losses) | Ideal converter |
| 7 | $X_{eq,SSSC} = \dfrac{V_{inj}}{I}$ | Equivalent series reactance seen by line | $V_{inj}$ quadrature to $I$ |
| 8 | $E_{C,\text{stored}} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_{dc} V_{dc}^2$ | DC link energy storage | Constant $V_{dc}$ |
| 9 | $\tau_{TSSC} = \dfrac{T}{2} = 0.01 \text{ s}$ (for 50 Hz) | Minimum switching interval | Line-frequency synchronized |
| 10 | $V_R = V_S - j X_L I - V_{inj}$ | Receiving-end voltage with SSSC | Phasor form |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Editing Note:** All absolute values use `\vert \cdot \vert` notation. The vertical bar `\|` character has been deliberately replaced with `\vert` to preserve the KTU-style markdown table format.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in the Field

| Application Area | Device Used | Real Project Example |
|---|---|---|
| Long EHV AC tie-lines | TSSC | **PGCIL** Raigarh–Pugulur HVDC adjacent series comp. |
| Power oscillation damping | SSSC | NYPA's **Marcy CSC** upgraded to SSSC scheme |
| Renewable interconnection | SSSC | Texas CREZ lines — variable compensation |
| SSR mitigation | TSSC + TCSC | Navajo Project (USA) |
| Smart grid fast control | SSSC | ABB & Siemens commercial products |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Equivalent Reactance of TSSC in Steady State

### Given:
- A series capacitor $C$ with capacitive reactance $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$.
- A line carrying sinusoidal current $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$.
- An anti-parallel thyristor pair across the capacitor.

### Step 1: Capacitor Voltage Without Bypass
The voltage across the capacitor is the integral of the current divided by $C$:

$$ v_C(t) = \frac{1}{C} \int i(t) \, dt $$

Substituting $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$:

$$ v_C(t) = \frac{1}{C} \int I_m \sin(\omega t) \, dt = -\frac{I_m}{\omega C} \cos(\omega t) = -I_m X_C \cos(\omega t) $$

### Step 2: Phasor Representation
The phasor form of capacitor voltage is:

$$ \tilde{V}_C = -j X_C \tilde{I} $$

This confirms the well-known relation $V_C = I \cdot X_C$ lagging the current by 90°.

### Step 3: Equivalent Reactance of TSSC
**Case A — Thyristor OFF (Inserted Mode):**
The line current flows entirely through the capacitor. The TSSC presents $X_{eq} = X_C$.

**Case B — Thyristor ON (Bypassed Mode):**
The thyristor shorts the capacitor. The line current bypasses the capacitor; the TSSC presents $X_{eq} = 0$.

### Step 4: Final Compact Form

$$ X_{TSSC} = \begin{cases} X_C, & \text{if thyristor OFF} \\ 0, & \text{if thyristor ON} \end{cases} $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Power Transfer Increase Due to TSSC

### Given:
- Sending-end voltage $V_S$, receiving-end voltage $V_R = V_S = V$ (for symmetry).
- Line reactance $X_L$.
- Series compensation $X_C$ from TSSC.
- Power-angle $\delta$ between $V_S$ and $V_R$.

### Step 1: Uncompensated Power Transfer

$$ P_0 = \frac{V^2}{X_L} \sin \delta $$

### Step 2: Compensated Power Transfer (TSSC Inserted)
The effective line reactance is $X_{\text{eff}} = X_L - X_C$:

$$ P_{\text{comp}} = \frac{V^2}{X_L - X_C} \sin \delta $$

### Step 3: Ratio of Compensated to Uncompensated Power

$$ \frac{P_{\text{comp}}}{P_0} = \frac{X_L}{X_L - X_C} = \frac{1}{1 - k} $$

where $k = \dfrac{X_C}{X_L}$ is the *compensation ratio* (typically 0.3 to 0.7).

### Step 4: Fractional Power Increase

$$ \Delta P = P_{\text{comp}} - P_0 = P_0 \left[ \frac{1}{1-k} - 1 \right] = P_0 \cdot \frac{k}{1 - k} $$

### Step 5: Example Calculation
If $k = 0.5$ (50% compensation):

$$ \Delta P = P_0 \cdot \frac{0.5}{0.5} = P_0 $$

This means power transfer **doubles** compared to the uncompensated line — a dramatic effect that justifies TSSC deployment in heavily loaded corridors.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — SSSC Injected Voltage & Equivalent Reactance

### Given:
- Sending-end voltage $\tilde{V}_S$ at angle $0$ (reference).
- Line current $\tilde{I}$ lagging $\tilde{V}_S$ by angle $\theta$.
- Injected series voltage $\tilde{V}_{inj}$ at angle $\phi$.

### Step 1: Phasor Diagram & Constraint
The SSSC enforces $V_{inj}$ to be **90° out of phase** with $I$, i.e., $\phi = \theta \pm 90°$. Hence:

$$ \tilde{V}_{inj} = \pm j V_q \cdot e^{j\theta} \cdot \frac{\tilde{I}}{\vert \tilde{I} \vert} $$

where $V_q = \vert V_{inj} \vert$.

### Step 2: Equivalent Reactance
Define the equivalent reactance of the SSSC as seen by the line:

$$ X_{eq,\text{SSSC}} = \frac{\tilde{V}_{inj}}{\tilde{I}} = \pm j V_q \cdot \frac{e^{j\theta}}{\vert \tilde{I} \vert e^{j\theta}} = \pm j \frac{V_q}{\vert \tilde{I} \vert} $$

### Step 3: Reactive Power Exchanged
The reactive power injected by the SSSC is:

$$ Q_{\text{SSSC}} = \text{Im}\{ \tilde{V}_{inj} \cdot \tilde{I}^* \} $$

Substituting $\tilde{V}_{inj} = \pm j V_q \cdot \dfrac{\tilde{I}}{\vert \tilde{I} \vert}$ and $\tilde{I}^* = \vert \tilde{I} \vert e^{-j\theta}$:

$$ Q_{\text{SSSC}} = \text{Im}\left\{ \pm j V_q \cdot \frac{\tilde{I}}{\vert \tilde{I} \vert} \cdot \vert \tilde{I} \vert e^{-j\theta} \right\} = \pm V_q \vert \tilde{I} \vert $$

### Step 4: Active Power Exchanged (Lossless Ideal Case)

$$ P_{\text{SSSC}} = \text{Re}\{ \tilde{V}_{inj} \cdot \tilde{I}^* \} = 0 $$

This confirms the SSSC exchanges **only reactive power** in steady state — no real power is drawn from the DC link.

### Step 5: Numerical Example
- Line current: $\vert \tilde{I} \vert = 1000 \text{ A (RMS)}$.
- DC link voltage: $V_{dc} = 20 \text{ kV}$.
- Modulation index: $K = 0.9$ (modulation depth for PWM).
- Injected voltage: $V_{inj} = K \cdot V_{dc} = 0.9 \times 20 = 18 \text{ kV}$.
- Reactive power: $Q_{\text{SSSC}} = 18 \text{ kV} \times 1000 \text{ A} = 18 \text{ MVar}$.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix (TSSC vs SSSC)

| Feature | TSSC | SSSC |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Passive + Switch | Active (VSC) |
| Polarity of $X_{eq}$ | Capacitive only | Capacitive **or** Inductive |
| Control granularity | Step (2 levels) | Continuous (analog) |
| Component count | Low (1 thyristor pair + cap) | High (VSC + DC cap + transformer) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Losses | Very low (cap + thyristor conduction) | Moderate (VSC switching + transformer) |
| SSR risk | Moderate (requires TCSC combo) | Negligible |
| Response time | Half-cycle (10 ms) | Sub-cycle (< 5 ms) |
| DC link required | No | Yes ($C_{dc}$) |
| Maturity | Commercial since 1990s | Commercial since 2000s |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TSSC — Schematic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeBusS[Bus S] --> nodeLine1[Transmission Line]
    nodeLine1 --> nodeCap[Series Capacitor Bank XC]
    nodeCap --> nodeThyr[Anti Parallel Thyristor Switch]
    nodeThyr --> nodeMOV[MOV Surge Arrester]
    nodeMOV --> nodeLine2[Transmission Line]
    nodeLine2 --> nodeBusR[Bus R]
    nodeCtrl[Gate Pulse Controller] --> nodeThyr
    nodeSens[Current and Voltage Sensor] --> nodeCtrl
    nodeSens -. monitors .-> nodeCap
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The series capacitor $X_C$ is in the line current path. The thyristor pair is in **parallel** with the capacitor, and when fired, it short-circuits the capacitor. The MOV protects against over-voltages during line faults. The controller senses current/voltage and issues gate pulses.

---

## 4.2 SSSC — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeBusS[Bus S Sending End] --> nodeLine1[Line Reactance XL]
    nodeLine1 --> nodePrim[Coupling Transformer Primary]
    nodePrim --> nodeBusR[Bus R Receiving End]
    nodeSec[Coupling Transformer Secondary] --> nodeVSC[Voltage Source Converter VSC]
    nodeVSC --> nodeDC[DC Link Capacitor Cdc]
    nodeDC -. energy storage .-> nodeVSC
    nodeCtrlPWM[PWM Gate Pulse Generator] --> nodeVSC
    nodeSensI[Line Current Sensor] --> nodeLock[PLL Phase Locked Loop]
    nodeSensV[Bus Voltage Sensor] --> nodeLock
    nodeLock --> nodeRefCalc[Reference Voltage Calculator]
    nodeRefCalc --> nodeCtrlPWM
    nodeBypass[Mechanical Bypass Breaker] -. protection .-> nodeVSC
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** Unlike a STATCOM (which is shunt), the SSSC injects voltage in *series* via a **coupling transformer**. The DC capacitor is *not* an energy source — it merely acts as a stiff voltage reference for the VSC. The PLL synchronizes the converter to the line frequency.

---

## 4.3 Phasor Comparison — TSSC vs SSSC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodePhasorA[Phasor: TSSC Inserted] --> nodeDescA[VC lags I by 90 degrees\nEquivalent to fixed XC]
    nodePhasorB[Phasor: SSSC Capacitive] --> nodeDescB[Vin leads I by 90 degrees\nActs like virtual XC]
    nodePhasorC[Phasor: SSSC Inductive] --> nodeDescC[Vin lags I by 90 degrees\nActs like virtual XL]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a Thyristor Switched Series Capacitor (TSSC). Mention its two principal operating states.**

**Model Answer:**
A TSSC is a series-connected FACTS controller that uses **anti-parallel thyristor switches** to insert or bypass a series capacitor segment in a transmission line. Its two principal operating states are:
1. **Bypassed Mode** — Thyristor conducting → capacitor short-circuited, $X_{eq} = 0$.
2. **Inserted Mode** — Thyristor blocking → capacitor in series, $X_{eq} = X_C$.

The transition occurs at line current zero-crossings for safe commutation, and control is discrete (step-wise).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**What is the function of the coupling transformer in a Static Synchronous Series Compensator (SSSC)? Why is the injected voltage maintained at 90° to the line current?**

**Model Answer:**
The **coupling transformer** in an SSSC serves two functions:
- **Voltage injection interface** — its primary winding is in series with the line, while the secondary connects to the VSC output, allowing the converter voltage to be injected into the line at appropriate level (via turns ratio).
- **Electrical isolation** — it isolates the VSC from the high-voltage line and provides a grounded reference for the converter.

The injected voltage is maintained at 90° to the line current so that **only reactive power is exchanged** (active power exchange = 0 in ideal case). This allows the SSSC to emulate a variable series reactance without consuming real power from the DC link.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — TSSC Focus

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **construction and operating principle of TSSC**. Discuss the role of the thyristor switch and MOV. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **400 kV, 50 Hz, 250 km** transmission line has $X_L = 0.4 \; \Omega/\text{km}$. A TSSC is used with compensation ratio $k = 0.5$. Determine:
- (i) The line reactance $X_L$ in $\Omega$.
- (ii) The compensation reactance $X_C$.
- (iii) The percentage increase in power transfer capability for a power angle of $\delta = 30°$. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**
**Construction:**
- A series capacitor bank of reactance $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$ is installed in the transmission line.
- An **anti-parallel thyristor switch** is connected across the capacitor.
- An **MOV (Metal Oxide Varistor)** is connected in parallel for over-voltage protection.
- A **gating control unit** is synchronized with the line current zero-crossings.

**Operating Principle:**
- When the thyristor is **OFF**, the line current flows through the capacitor, providing series compensation (boost).
- When the thyristor is **ON**, the capacitor is short-circuited and the line behaves as uncompensated.
- The thyristor is fired at the current zero-crossing to ensure **soft commutation** (the capacitor voltage is naturally zero).
- The MOV clamps any over-voltage arising from line faults or external disturbances.

**Role of Thyristor Switch:** Acts as a high-speed, line-frequency-synchronized bypass element. Its only function is to engage or disengage the capacitor.

**Role of MOV:** Limits the voltage across the capacitor to safe values (typically 2–2.5 p.u.) during external faults, protecting the thyristor and capacitor from thermal/dielectric failure.

**Part (b):**

(i) Line reactance:
$$ X_L = 0.4 \;\Omega/\text{km} \times 250 \;\text{km} = 100 \;\Omega $$

(ii) Compensation reactance:
$$ X_C = k \cdot X_L = 0.5 \times 100 = 50 \;\Omega $$

(iii) Power transfer with and without compensation:
- Uncompensated: $P_0 = \dfrac{V^2}{X_L} \sin \delta = \dfrac{V^2}{100} \sin 30° = 0.005 V^2$
- Compensated: $P_{\text{comp}} = \dfrac{V^2}{X_L - X_C} \sin \delta = \dfrac{V^2}{50} \sin 30° = 0.01 V^2$
- Percentage increase:
$$ \%\text{Increase} = \frac{P_{\text{comp}} - P_0}{P_0} \times 100\% = \frac{0.01 - 0.005}{0.005} \times 100\% = 100\% $$

The power transfer capability **doubles** with 50% compensation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — SSSC Focus

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Explain the **schematic and operating principle of the SSSC**. How does it differ from a TSSC in terms of polarity of compensation? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An SSSC is installed in a **220 kV, 50 Hz line** carrying a current of **1500 A (RMS)**. The DC link voltage is **15 kV** and the modulation index is **0.85**. Compute:
- (i) The peak injected voltage $V_{inj}$.
- (ii) The reactive power exchanged with the system.
- (iii) The equivalent reactance $X_{eq,\text{SSSC}}$ as seen by the line. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**
**Schematic:** The SSSC consists of a VSC, a DC link capacitor $C_{dc}$, a series coupling transformer, and a control system.

**Operating Principle:**
- The VSC generates a controllable AC voltage $\tilde{V}_{inj}$ from the DC link.
- The coupling transformer injects this voltage in **series** with the line.
- The injected voltage is **maintained at 90° to the line current** (quadrature).
- If $V_{inj}$ leads $I$ → SSSC acts as a **capacitor** (supplies reactive power).
- If $V_{inj}$ lags $I$ → SSSC acts as a **reactor** (absorbs reactive power).
- The DC link capacitor maintains a stiff DC voltage; small in-phase component compensates for losses.

**Difference from TSSC:**
- TSSC uses a *passive* capacitor — equivalent reactance is **always capacitive** when active.
- SSSC uses *active* voltage injection — equivalent reactance can be **capacitive OR inductive** depending on the polarity of $V_{inj}$. This reversibility is the **unique advantage** of SSSC.

**Part (b):**

(i) Peak injected voltage:
$$ V_{inj} = K \cdot V_{dc} = 0.85 \times 15 = 12.75 \;\text{kV (peak)} $$

In RMS terms:
$$ V_{inj,\text{rms}} = \frac{12.75}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 9.02 \;\text{kV} $$

(ii) Reactive power exchanged:
$$ Q_{\text{SSSC}} = V_{inj,\text{rms}} \cdot I_{\text{rms}} = 9.02 \times 10^3 \times 1500 = 13.53 \;\text{MVAr} $$

(iii) Equivalent reactance:
$$ X_{eq,\text{SSSC}} = \frac{V_{inj,\text{rms}}}{I_{\text{rms}}} = \frac{9020}{1500} \approx 6.01 \;\Omega $$

If the polarity is reversed (inductive mode), $X_{eq,\text{SSSC}} = -6.01 \;\Omega$ (negative series reactance → capacitive).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Confusing TSSC with TCSC**: TCSC uses a *TCR (thyristor-controlled reactor)* in parallel with a capacitor and provides *continuous* control. TSSC is a *discrete* device with *only a thyristor switch* (no reactor). Marks lost: **2 marks** if confused.
> 2. **Forgetting the 90° constraint in SSSC**: Students often write the SSSC injected voltage as having *any* arbitrary angle. It must be **exactly 90°** to line current for lossless reactive exchange. Marks lost: **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Missing the polarity-reversal advantage**: Many answers describe SSSC as merely "an advanced version of TSSC" without highlighting the **inductive/capacitive reversibility**. This is the *defining* feature. Marks lost: **2–3 marks**.
> 4. **DC link treated as a battery**: The DC link of SSSC is a *capacitor*, not a battery or external source. It is *charged initially* and then *self-sustained* (with small active power draw to cover losses). Marks lost: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Unit errors in numericals**: Always use *consistent units* — $V$ in kV, $I$ in kA, $Q$ in MVAr. Marks lost: **1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 (TSSC & SSSC)**

- **TSSC = Thyristor Switched Series Capacitor** → discrete compensation ($X_C$ or $0$).
- **TSSC always acts as a capacitor** when inserted; cannot emulate inductance.
- **TSSC uses anti-parallel thyristors** *across* a capacitor (no reactor, unlike TCSC).
- **Thyristor commutation is at line current zero** — soft switching, no commutation failure.
- **SSSC = Static Synchronous Series Compensator** → continuous, reversible compensation.
- **SSSC injects a voltage 90° to line current** via a VSC + coupling transformer.
- **SSSC can act as capacitor OR inductor** by reversing $V_{inj}$ polarity — its hallmark advantage.
- **SSSC DC link is a capacitor**, not a battery; $V_{dc}$ must be maintained by small active power intake.
- **Power transfer formula with series comp:** $P = \dfrac{V^2}{X_L - X_C} \sin \delta$.
- **TSSC response time ≈ half cycle (10 ms); SSSC response time ≈ 1–5 ms (PWM).**
- **Q exchange**: SSSC $= V_{inj} \cdot I$ (reactive only); TSSC $= I^2 \cdot X_C$ (only when inserted).
- **No resonance risk with SSSC**; TSSC may need TCSC combination for SSR mitigation.
- **MOV protects the capacitor** in TSSC from over-voltages during faults.
- **PLL (Phase-Locked Loop) is mandatory** in SSSC control to track line frequency.
- **Equivalent reactance of SSSC**: $X_{eq} = \pm \dfrac{V_{inj}}{I}$ — sign decides capacitive/inductive.
- **TSSC is cheaper, simpler, more rugged**; SSSC is costlier but offers unmatched flexibility.
- **Real-world applications** include EHV tie-lines, renewable integration, and oscillation damping.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unified Power Flow Controller: Circuit Arrangement, Operation of UPFC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET862 - HVDC AND FACTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Unified Power Flow Controller: Circuit Arrangement, Operation of UPFC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔌 Unified Power Flow Controller (UPFC) — Circuit Arrangement & Operation

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Unified Power Flow Controller (UPFC)** is the most versatile and powerful member of the **FACTS (Flexible AC Transmission Systems)** controller family, conceived by **Gyugyi (1991)**. It consists of **two voltage-source converters (VSCs)** coupled to a **common DC link capacitor**, with one converter connected in **shunt** (acting as a **Static Synchronous Compensator – STATCOM**) and the other connected in **series** (acting as a **Static Synchronous Series Compensator – SSSC**) with the transmission line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEEET862 / Module 4):**
> "The UPFC is a combination of a STATCOM and an SSSC that are coupled via a common DC link to allow bidirectional flow of real power between the series and shunt inverters. It provides simultaneous, independent control of **bus voltage magnitude, line active power, and line reactive power**."

The series branch injects a **synchronous voltage `V_pq`** of controllable magnitude (0 to `V_pq_max`) and phase angle (0° to 360°) at the fundamental system frequency. The shunt branch maintains the **DC link voltage** and provides **reactive power** to the line, while supplying/absorbing the real power demanded by the series branch.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 Analogy: The "Universal Power Steering" of a Transmission Line**
>
> Imagine a long highway (the transmission line) with traffic flowing from City A to City B. A **traffic control tower** (the UPFC) can do THREE things at once:
>
> 1. **Open extra lanes** (series voltage injection) → controls how much power flows on the line.
> 2. **Change the speed limit at the entry ramp** (shunt voltage regulation) → controls the local bus voltage.
> 3. **Borrow energy from the entry ramp to power the lane controls** (DC link exchange) → exchanges real power internally.
>
> The two parts of the control tower share a **common fuel tank (DC capacitor)**, so energy can be transferred between them instantaneously. The series part is the *active* worker; the shunt part is the *supporter* that keeps the system alive.

### Why UPFC is Called "Unified"

Unlike a SSSC or STATCOM, the UPFC can perform **all three power-flow control functions** (voltage, impedance, and angle regulation) **simultaneously** because its series-injected voltage `V_pq` is a free phasor in the complex plane — its magnitude **AND** angle are independently controllable.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Ratings (Bolded)

| Parameter | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **DC link voltage `V_dc`** | 5 – 50 | **kV** |
| **Series injected voltage `V_pq`** | 0.1 – 0.3 pu | **pu** |
| **Reactive power rating (each VSC)** | ± 100 – 400 | **MVAR** |
| **Real power exchange via DC link** | ± 50 – 200 | **MW** |
| **Switching frequency (PWM carrier)** | 1 – 2 | **kHz** |
| **Response time of UPFC** | 1 – 10 | **ms** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Circuit Arrangement (Two-Converter Back-to-Back)

The UPFC consists of the following key hardware blocks:

1. **Shunt Converter (VSC-1 / STATCOM part):**
   - Voltage Source Converter using **IGBT / GTO / IGCT** switches.
   - Connected to the line through a **shunt coupling transformer**.
   - Maintains the **DC bus voltage** `V_dc` constant by absorbing/supplying real power.
   - Independently controls **reactive power** at the local bus (STATCOM function).

2. **Series Converter (VSC-2 / SSSC part):**
   - Identical VSC topology as VSC-1.
   - Connected to the line via a **series injection (booster) transformer**.
   - Injects a controlled voltage `V_pq ∠ ρ` in series with the line.
   - Draws the real power it needs from the DC link (which is replenished by VSC-1).

3. **Common DC Link Capacitor:**
   - Single storage element `C_dc` shared by both converters.
   - Acts as the **real-power exchange medium** between VSC-1 and VSC-2.
   - Voltage `V_dc` must be tightly regulated to ensure proper PWM operation.

4. **Control System (Hierarchical):**
   - **Outer loop:** System-level power-flow references (`P_ref`, `Q_ref`, `V_ref`).
   - **Inner loop:** d-q axis current controllers (vector control).
   - **Switching level:** Sinusoidal PWM or SVPWM for each VSC.

---

## 2.2 Equivalent Circuit of the UPFC

```
        I_sh        I_line                    I_line
         ↓            ↓                          ↓
   ●──[ jX_sh ]──●────────────[ jX_line ]──────────────●─────●
   V_s          A                                        B    V_r
                │                                           │
                │ (Shunt)                                  │ (Series)
                │                                          │
            [Shunt VSC-1]  ⇄═══[ V_dc ]═══⇄  [Series VSC-2]
            (STATCOM)         (Capacitor)        (SSSC, V_pq)
```

- `V_s` → Sending-end voltage phasor.
- `V_r` → Receiving-end voltage phasor.
- `V_pq ∠ ρ` → Series voltage injected by VSC-2.
- `I` → Series line current (same through line and VSC-2).
- `I_sh` → Current exchanged by VSC-1 with the line.

---

## 2.3 Mathematical Model — Power Flow With UPFC

Consider a lossless transmission line of reactance `X`. Without UPFC:

$$P_0 = \frac{\vert V_s \vert \cdot \vert V_r \vert}{X} \sin(\delta_s - \delta_r)$$

When the UPFC injects `V_pq ∠ ρ`, the effective sending-end voltage becomes `(V_s + V_pq)`, and the active power transmitted becomes:

$$P = \frac{\vert V_s + V_pq \vert \cdot \vert V_r \vert}{X} \sin(\delta_{sr} - \delta_r)$$

For a more useful form, the power flow can be decomposed as:

$$P = \frac{\vert V_s \vert \cdot \vert V_r \vert}{X} \sin(\delta) + \frac{\vert V_s \vert \cdot \vert V_pq \vert}{X} \sin(\rho - \delta)$$

where:
- `δ` = angle between `V_s` and `V_r` (i.e., `δ_s − δ_r`)
- `ρ` = angle of the injected series voltage `V_pq` w.r.t. `V_s`

> [!NOTE]
> The **first term** is the natural power flow of the uncompensated line.
> The **second term** is the **incremental power** due to UPFC injection, which can be **positive, negative, or zero** depending on `ρ`.

---

## 2.4 Operating Modes of UPFC (KTU High-Yield)

The injected voltage `V_pq` can be decomposed into two orthogonal components:

| Mode | Component Active | Effect | Angle of `V_pq` w.r.t. `I` |
|---|---|---|---|
