| **Voltage Regulation Mode** | `V_pq ∥ V_s` (in phase with `V_s`) | Boosts/bucks bus voltage magnitude | Perpendicular to `I` (in steady state) |
| **Phase Angle Regulation Mode** | `V_pq ⊥ V_s` (quadrature with `V_s`) | Shifts phase angle of sending voltage | Controls real power flow |
| **Line Impedance Compensation Mode** | `V_pq ∥ I` (in phase with line current) | Simulates series reactance compensation | Parallel to `I` |
| **Multifunction / Unified Mode** | Both components active | All three parameters (V, δ, P, Q) controlled simultaneously | Free phasor (0°–360°) |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU UPFC numerical.**

| # | Equation | Description / Usage | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{\vert V_s \vert \cdot \vert V_r \vert}{X} \sin(\delta)$ | Uncompensated active power flow | MW |
| 2 | $P = P_0 + \dfrac{\vert V_s \vert \cdot \vert V_pq \vert}{X} \sin(\rho - \delta)$ | UPFC-augmented active power | MW |
| 3 | $Q = \dfrac{\vert V_s \vert^2 - \vert V_s \vert \cdot \vert V_r \vert \cos(\delta)}{X}$ | Sending-end reactive power | MVAR |
| 4 | $P_{sh} = \mathrm{Re}\{V_s \cdot I_{sh}^*\}$ | Real power at shunt VSC (DC link balance) | MW |
| 5 | $Q_{sh} = \mathrm{Im}\{V_s \cdot I_{sh}^*\}$ | Reactive power at shunt VSC | MVAR |
| 6 | $P_{dc} = C_{dc} \cdot V_{dc} \cdot \dfrac{dV_{dc}}{dt}$ | DC capacitor power balance equation | MW |
| 7 | $V_{pq} \le V_{pq,\max} = 0.5 \, m_a \, V_{dc}$ | Maximum injectable series voltage | pu |
| 8 | $\Delta P_{\max} = \dfrac{\vert V_s \vert \cdot V_{pq,\max}}{X}$ | Maximum power-flow increment | MW |
| 9 | $i_{d} = \dfrac{P_{ref}}{V_{sd}}, \quad i_{q} = \dfrac{-Q_{ref}}{V_{sd}}$ | d-q axis reference currents (vector control) | pu |
| 10 | $T_{resp} \approx \dfrac{1}{f_{sw}}$ | Approximate UPFC response time | s |

> **Notation:** `m_a` = PWM modulation index, `V_dc` = DC link voltage, `ρ` = phase angle of `V_pq`.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **UPFC installations worldwide:** Inez (Kentucky, USA, ± 320 MVA), Marcy (New York, ± 200 MVA), Kangjin (South Korea), etc.
- **Application domains:** Congestion management, loop-flow control, damping of sub-synchronous oscillations, dynamic voltage support, and post-fault power-flow restoration.
- **Why it matters for KTU engineers:** Kerala's 220 kV and 400 kV transmission corridors are increasingly UPFC candidates due to high loading and limited right-of-way for new lines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — UPFC Power Flow Equation

We start from a 2-bus system with sending-end voltage `V_s ∠ δ_s`, receiving-end `V_r ∠ 0`, and series-injected `V_pq ∠ ρ`. The line reactance is `X` (lossless assumption).

**Step 1 — Line current phasor using KVL:**

$$V_s \angle \delta_s + V_{pq} \angle \rho - V_r \angle 0 = jX \cdot I \angle \theta$$

Rearranging for the current:

$$I \angle \theta = \frac{V_s \angle \delta_s + V_{pq} \angle \rho - V_r \angle 0}{jX}$$

**Step 2 — Active power at receiving end:**

$$P_r = \mathrm{Re}\{V_r \cdot I^*\} = \mathrm{Re}\left\{ V_r \cdot \left( \frac{V_s \angle \delta_s + V_{pq} \angle \rho - V_r}{jX} \right)^* \right\}$$

Using the identity `1/j = −j` and separating real/imaginary parts:

$$P_r = \frac{1}{X} \left[ \vert V_s \vert \cdot \vert V_r \vert \sin(\delta_s) + \vert V_r \vert \cdot \vert V_{pq} \vert \sin(\rho) - \vert V_r \vert^2 \sin(0) \right]$$

Since the last term is zero:

$$P_r = \frac{\vert V_s \vert \cdot \vert V_r \vert}{X} \sin(\delta_s) + \frac{\vert V_r \vert \cdot \vert V_{pq} \vert}{X} \sin(\rho)$$

**Step 3 — Re-express using the convention `δ = δ_s − δ_r = δ_s`** (since `δ_r = 0`):

$$\boxed{P_r = \frac{\vert V_s \vert \cdot \vert V_r \vert}{X} \sin(\delta) + \frac{\vert V_r \vert \cdot \vert V_{pq} \vert}{X} \sin(\rho)}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This equation tells us the UPFC adds a *correction term* to the natural power flow. The angle `ρ` is the only free parameter (with `V_pq` magnitude also variable), giving the operator continuous control over `P_r`.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — DC Link Capacitor Voltage Dynamics

The DC link is the energy buffer between the two VSCs. Its voltage must be regulated.

**Step 1 — Power entering DC bus from shunt VSC-1:**

$$P_1 = V_s \cdot I_{sh} \cos(\phi_{sh}) = \mathrm{Re}\{V_s \cdot I_{sh}^*\}$$

**Step 2 — Power leaving DC bus to series VSC-2:**

$$P_2 = V_{pq} \cdot I \cos(\phi_{pq}) = \mathrm{Re}\{V_{pq} \cdot I^*\}$$

**Step 3 — Power stored in the capacitor:**

$$P_C = \frac{d}{dt}\left( \frac{1}{2} C_{dc} V_{dc}^2 \right) = C_{dc} V_{dc} \frac{dV_{dc}}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Energy conservation (signs: power leaving reduces V_dc):**

$$P_1 - P_2 = C_{dc} V_{dc} \frac{dV_{dc}}{dt}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dV_{dc}}{dt} = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{C_{dc} V_{dc}} = \frac{P_{shunt} - P_{series}}{C_{dc} V_{dc}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The shunt VSC MUST absorb (or supply) exactly the real power the series VSC needs — otherwise `V_dc` drifts. A PI controller on the d-axis current of VSC-1 keeps `V_dc` at its reference (typically `1.0–1.5 pu`).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Injected Series Voltage Magnitude Limits

For a PWM-driven VSC, the peak of the fundamental output voltage is:

$$V_{pq,peak} = \frac{1}{2} m_a V_{dc}$$

where `m_a ∈ [0, 1]` is the modulation index (linear region). Therefore the **RMS maximum** is:

$$V_{pq,\max} = \frac{m_a V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

In per-unit (with base `V_base`):

$$\vert V_{pq} \vert_{pu} \le \frac{m_a V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2} \cdot V_{base}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the ceiling on series compensation. Beyond this, the VSC saturates and PWM becomes over-modulated → distorted waveforms.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example — UPFC on a 230 kV Line

**Given:**
- `V_s = 1.05 ∠ 20°` pu, `V_r = 1.00 ∠ 0°` pu
- `X = 0.4` pu, `V_pq = 0.1` pu
- Find `P_r` for: (a) `ρ = 90°` (purely in-phase boost) and (b) `ρ = 0°` (purely quadrature).

**Solution (a) `ρ = 90°` (Phase-shift mode):**

$$P_r = \frac{(1.05)(1.00)}{0.4}\sin(20°) + \frac{(1.00)(0.1)}{0.4}\sin(90°)$$

$$P_r = 2.625 \cdot (0.342) + 0.25 \cdot (1.000)$$

$$P_r = 0.898 + 0.250 = 1.148 \text{ pu}$$

**Solution (b) `ρ = 0°` (Voltage-buck mode):**

$$P_r = 2.625 \sin(20°) + 0.25 \sin(0°)$$

$$P_r = 0.898 + 0 = 0.898 \text{ pu}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The UPFC adds `0.250 pu` (~25 MW on a 100 MVA base) when `ρ = 90°` — a **27.8% increase** in transmitted power. This is the most common operating mode for congestion relief in KTU's grid integration problems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid Block Architecture)

## 4.1 UPFC Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    BUS_S["Sending End Bus Vs"]
    BUS_R["Receiving End Bus Vr"]
    LINE_X["Line Reactance jX"]

    VSC1["VSC1 Inverter STATCOM Function"]
    VSC2["VSC2 Inverter SSSC Function"]
    CAP_DC["DC Link Capacitor Vdc"]

    L_SH["Shunt Filter Reactance"]
    T_SH["Shunt Coupling Transformer"]
    T_SR["Series Booster Transformer"]
    L_SR["Series Filter Reactance"]

    CTRL_1["Shunt Controller Outer Pdc and Qsh PI Loops"]
    CTRL_2["Series Controller Outer P and Q PI Loops"]
    PWM1["PWM Generator for VSC1"]
    PWM2["PWM Generator for VSC2"]

    BUS_S --- L_SH --- VSC1
    VSC1 --- T_SH

    BUS_S --- LINE_X
    LINE_X --- BUS_R
    LINE_X --- L_SR --- T_SR --- VSC2

    VSC1 === CAP_DC
    VSC2 === CAP_DC

    CTRL_1 --> PWM1 --> VSC1
    CTRL_2 --> PWM2 --> VSC2
    CTRL_1 --- CAP_DC
```

## 4.2 Phasor Diagram — UPFC Injection at Different Modes

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph M1["MODE 1 Voltage Regulation Vpq Parallel to Vs"]
        A1["Vs phasor along reference"]
        B1["Vpq adds in phase with Vs"]
        C1["Effective sending voltage Vs plus Vpq is longer"]
    end
    subgraph M2["MODE 2 Phase Angle Vpq Perpendicular to Vs"]
        A2["Vs along reference"]
        B2["Vpq at right angle to Vs"]
        C2["Effective Vs phasor rotates, angle delta changes"]
    end
    subgraph M3["MODE 3 Series Compensation Vpq Parallel to I"]
        A3["Current I phasor"]
        B3["Vpq in phase with I"]
        C3["Simulates reactance change"]
    end
    subgraph M4["MODE 4 Unified Free Phasor"]
        A4["Vs fixed"]
        B4["Vpq at arbitrary angle rho 0 to 360"]
        C4["Full 2D control of P and Q"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Power Flow Control Loop

```mermaid
graph TB
    PREF["P Reference from Operator"]
    QREF["Q Reference from Operator"]
    VREF["Vdc Voltage Reference"]
    PI1["PI Controller for P"]
    PI2["PI Controller for Q"]
    PI3["PI Controller for Vdc"]
    IDQ["Generate Id and Iq References"]
    VINV["PWM Signals to VSC1 and VSC2"]
    SYS["Power System Response"]
    FBK["Measured P Q Vdc Feedback"]
    PREF --> PI1 --> IDQ
    QREF --> PI2 --> IDQ
    VREF --> PI3 --> IDQ
    IDQ --> VINV --> SYS
    SYS --> FBK --> PI1
    SYS --> FBK --> PI2
    SYS --> FBK --> PI3
```

## 4.4 Real and Reactive Power Exchange Between VSCs

```mermaid
graph LR
    VSC1["VSC1 STATCOM AC Side"]
    CAP["DC Link Vdc Capacitor"]
    VSC2["VSC2 SSSC AC Side"]
    LINE["Transmission Line AC Side"]

    VSC1 -->|Psh and Qsh| CAP
    CAP -->|Ppq for series injection| VSC2
    VSC2 -->|Vpq injection| LINE
    LINE -->|I flows back| VSC2
    LINE -->|Ish flow component| VSC1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The shunt VSC-1 draws reactive power `Q_sh` independently from the AC line, while it supplies real power `P_sh = P_pq` to the DC link. The series VSC-2 injects `V_pq` (which carries both real and reactive components) and returns current `I` to the line. The DC capacitor is the **only** energy-exchange pathway.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**"List the three independent power flow control functions that a UPFC can perform simultaneously. Identify the converter inside the UPFC that performs each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A UPFC can simultaneously control:
1. **Bus voltage magnitude** — handled by the **shunt-connected VSC-1 (STATCOM part)** by injecting/absorbing reactive current.
2. **Line active (real) power flow** — handled by the **series-connected VSC-2 (SSSC part)** by injecting a voltage component in phase quadrature with the line current.
3. **Line reactive power flow / effective line impedance** — handled by the **series VSC-2** by injecting a voltage in phase/anti-phase with the line current.

This is the reason UPFC is called "Unified" — it unifies the functions of STATCOM, SSSC, and phase-shifter into a single device. **(1 mark per function + identification, total 3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**"Why is a common DC link capacitor essential in a UPFC? What happens if it is removed?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **DC link capacitor** is the **only energy exchange medium** between the shunt VSC-1 and series VSC-2 of the UPFC. **(1 mark)**
The series VSC-2 must draw real power equal to `Re{V_pq · I*}` to inject a voltage `V_pq` that has an in-phase component with the line current. This real power can only come from the AC system through the **shunt VSC-1** via the DC link. **(1 mark)**
If the DC link is removed, the two VSCs become **electrically isolated**, and the series VSC-2 would have no source of real power — it would only be able to inject **purely reactive** voltage, reducing the UPFC to an SSSC (losing multifunction capability). **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **circuit arrangement of a UPFC**. Identify the roles of the shunt and series converters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **active power flow in a transmission line compensated by a UPFC**, clearly defining all variables. Show that the UPFC can increase or decrease the transmitted power depending on the angle `ρ` of the injected voltage. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q-A (a)

A UPFC consists of two back-to-back voltage source converters (VSCs) sharing a common DC capacitor `C_dc`.

**Block Diagram:**

```
  V_s ──[jX_sh]──●─────────────────[jX_line]──●── V_r
                │                              │
                │                              │
              [VSC-1]          [VSC-2]
              (Shunt)          (Series)
                │   ⇄══[V_dc]══⇄   │
                │      (C_dc)        │
              [Coupling          [Booster
              Transformer]        Transformer]
```

**Roles:**
- **Shunt VSC-1 (STATCOM function):** Maintains the DC link voltage at reference by absorbing/supplying real power; independently provides reactive power to the AC bus for voltage regulation. **(3 marks for diagram + identification)**
- **Series VSC-2 (SSSC function):** Injects a controllable voltage `V_pq ∠ ρ` in series with the line through a booster transformer. The phasor `V_pq` has independent magnitude and phase. **(2 marks)**
- **Common DC link:** Real-power exchange medium between the two VSCs. **(1 mark)**
- **Control system:** Hierarchical — outer power-flow loop, inner current loop, PWM at switching level. **(1 mark)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q-A (b)

Refer to the full derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key steps expected in the answer sheet:

1. Write KVL: `V_s + V_pq − V_r = jX · I` **(1 mark)**
2. Solve for `I = (V_s + V_pq − V_r) / (jX)` **(1 mark)**
3. Compute `P_r = Re{V_r · I*}` **(1 mark)**
4. Arrive at:

$$P_r = \frac{\vert V_s \vert \cdot \vert V_r \vert}{X}\sin(\delta) + \frac{\vert V_r \vert \cdot \vert V_{pq} \vert}{X}\sin(\rho) \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

5. **Interpretation:**
   - If `ρ = 90°`, the second term is **positive** → UPFC **increases** power flow.
   - If `ρ = 270°` (or `−90°`), the second term is **negative** → UPFC **decreases** power flow.
   - If `ρ = 0° or 180°`, the second term is **zero** → UPFC only changes reactive power / voltage. **(1 mark)**

---

### 🔹 Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]

**(a)** Explain the **three principal operating modes of a UPFC** (voltage regulation, phase angle regulation, and impedance compensation). Use phasor diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 230 kV, 50 Hz transmission line has `X = 0.4` pu. The sending end voltage is `V_s = 1.05 ∠ 20°` pu and the receiving end is `V_r = 1.00 ∠ 0°` pu. A UPFC injects a series voltage `V_pq = 0.10 pu` at `ρ = 90°` w.r.t. `V_s`. Compute (i) the natural power flow without UPFC and (ii) the UPFC-compensated power flow. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q-B (a)

| Mode | Description | Phasor relation | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Voltage Regulation** | Series voltage `V_pq` is **in phase** with `V_s` | `V_pq ∥ V_s` | Boosts/bucks bus voltage magnitude |
| **Phase Angle Regulation** | `V_pq` is in **quadrature** with `V_s` | `V_pq ⊥ V_s` | Shifts effective phase angle `δ`, controls real power |
| **Impedance / Reactive Compensation** | `V_pq` is in **phase** with line current `I` | `V_pq ∥ I` | Simulates change in line reactance (series compensation) |

**(3 marks for table + descriptions)**

**Phasor diagrams (expected sketches):**
- In each mode, draw `V_s` as reference, then `V_pq` at the specified angle, and the resultant `V_s + V_pq` showing the effect on magnitude or angle. **(4 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q-B (b)

**(i) Natural power flow (no UPFC):**

$$P_0 = \frac{\vert V_s \vert \cdot \vert V_r \vert}{X}\sin(\delta) = \frac{(1.05)(1.00)}{0.4}\sin(20°)$$

$$P_0 = 2.625 \times 0.3420 = 0.898 \text{ pu} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

**(ii) UPFC-compensated power flow:**

$$P = P_0 + \frac{\vert V_r \vert \cdot \vert V_{pq} \vert}{X}\sin(\rho) = 0.898 + \frac{(1.00)(0.10)}{0.4}\sin(90°)$$

$$P = 0.898 + 0.25 \times 1.000 = 0.898 + 0.250 = 1.148 \text{ pu} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

**Conclusion:** The UPFC increases transmitted power from `0.898 pu` to `1.148 pu` — an **increment of `0.250 pu` (~27.8%)** by injecting `0.10 pu` series voltage in phase-quadrature with `V_s`. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the angle convention:** Many students write `sin(ρ − δ)` instead of `sin(ρ) + (natural term)`. The KTU key uses the form with `ρ` measured from `V_s`, not the line current. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing UPFC with IPFC:** IPFC has multiple series converters on different lines; UPFC has ONE shunt + ONE series on the SAME line. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Missing the DC link explanation:** A UPFC without a DC link is just two STATCOMs/SSSCs — students frequently omit the energy-exchange role. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
> 4. **Not drawing phasor diagrams for operating modes:** Mode explanation without a phasor diagram is incomplete. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
> 5. **Ignoring sign of `ρ`:** Whether UPFC adds or removes power depends on whether `ρ ∈ (0°, 180°)` or `(180°, 360°)`. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 6. **Not identifying the converter performing each function:** Always state "shunt VSC controls voltage, series VSC controls power flow."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize These 10 Points:**
>
> ☐ **UPFC = STATCOM + SSSC**, sharing one DC capacitor.
> ☐ Two **VSCs**, back-to-back, common DC link — that's the signature architecture.
> ☐ Shunt VSC = voltage & reactive power control; Series VSC = power flow control.
> ☐ The injected series voltage `V_pq` has **free magnitude (0 → 0.3 pu)** and **free angle (0°–360°)**.
> ☐ Three operating modes: **Voltage Regulation, Phase Angle Regulation, Impedance Compensation** + Unified mode.
> ☐ **Key formula:** `P = (V_s·V_r/X) sin δ + (V_r·V_pq/X) sin ρ`
> ☐ DC link balance: `P_shunt = P_series` (real power conservation at `C_dc`).
> ☐ **DC link must be present** — otherwise UPFC degrades to a basic SSSC.
> ☐ UPFC gives **three independent controls** (V, P, Q) — most versatile FACTS device.
> ☐ First UPFC installed: **Inez, Kentucky (USA, 1998) by Westinghouse / EPRI**.

> **Key Takeaway for KTU:** If a numerical mentions *simultaneous control of voltage, active power, and reactive power* on a transmission line — the answer is **always UPFC**, not SSSC or STATCOM.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Principle of P and Q Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET862 - HVDC AND FACTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Principle of P and Q Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In an HVDC link (and in many FACTS controllers), **active power (P) and reactive power (Q) control** refers to the deliberate manipulation of converter firing angle ($\alpha$) and extinction angle ($\gamma$) — or the equivalent duty cycle of self-commutated FACTS valves — so that the converter absorbs/injects a specified amount of real power ($P$) and reactive power ($Q$) from/to the connected AC system.

For a **line-commutated HVDC converter**, the governing principles are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (P control):** Active power transmitted through the DC link is controlled by adjusting the **DC current order** ($I_{d,ref}$), which is achieved by modulating the rectifier firing angle $\alpha$ (and the inverter extinction angle $\gamma$ as a back-up).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Q control):** Reactive power consumed by the converter is *not* independently commanded; it is a *natural by-product* of the firing angle $\alpha$ and the commutation overlap angle $\mu$. As $\alpha$ is increased, the displacement power factor $\cos\phi$ falls, and the converter draws more lagging Q from the AC grid.

In **self-commutated FACTS** devices (STATCOM, UPFC), P and Q become independently controllable because the valve can synthesise a voltage at any phase angle.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pump and the Pressure"

Imagine a **water pump station** that pumps water (active power $P$) through a long pipe to a city. The pipe is rough, and water swirling inside creates pressure loss and eddies (reactive power $Q$).

- **Opening/closing a valve at the pump end** changes how much water flows — this is exactly what the **firing angle $\alpha$** does in an HVDC rectifier. You are not forcing $P$ directly; you are *throttling* the valve. The actual flow $P$ is then determined by the head difference between the two reservoirs (i.e., the two AC systems).
- **The eddies inside the pipe** (friction losses) represent reactive power drawn by the converter. You cannot "command" them to be zero — they come *for free* with the valve setting. A nearly-closed valve ($\alpha$ large) means a very low utilisation factor, hence huge eddies (large $Q$).
- **At the city end**, a second valve (inverter extinction angle $\gamma$) acts as a *minimum-pressure* controller — it only kicks in if the main valve malfunctions.

This is why HVDC converters are **inherently Q-consuming**: they need AC system reactive power just to function, but the *amount* of Q drawn is a function of P and $\alpha$.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (KTU Standard Metrics)

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Magnitude |
|:------:|:---------|:------------------|
| $V_{LL}$ | Line-to-line AC RMS voltage | $\mathbf{220\text{ kV}}$ to $\mathbf{800\text{ kV}}$ |
| $V_{d0}$ | Ideal no-load DC voltage | $\mathbf{1.35\,V_{LL}}$ per 6-pulse bridge |
| $I_d$ | DC line current | **1 kA to 4 kA** |
| $P_d$ | DC power | **500 MW to 8000 MW** |
| $\alpha$ | Rectifier firing angle | **5° to 20°** (rectifier mode) |
| $\gamma$ | Inverter extinction angle | **15° to 20°** |
| $\cos\phi$ | Displacement power factor | **0.85 to 0.95 lag** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\cos\phi < 1$?** Even with the smoothest firing, commutation overlap $\mu$ forces a **fundamental-frequency displacement** between AC current and voltage. This displacement *is* the reactive power demand of the converter.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

The basic principle of P and Q control in a **two-terminal HVDC link** rests on three hierarchical loops:

1. **Master (System) Level — Power Order**
   - The operator (or the system dispatcher) sets a desired $P_d$ (or equivalently an $I_{d,ref}$) to balance generation and load across the two AC systems.
   - This is the *outermost* loop and is the slowest (time constant: **hundreds of ms**).

2. **Converter (Station) Level — Firing-Angle Modulation**
   - The power order is converted into a DC current command. Each pole-controller compares measured $I_d$ with $I_{d,ref}$ and modulates the **rectifier firing angle $\alpha$** to enforce the current.
   - The inverter is *not* controlling current in normal operation. Instead, it maintains a constant **DC voltage $V_d$** by holding $\alpha$ (or its equivalent extinction angle $\gamma$) at a fixed value.
   - The rectifier's $\alpha$ *floats* to whatever value is needed to push the current through the line.

3. **Valve (Bypass) Level — Firing-Pulse Generation**
   - Individual thyristor/IGBT gates are fired using the **equidistant pulse scheme** (Phase-Locked Loop synchronised to the AC bus). This is the innermost loop, time constant: **microseconds**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this hierarchy?** Decoupling the loops by speed prevents instability. A valve-level fault must not propagate up to the master power order. KTU examiners *love* asking why a "current margin" is necessary — see the derivation in Section 3.

### 2.1.1 Reactive Power Logic

Reactive power control is **NOT** a closed-loop command in classical HVDC. Instead:

- The **rectifier** naturally consumes $Q_{conv} \approx P_d \tan\phi$, where $\phi$ depends on $\alpha$ and $\mu$.
- The AC system must *supply* this $Q$, or local **shunt capacitor banks / SVCs** must compensate it.
- In modern HVDC stations, about **50–60%** of the converter station footprint is dedicated to harmonic filters and reactive power support.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Hint:** A common trick question is "Can a line-commutated converter *generate* reactive power?" The answer is **No** — it can only *consume* Q. Reactive power *generation* requires self-commutated valves (i.e., a STATCOM or VSC-HVDC).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The following table compiles **every** equation the KTU examiner can ask on this topic. **No vertical bars `|` appear inside the cells** — absolute values use the $\vert$ command.

| # | Formula | Description / When to Use |
|:-:|:--------|:--------------------------|
| 1 | $V_{d0} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi}\,V_{LL}$ | Ideal no-load DC voltage of a 6-pulse bridge |
| 2 | $V_d = V_{d0}\cos\alpha - R_c I_d$ | Rectifier DC voltage (with overlap) |
| 3 | $V_d = V_{d0}\cos\gamma - R_c I_d$ | Inverter DC voltage (back EMF) |
| 4 | $I_d = \dfrac{V_{d1}\cos\alpha - V_{d2}\cos\gamma}{R_c + R_L}$ | Steady-state DC current (line + commutation resistance) |
| 5 | $P_d = V_d \cdot I_d$ | DC-side real power |
| 6 | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{V_d}{V_{d0}} = \cos\alpha - \dfrac{R_c I_d}{V_{d0}}$ | Displacement power factor (approximate, ignoring $\mu$) |
| 7 | $Q_{conv} = P_d \tan\phi$ | Reactive power consumed by the converter |
| 8 | $\mu = \gamma - \alpha_{inv}$ | Commutation overlap (inverter side) |
| 9 | $\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha+\mu) = \dfrac{2 I_d X_c}{\sqrt{2}\,V_{LL}}$ | Overlap from converter theory |
| 10 | $V_{d0}\cos\gamma = V_{d0}\cos\alpha - (R_c + R_L) I_d$ | Power-balance along the DC link |
| 11 | $I_{d,margin} = \Delta I$ | Current margin (typically 0.1 pu) |
| 12 | $P_{ac} = \sqrt{3}\,V_{LL} I_{ac} \cos\phi$ | AC-side real power into rectifier |
| 13 | $Q_{ac} = \sqrt{3}\,V_{LL} I_{ac} \sin\phi$ | AC-side reactive power into rectifier |

### Real-World Utility of These Equations

- **Eq. 6** is the cornerstone of HVDC reactive power planning. Power utilities use it to size filter banks and SVCs.
- **Eq. 11** (current margin) is what makes the **rectifier-current / inverter-voltage** control mode work reliably — without it, both stations fight for the same variable.
- **Eq. 9** shows that overlap $\mu$ *increases* with $I_d$, so the Q demand grows non-linearly at high loading.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Converter Voltage to P and Q

We derive the relationships from the **fundamental Fourier components** of the converter waveforms. Recall that the DC-side voltage of a 6-pulse bridge is the average of the line-to-line voltages over the firing interval.

### Step 1: Average DC Voltage Without Overlap

For a 6-pulse bridge supplied by a balanced 3-phase source of RMS line voltage $V_{LL}$:

$$
V_{d0} = \frac{1}{\pi/3}\int_{-\pi/6}^{+\pi/6} \sqrt{2}\,V_{LL}\cos\theta\,d\theta
$$

**Logic:** The integral of a cosine over a symmetric $\pm 30°$ window centred on the peak of a line-to-line voltage yields the average. Evaluating:

$$
V_{d0} = \frac{3}{\pi}\,\sqrt{2}\,V_{LL}\left[\sin\theta\right]_{-\pi/6}^{+\pi/6} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}\,V_{LL}}{\pi}\cdot 2\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)
$$

$$
V_{d0} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}\,V_{LL}}{\pi}\cdot 2\cdot\frac{1}{2} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi}\,V_{LL}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,V_{d0} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi}\,V_{LL} \approx 1.3505\,V_{LL}\,}
$$

---

### Step 2: Average DC Voltage With Firing Delay $\alpha$

If the valves are fired at a delay angle $\alpha$ after the natural commutation point:

$$
V_d = \frac{1}{\pi/3}\int_{\alpha - \pi/6}^{\alpha + \pi/6} \sqrt{2}\,V_{LL}\cos\theta\,d\theta
$$

**Logic:** We simply shift the integration window by $\alpha$. Because the cosine is integrated over a window of width $60°$ regardless of position, the magnitude of the integral is the same; only its centre matters. By substitution $\theta' = \theta - \alpha$:

$$
V_d = \frac{3\sqrt{2}\,V_{LL}}{\pi}\,\frac{1}{2}\left[\sin(\theta - \alpha)\right]_{\alpha - \pi/6}^{\alpha + \pi/6}
$$

$$
V_d = \frac{3\sqrt{2}\,V_{LL}}{\pi}\cdot \frac{1}{2}\cdot 2\cos\alpha\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_d = V_{d0}\cos\alpha\,}
$$

---

### Step 3: Including Source Inductance (Commutation Overlap $\mu$)

Real AC sources have inductance $L_c$ per phase, giving a commutation reactance $X_c = \omega L_c$. During the commutation interval of angle $\mu$, *two* valves conduct, and the average DC voltage drops by an amount:

$$
\Delta V_{\mu} = \frac{3}{\pi}\,X_c I_d
$$

Adding the resistive drop $R_c I_d$:

$$
\boxed{\,V_d = V_{d0}\cos\alpha - \frac{3}{\pi}X_c I_d - R_c I_d\,}
$$

$$
V_d = V_{d0}\cos\alpha - \left(R_c + \frac{3 X_c}{\pi}\right) I_d
$$

For the **inverter** in extinction-angle control, replace $\alpha$ with $\gamma$:

$$
V_d = V_{d0}\cos\gamma - \left(R_c + \frac{3 X_c}{\pi}\right) I_d
$$

---

### Step 4: The DC Link Current (Two-Terminal Power Flow)

For a two-terminal HVDC link, applying KVL on the DC loop:

$$
V_{d,rect} - V_{d,inv} = (R_L + 2R_c)\,I_d
$$

Expanding:

$$
V_{d0,rect}\cos\alpha - \frac{3 X_c}{\pi}I_d - R_c I_d \;-\; \left(V_{d0,inv}\cos\gamma - \frac{3 X_c}{\pi}I_d - R_c I_d\right) = (R_L + 2R_c) I_d
$$

**Logic:** The reactance terms *cancel* when both stations have the same $X_c$ and the two $\frac{3 X_c}{\pi}I_d$ terms subtract. Collect terms:

$$
V_{d0,rect}\cos\alpha - V_{d0,inv}\cos\gamma = (R_L + 2R_c) I_d + 2 R_c I_d
$$

$$
\boxed{\,I_d = \frac{V_{d0,rect}\cos\alpha - V_{d0,inv}\cos\gamma}{R_L + 4R_c}\,}
$$

This is the **fundamental power-flow equation** of a two-terminal HVDC link.

---

### Step 5: Active Power $P_d$

$$
P_d = V_d \cdot I_d
$$

Using the rectifier-side expression:

$$
\boxed{\,P_d = \left(V_{d0,rect}\cos\alpha - \frac{3X_c + \pi R_c}{\pi}I_d\right) I_d\,}
$$

---

### Step 6: Displacement Power Factor and Reactive Power $Q$

The fundamental-frequency component of the AC line current is in phase with the **midpoint of the commutation interval**, not with the firing instant. The **displacement angle** $\phi$ between the AC voltage and current is:

$$
\phi = \alpha + \frac{\mu}{2}
$$

For small $\mu$, $\phi \approx \alpha$, giving the classic approximation:

$$
\cos\phi \approx \cos\alpha
$$

Combined with the overlap-induced voltage drop:

$$
\cos\phi = \frac{V_d}{V_{d0}} = \cos\alpha - \frac{R_c I_d}{V_{d0}}
$$

The **reactive power** absorbed by the converter is:

$$
Q_{conv} = P_d \tan\phi
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,Q_{conv} = P_d \cdot \tan\!\left(\alpha + \frac{\mu}{2}\right)\,}
$$

Equivalently, using $\cos\phi = V_d / V_{d0}$ and $\sin\phi = \sqrt{1-(V_d/V_{d0})^2}$:

$$
Q_{conv} = P_d \cdot \frac{\sqrt{V_{d0}^2 - V_d^2}}{V_d}
$$

---

### Step 7: Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Given:** A monopolar HVDC link with $V_{LL} = 220\,\text{kV}$, $\alpha = 15°$, $\gamma = 18°$, $X_c/\pi = 8\,\Omega$, $R_c = 1\,\Omega$, $R_L = 10\,\Omega$.
> **Find:** $I_d$, $P_d$, $Q_{conv}$, and $\cos\phi$.

**Solution:**

$$
V_{d0} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot 220{,}000 = 1.3505 \cdot 220{,}000 \approx 297{,}110\;\text{V}
$$

Assuming both stations have the same $V_{d0}$:

$$
I_d = \frac{297{,}110(\cos 15° - \cos 18°)}{10 + 4(1)} = \frac{297{,}110(0.9659 - 0.9511)}{14}
$$

$$
I_d = \frac{297{,}110 \cdot 0.0148}{14} = \frac{4397.2}{14} \approx 314.1\;\text{A}
$$

$$
V_d = V_{d0}\cos\alpha - (R_c + 3X_c/\pi) I_d = 297{,}110(0.9659) - 25(314.1)
$$

$$
V_d \approx 286{,}963 - 7853 = 279{,}110\;\text{V}
$$

$$
P_d = V_d \cdot I_d = 279{,}110 \cdot 314.1 \approx 87.68\;\text{MW}
$$

$$
\cos\phi = \frac{V_d}{V_{d0}} = \frac{279{,}110}{297{,}110} \approx 0.9395
$$

$$
Q_{conv} = P_d \tan(\arccos 0.9395) = 87.68 \cdot 0.347 \approx 30.4\;\text{MVAr}
$$

**Interpretation:** For every **87.7 MW of DC power** transmitted, the rectifier draws about **30.4 MVAr** of reactive power from the AC grid. The AC system must supply this, or local filter banks must do so.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Control Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram shows the **three-level control hierarchy** of an HVDC station as it relates to P and Q. All node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid safety rules.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[MasterLevel: PowerOrder Pd_ref] --> A2[StationLevel: CurrentOrder Id_ref]
    A2 --> A3[ValveLevel: FiringPulse Generation]
    A3 --> A4[ThyristorValve Stack]
    A4 --> A5[DC Bus and DC Line]
    A5 --> A6[InverterStation at Far End]
    A6 --> A3

    B1[AC Bus Voltage Measurement] --> B2[PLL Synchronisation]
    B2 --> A3

    C1[Reactive Power Demand Qconv] --> C2[Filter Banks and SVC]
    C2 --> C3[AC Grid Supply or Compensation]
    C1 --> C4[Operator Alarm if Q Exceeds Limit]

    A4 -. Commutation Overlap mu .-> C1
    A3 -. Firing Angle alpha .-> C1
```

### Reading the Diagram

- **Solid arrows** = command / control signal flow.
- **Dotted arrows** = derived quantities that influence Q (but are not directly commanded).
- The **Master Level** issues a *power order*; the **Station Level** translates it to a *current order*; the **Valve Level** generates the actual thyristor firing pulses.
- $Q$ is a *consequence*, not a *command* — the operator can only monitor it and react.

---

## 4.2 Power-Flow Schematic (Functional Architecture)

A back-to-back or two-terminal HVDC link, with arrows showing where P and Q flow.

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[AC System 1: Sending End] -->|P1, Q1 consumed| REC[Rectifier Station: alpha controlled]
    REC -->|Pd DC power| INV[Inverter Station: gamma controlled]
    INV -->|P2, Q2 consumed| LOAD[AC System 2: Receiving End]

    REC -. Q drawn from AC1 .-> SRC
    INV -. Q drawn from AC2 .-> LOAD

    FB1[Filter + Capacitor Bank] -. Q compensation .-> REC
    FB2[Filter + Capacitor Bank] -. Q compensation .-> INV
```

### Key Insights from the Schematic

- **Both ends consume Q.** Rectifier and inverter each draw about **50–60% of $P_d$ as $Q$** at typical firing angles.
- The **DC link itself carries only $P_d$** — it has no reactive power (no inductance in the DC sense, only resistance).
- Filter banks supply *most* of the Q locally; the AC grid supplies only the *net* deficit.

---

## 4.3 P-Q Capability Chart of an HVDC Converter (Text Schematic)

```
        Q (lag)  +Q consumed
                  ^
                  |
       ___________|___________
      /           |           \
     /   [Rectifier  Operating |
    /     Region]      |        \
   /--------------------|-------->  P (DC Power)
   \                    |        /
    \                   |       /
     \__________________|______/
                       |
                       +Q generated (only in VSC / STATCOM)
```

For a line-commutated HVDC, the operating region is **always in the upper half-plane** (lagging Q). For a self-commutated VSC-HVDC or STATCOM, the operating region extends into **all four quadrants**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**"Why is a line-commutated HVDC converter unable to generate reactive power?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A line-commutated HVDC converter uses **thyristors**, which can be *turned on* by a gate pulse but cannot be *turned off* by the gate — turn-off depends on the natural reversal of the AC line voltage. As a result, the AC current waveform lags the AC voltage by a displacement angle $\phi = \alpha + \mu/2$. This means the converter **always operates at lagging power factor**, drawing reactive power from the AC grid. Reactive power generation requires the valve to *lead* the AC voltage, which needs **forced commutation** (IGBT-based VSC). Hence, line-commutated converters can only **consume** Q, never generate it. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Series Question 1]`
**"Define the current margin in HVDC control. Why is it necessary?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **current margin** ($\Delta I$, typically **0.1 pu** or 10% of rated current) is the difference between the **rectifier current order** and the **inverter current order** (with the inverter order being lower by $\Delta I$). It is necessary because, in normal operation, **only one converter can control the DC current** — if both tried simultaneously, the system would oscillate or become unstable. The margin ensures that the rectifier operates in *current-control mode* (with $\alpha$ floating) while the inverter operates in *voltage-control mode* (with $\gamma$ fixed), and the roles never collide. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, Main Question]`

> **A(a) [7 marks]** Derive the steady-state DC current equation for a two-terminal HVDC link in terms of the rectifier firing angle $\alpha$ and inverter extinction angle $\gamma$. State clearly all assumptions.
>
> **A(b) [7 marks]** For a 500 kV, 1000 A monopolar HVDC link, the rectifier AC voltage is 220 kV (line) and the inverter AC voltage is 215 kV (line). Both stations have $\alpha = 15°$, $\gamma = 18°$, $X_c = 12\,\Omega$, $R_c = 1.5\,\Omega$, and DC line resistance $R_L = 20\,\Omega$. Calculate (i) the DC power transmitted, (ii) the reactive power consumed by the rectifier.

---

### Model Solution for A(a) — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Quasi-steady-state operation (DC quantities constant).
- Both converters are 6-pulse bridges (or equivalent 12-pulse).
- AC sources are balanced and sinusoidal.
- DC line has only resistance $R_L$ (no DC-side inductance included in average model).
- Commutation reactance $X_c$ is the same at both ends.

**Step 2 — Write the rectifier DC voltage (2 marks):**

$$
V_{d,rect} = V_{d0,rect}\cos\alpha - \frac{3X_c}{\pi}I_d - R_c I_d
$$

**Step 3 — Write the inverter DC voltage (1 mark):**

$$
V_{d,inv} = V_{d0,inv}\cos\gamma - \frac{3X_c}{\pi}I_d - R_c I_d
$$

**Step 4 — Apply KVL on the DC loop (2 marks):**

$$
V_{d,rect} - V_{d,inv} = R_L I_d
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify (1 mark):**

$$
V_{d0,rect}\cos\alpha - V_{d0,inv}\cos\gamma = (R_L + 2R_c) I_d
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,I_d = \frac{V_{d0,rect}\cos\alpha - V_{d0,inv}\cos\gamma}{R_L + 2R_c}\,}
$$

---

### Model Solution for A(b) — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Compute $V_{d0}$ for each end (1 mark):**

$$
V_{d0,rect} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi}\cdot 220{,}000 \approx 297{,}110\;\text{V}
$$

$$
V_{d0,inv} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi}\cdot 215{,}000 \approx 290{,}358\;\text{V}
$$

**Step 2 — Plug into the current equation (1 mark):**

$$
I_d = \frac{297{,}110\cos 15° - 290{,}358\cos 18°}{20 + 2(1.5)} = \frac{297{,}110(0.9659) - 290{,}358(0.9511)}{23}
$$

$$
I_d = \frac{286{,}963 - 276{,}187}{23} = \frac{10{,}776}{23} \approx 468.5\;\text{A}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $V_{d,rect}$ (1 mark):**

$$
V_{d,rect} = 297{,}110(0.9659) - \frac{3(12)}{\pi}(468.5) - 1.5(468.5)
$$

$$
V_{d,rect} = 286{,}963 - 5367 - 703 = 280{,}893\;\text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — DC Power (1 mark):**

$$
P_d = V_{d,rect}\cdot I_d = 280{,}893 \cdot 468.5 \approx 131.6\;\text{MW}
$$

**Step 5 — Displacement power factor and Q (2 marks):**

$$
\cos\phi = \frac{V_{d,rect}}{V_{d0,rect}} = \frac{280{,}893}{297{,}110} \approx 0.9455
$$

$$
\phi = \arccos(0.9455) \approx 19.0°
$$

$$
Q_{conv} = P_d \tan\phi = 131.6 \cdot \tan 19.0° = 131.6 \cdot 0.3443 \approx 45.3\;\text{MVAr}
$$

**Final Answer:** (i) $P_d \approx \mathbf{131.6\;MW}$  (ii) $Q_{conv} \approx \mathbf{45.3\;MVAr}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

> **B(a) [7 marks]** With the help of the converter P–Q capability chart, explain why reactive power consumption of an HVDC rectifier increases with firing angle $\alpha$ and with DC current $I_d$.
>
> **B(b) [7 marks]** A 6-pulse HVDC rectifier is supplied from a 220 kV, 50 Hz AC source. The commutation reactance is $X_c = 10\,\Omega$, commutation resistance $R_c = 0$, and the firing angle is $\alpha = 20°$. If the DC current is $I_d = 1000\,\text{A}$, calculate (i) the no-load DC voltage $V_{d0}$, (ii) the actual DC voltage $V_d$ including commutation overlap $\mu$, (iii) the displacement power factor, and (iv) the reactive power consumed by the converter.

---

### Model Solution for B(a) — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Recall the P–Q relationship (2 marks):**

The converter behaves like a load with active power $P_d = V_d I_d$ and reactive power $Q = P_d \tan\phi$, where $\cos\phi = V_d/V_{d0}$.

**Step 2 — Why Q grows with $\alpha$ (2 marks):**
As $\alpha$ increases, $\cos\alpha$ decreases, so $V_d = V_{d0}\cos\alpha$ decreases. Therefore $\cos\phi = V_d/V_{d0}$ decreases, meaning $\tan\phi$ increases, and hence $Q = P_d \tan\phi$ increases. Physically, a larger $\alpha$ means thyristors fire later in the cycle, so the fundamental AC current lags the voltage by a larger angle.

**Step 3 — Why Q grows with $I_d$ (2 marks):**
At higher $I_d$, the commutation overlap angle $\mu$ increases (since $\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha+\mu) = 2I_d X_c/(\sqrt{2} V_{LL})$). The extra overlap adds to the effective displacement angle $\phi = \alpha + \mu/2$, further reducing $\cos\phi$ and increasing $\tan\phi$. Combined with the linear increase in $P_d$, this gives a super-linear increase in $Q$.

**Step 4 — P–Q chart interpretation (1 mark):**
On a P–Q chart, the constant-$\alpha$ curve is approximately parabolic in the upper half-plane, with $Q$ rising steeply as $P$ approaches its maximum (which occurs at $\alpha = 0$, where $\cos\phi = 1$ and $Q \to 0$).

---

### Model Solution for B(b) — Step by Step

**Step 1 — No-load DC voltage (1 mark):**

$$
V_{d0} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi}\cdot 220{,}000 = 297{,}110\;\text{V} \approx 297.1\;\text{kV}
$$

**Step 2 — Commutation overlap $\mu$ (2 marks):**

$$
\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha + \mu) = \frac{2 I_d X_c}{\sqrt{2}\,V_{LL}}
$$

$$
\cos 20° - \cos(20° + \mu) = \frac{2(1000)(10)}{\sqrt{2}(220{,}000)} = \frac{20{,}000}{311{,}127} \approx 0.0643
$$

$$
0.9397 - \cos(20° + \mu) = 0.0643 \;\Longrightarrow\; \cos(20° + \mu) = 0.8754
$$

$$
20° + \mu = \arccos(0.8754) \approx 28.93° \;\Longrightarrow\; \mu \approx 8.93°
$$

**Step 3 — Actual DC voltage $V_d$ (1 mark):**

$$
V_d = V_{d0}\cos\alpha - \frac{3 X_c}{\pi}I_d = 297{,}110(0.9397) - \frac{3(10)}{\pi}(1000)
$$

$$
V_d = 279{,}225 - 9549 = 269{,}676\;\text{V} \approx 269.7\;\text{kV}
$$

**Step 4 — Displacement power factor (1 mark):**

The fundamental displacement angle is $\phi = \alpha + \mu/2 = 20° + 4.47° = 24.47°$.

$$
\cos\phi = \cos 24.47° \approx 0.9101
$$

**Step 5 — Reactive power (2 marks):**

$$
P_d = V_d I_d = 269{,}676 \cdot 1000 = 269.7\;\text{MW}
$$

$$
Q_{conv} = P_d \tan\phi = 269.7 \cdot \tan 24.47° = 269.7 \cdot 0.4549 \approx 122.7\;\text{MVAr}
$$

**Final Answer:** (i) $V_{d0} = \mathbf{297.1\;kV}$ (ii) $V_d = \mathbf{269.7\;kV}$ (iii) $\cos\phi = \mathbf{0.910}$ (iv) $Q_{conv} = \mathbf{122.7\;MVAr}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert line-to-line to phase.** All HVDC equations use **line-to-line RMS** ($V_{LL}$), not phase voltage.
> 2. **Confusing $\alpha$ and $\gamma$.** In a rectifier, the variable is $\alpha$ (firing delay). In an inverter in extinction-angle control, the variable is $\gamma$ (extinction advance). Marking them as interchangeable is a 2-mark penalty.
> 3. **Dropping the overlap term $3X_c I_d / \pi$.** Many students remember $V_d = V_{d0}\cos\alpha$ but forget the overlap drop. This costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing the current margin sign.** The rectifier current order is *higher* than the inverter current order by $\Delta I$. Reversing the sign produces a *negative* $I_d$ and full marks loss.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert angles to radians** when using trigonometric identities. Most KTU problems keep everything in **degrees**, but if you reach for a Taylor series or integration, switch to radians.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **$P$ is controlled by $\alpha$ (rectifier) and $\gamma$ (inverter)**, not directly set. The DC current order is the lever.
- [ ] **$Q$ is a *consequence*, not a *command*,** in line-commutated HVDC. It is **always lagging** (absorbed).
- [ ] The **current margin** ($\Delta I \approx 0.1$ pu) is what lets rectifier-current and inverter-voltage control coexist.
- [ ] **$V_{d0} = 1.3505\,V_{LL}$** — a 6-pulse constant you must memorise.
- [ ] The **displacement power factor** is $\cos\phi = V_d / V_{d0}$ (approximate, small $\mu$).
- [ ] **$Q = P \tan\phi$** — the relationship that makes Q planning inseparable from P scheduling.
- [ ] As $\alpha$ increases, $Q$ increases *faster* than linearly because both $P$ drops and $\tan\phi$ rises.
- [ ] The **DC link carries no reactive power** — only the AC sides do.
- [ ] **VSC-HVDC / STATCOM** can generate Q (all four quadrants) because they use **forced commutation** (IGBTs).
- [ ] **Filters + capacitor banks** supply most of $Q$ locally; the AC grid supplies the net deficit.
- [ ] **Time-scale hierarchy:** Master (s) → Station (100 ms) → Valve ($\mu$s). Don't mix them.
- [ ] **Overlap $\mu$ grows with $I_d$ and with $X_c$** — the commutation reactance is the *price* of AC–DC conversion.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Independent Real and Reactive Power Flow Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET862 - HVDC AND FACTS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Independent Real and Reactive Power Flow Control # ⚡ Module 4 — Independent Real and Reactive Power Flow Control ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In a conventional AC transmission line, the **real power (P)** flow is governed predominantly by the **load angle (δ)** between the two buses, while the **reactive power (Q)** flow is governed by the **voltage magnitude difference** between the buses. In a classical unregulated system, these two quantities are **coupled** — an attempt to control one inevitably disturbs the other. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Independent Real and Reactive Power Flow Control** is defined as the ability of a power-electronic compensator to regulate the active power (P) and reactive power (Q) transmitted through a transmission line as **two decoupled control variables**, without one control action adversely affecting the other. This is the central operating principle of the **Unified Power Flow Controller (UPFC)**, the most versatile member of the FACTS family. The UPFC (introduced by Gyugyi, 1991) achieves this by injecting a **synchronous voltage source $V_{pq}$** of controllable **magnitude and phase angle** in series with the transmission line, simultaneously supported by a **shunt-connected voltage source (STATCOM)** that maintains the DC link and provides local reactive support. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Tap with Two Knobs" Imagine a water pipeline carrying flow between two reservoirs. The **flow rate (P)** is set by the *pressure difference* (analogous to the load angle δ), while the **pressure (Q)** depends on the *height of water in the source tank* (analogous to voltage magnitude). In the classical system, you have **only one knob** — turning it changes both flow and pressure together (coupled control). A UPFC, however, is like fitting a **smart in-line pump and pressure regulator with two independent knobs**: one knob changes only the flow (P), the other only the pressure (Q). You can push more water through without raising the pressure, or raise the pressure without forcing more water through. > [!NOTE] > The **"in-phase" component** of the injected series voltage is the *pressure knob* (reactive), and the **"quadrature" component** is the *flow knob* (real power). This decoupling is the very essence of independent P–Q control. ## 1.3 Why is Independent Control Essential? | Phenomenon | Effect on Power System | |---|---| | Loop flows | Unscheduled power circulation through parallel corridors | | Congestion | One line overloaded while another is under-loaded | | Voltage instability | Inability to maintain bus voltages under stress | | Transient swings | Poor damping after disturbances | | Economic dispatch | Inability to enforce contractual power transactions | By decoupling P and Q control, the UPFC enables **simultaneous, optimal, and contractual** operation of transmission corridors. ## 1.4 The Two Sub-Systems of a UPFC A UPFC physically consists of **two Voltage Source Converters (VSCs)** sharing a common DC capacitor: | Branch | Converter | Function | |---|---|---| | **Shunt (Br. 1)** | STATCOM | Maintains DC-link voltage, supplies/absorbs reactive power at the bus | | **Series (Br. 2)** | SSSC | Injects a controllable series voltage $V_{pq} \angle \theta_{pq}$ into the line | > [!NOTE] > The **DC link** allows real power to be exchanged between the shunt and series branches — a feature absent in any other FACTS device. This is what makes the UPFC *unified*. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Phasor Principle Behind Decoupling Consider a simplified 2-bus transmission system with sending-end voltage $\bar V_s = V_s \angle 0^\circ$ (reference) and receiving-end voltage $\bar V_r = V_r \angle -\delta$, connected through a line reactance $X$. The UPFC injects a series voltage $\bar V_{pq}$ of magnitude $V_{pq}$ at an angle $\theta_{pq}$ w.r.t. $\bar V_s$. The **decomposition trick** that achieves independent P–Q control is: $$ \bar V_{pq} \;=\; V_P \;+\; j\,V_Q $$ where: - **$V_P$ (in-phase component)** → aligned with $\bar V_s$ - **$V_Q$ (quadrature component)** → leads $\bar V_s$ by $90^\circ$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Interpretation:** > - $V_P$ acts *along* the voltage phasor → changes the **magnitude** of the bus voltage → primarily affects **reactive power** $Q$. > - $V_Q$ acts *perpendicular* to the voltage phasor → changes the **phase angle** of the bus voltage → primarily affects **real power** $P$. Because $V_P$ and $V_Q$ are **orthogonal components**, they can be varied **independently** by the controller. This is the foundation of UPFC's unique capability. ## 2.2 Equivalent Circuit Model The UPFC is modelled as a **shunt-connected current source** $I_{sh}$ (or controllable reactive current) in parallel with a **series-connected voltage source** $\bar V_{pq}$, both referenced to a common DC bus. $$ \bar V_s \;\longrightarrow\; jX \;\longrightarrow\; \bar V_{pq} \;\longrightarrow\; jX \;\longrightarrow\; \bar V_r $$ The mid-point series voltage model is the standard textbook approximation. The bus voltage equations become: $$ \bar V_s \;-\; \bar V_{pq} \;-\; \bar V_r \;=\; jX\,\bar I $$ $$ \therefore \quad \bar I \;=\; \dfrac{\bar V_s \;-\; \bar V_{pq} \;-\; \bar V_r}{jX} $$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Equation | Description | Unit | |---|---|---| | $V_s,\; V_r$ | Sending- and receiving-end voltage magnitudes | pu (kV base) | | $\delta$ | Power angle (lead of $V_s$ over $V_r$) | rad / deg | | $X$ | Line (or equivalent) reactance | pu | | $\bar V_{pq} = V_P + jV_Q$ | Injected series voltage in rectangular form | pu | | $\lvert V_{pq}\rvert = \sqrt{V_P^{\,2} + V_Q^{\,2}}$ | Magnitude of injected voltage | pu | | $\theta_{pq} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(V_Q / V_P\right)$ | Angle of injected voltage w.r.t. $V_s$ | rad | | $P_s = \dfrac{V_s V_r}{X}\sin\delta \;+\; \dfrac{V_s V_Q}{X}$ | Sending-end real power | pu (MW base) | | $Q_s = \dfrac{V_s^{\,2} - V_s V_r \cos\delta}{X} \;-\; \dfrac{V_s V_P}{X}$ | Sending-end reactive power | pu (MVAR base) | | $P_r = \dfrac{V_s V_r}{X}\sin\delta \;-\; \dfrac{V_s V_Q}{X}$ | Receiving-end real power | pu | | $Q_r = \dfrac{V_s V_r \cos\delta - V_r^{\,2}}{X} \;+\; \dfrac{V_s V_P}{X}$ | Receiving-end reactive power | pu | | $\Delta P_s \big\vert_{V_P\,=\,0} = \dfrac{V_s V_Q}{X}$ | Pure-quadrature control → real power shift | pu | | $\Delta Q_s \big\vert_{V_Q\,=\,0} = -\dfrac{V_s V_P}{X}$ | Pure-in-phase control → reactive power shift | pu | | $P_{DC} = \mathrm{Re}\{\bar V_{pq}\, \bar I^{*}\}$ | Real power exchanged through DC link | pu | | Rating constraint: $\sqrt{V_P^{\,2}+V_Q^{\,2}} \le V_{pq}^{\,\max}$ | Operating circle in $(V_P, V_Q)$ plane | pu | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Most students miss that the $\Delta Q_s$ has a **negative sign** — the in-phase component $V_P$ *reduces* the sending-end reactive power when $V_P$ is positive. This is because an in-phase series voltage essentially boosts the line voltage, decreasing the reactive drop. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Independent P–Q Control 1. **Flexible AC Transmission:** Enables dynamic routing of power across multiple corridors. 2. **Power Market & Deregulation:** Enforces bilateral power contracts by guaranteeing specified $P$ flows. 3. **Loop-Flow Mitigation:** Cancels unscheduled MW circulation in meshed networks. 4. **Voltage Stability:** The shunt branch holds the bus voltage while the series branch optimizes $P/Q$ flow. 5. **Damping of Oscillations:** Real-power modulation through $V_Q$ can damp inter-area swings. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations ## 3.1 Derivation of the Sending-End Complex Power ### Step 1 — Define the system reference Place the sending-end voltage as the reference phasor: $$ \bar V_s \;=\; V_s \angle 0^\circ \;=\; V_s $$ The receiving-end voltage lags by the load angle $\delta$: $$ \bar V_r \;=\; V_r \angle -\delta $$ The UPFC-injected series voltage has arbitrary magnitude and phase: $$ \bar V_{pq} \;=\; V_{pq} \angle \theta_{pq} $$ ### Step 2 — Apply KVL to obtain the line current $$ \bar V_s \;-\; \bar V_{pq} \;-\; \bar V_r \;=\; jX\,\bar I $$ $$ \therefore \quad \bar I \;=\; \dfrac{\bar V_s - \bar V_{pq} - \bar V_r}{jX} \;=\; -\,\dfrac{j}{X}\bigl(\bar V_s - \bar V_{pq} - \bar V_r\bigr) $$ > *Justification:* The line is assumed purely reactive (resistance neglected in the typical UPFC analysis, consistent with the EHV line approximation). ### Step 3 — Form the complex conjugate of the current $$ \bar I^{*} \;=\; +\,\dfrac{j}{X}\bigl(\bar V_s^{*} - \bar V_{pq}^{*} - \bar V_r^{*}\bigr) $$ ### Step 4 — Compute the sending-end complex power $$ \bar S_s \;=\; \bar V_s \cdot \bar I^{*} $$ $$ \bar S_s \;=\; \bar V_s \cdot \dfrac{j}{X}\bigl(\bar V_s^{*} - \bar V_{pq}^{*} - \bar V_r^{*}\bigr) \;=\; \dfrac{j}{X}\,V_s \bigl(V_s - V_{pq}\angle\!-\theta_{pq} - V_r\angle\delta\bigr) $$ ### Step 5 — Expand the bracketed term Using Euler's form $V_r\angle\delta = V_r(\cos\delta + j\sin\delta)$ and $V_{pq}\angle\!-\theta_{pq} = V_{pq}(\cos\theta_{pq} - j\sin\theta_{pq})$: $$ V_s - V_{pq}\angle\!-\theta_{pq} - V_r\angle\delta $$ $$ = \;V_s \;-\; V_{pq}\cos\theta_{pq} \;-\; V_r\cos\delta \;+\; j\!\left(V_{pq}\sin\theta_{pq} + V_r\sin\delta\right) $$ Define the two real components: $$ A \;=\; V_s - V_{pq}\cos\theta_{pq} - V_r\cos\delta $$ $$ B \;=\; V_{pq}\sin\theta_{pq} + V_r\sin\delta $$ So the bracket equals $A + jB$. ### Step 6 — Multiply by $j$ and divide by $X$ $$ \dfrac{j}{X}\,(A + jB) \;=\; \dfrac{jA}{X} - \dfrac{B}{X} $$ ### Step 7 — Extract real and reactive parts $$ P_s \;=\; \mathrm{Re}\{\bar S_s\} \;=\; -\,\dfrac{B}{X} \;=\; \dfrac{V_s V_{pq}\sin\theta_{pq}}{X} \;+\; \dfrac{V_s V_r \sin\delta}{X} $$ $$ Q_s \;=\; \mathrm{Im}\{\bar S_s\} \;=\; \dfrac{A}{X} \;=\; \dfrac{V_s^{\,2}}{X} \;-\; \dfrac{V_s V_{pq}\cos\theta_{pq}}{X} \;-\; \dfrac{V_s V_r \cos\delta}{X} $$ ### Step 8 — Rearrange into the canonical KTU form $$ P_s \;=\; \dfrac{V_s V_r}{X}\sin\delta \;+\; \dfrac{V_s V_{pq}\sin\theta_{pq}}{X} $$ $$ Q_s \;=\; \dfrac{V_s^{\,2} - V_s V_r \cos\delta}{X} \;-\; \dfrac{V_s V_{pq}\cos\theta_{pq}}{X} $$ > *This is the key result the KTU board expects you to derive cold in the exam hall.* ## 3.2 Derivation of the Independent P–Q Decoupling ### Step 9 — Substitute $V_{pq}\cos\theta_{pq} = V_P$ and $V_{pq}\sin\theta_{pq} = V_Q$ These equalities follow directly from the rectangular representation: $$ \bar V_{pq} \;=\; V_{pq}(\cos\theta_{pq} + j\sin\theta_{pq}) \;=\; \underbrace{V_{pq}\cos\theta_{pq}}_{V_P} \;+\; j\!\underbrace{V_{pq}\sin\theta_{pq}}_{V_Q} $$ Hence the canonical UPFC power-flow equations are: $$ \boxed{\;P_s \;=\; \dfrac{V_s V_r}{X}\sin\delta \;+\; \dfrac{V_s\,V_Q}{X}\;} $$ $$ \boxed{\;Q_s \;=\; \dfrac{V_s^{\,2} - V_s V_r \cos\delta}{X} \;-\; \dfrac{V_s\,V_P}{X}\;} $$ ### Step 10 — Establish the decoupling Consider an *uncompensated* baseline (no UPFC, $V_{pq}=0$): $$ P_s^{(0)} \;=\; \dfrac{V_s V_r}{X}\sin\delta^{(0)}, \qquad Q_s^{(0)} \;=\; \dfrac{V_s^{\,2} - V_s V_r \cos\delta^{(0)}}{X} $$ The change in $P_s$ and $Q_s$ due to UPFC injection (assuming $V_s, V_r, \delta$ held constant by the controller): $$ \Delta P_s \;=\; \dfrac{V_s V_Q}{X} $$ $$ \Delta Q_s \;=\; -\,\dfrac{V_s V_P}{X} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion of Derivation:** > 1. $\Delta P_s$ depends **only** on $V_Q$ — therefore **$V_Q$ controls real power $P$**. > 2. $\Delta Q_s$ depends **only** on $V_P$ — therefore **$V_P$ controls reactive power $Q$**. > 3. Because $V_P$ and $V_Q$ are orthogonal and controller-set independently, the UPFC achieves **decoupled, independent control of $P$ and $Q$**. ### Step 11 — Operating region in the $(V_P, V_Q)$ plane Since the converter has a finite voltage limit, the UPFC operates inside a circle: $$ V_P^{\,2} \;+\; V_Q^{\,2} \;\le\; \bigl(V_{pq}^{\,\max}\bigr)^{2} $$ This circular boundary is the **UPFC operating circle** in the $V_P$–$V_Q$ plane. Any $(V_P, V_Q)$ pair inside this circle is feasible for controlling $P$ and $Q$ simultaneously. ### Step 12 — The role of the shunt branch (STATCOM) The series branch's injected voltage, when carrying line current, draws a real power: $$ P_{series} \;=\; \mathrm{Re}\{\bar V_{pq}\,\bar I^{*}\} \;=\; V_{pq}\,I\,\cos(\theta_{pq} - \phi) $$ where $\phi$ is the current angle. This real power must be supplied by the DC capacitor, which would otherwise discharge. The **shunt branch (STATCOM)** draws the exact amount of real power from the AC bus to **maintain $V_{DC}$ constant**, simultaneously providing or absorbing reactive power at the bus for voltage regulation. This DC-link real-power exchange is what makes the UPFC *unified* and is impossible in a stand-alone SSSC or STATCOM. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Illustration A 400 kV, 50 Hz line has $X = 0.5\;\text{pu}$ with $V_s = 1.05\;\text{pu}$ and $V_r = 1.00\;\text{pu}$, $\delta = 15^\circ$. A UPFC injects $V_P = 0.08\;\text{pu}$, $V_Q = 0.10\;\text{pu}$. Find $P_s$ and $Q_s$. **Uncompensated terms:** $$ P^{(0)} = \dfrac{(1.05)(1.00)}{0.5}\sin 15^\circ = 2.10 \times 0.2588 = 0.5435 \;\text{pu} $$ $$ Q^{(0)} = \dfrac{(1.05)^2 - (1.05)(1.00)\cos 15^\circ}{0.5} = \dfrac{1.1025 - 1.0142}{0.5} = 0.1766\;\text{pu} $$ **UPFC contributions:** $$ \Delta P = \dfrac{V_s V_Q}{X} = \dfrac{(1.05)(0.10)}{0.5} = 0.21\;\text{pu} $$ $$ \Delta Q = -\dfrac{V_s V_P}{X} = -\dfrac{(1.05)(0.08)}{0.5} = -0.168\;\text{pu} $$ **Final powers:** $$ P_s = 0.5435 + 0.21 = 0.7535\;\text{pu} \approx 753.5\;\text{MW (on 1000 MVA base)} $$ $$ Q_s = 0.1766 - 0.168 = 0.0086\;\text{pu} \approx 8.6\;\text{MVAR} $$ > The real power increased by **0.21 pu** because $V_Q > 0$, while the reactive power was driven to nearly **zero**, demonstrating **independent** P and Q regulation. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a UPFC The following Mermaid diagram maps the **block-level functional architecture** of a UPFC, showing the power flow path and the orthogonal control inputs ($V_P$ and $V_Q$). ```mermaid graph TD BusS[("Sending End Bus
Vs = 1.05 pu")]:::bus Br1["Shunt VSC
STATCOM
(VSC-1)"]:::shunt Br2["Series VSC
SSSC
(VSC-2)"]:::series DC[("DC Link
Capacitor C
Vdc maintained by STATCOM")]:::dc Inj[("Injected Series Voltage
Vpq = Vp + jVq")]:::inject Line[/"Transmission Line
Reactance X"/]:::line BusR[("Receiving End Bus
Vr = 1.00 pu")]:::bus BusS --> Br1 Br1 -- reactive Ish --> BusS Br1 --- DC DC --- Br2 Br2 --> Inj Inj --> Line Line --> BusR BusS -. Vs reference .-> Inj CtrlP(("Controller
Vp axis")):::ctrl CtrlQ(("Controller
Vq axis")):::ctrl CtrlP -- V_P command --> Br2 CtrlQ -- V_Q command --> Br2 classDef bus fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef shunt fill:#A8D8B9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef series fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#1E40AF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef dc fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C2185B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef inject fill:#D7BDE2,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef line fill:#F5CBA7,stroke:#A04000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ctrl fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **shunt VSC** regulates bus voltage and supplies DC-link real power. - The **series VSC** synthesises $V_{pq}$, whose in-phase ($V_P$) and quadrature ($V_Q$) components are set by **two orthogonal controllers** — yielding decoupled P–Q control. - Real power flows seamlessly between the two VSCs through the DC capacitor. ## 4.2 Phasor Decomposition of the Injected Series Voltage ```mermaid graph LR VsAxis(("Real Axis
Vs reference")):::axis VsVec["Vs
(1.05 pu)"]:::phasor VpVec["Vp
in-phase with Vs
CONTROLS Q"]:::vpe VqVec["Vq
90 deg leading Vs
CONTROLS P"]:::vqe VpqVec["Vpq = Vp + jVq
|Vpq| = sqrt(Vp^2 + Vq^2)"]:::vpq VrVec["Vr
(1.00 pu)
lagging by delta"]:::phasor VsAxis --- VsVec VsVec -- "Vp component" --> VpVec VsVec -- "Vq component" --> VqVec VpVec --> VpqVec VqVec --> VpqVec VsVec -- "rotated by -delta" --> VrVec classDef axis fill:#ECF0F1,stroke:#34495E,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef phasor fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#1B4F72,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef vpe fill:#FAD7A0,stroke:#B9770E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef vqe fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#1E8449,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef vpq fill:#E8DAEF,stroke:#6C3483,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Interpretation of the phasor diagram:** - $V_P$ extends along the $V_s$ axis → it acts on the **voltage magnitude** → modulates $Q$. - $V_Q$ extends perpendicular to $V_s$ → it acts on the **voltage angle** → modulates $P$. - $V_{pq}$ is the phasor sum, and any rotation in the $V_P$–$V_Q$ plane corresponds to a different mix of $P$ and $Q$ control. ## 4.3 UPFC Operating Circle in the $V_P$–$V_Q$ Plane ```mermaid graph TD Origin[("0,0
No Compensation")]:::origin QAxis[("Qp axis - Q Control
(Vp varies)")]:::qax PAxis[("Pq axis - P Control
(Vq varies)")]:::pax Point1["Operating Point 1
Vp=0.08, Vq=0.10
Independent P-Q"]:::op Point2["Operating Point 2
Vp=0.10, Vq=0.00
Pure Q Control"]:::op Point3["Operating Point 3
Vp=0.00, Vq=0.10
Pure P Control"]:::op Circle["Boundary:
Vp^2 + Vq^2 = Vpq_max^2"]:::boundary Origin --> QAxis Origin --> PAxis QAxis --> Point2 PAxis --> Point3 Point2 --- Point1 Point3 --- Point1 Point1 -. lies inside .-> Circle classDef origin fill:#F8F9F9,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef qax fill:#FCF3CF,stroke:#7D6608,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef pax fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#196F3D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef op fill:#F5B7B1,stroke:#922B21,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef boundary fill:#E8DAEF,stroke:#6C3483,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **Q-axis** represents pure reactive-power control ($V_Q = 0$). - The **P-axis** represents pure real-power control ($V_P = 0$). - Any point *inside* the operating circle represents a feasible $(V_P, V_Q)$ pair that delivers **simultaneous, independent P and Q control**. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > All questions are aligned with the **KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech ESE pattern**: Part A (2 × 3 = 6 marks) and Part B (1 × 14 marks, with internal choice). --- ## 📘 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]** **Define the Unified Power Flow Controller (UPFC). State any two of its salient features.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Unified Power Flow Controller (UPFC)** is the most versatile FACTS device, conceived by L. Gyugyi (1991). It consists of two voltage-source converters (VSCs) — a **shunt-connected STATCOM (VSC-1)** and a **series-connected SSSC (VSC-2)** — coupled through a **common DC-link capacitor**. **Two salient features (1.5 marks each):** 1. It can **simultaneously and independently** control all three transmission parameters: bus voltage magnitude, line reactance, and power angle — and hence control real and reactive power flow as two fully decoupled variables. 2. Real power can be **exchanged bidirectionally** between the shunt and series branches through the DC link, a feature not available in any other FACTS controller. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **How is the injected series voltage of a UPFC decomposed to achieve independent control of P and Q?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The injected series voltage $\bar V_{pq} = V_P + jV_Q$ is decomposed into two **orthogonal components** in the $V_s$ reference frame: $$ \bar V_{pq} = \underbrace{V_P}_{\text{in-phase with } V_s} \;+\; j\!\underbrace{V_Q}_{\text{90° leading } V_s} $$ - $V_P$ lies along the $V_s$ axis → affects the **voltage magnitude** of the bus → controls **reactive power $Q$** via $\Delta Q_s = -V_s V_P / X$. - $V_Q$ lies perpendicular to $V_s$ → affects the **phase angle** → controls **real power $P$** via $\Delta P_s = V_s V_Q / X$. Since $V_P$ and $V_Q$ are **mathematically orthogonal** and set by **independent controllers**, real and reactive power flows can be regulated **without cross-coupling** — this is the essence of independent P–Q flow control. **(1 mark for the decomposition, 1 mark for control mapping, 1 mark for orthogonal/independent explanation.)** --- ## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > **Internal-Choice Pattern (KTU 2024):** Two independent alternatives; answer ANY ONE. --- ### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]` **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat phasor diagram, derive the sending-end real and reactive power expressions for a transmission line incorporating a UPFC. Clearly indicate which component of the injected series voltage controls P and which controls Q. **(b) [7 marks]** A 220 kV, 50 Hz transmission line has $V_s = 1.06$ pu, $V_r = 1.00$ pu, $X = 0.4$ pu, and $\delta = 12^\circ$. A UPFC injects a series voltage with components $V_P = 0.05$ pu and $V_Q = 0.08$ pu. Compute the sending-end real and reactive power with and without the UPFC. Comment on the independence of P and Q control. --- #### ✅ Model Solution **(a) Derivation [7 marks]** Refer to **Section 3.1** above for the full step-by-step derivation. The KTU mark breakdown is: | Step | Description | Marks | |---|---|---| | 1 | Phasor diagram showing $V_s$, $V_r$, $V_{pq}$, and orthogonal $V_P, V_Q$ | 2 | | 2 | KVL: $V_s - V_{pq} - V_r = jXI$, hence current expression | 1 | | 3 | Complex power $S_s = V_s I^*$ with conjugates expanded | 1.5 | | 4 | Final canonical equations: $P_s = V_s V_r \sin\delta / X + V_s V_Q / X$ and $Q_s = (V_s^{\,2} - V_s V_r \cos\delta)/X - V_s V_P / X$ | 1.5 | | 5 | Conclusion: $V_Q$ controls $P$, $V_P$ controls $Q$ — independence established | 1 | **(b) Numerical [7 marks]** **Without UPFC ($V_{pq} = 0$):** $$ P_s^{(0)} = \frac{(1.06)(1.00)}{0.4}\sin 12^\circ = 2.65 \times 0.2079 = 0.551\;\text{pu} $$ $$ Q_s^{(0)} = \frac{(1.06)^2 - (1.06)(1.00)\cos 12^\circ}{0.4} = \frac{1.1236 - 1.0369}{0.4} = 0.2168\;\text{pu} $$ **With UPFC ($V_P = 0.05$, $V_Q = 0.08$):** $$ \Delta P_s = \frac{V_s V_Q}{X} = \frac{(1.06)(0.08)}{0.4} = 0.212\;\text{pu} $$ $$ \Delta Q_s = -\frac{V_s V_P}{X} = -\frac{(1.06)(0.05)}{0.4} = -0.1325\;\text{pu} $$ $$ \therefore P_s = 0.551 + 0.212 = \mathbf{0.763}\;\text{pu} \approx \mathbf{763\;\text{MW}} $$ $$ \therefore Q_s = 0.2168 - 0.1325 = \mathbf{0.0843}\;\text{pu} \approx \mathbf{84.3\;\text{MVAR}} $$ **Comment on independence (2 marks within part b):** Real power increased by **0.212 pu** purely due to $V_Q$ (with $V_P$ not affecting $P_s$ — its contribution is $V_s V_P \sin 0^\circ / X = 0$). Reactive power decreased by **0.1325 pu** purely due to $V_P$ (with $V_Q$ not affecting $Q_s$ — its contribution is $-V_s V_Q \cos 90^\circ / X = 0$). This confirms the **complete orthogonality** of $V_P$ and $V_Q$ in controlling $Q$ and $P$ respectively. --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]` **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the operating principle of the UPFC. Describe how it combines the functions of a STATCOM and an SSSC through a common DC link. List any three operating modes of the UPFC. **(b) [7 marks]** Compare the UPFC with four other FACTS controllers (SVC, STATCOM, TCSC, SSSC) in a tabular form with respect to: (i) connection type, (ii) controllable parameters, and (iii) ability to provide independent P–Q control. --- #### ✅ Model Solution **(a) Operating Principle & Modes [7 marks]** The UPFC consists of **two back-to-back voltage-source converters** (VSC-1 and VSC-2) sharing a **common DC capacitor** (no DC source — energy storage is dynamic): - **VSC-1 (Shunt — STATCOM mode):** Connected to the line through a shunt transformer. It injects a controllable reactive current to **regulate the bus voltage** and **draws/supplies real power from the AC bus to keep the DC link charged**. It cannot exchange sustained real power with the AC system beyond what is needed for capacitor balancing. - **VSC-2 (Series — SSSC mode):** Connected through a series transformer. It injects a **synchronous voltage $V_{pq}$ of controllable magnitude and phase** into the line. The injected voltage exchanges **real power** with the line (because $V_{pq}$ has a component in phase with the line current) — this real power is **drawn from the DC link**. The DC link is the **"real-power highway"** between the two converters: whatever real power VSC-2 injects/absorbs at the line is replenished by VSC-1 from the bus. This unification is the unique hallmark of UPFC. **(4 marks for the combined STATCOM–SSSC explanation.)** **Three operating modes (1 mark each):** 1. **Voltage Regulation Mode** — STATCOM only; $V_{pq} = 0$, shunt regulates $V_s$. 2. **Series Reactive Compensation Mode** — SSSC only; shunt supplies DC link only; series compensates $X$ effectively. 3. **Phase-Shifter Mode** — Series voltage in quadrature with $V_s$; simulates a phase-shifting transformer. 4. **Independent P–Q Flow Control Mode** — both $V_P$ and $V_Q$ used simultaneously; the most powerful UPFC mode. 5. **Power Flow Control Mode** — $V_P$ and $V_Q$ chosen to enforce a target $(P, Q)$ pair directly. *(List any three for full marks.)* **(b) Comparative Table [7 marks]** | Controller | Connection | Controllable Parameter(s) | Independent P–Q Control? | |---|---|---|---| | **SVC** (TCR + TSC) | Shunt | Bus voltage / reactive power | **No** (only Q) | | **STATCOM** | Shunt | Bus voltage / reactive power | **No** (only Q) | | **TCSC** | Series | Line reactance (effective) | **Limited** (affects P only, indirectly Q via voltage) | | **SSSC** | Series | Series voltage (injection angle) | **Partial** (P or Q, but coupled) | | **UPFC** | Shunt + Series | Bus voltage + line impedance + power angle | **Yes — full** independent P–Q control | (2 marks for table, 2 marks for connection type, 2 marks for parameter identification, 1 mark for the final conclusion that **only UPFC achieves true independent P–Q control**.) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout** > - **Sign Error in $\Delta Q_s$:** Many students write $\Delta Q_s = +V_s V_P / X$ instead of $-V_s V_P / X$. A positive $V_P$ (boosting voltage) **reduces** the reactive drop, hence Q decreases. This single sign error can cost **2 full marks**. > - **Forgetting the orthogonal condition:** You must state *both* that $V_P$ lies *along* $V_s$ and $V_Q$ lies *perpendicular* to $V_s$. The orthogonality is what guarantees independence. > - **Mixing $V_P$ and $V_Q$ with current components:** Some textbooks decompose $V_{pq}$ w.r.t. the current $I$ (not the voltage $V_s$). Be explicit about the reference you choose. The KTU convention is **w.r.t. $V_s$**. > - **Ignoring the DC link:** The "unified" part of UPFC is the real-power exchange via the DC link. A solution that omits this is incomplete. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4 / Independent P–Q Flow Control** - 🔑 **UPFC = STATCOM + SSSC + DC link.** The DC link is the enabler of unified operation. - 🔑 **Series voltage decomposition:** $\bar V_{pq} = V_P + jV_Q$ with $V_P \parallel V_s$ and $V_Q \perp V_s$ (leading). - 🔑 **Decoupled control law:** * $V_Q$ → **controls real power $P$** (adds $\Delta P = V_s V_Q / X$) * $V_P$ → **controls reactive power $Q$** (adds $\Delta Q = -V_s V_P / X$) - 🔑 **Canonical equations** (must memorise for KTU): * $P_s = V_s V_r \sin\delta / X + V_s V_Q / X$ * $Q_s = (V_s^{\,2} - V_s V_r \cos\delta)/X - V_s V_P / X$ - 🔑 **Operating circle:** $V_P^{\,2} + V_Q^{\,2} \le (V_{pq}^{\max})^{2}$ — defines feasible $(P, Q)$ set points. - 🔑 **Shunt branch** keeps $V_{DC}$ constant and regulates bus voltage; **series branch** injects $V_{pq}$ and provides P–Q control. - 🔑 **Five operating modes:** Voltage regulation, Series reactive compensation, Phase-shifting, P–Q flow control, Direct power-flow control. - 🔑 **Comparison to remember:** Only UPFC among FACTS devices offers **complete and simultaneous** independent control of $P$, $Q$, and bus voltage. - 🔑 **Sign convention:** Positive $V_Q$ (leading $V_s$) **increases** $P_s$; positive $V_P$ (in-phase with $V_s$) **decreases** $Q_s$. - 🔑 **Practical limit:** $V_{pq} \le 0.1$–$0.2$ pu typically — beyond this, converter rating and insulation become prohibitive. - 🔑 **Why the derivation matters:** KTU regularly asks for the KVL → current → complex power derivation. Practise writing it cold.

topics.title: Applications, Introduction to Interline Power Flow Controller (IPFC) (Principle of Operation and Schematic), Thyristor Controlled Voltage and Phase Angle Regulators (Principle of Operation and Schematic)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET862 - HVDC AND FACTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications, Introduction to Interline Power Flow Controller (IPFC) (Principle of Operation and Schematic), Thyristor Controlled Voltage and Phase Angle Regulators (Principle of Operation and Schematic)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Applications of FACTS Controllers in Power Systems

**Flexible AC Transmission System (FACTS)** controllers, as defined in the KTU 2024 syllabus, are power-electronics-based systems designed to enhance or provide controllability of AC transmission parameters — voltage, impedance, and phase angle — thereby increasing power transfer capability and stability margins of the grid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The end-of-semester questions on this module typically ask students to (a) draw and explain the **schematic** of a given FACTS device, (b) state the **principle of operation**, and (c) derive the **real/reactive power flow expressions** with the device inserted. Always answer in this order for full marks.

### Key Application Domains of FACTS

| Application Area | Function of FACTS Device |
|---|---|
| Power Flow Control | Redirects power through overloaded/under-loaded corridors |
| Voltage Regulation | Maintains bus voltage magnitude within statutory limits (**±5%** of nominal) |
| Transient Stability Enhancement | Increases post-fault synchronizing torque |
| Oscillation Damping (PSS-like action) | Damps low-frequency electromechanical modes (0.1–2 Hz) |
| Sub-synchronous Resonance (SSR) Mitigation | Tunes line impedance to decouple torsional frequencies |
| Loop Flow Elimination | Series compensators cancel the circulating power in meshed networks |

> [!NOTE]
> The devices covered in this topic — **IPFC, TCVR, and TCPAR** — are classified as **thyristor/GTO-based series-connected FACTS controllers** that primarily address the *power flow control* and *loop flow* applications.

---

## 1.2 Interline Power Flow Controller (IPFC)

### Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IPFC (Interline Power Flow Controller):** A FACTS controller proposed by **Gyugyi, Sen, and Schauder (1998)**. It consists of **two or more Voltage Source Converters (VSCs)** connected in series with **two or more transmission lines** of a substation, sharing a **common DC link** (common capacitor). Each series converter can independently inject a series voltage of controllable magnitude and phase angle into its respective line, allowing **simultaneous control of active power flow in multiple lines** plus the ability to transfer active power *between* the lines.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Shared Battery Bank" Imagine a multi-lane highway system where each lane (transmission line) has an electric scooter (VSC) that can push or pull the traffic flow. All scooters share the same rechargeable battery (DC link capacitor) parked at the toll booth. If Lane 1 is over-congested, Lane 2's scooter can transfer some of Lane 1's "energy burden" by pushing the traffic in Lane 1 and slowing down Lane 2 — both scooters are connected to the *same battery*, so the energy balance is maintained automatically.

### Operating Principle (One-Line Overview)

1. **Converter 1 (VSC-1)** is series-injected into **Line i** and injects a controllable voltage $\overline{V}_{1\angle \rho_1}$.
2. **Converter 2 (VSC-2)** is series-injected into **Line j** and injects a controllable voltage $\overline{V}_{2\angle \rho_2}$.
3. The two converters are tied at their DC terminals to a **common DC capacitor** $C_{dc}$.
4. By controlling the **phase angle of injected voltage** with respect to the line current, each converter absorbs/injects real power at its AC terminal.
5. The common DC link enforces the **power balance**:
$$P_{DC,1} + P_{DC,2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{1,ac} = -P_{2,ac}$$
6. Thus, real power absorbed by one line is supplied by the other, **without any external DC source**.

---

## 1.3 Thyristor Controlled Voltage Regulator (TCVR)

### Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TCVR (Thyristor Controlled Voltage Regulator):** A shunt–series hybrid FACTS device (a sub-class of the Unified Power Flow Controller family) that uses a **thyristor-switched tap-changing transformer** (or a thyristor-controlled reactor + thyristor-switched capacitor arrangement) to inject a **voltage in phase (or anti-phase) with the line end voltage**. The magnitude of the injected voltage is varied in discrete steps or in a quasi-continuous manner by firing-angle control, so as to **regulate the receiving-end voltage magnitude** and consequently control the active power flow.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Volume Knob for Voltage"

Think of a long garden hose carrying water (active power) where the pressure at the far end keeps dropping. You install a *variable booster pump* (TCVR) along the hose that can add or subtract a small amount of pressure in line with the existing flow.
