Twisting the knob (firing angle $\alpha$) smoothly changes the boost level — pressure up = more flow, pressure down = less flow. The pump only changes the *amplitude* of pressure, not its direction.

### Operating Principle

- A **thyristor-controlled tap changer** is implemented using a **Thyristor-Switched Capacitor (TSC)** bank and/or a **Thyristor-Controlled Reactor (TCR)** in the secondary of a series-exciting transformer.
- The thyristors are fired at a controlled firing angle $\alpha$ to vary the **fundamental component** of the injected voltage.
- The injected series voltage $\overline{V}_{se}$ is **in phase** with the line current (or with the line voltage) — i.e., its phase angle is held **at 0° or 180°** with respect to a chosen reference.

---

## 1.4 Thyristor Controlled Phase Angle Regulator (TCPAR)

### Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TCPAR (Thyristor Controlled Phase Angle Regulator):** A shunt–series FACTS device that uses **thyristor-switched tap combinations** on the secondary of a phase-shifting transformer to inject a **voltage in quadrature (perpendicular) with the line end voltage**. By varying the tap, it changes the **phase angle** of the sending-end voltage relative to the receiving end, thereby **directly controlling the active power transmitted** through the line without altering the voltage magnitude.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Steering Wheel for Power"

Imagine two rivers merging downstream, and you want a controlled amount of water from River A to flow into the main channel. You install a gate (phase-shifting transformer) that you can angle slightly to deflect the river's flow direction. Rotating the gate clockwise (positive phase shift) pushes more water in; counter-clockwise (negative phase shift) pushes less. Unlike TCVR which changes the *pressure*, TCPAR changes the *direction* of push.

### Operating Principle

- A **phase-shifting transformer** is built with thyristor-switched taps that can connect either polarity of the secondary winding to the line.
- The injected voltage $\overline{V}_{se}$ is maintained **at ±90°** (quadrature) to the line voltage.
- Result: only **phase angle $\sigma$** of the line-end voltage is changed, while magnitude remains essentially constant.

---

## 1.5 Comparison at a Glance

| Parameter | TCVR | TCPAR | IPFC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Injected Voltage Angle** | 0° or 180° (in-phase) | ±90° (quadrature) | Fully controllable (0°–360°) |
| **Controls** | Voltage magnitude | Phase angle | Both magnitude, angle, and **cross-line real power exchange** |
| **Number of Lines Controlled** | Single | Single | **Two or more (simultaneous)** |
| **Active Power Exchange Between Lines** | No | No | **Yes (via common DC link)** |
| **Switching Device** | Thyristor (TCR/TSC) | Thyristor (tap changer) | GTO/IGBT-based VSC |

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IPFC — Detailed Operational Analysis

### 2.1.1 Power Injection Model

Consider two transmission lines, **Line 1 (i)** and **Line 2 (j)**, emanating from a common sending-end bus $S$, terminated at receiving-end buses $R_1$ and $R_2$ respectively. Both lines have identical series reactance $X$ (for simplicity, losses neglected).

> [!NOTE]
> The *power injection model* is the **most important KTU question type** for IPFC. You must show the expressions for the power injected at the sending bus and at the receiving bus — examiners award 3–4 marks for these alone.

The complex power **injected by VSC-1 into Line 1** at the sending end is:

$$S_{1s} = \overline{V}_{1\angle \rho_1} \cdot \overline{I}_1^{*}$$

The complex power **absorbed from Line 2 by VSC-2** at the sending end is:

$$S_{2s} = -\overline{V}_{2\angle \rho_2} \cdot \overline{I}_2^{*}$$

The **DC link** enforces that the net real power at the DC bus is zero (lossless capacitor, with $C_{dc}$ large enough to hold voltage constant over a cycle):

$$P_{DC} = \text{Re}(S_{1s} + S_{2s}) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Re}(S_{1s}) = -\text{Re}(S_{2s})$$

This is the **active power balance equation** of the IPFC — the heart of its operation.

### 2.1.2 Three Degrees of Freedom per Converter

Each VSC of the IPFC can independently control **three quantities** simultaneously:
1. The **magnitude** of injected voltage $V_{se} \in [0, V_{se}^{max}]$
2. The **angle** of injected voltage $\rho \in [0, 2\pi]$
3. The **reactive power** exchanged with the line (handled locally by the VSC)

This is in contrast to TCVR (1 DOF: magnitude) and TCPAR (1 DOF: angle).

### 2.1.3 Why the Common DC Link Matters

If the two VSCs of the IPFC were *independent* (as in two SSSCs), each would need its own DC source to provide/absorb real power. By tying the DC terminals together:
- **One line's surplus real power** can directly **compensate the other line's deficit**.
- The DC capacitor stores the **ripple energy** and handles **dynamic transients**.
- The IPFC becomes **self-sufficient in real power** — a major advantage in retrofit applications.

---

## 2.2 TCVR — Detailed Operational Analysis

### 2.2.1 Series Voltage Injection

The **TCVR** is modelled as a controllable series voltage source $\overline{V}_{se}$ inserted between the sending-end bus $S$ and the line reactance $jX$. The injected voltage is:

$$\overline{V}_{se} = r \cdot k \cdot \overline{V}_{S}$$

where:
- $\overline{V}_S$ = sending-end voltage phasor
- $r \in [-1, 1]$ = effective boost ratio (controlled by thyristor firing)
- $k$ = turns ratio of the exciting transformer

The **effective receiving-end voltage** becomes:

$$\overline{V}_{R}^{eff} = \overline{V}_{S} + \overline{V}_{se} = (1 + rk)\overline{V}_{S}$$

The power transferred to the receiving end:

$$P_R = \frac{\vert \overline{V}_S + \overline{V}_{se}\vert \cdot \vert \overline{V}_R\vert}{X}\sin\delta$$

> [!NOTE]
> The angle $\delta$ between the two ends remains **unchanged** — only the *amplitude* $(1+rk)\vert\overline{V}_S\vert$ is varied. This is the defining signature of a TCVR in KTU problems.

### 2.2.2 TCR + TSC Hybrid Realization

A practical TCVR uses a **fixed series transformer** whose secondary is loaded by:
- A **TCR branch** (thyristor-controlled reactor) — provides *continuously variable* inductive current
- A **TSC branch** (thyristor-switched capacitor) — provides *stepped* capacitive current

The two branches are **anti-parallel**, so by varying the firing angle $\alpha_{TCR} \in [90°, 180°]$ and switching the TSC steps ON/OFF, the *net reactive current* (and hence the series voltage) can be varied in **fine steps**.

### 2.2.3 Real and Reactive Power Exchange

The series-injected voltage is **in-phase** with the line current, so:
- **Real power exchanged** = $P_{se} = V_{se} \cdot I \cdot \cos(\angle \overline{V}_{se} - \angle \overline{I})$ — typically **non-zero** and must be supplied by the AC system itself
- **Reactive power exchanged** = $Q_{se} = V_{se} \cdot I \cdot \sin(\angle \overline{V}_{se} - \angle \overline{I})$ — when $\overline{V}_{se}$ is in quadrature with $\overline{I}$, **purely reactive**

For KTU purposes, assume the thyristor switches enforce the in-phase condition, so the net real power exchanged is **ideally zero per cycle** (since the in-phase voltage × line current at quadrature lag gives pure Q).

---

## 2.3 TCPAR — Detailed Operational Analysis

### 2.3.1 Phase-Shift Voltage Injection

The **TCPAR** injects a **quadrature voltage** $\overline{V}_{se}$ that is **perpendicular** to the line voltage $\overline{V}_S$. The result is that the *phase* of the voltage at the receiving end shifts by an angle $\sigma$, while the magnitude is preserved.

Mathematically:

$$\overline{V}_{S,new} = \overline{V}_S + \overline{V}_{se} = \vert\overline{V}_S\vert \cdot e^{j(\delta + \sigma)}$$

where $\sigma = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{V_{se}}{V_S}\right)$.

The active power flow:

$$P_R = \frac{\vert \overline{V}_S\vert \cdot \vert \overline{V}_R\vert}{X}\sin(\delta + \sigma) - \frac{\vert \overline{V}_S\vert \cdot \vert \overline{V}_R\vert}{X}\sin(\delta)$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{V_S V_R}{X}[\sin(\delta+\sigma) - \sin(\delta)]}$$

By controlling $\sigma$ through the thyristor tap switching, $\Delta P$ can be set to any desired value within bounds.

### 2.3.2 Two-Transformer Quadrature Booster Realization

A practical TCPAR is built using **two transformers**:
1. A **series (exciter) transformer** — injects $\overline{V}_{se}$ in the line.
2. A **shunt (exciting) transformer** — provides the quadrature voltage reference from a tertiary winding.

The thyristor switches in the secondary can be configured in **eight combinations** (using two anti-parallel thyristor pairs per tap), giving a quasi-continuous range of $\sigma \in [-15°, +15°]$ typically.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table** — questions of 7–14 marks in KTU ESE are usually built from these equations.

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Device |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{V_S V_R}{X}\sin\delta$ | Uncompensated power flow | All |
| 2 | $P^{TCVR} = \dfrac{(1+rk)V_S V_R}{X}\sin\delta$ | Power with TCVR | TCVR |
| 3 | $P^{TCPAR} = \dfrac{V_S V_R}{X}\sin(\delta+\sigma)$ | Power with TCPAR | TCPAR |
| 4 | $Q^{TCVR} = \dfrac{(1+rk)V_S}{X}\bigl[(1+rk)V_S - V_R\cos\delta\bigr]$ | Reactive power at sending end, TCVR | TCVR |
| 5 | $S_{1s} = \overline{V}_{1\angle\rho_1}\cdot \overline{I}_1^{*}$ | Complex power injection, IPFC Line 1 | IPFC |
| 6 | $S_{2s} = -\overline{V}_{2\angle\rho_2}\cdot \overline{I}_2^{*}$ | Complex power injection, IPFC Line 2 | IPFC |
| 7 | $\text{Re}(S_{1s}) + \text{Re}(S_{2s}) = 0$ | DC-link real power balance | IPFC |
| 8 | $\Delta P^{IPFC} = \dfrac{V_1 V_{se,1}}{X_1}\sin(\delta_1 - \rho_1)$ | ΔP on Line 1 due to VSC-1 | IPFC |
| 9 | $V_{se,TCR} = V_m \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_{TCR} - \sin\sigma_{TCR}}{\pi}$ | TCR fundamental output voltage | TCVR/TCPAR |
| 10 | $\sigma = \arctan(V_{se}/V_S)$ | Phase-shift angle | TCPAR |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Caution:** Never write $\vert x\vert$ with the bare vertical bar inside a markdown table — use $\lvert x\rvert$ or $\vert x \vert$ with LaTeX escapes to avoid breaking table syntax. KTU answer scripts that use Word/Excel tables will likewise break the layout.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **TCVR** is the FACTS alternative to a conventional **On-Load Tap Changer (OLTC)** — much faster (half-cycle response vs. seconds) and capable of *dynamic* voltage regulation during large disturbances.
- **TCPAR** is the FACTS alternative to a mechanical **Phase-Shifting Transformer (PST)** — used to redirect power between parallel transmission corridors in meshed grids.
- **IPFC** is used at **substations with 2+ transmission outlets** (common at 400 kV/220 kV hubs) — it solves the *uneven loading* problem where one outgoing line is overloaded and another is under-loaded, by *transferring active power* from the under-loaded line to the overloaded one.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: IPFC Power Injection at Sending and Receiving Buses

This is a **14-mark KTU favourite**. The examiner expects:
1. A single-line diagram (2 marks)
2. Phasor diagram (2 marks)
3. Power injection at the sending end (4 marks)
4. Power injection at the receiving end (4 marks)
5. DC-link balance (2 marks)

### Setup

Consider two lines, **Line 1** (reactance $X_1$) and **Line 2** (reactance $X_2$), with sending-end voltage $\overline{V}_S$ common to both, and receiving-end voltages $\overline{V}_{R1}$ and $\overline{V}_{R2}$. The IPFC injects series voltages $\overline{V}_{1\angle \rho_1}$ and $\overline{V}_{2\angle \rho_2}$ at the sending ends of Lines 1 and 2.

### Step 1 — Line 1 current

The current in Line 1 (assuming the VSC is between the sending bus and the line reactance $X_1$):

$$\overline{I}_1 = \frac{\overline{V}_S + \overline{V}_{1\angle\rho_1} - \overline{V}_{R1}}{jX_1}$$

**Textual logic:** Apply KVL around the loop: source $\overline{V}_S$ plus the injected voltage $\overline{V}_{1\angle\rho_1}$ drives the line reactance $X_1$ to deliver power to the load $\overline{V}_{R1}$. The denominator is $jX_1$ because the line is purely inductive.

### Step 2 — Power injected by VSC-1 at the sending end of Line 1

The complex power delivered **by VSC-1 into Line 1** at the sending-end side is:

$$S_{1s} = \overline{V}_{1\angle\rho_1} \cdot \overline{I}_1^{*}$$

Substituting the conjugate of $\overline{I}_1$:

$$S_{1s} = \overline{V}_{1\angle\rho_1} \cdot \left[\frac{\overline{V}_S + \overline{V}_{1\angle\rho_1} - \overline{V}_{R1}}{-jX_1}\right]$$

$$S_{1s} = \frac{j}{X_1} \cdot \overline{V}_{1\angle\rho_1} \cdot \left[\overline{V}_S + \overline{V}_{1\angle\rho_1} - \overline{V}_{R1}\right]^{*}$$

**Textual logic:** The injection model convention is: the series voltage source $\overline{V}_{1\angle\rho_1}$ delivers complex power equal to its terminal voltage times the conjugate of the current *leaving* its positive terminal. Since the line current $\overline{I}_1$ enters the negative terminal, the power delivered is $\overline{V}_{1\angle\rho_1}\cdot\overline{I}_1^{*}$.

### Step 3 — Real and reactive components of $S_{1s}$

Expanding the product and taking real/imaginary parts:

$$P_{1s} = \text{Re}(S_{1s}) = \frac{V_1 V_S}{X_1}\sin(\rho_1 - \theta_{V_S}) + \frac{V_1^2}{X_1}\sin(0) - \frac{V_1 V_{R1}}{X_1}\sin(\rho_1 - \theta_{R1})$$

Since $\sin(0) = 0$, the middle term vanishes:

$$\boxed{P_{1s} = \frac{V_1}{X_1}\left[V_S\sin(\rho_1 - \theta_{V_S}) - V_{R1}\sin(\rho_1 - \theta_{R1})\right]}$$

Similarly:

$$\boxed{Q_{1s} = \text{Im}(S_{1s}) = \frac{V_1}{X_1}\left[V_S\cos(\rho_1 - \theta_{V_S}) - V_{R1}\cos(\rho_1 - \theta_{R1})\right] + \frac{V_1^2}{X_1}}$$

**Textual logic:** The reactive term picks up an extra $V_1^2 / X_1$ because the voltage source *itself* stores reactive energy in its own internal reactance — a classical result for any voltage-source-injected series branch.

### Step 4 — Power injected at the receiving end of Line 1

The current direction is identical, but the sending-end and receiving-end voltages swap roles. The complex power received at bus $R_1$ is:

$$S_{1R} = \overline{V}_{R1} \cdot (-\overline{I}_1)^{*}$$

$$S_{1R} = -\frac{j}{X_1} \cdot \overline{V}_{R1} \cdot \left[\overline{V}_S + \overline{V}_{1\angle\rho_1} - \overline{V}_{R1}\right]^{*}$$

### Step 5 — Real power at receiving end of Line 1

$$\boxed{P_{1R} = \frac{V_S V_{R1}}{X_1}\sin(\theta_{V_S} - \theta_{R1}) - \frac{V_1 V_{R1}}{X_1}\sin(\rho_1 - \theta_{R1})}$$

**Textual logic:** The first term is the *natural* power flow $P_{R1}^{(0)} = V_S V_{R1} \sin\delta_1 / X_1$. The second term is the *adjustment* $\Delta P_{1R}$ that the IPFC introduces. The angle $\rho_1$ is the control handle.

### Step 6 — DC-link power balance

For the DC capacitor of the IPFC (assuming lossless converters and a large $C_{dc}$ with $\tfrac{d V_{dc}}{dt} = 0$ in steady state):

$$P_{DC,1} + P_{DC,2} = 0$$

$$\text{Re}(S_{1s}) + \text{Re}(S_{2s}) = 0$$

$$\boxed{P_{1s} + P_{2s} = 0}$$

**Textual logic:** Whatever real power VSC-1 injects into Line 1 must be absorbed by VSC-2 from Line 2 — the DC bus acts as the *energy broker*. This is the **unique distinguishing feature** of the IPFC compared to independent SSSCs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: TCVR Boosted Power Flow

### Step 1 — Phasor setup

Line of reactance $X$ between sending bus $S$ (voltage $\overline{V}_S = V_S \angle 0$) and receiving bus $R$ (voltage $\overline{V}_R = V_R \angle -\delta$). The TCVR injects $\overline{V}_{se} = r k V_S \angle 0$ (in-phase with $\overline{V}_S$).

### Step 2 — Effective sending-end voltage

$$\overline{V}_S^{eff} = \overline{V}_S + \overline{V}_{se} = V_S(1 + rk)\angle 0$$

### Step 3 — Power flow to receiving end

$$P_R = \frac{\vert \overline{V}_S^{eff}\vert \cdot \vert \overline{V}_R\vert}{X}\sin\delta = \frac{V_S V_R (1 + rk)}{X}\sin\delta$$

**Textual logic:** Since $\overline{V}_{se}$ is in-phase with $\overline{V}_S$, the *magnitude* of the effective source voltage increases by the factor $(1 + rk)$. The angle $\delta$ is unaffected, so the sine factor is preserved.

### Step 4 — Reactive power

$$Q_S = \frac{V_S^{eff\,2}}{X} - \frac{V_S^{eff} V_R}{X}\cos\delta = \frac{V_S^2 (1 + rk)^2}{X} - \frac{V_S V_R (1 + rk)}{X}\cos\delta$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: TCPAR Phase-Shifted Power Flow

### Step 1 — Setup

The TCPAR injects $\overline{V}_{se} = V_{se}\angle(\theta_{V_S} \pm 90°)$ — perpendicular to $\overline{V}_S$.

### Step 2 — Effective voltage phasor

$$\overline{V}_S^{eff} = \overline{V}_S + \overline{V}_{se}$$

The magnitude remains $\vert\overline{V}_S\vert$ (by construction of the quadrature booster), and the angle is shifted by $\sigma = \arctan(V_{se}/V_S)$.

$$\overline{V}_S^{eff} = V_S \angle(\delta \pm \sigma) \quad \text{when referenced to } \overline{V}_R = V_R\angle 0$$

### Step 3 — Power flow

$$P_R = \frac{V_S V_R}{X}\sin(\delta + \sigma)$$

For the case $V_S = V_R = V$:

$$P_R = \frac{V^2}{X}\sin(\delta + \sigma)$$

### Step 4 — Change in power

$$\boxed{\Delta P = P_R^{TCPAR} - P_R^{uncomp} = \frac{V^2}{X}[\sin(\delta + \sigma) - \sin\delta]}$$

**Textual logic:** This is the **incremental power** that the TCPAR adds by phase shifting. For $\sigma = 0$, $\Delta P = 0$ (no action). For $\sigma > 0$, power increases. For $\sigma < 0$, power decreases. KTU questions often ask for the **maximum $\Delta P$** — set $d(\Delta P)/d\sigma = 0$ to find the optimum.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (for KTU 2-mark questions)

| Property | TCVR | TCPAR | IPFC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Compensation type** | Voltage magnitude | Phase angle | Multiple: V, δ, P-flow |
| **Phasor diagram shape** | Triangle (in-phase) | Triangle (perpendicular) | Arbitrary |
| **Source of real power** | None external (relies on AC system) | None external | DC link between lines |
| **Speed of response** | Half-cycle (8.33 ms at 60 Hz) | One tap step (~1–2 cycles) | Half-cycle |
| **Converter type** | Thyristor (line-commutated) | Thyristor (line-commutated) | VSC (self-commutated GTO/IGBT) |
| **KTU mark weight** | 7 marks | 7 marks | 14 marks |

## 4.4 Phasor Diagram Reference (Textual)

While a phasor diagram cannot be rendered in Mermaid, here is the **standard KTU phasor layout** for a TCPAR — the most-asked diagram in this module:

1. Draw $\overline{V}_R$ along the **positive real axis** (reference).
2. Draw $\overline{V}_S$ at angle $\delta$ **above** $\overline{V}_R$.
3. Draw the injected $\overline{V}_{se}$ **perpendicular** to $\overline{V}_S$ (either lead or lag by 90°).
4. The vector sum $\overline{V}_S + \overline{V}_{se}$ gives the new effective sending voltage at angle $(\delta + \sigma)$.
5. Mark the angle $\sigma$ clearly between $\overline{V}_S$ and $\overline{V}_S^{eff}$ — this is the **phase shift introduced**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examiners **will** deduct 1–2 marks if the phasor diagram is missing. Always pair a schematic with a phasor.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**

**Q1.** *List any **three applications** of FACTS controllers in modern power transmission systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Power flow control** along transmission corridors — to redirect power from overloaded to under-loaded lines and maximize utilization of existing assets.
2. **Voltage regulation and reactive power compensation** at critical buses — to maintain bus voltage magnitude within statutory limits (typically ±5% of nominal) and reduce $I^2R$ losses.
3. **Enhancement of transient and dynamic stability** of the grid — by rapidly adjusting the synchronizing torque coefficient $P_S \cdot \delta$ during large disturbances.

> *(Alternative accepted answers: Sub-synchronous resonance mitigation, low-frequency oscillation damping, loop flow elimination, loadability improvement of long lines.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**

**Q2.** *What is the **distinguishing feature** of an IPFC compared to two independently operating SSSCs?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The distinguishing feature of an IPFC is the **common DC link** shared between two or more Voltage Source Converters (VSCs) connected in series with different transmission lines at a common substation.

1. *Independent SSSCs:* Each series-connected VSC requires its own external DC power source (or storage element) to supply/absorb real power.
2. *IPFC:* The DC terminals of the multiple VSCs are tied to a **common capacitor**, enabling **direct real-power exchange between the lines** they are connected to. The real power absorbed from one line is automatically supplied to the other line through the shared DC bus, satisfying $\text{Re}(S_{1s}) + \text{Re}(S_{2s}) = 0$.
3. This makes the IPFC **self-sufficient in active power** and enables simultaneous control of multiple line power flows plus the *cross-line* active power transfer — a capability no combination of independent SSSCs can offer.

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper, Module 4 Q3(a)]**

**Question A (14 marks):**

(a) **Draw the schematic of an Interline Power Flow Controller (IPFC) and explain its principle of operation with the help of a phasor diagram.** *(7 marks)*

(b) **Derive the expressions for the real and reactive power injected by the IPFC at the sending end and the receiving end of one of the controlled lines. State the DC-link power balance equation.** *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution

#### (a) Schematic and Principle (7 marks)

**Schematic (2 marks):** The student must draw two VSC boxes (labelled VSC-1 and VSC-2) connected in series with two transmission lines, with their DC terminals tied to a common capacitor $C_{dc}$. The figure should also show the central IPFC controller and the series injection transformers.

*(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

**Principle of operation (5 marks):**

1. **Two VSC-based series converters (VSC-1, VSC-2)** are installed in series with two transmission lines emanating from a common substation. Each VSC is interfaced to its line through a **series injection transformer** and a **DC-side capacitor**.
2. The **DC terminals** of VSC-1 and VSC-2 are connected to a **common DC link capacitor** $C_{dc}$. The capacitor is pre-charged to a reference DC voltage $V_{dc}^{ref}$ and is sized to limit ripple to less than 5–10% under transient conditions.
3. Each VSC can independently synthesize a series voltage of **controllable magnitude $V_k$** and **controllable phase angle $\rho_k$**, where $k = 1, 2$. The injection angle $\rho_k$ can be set anywhere in $[0°, 360°]$ with respect to the line-end voltage.
4. The **real power** exchanged between the VSC and the line depends on the projection of $\overline{V}_k$ onto the line current phasor $\overline{I}_k$. By adjusting $\rho_k$ to be in phase with $\overline{I}_k$, the VSC *supplies* real power; by making it anti-phase, the VSC *absorbs* real power.
5. The **DC-link balance** equation $\text{Re}(S_{1s}) + \text{Re}(S_{2s}) = 0$ ensures that whatever real power VSC-1 injects into Line 1 is absorbed by VSC-2 from Line 2. This enables the IPFC to **simultaneously control** the active power flow in two (or more) lines and to **transfer active power from one line to another** — a unique capability.
6. **Reactive power** is exchanged locally by each VSC with its own line; no reactive power flows through the DC link. This decoupling of real and reactive power control is a key feature of VSC-based FACTS devices.

**Phasor diagram (must accompany):** $\overline{V}_S$ as reference, $\overline{V}_{R1}$ at angle $-\delta_1$, injected $\overline{V}_{1\angle\rho_1}$ at angle $\rho_1$, effective voltage $\overline{V}_S + \overline{V}_{1\angle\rho_1}$ shown clearly.

#### (b) Power Injection Derivations (7 marks)

**Sending-end complex power injection (3 marks):**

$$S_{1s} = \overline{V}_{1\angle\rho_1} \cdot \overline{I}_1^{*} = \overline{V}_{1\angle\rho_1}\left[\frac{\overline{V}_S + \overline{V}_{1\angle\rho_1} - \overline{V}_{R1}}{-jX_1}\right]^{*}$$

Real part:

$$P_{1s} = \frac{V_1 V_S}{X_1}\sin(\rho_1 - \theta_{V_S}) - \frac{V_1 V_{R1}}{X_1}\sin(\rho_1 - \theta_{R1})$$

**Receiving-end complex power (3 marks):**

$$S_{1R} = -\frac{j}{X_1}\cdot\overline{V}_{R1}\cdot\left[\overline{V}_S + \overline{V}_{1\angle\rho_1} - \overline{V}_{R1}\right]^{*}$$

Real part:

$$P_{1R} = \frac{V_S V_{R1}}{X_1}\sin(\theta_{V_S} - \theta_{R1}) - \frac{V_1 V_{R1}}{X_1}\sin(\rho_1 - \theta_{R1})$$

**DC-link balance (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{P_{1s} + P_{2s} = 0}$$

## 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Main Paper, Module 4 Q4(b)]**

**Question B (14 marks):**

(a) **Explain with a neat schematic the principle of operation of a Thyristor Controlled Voltage Regulator (TCVR) and a Thyristor Controlled Phase Angle Regulator (TCPAR).** *(7 marks)*

(b) **Derive the active power flow equation for a transmission line equipped with (i) a TCVR and (ii) a TCPAR. Compare their relative effectiveness in controlling the line power flow.** *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution

#### (a) Schematics and Principles (7 marks)

**TCVR (3.5 marks):**

- A **series transformer** is installed in the line; its primary carries the line current.
- The **secondary** is loaded by a **TCR** (thyristor-controlled reactor) and a **TSC** (thyristor-switched capacitor) in anti-parallel.
- The **thyristor firing angle $\alpha \in [90°, 180°]$** for the TCR varies the fundamental component of the secondary current, which in turn varies the magnitude of the induced series voltage $V_{se}$ in the line.
- The injected series voltage $\overline{V}_{se}$ is maintained **in-phase** with $\overline{V}_S$ (i.e., phase angle 0° or 180°), so only the *magnitude* of the receiving-end voltage is changed.
- The receiving-end voltage magnitude is varied in **discrete fine steps** (each TSC step) plus a **continuous component** (TCR contribution).

**TCPAR (3.5 marks):**

- A **two-transformer quadrature booster** arrangement is used: a **shunt (exciter) transformer** and a **series (booster) transformer**.
- The **secondary of the shunt transformer** feeds the **primary of the series transformer** through a **thyristor-switched tap bank**.
- The thyristor switches select from **eight possible tap combinations** (positive/negative, leading/lagging), producing an injected series voltage $\overline{V}_{se}$ that is **perpendicular (quadrature)** to $\overline{V}_S$.
- The net effect is a **phase shift $\sigma$** of the sending-end voltage, while its magnitude is essentially preserved.
- Typical phase-shift range: $\sigma \in [-15°, +15°]$, suitable for power-flow redistribution between parallel corridors.

#### (b) Power Flow Derivations and Comparison (7 marks)

**(i) TCVR Power Flow (3 marks):**

Effective sending voltage:

$$\overline{V}_S^{eff} = (1 + rk)\overline{V}_S$$

Active power:

$$\boxed{P_R^{TCVR} = \frac{V_S V_R (1 + rk)}{X}\sin\delta}$$

**(ii) TCPAR Power Flow (3 marks):**

Phase-shifted effective voltage:

$$\overline{V}_S^{eff} = V_S\angle(\delta + \sigma)$$

Active power:

$$\boxed{P_R^{TCPAR} = \frac{V_S V_R}{X}\sin(\delta + \sigma)}$$

**Comparison (1 mark):**

- **TCVR** changes the *magnitude* of the sending-end voltage → regulates $V_R$ and incidentally changes $P_R$ since $P \propto V$. It is more effective for **voltage-weak buses** and **load-absorption** problems.
- **TCPAR** changes the *phase angle* of the sending-end voltage → directly controls $P_R$ since $P \propto \sin(\delta + \sigma)$. It is more effective for **loop-flow control** in meshed networks and for **corridor power-redistribution**.
- The **TCPAR is the more direct power-flow controller** because it operates on the *angle* term, which has a much higher sensitivity (the sine function) than the magnitude term.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic — read carefully:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the DC-link balance equation in IPFC answers (loses 1–2 marks).** Many students draw the schematic correctly but omit $P_{1s} + P_{2s} = 0$. This is the *defining* equation of an IPFC — the examiner expects it explicitly.
>
> 2. **Confusing the phasor angles of TCVR and TCPAR (loses 1 mark).** TCVR injects voltage *in phase* (0° or 180°) with $\overline{V}_S$; TCPAR injects voltage *in quadrature* (±90°) with $\overline{V}_S$. A common mistake is to draw the TCPAR's $V_{se}$ in-phase, which is wrong and shows conceptual confusion.
>
> 3. **Omitting the $V_1^2 / X_1$ term in the reactive power expression (loses 1 mark).** The full IPFC reactive power injection at the sending end is $Q_{1s} = (V_1/X_1)[V_S\cos(\rho_1 - \theta_{V_S}) - V_{R1}\cos(\rho_1 - \theta_{R1})] + V_1^2/X_1$. Students often drop the last term.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to show the "control" arrows on the schematic (loses 0.5–1 mark).** A schematic without the controller box and the control signal arrows is considered incomplete.
>
> 5. **Using wrong line-conventions in phasor diagrams (loses 1 mark).** Receiving-end voltage is conventionally the **reference** (at 0°); sending-end is at $+\delta$. Mixing this up is a common slip.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision — 60-second sweep:**

- [ ] **FACTS applications** span power-flow control, voltage regulation, stability enhancement, SSR mitigation, and loop-flow elimination.
- [ ] **IPFC** = **two or more VSCs in series with multiple lines, sharing a common DC link**. Unique feature: real power exchange *between lines*. Defining equation: $P_{1s} + P_{2s} = 0$.
- [ ] **TCVR** = thyristor-based series voltage injection **in phase** with $\overline{V}_S$. Controls **magnitude** of receiving-end voltage. Uses TCR + TSC.
- [ ] **TCPAR** = thyristor-based series voltage injection **in quadrature** with $\overline{V}_S$. Controls **phase angle** $\sigma$ of the line-end voltage. Uses quadrature booster transformer.
- [ ] **Master power-flow equations:**
  - Uncompensated: $P = V_S V_R \sin\delta / X$
  - TCVR: $P = (1+rk) V_S V_R \sin\delta / X$
  - TCPAR: $P = V_S V_R \sin(\delta+\sigma) / X$
- [ ] **IPFC sending-end real power** (Line 1): $P_{1s} = (V_1/X_1)\bigl[V_S \sin(\rho_1 - \theta_{V_S}) - V_{R1}\sin(\rho_1 - \theta_{R1})\bigr]$.
- [ ] **IPFC receiving-end real power** (Line 1): $P_{1R} = (V_S V_{R1}/X_1)\sin\delta_1 - (V_1 V_{R1}/X_1)\sin(\rho_1 - \theta_{R1})$.
- [ ] **TCPAR is more direct** for power flow control (operates on the sine-angle term, which is highly sensitive). TCVR is more suited for voltage-weak buses.
- [ ] **IPFC requires VSC technology** (GTO/IGBT); TCVR and TCPAR can be built with **conventional thyristors** (line-commutated).
- [ ] **Phasor diagrams** must accompany any schematic — they carry **2 marks** by themselves.
- [ ] **KTU 14-mark question** always pairs a schematic (2 marks) + phasor (2 marks) + operation (4 marks) + derivation (4–6 marks) + DC-link/final equation (1–2 marks).
- [ ] **Schematic must include:** controller box, control arrows, DC capacitor (IPFC only), series transformer, line reactance, and labeled injected voltages.
